6.3: kadosh kadosh kadosh YHWH tsvaot melo kol-haaretz kevodo (qādôš qādôš qādôš Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt, mĕlōʾ kāl-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdô): 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory.' The Trisagion is the foundational liturgical text of Jewish and Christian worship. Kadosh (holy) in Hebrew means primarily 'set apart, other, distinct' — YHWH is wholly other from creation. The threefold repetition (shalosh) conveys absolute superlative in Hebrew (cf. Gen 14:10: 'many/many/many pits' = full of pits; Jer 22:29: 'O earth, earth, earth') — not a Trinitarian formula in context, but available for Trinitarian appropriation given its threefold address. Tsvaot (of hosts/armies): YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies — the most frequent divine title in Isaiah (62 occurrences in the book).
6.1: bišnat-mût hammelek ʿuzziyyāhû = "in the year that King Uzziah died" — 740 BCE. The historical anchor is also theological: Uzziah died as a leper after attempting to usurp the priestly prerogative (2 Chr 26:16-21). Isaiah enters the holy exactly where Uzziah failed — but through vision and prophetic commission. wāʾereʾ ʾet-ʾădōnāy yōšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ rām wĕniśśāʾ = "I saw the Lord (ʾădōnāy, sovereign title) sitting on a high and exalted (rām wĕniśśāʾ) throne." This same pair (yārûm wĕniśśāʾ) is applied to the Suffering Servant in 52:13 — the Servant's exaltation echoes the divine throne-vision. wĕšûlāyw mĕlĕʾîm ʾet-hahêkāl = "and his skirt-train (šûl) filled the temple" — the divine robe's overflow fills the entire sacred space. Divine plenitude overflowing the sacred container.
6.2: śĕrāpîm ʿōmĕdîm mimmaʿal lô = "seraphim were standing above him" — śārāp (H8314) = burning ones, from śārap (to burn). These beings appear only in this passage. šēš kĕnāpayim šēš kĕnāpayim = "six wings, six wings" — two cover the face (divine presence is overwhelming even for these beings), two cover the feet (euphemism for genitals: covering their own creaturely nakedness before God), two for flying. The posture: both homage (self-covering) and readiness (wings for flight).
6.4: wayyānūʿû ʾammôt hassippîm miqōl haqqôrēʾ = "the foundations of the thresholds (ʾammôt hassippîm) shook at the voice of the one calling" — theophanic structural trembling (cf. Exod 19:18; Ps 29:3-9; Rev 15:8). wĕhabbayit yimmālēʾ ʿāšān = "and the house was filled with smoke (ʿāšān)" — divine smoke/cloud as the medium of theophany (cf. Exod 19:18; 1 Kgs 8:10-12). The visual, auditory, and structural elements of the theophany are total.
6.5: wāʾōmar ʾôy-lî kî-nidmêtî = "Woe to me, for I am undone/destroyed (nidmêtî)." Nidmêtî = niphal perfect of dāmâ (H1820) = to be cut off, destroyed, silenced. Isaiah experiences the approach of death that proximity to the holy court risks without purification (cf. Exod 33:20). kî ʾîš ṭĕmēʾ-śĕpātayim ʾānōkî = "for I am a man of unclean lips" — the mouth is the prophet's instrument; his self-indictment goes to the very organ of his calling. ûḇĕtôk ʿam-ṭĕmēʾ śĕpātayim ʾānōkî yōšēḇ = "and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips" — the prophet shares the collective guilt of those he will address.
6.6: wayyāʿop ʾēlay ʾeḥāḏ min-haśśĕrāpîm = "and one of the seraphim flew to me." ûḇĕyādô rizpâ = "and in his hand was a live coal (rizpâ, H7531)" — glowing coal from the altar. bĕmilqāḥayim lāqaḥ mēʿal hammizḇēaḥ = "which he had taken with tongs (milqāḥayim) from the altar" — even the seraph does not handle the altar-fire bare; the holiness of the altar-fire is mediated even for heavenly beings.
6.7: wayyaggaʿ ʿal-pî = "and he touched it to my mouth" — the organ of prophetic speech is the point of purification. hinnēh nāgaʿ zeh ʿal-śĕpātêkā wĕṣāḇ ʿăwōnêkā = "Behold, this has touched your lips and your iniquity (ʿāwōn) is removed (ṣāḇ, from sûr) and your sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt) is atoned (kāpar, pual passive)." Two distinct terms: ʿāwōn (guilt-status) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (the act and consequence). Both are dealt with through the burning coal from the altar — pre-Calvary atonement pointing forward to its ground in Christ's sacrifice.
6.8:
wāʾešmaʿ ʾet-qôl ʾădōnāy ʾōmēr ʾet-mî ʾešlaḥ ûmî yēlēk-lānû = "I heard the voice of the Lord saying: Whom shall I send and who will go for us?" — the divine deliberation in the heavenly council (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19-22; Job 1-2). Lānû = "for us" — the plural is theologically significant: the divine council, anticipating Trinitarian plurality. YHWH does not conscript; he asks. The open question invites voluntary response.

wāʾōmar hinnēnî šĕlāḥēnî = "Here am I (hinnēnî)! Send me." Hinnēnî = the word of divine and human readiness/availability (Gen 22:1; 1 Sam 3:4-8). Isaiah's response comes from someone who has experienced both his own sinfulness (v5) and purification (vv6-7); the "send me" is informed, not naive. NT: the identical posture is attributed to the incarnate Christ in Heb 2:13.
6.9:
wayyōʾmer lēk wĕʾāmartā lāʿām hazzeh šimʿû šāmôaʿ wĕʾal-tāḇînû = "Go and say: Keep on hearing (šāmôaʿ, infinitive absolute + finite verb = emphatic continual hearing) but do not understand (bîn)." The hardening commission. The doubled infinitive absolute (šāmôaʿ/rĕʾô) makes the seeing and hearing infinitely repeated but without resulting comprehension.

This is among the most NT-cited OT passages: Matt 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27; Rom 11:8. The hardening results from persistent rejection of the prophetic word — each refusal desensitizes further; the prophet's ministry accelerates this process by confronting a people already committed to rejection. wĕrĕʾû rāʾô wĕʾal-tēdāʿû = "keep on seeing but do not know" — perception without transformation.
6.10:
hašmēn lēḇ hāʿām hazzeh = "Make the heart of this people fat (šāmēn)" — šāmēn (piel imperative) = to make fat, dull, insensitive; a heart so covered in fat it cannot feel. wĕʾoznāyw haḵḇēd = "make its ears heavy (kāḇēd)" — unable to lift/respond. wĕʿênāyw hāšaʿ = "and blind its eyes."

Theologically: Isaiah is commanded to declare truth in a way that results in hardening. The historical explanation: each refusal desensitizes further; the prophet's ministry accelerates the process already underway. John 12:40 applies it to Jewish rejection of Jesus; Paul (Rom 11:25) calls it a partial hardening until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in. The hardening is never YHWH's final word: v13 ends with the holy seed in the stump.
6.11: wāʾōmar ʿad-māttay ʾădōnāy = "And I said, How long, O Lord?" — Isaiah's intercession. wāyyōʾmer ʿad ʾăšer-ʾim-šāʾû ʿārîm mēʾên yôšēḇ = "Until cities lie waste without inhabitant." The hardening commission lasts until total desolation — exile, abandoned houses, devastated land. The answer is sobering: the darkening continues until judgment is complete.
6.12: wĕriḥaq YHWH ʾet-hāʾādām = "and YHWH removes mankind far away" — the exile described at its theological core: not merely deportation by armies but YHWH himself removing (riḥaq = to put far away) the people. wĕraḇâ hāʿăzûḇâ bĕqereḇ hāʾāreṣ = "and the abandonment (ʿăzûḇâ) multiplied in the land" — the deserted landscape. ʿĂzûḇâ is also a personal name meaning "forsaken," making the land itself personified as the abandoned one.
6.13: wĕʿôd bāh ʿăśîriyyâ wĕšāḇâ wĕhāyĕtâ lĕḇaʿēr = "if a tenth remains and returns, it will be burned again" — even the tenth (the tithe-proportion) faces the fire. Yet: kāʾēlâ wĕkāʾallôn ʾăšer bĕšalleket maṣṣeḇet bām = "like a terebinth and oak whose stump (maṣṣeḇet) remains when felled" — apparent totality of destruction leaves a stump. zereʿ qōdeš maṣṣaḇtāh = "the holy seed (zereʿ qōdeš) is its stump." The stump = the remnant from which growth comes again. This is the seed of the Messiah, the "branch" of 11:1 that grows from Jesse's stump. The entire hardening commission (vv9-13) ends with a seed-promise.

7.14: hinei haalmah harah veyoledet ben veqarat shemo immanuel (hinnēh hāʿalmāh hārāh wĕyōledet bēn wĕqārāt šĕmô ʿimmānûʾēl): 'Behold the young woman is with child and shall bear a son and shall call his name Immanuel.' The philological crux: almah (young woman) vs. the LXX parthenos (virgin). Almah means 'young woman of marriageable age' — not a technical term for biological virginity (which is betulah). However, in context an unmarried almah would normally be a virgin; Matthew's use of LXX parthenos sees the deeper Christological fulfillment where the historical sign (birth within a year to a young woman in Ahaz's time) is transcended by the ultimate Immanuel born without a human father — the literal 'God with us.'
7.1: Historical context: the Syro-Ephraimitic War (ca. 735 BCE). Reṣîn melek ʾărām wûpĕqaḥ ben-Rĕmalyāhû melek yiśrāʾēl = Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel — the northern coalition forcing Judah (under Ahaz) to join their anti-Assyrian alliance. wĕlōʾ yāḵĕlû lĕhillāḥēm ʿālāyw = "but they were not able to fight against it" — despite threatening Jerusalem, the attack failed. Isaiah's task (vv3-9): assure Ahaz that this threat will not succeed.
7.2: wayyānāʿ lĕḇāḇô ûlĕḇab ʿammô kĕnôaʿ ʿăṣê-yaʿar = "his heart and the heart of his people shook like trees of the forest before the wind." Nûaʿ (H5128) = to shake, totter. The collective panic is portrayed with the simile of wind-shaken trees — the same verb (nûaʿ) appears in 10:34 when YHWH shakes the forest of Assyrian pride. The instrument of terror will itself be shaken.
7.3: YHWH sends Isaiah and šĕʾār yāšûḇ bĕnô = "his son Shear-jashub" — the name means "a remnant will return." The child's presence is a walking sign-act: there will be a remnant, but only a remnant. Isaiah meets Ahaz biqṣēh tĕʿālat habĕrēkâ hāʿelyônâ = "at the end of the conduit of the upper pool" — an engineering landmark related to Jerusalem's water supply, possibly where Hezekiah later built his tunnel (2 Kgs 20:20). The mundane location for a divine message is characteristically prophetic.
7.4: hiššāmer wĕhašqēṭ ʾal-tîrāʾ = "Take heed, be quiet, do not fear" — three imperatives of the prophetic assurance formula. ûlĕḇāḇĕkā ʾal-yirk = "do not let your heart grow soft (rākaḵ)" — rākaḵ = to be timid (opposite of firm). mišĕnê zanĕḇôt hāʾûdîm hāʿăšēnîm hāʾēlleh = "before these two smoldering (ʿāšēn) stumps of firebrands (ʾûd)" — Rezin and Pekah are not roaring lions but dying embers, stumps of old torches with more smoke than fire.
7.5: yaʿan kî yāʿaṣ ʿālêkā ʾărām rāʿâ = "because Syria has planned evil against you." Eprayim ûben-Rĕmalyāhû = Ephraim (northern Israel) and "the son of Remaliah" — the latter used 5 times in this section instead of Pekah's name; a subtle dismissal of his royal dignity (cf. v9: "the head of Ephraim is the son of Remaliah").
7.6: naʿăleh ḇiyĕhûdâ ûnĕqîṣennâ = "Let us go up against Judah and terrorize it." The coalition plans to replace Ahaz with a puppet king, ben-ṭāḇĕʾal = "son of Tabeel" — a non-Davidic figure. The threat is not merely military conquest but termination of the Davidic dynasty — which is why the Immanuel promise matters: the house of David will not be extinguished.
7.7: kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy YHWH lōʾ tāqûm wĕlōʾ tihyeh = "Thus says the Lord YHWH: It shall not stand, and it shall not come to pass." Qûm = to arise/stand (of a plan becoming reality). The conspiracy against the Davidic house will fail because YHWH's promise to David's line (2 Sam 7; Ps 89) is irrevocable.
7.8: kî rōʾš ʾărām Dammāśeq wĕrōʾš Dammāśeq Reṣîn = "For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin" — the political argument: the coalition's strength is limited by geography; Damascus is merely its capital and Rezin merely its king. wĕʾôd šiššîm wĕḥāmēš šānâ yēḥat ʾEprayim mēʿām = "and within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered from being a people" — fulfilled in stages: 722 BCE (Sargon II's deportation) and ca. 670 BCE (Esarhaddon's repopulation).
7.9:
wĕrōʾš ʾEprayim Šōmrôn wĕrōʾš Šōmrôn ben-Rĕmalyāhû = "the head of Ephraim is Samaria and the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah" — Pekah deflated to mere "son of Remaliah," a regional governor at best.

ʾim lōʾ taʾămînû kî lōʾ tēʾāmēnû = "If you will not believe (taʾămînû), surely you shall not be established (tēʾāmēnû)" — the famous Hebrew wordplay on ʾāman (H539): the root of both "believe/trust" and "be established/firm." Faith in YHWH's word is the basis of political and personal stability; without the former the latter cannot exist. This verse is crucial for NT understanding of faith-as-foundation (cf. Rom 1:17; Heb 11:1: pistis as the "substance/foundation" of things hoped for).
7.10: wayyôsep YHWH dabbēr ʾel-ʾāḥāz = "and YHWH spoke again to Ahaz" — YHWH presses past Ahaz's silence or evasion; the offer of a sign (ʾôt) is a direct invitation to trust. The prophetic word pursues even the unwilling king.
7.11: šĕʾal-lĕkā ʾôt mēʿim YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā = "Ask for a sign (ʾôt) from YHWH your God." hămēq šĕʾālâ ô hāgbēah lĕmāʿĕlâ = "as deep as Sheol or as high as the heights above" — the extremes of the cosmos are offered. YHWH's generosity will be contrasted with Ahaz's refusal.
7.12: wāyyōʾmer ʾāḥāz lōʾ-ʾešʾal wĕlōʾ-ʾănasseh ʾet-YHWH = "I will not ask and I will not put YHWH to the test." Ahaz quotes Deut 6:16 piously — but this is evasion, not faithfulness. He has already decided to seek the Assyrian alliance (2 Kgs 16:7-8) rather than trust YHWH. The refusal of the sign is the refusal of faith, with Deuteronomic language as cover.
7.13: wāyyōʾmer šimʿû-nāʾ bêt Dāwid = "Hear then, O house of David" — Isaiah addresses not just Ahaz but the entire Davidic dynasty; the sign will be for the royal house and its ultimate future. hĕmeʿaṭ mikkĕm halĕʾôt ʾănāšîm kî talĕʾû gam ʾet-ʾĕlōhāy = "Is it too little for you to weary men, that you weary my God also?" — Ahaz has exhausted both Isaiah's patience and YHWH's.
7.15: ḥemʾâ ûdĕḇaš yōʾkēl = "Curds (ḥemʾâ) and honey (dĕḇaš) he shall eat." Canaan was "flowing with milk and honey" (Exod 3:8), but these subsistence foods in the context of invasion mean the child will grow up in a reduced, war-impoverished land. Curds-and-honey = the food of disrupted agriculture when only pastoral herding and wild honey remain. lĕdaʿtô lĕmāʾōs bārāʿ ûḇāḥôr baṭṭôḇ = "by the time he knows to reject the evil and choose the good" — moral discernment as developmental marker; the sign will be fulfilled within a child's growing-up span.
7.16: kî bĕṭerem yēdaʿ hannaʿar mĕʾōs bārāʿ ûḇāḥōr baṭṭôḇ = "for before the child knows to refuse the evil and choose the good, the land whose two kings you dread will be deserted." Near-fulfillment: both Rezin and Pekah were defeated (Rezin by 732 BCE, Pekah assassinated ca. 731 BCE) within years — within a child's early development. Structure of prophetic fulfillment: partial near-fulfillment (Isaiah's era) → ultimate fulfillment (incarnation).
7.17: yāḇîʾ YHWH ʿālêkā... yāmîm ʾăšer lōʾ-bāʾû... ʾēt melek ʾAššûr = "YHWH will bring upon you days not seen since Ephraim separated from Judah — the king of Assyria." The judgment pivots: Ahaz thought Assyria would save him (2 Kgs 16:7); YHWH warns that the Assyrian he courts will become Judah's greatest threat. The very instrument Ahaz seeks for protection becomes the instrument of punishment for Ahaz's unfaith.
7.18: yišrōq YHWH laḏḏĕḇôrâ = "YHWH will whistle for the fly... and the bee in the land of Assyria" — two great powers, both summoned by YHWH's whistle (cf. 5:26). They will cover the land like insect swarms — overwhelming by sheer density. Ahaz's political calculus fails to account for YHWH's total sovereignty over both powers simultaneously.
7.19: ûḇāʾû wĕnāḥû kullām = "and they shall come and settle everywhere" — in steep ravines (naḥălê bĕṭṭôt), rock crevices (nĕqîqê hassĕlāʿîm), thorn-bushes (nĕʿāṣôṣ), and watered pastures (nĕhālōlîm). No hiding place is exempt; every corner of the land is reached by the invaders.
7.20: yĕgallaḥ ʾădōnāy bĕtaʿar haśśĕkîrâ... bĕmelek ʾAššûr = "the Lord will shave with a razor that is hired — the king of Assyria." The shaving metaphor describes comprehensive defeat/humiliation: the head, the beard, the hair of the legs removed. Šĕʿar hārāglayim = "hair of the feet/legs" = euphemism for pubic hair — the shame is total. Assyria is YHWH's barber for a disgraced Judah.
7.21: yĕḥayyeh ʾîš ʿeglat bāqār ûštê ṣōʾn = "a man will keep alive a young cow and two sheep" — the pastoral image of a reduced post-invasion economy. The contrast with pre-invasion prosperity (vineyards, grain fields) is stark: survival subsistence farming replaces commercial agriculture.
7.22: wĕhāyâ mērōḇ ʿăśôt ḥālāḇ yōʾkal ḥemʾâ = "because of the abundance of milk they produce, he will eat curds" — not prosperity but the natural produce of a reduced herd when the complex agricultural economy has been destroyed. Curds and honey = pastoral survivor diet in a depopulated landscape (cf. v15).
7.23: kol-māqôm ʾăšer yihyeh-šām ʾelep gepen bĕ-ʾelep kesep lašāmîr wāšît yihyeh = "every place where there were a thousand vines worth a thousand pieces of silver will become briars and thorns" — the expensive vineyard (1,000 vines = 1,000 shekels) returns to šāmîr wāšît (the Isaiah-pair for cursed wilderness, cf. 5:6). Economic collapse total.
7.24: baḥiṣṣîm ûḇaqqeśet yāḇôʾ šāmmâ = "with arrows and bow one will come there" — the cultivated land becomes hunting ground; former vineyards become wilderness for archers. Inversion of cultivation to wilderness is the judgment pattern of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 enacted.
7.25: wĕkol-hehārîm ʾăšer bammĕʿdēr yēʿādērûn lōʾ-tāḇôʾ šāmmâ yirʾat šāmîr wāšît = "all the hills that were cultivated with a hoe — you will not come there for fear of briars and thorns." The terraced hillside vineyards, centuries of labor, revert to thorn-wilderness. wĕhāyâ lĕmišlaḥ šôr ûlĕmirmas śeh = "but they will be a place for oxen to roam and for sheep to tread" — subsistence pastoralism replaces commercial viticulture. The judgment of ch5 (the vineyard woes) is now fully enacted.

40.3: qol qore bamidbar panu derek YHWH yasheru baaravah mesillah lelohenu (qôl qôrēʾ bammidbar pannû derek Yhwh yašĕrû bāʿărābāh mĕsillāh lēʾlōhênû): 'A voice crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God.' The four Gospels unanimously apply this voice to John the Baptist. Critically, the original syntax is ambiguous: is the voice 'in the wilderness' (most translations) or does the voice cry 'Prepare the way of the LORD in the wilderness' (Mark 1:3 seems to read the wilderness phrase with the road-preparation)? The Qumran community (1QS 8:14) cited this same verse for their own desert withdrawal — interpreting 'preparing the way' as Torah-study. The NT's application to John is the contested alternative: the way is prepared through proclamation, not desert community-formation.
40.1: naḥămû naḥămû ʿammî — "Comfort, comfort my people" — the opening imperative of the great prologue, doubled for emphasis (infinitive absolute construction: absolute certainty). The structural break from chs 1-39 is marked by content, not form — no superscription, but a complete change of tone from judgment to consolation. yōʾmar ʾĕlōhêkem — "says your God" — the comfort is commanded by God and addressed to God's own people in the possessive (ʿammî, "my people").
40.2: dibbĕrû ʿal-lēb yĕrûšālaim — "speak to the heart of Jerusalem" — the intimate address formula (dibbēr ʿal-lēb, to speak to the heart, used in courtship: Gen 34:3; Ruth 2:13; Hos 2:14). The comfort has the character of a lover's tender speech, not a legal announcement. ṣĕbāʾāh mālĕʾâ — "her hard service is completed" (ṣāḇāʾ, H6635, army/forced service); nirsâ ʿăwōnāh — "her iniquity is pardoned/accepted." kî lāqĕḥâ miyyad Yhwh kiflayim — "she has received from YHWH's hand double for all her sins" — the double payment formula implies both the debt is fully settled and the grace exceeds the requirement.
40.4: kol-gay yinnāśēʾ wĕkol-har wĕgibʿâ yišpālû — "every valley will be lifted up, and every mountain and hill made low" — the cosmogonic leveling of creation to prepare the divine highway (derekh). The reversal of terrain — valley elevated, mountain lowered — describes not physical geography but the removal of all obstacles to YHWH's coming. wĕhāyâ heʿāqōb lĕmîšôr — "the rough ground will become level" (mîšôr, H4334, a level plain).
40.5: wĕniglâ kĕbôd Yhwh — "the glory of YHWH will be revealed" (gālâ, niphal — disclosed, unveiled). wĕrāʾû kol-bāśār yaḥdāw — "and all flesh will see it together" — the revelation of divine glory to all humanity simultaneously and without exception (kol-bāśār, all flesh). kî pî Yhwh dibber — "for the mouth of YHWH has spoken" — the guarantee is the divine speech-act itself, which cannot be undone.
40.6: qôl ʾōmēr qĕrāʾ wĕʾāmar mā ʾeqrāʾ — "a voice says 'Call out!' and one asks 'What shall I call out?'" — the dialogue introducing the grass/flower oracle. kol-habbāśār ḥāṣîr wĕkol-ḥasdô kĕṣîṣ haśśādeh — "all flesh is grass, and all its steadfast love/glory is like the flower of the field." The word ḥeseḏ here is unusual in this context — it is usually YHWH's characteristic; applied to human beings it may be ironic: what humans regard as their greatest virtue is as ephemeral as a wildflower. 1 Pet 1:24-25 quotes vv.6-8.
40.7: yābēš ḥāṣîr nābēl ṣîṣ kî rûaḥ Yhwh nāšĕpâ bô — "the grass withers, the flower fades when the breath/Spirit of YHWH blows on it" — YHWH's rûaḥ both gives life (Gen 2:7) and withdraws it. The rûaḥ here is the scorching desert wind, but also implicitly the divine breath that makes life contingent. ʾākēn ḥāṣîr hāʿām — "surely the people are grass" — the application to humanity is explicit. All human civilization is as impermanent as vegetation.
40.8: yābēš ḥāṣîr nābēl ṣîṣ — "the grass withers, the flower fades" — repeated from v.7 for emphasis and then completed with the contrast: ûdĕbar ʾĕlōhênû yāqûm lĕʿôlām — "but the word of our God stands forever." The contrast is absolute: created matter is ephemeral; divine word is permanent. 1 Pet 1:25 applies this to the gospel: "this word is the good news that was preached to you." The eternal word takes the form of the preached gospel.
40.9: mĕbaśśeret ṣiyyôn — "herald of good news to Zion" — the root bāśar (H1319) is the verb underlying bĕśôrâ (good news) and the Greek evangelion. The herald who brings good news of YHWH's coming is the precursor to the NT concept of gospel proclamation. rômî bakkōaḥ qôleḵ — "lift up your voice with strength" — the herald is commanded to amplify, not whisper. ʾimrî lĕʿārê yĕhûdâ hinnēh ʾĕlōhêkem — "say to the cities of Judah, 'Behold, your God!'" — the content of the gospel is the announcement of God's arrival.
40.10: hinnēh ʾădōnāy Yhwh bĕḥāzāq yābōʾ — "behold, Lord YHWH comes with might/power" — the coming is not quiet but powerful (bĕḥāzāq, with strength). ûzrōʿô mōšlâ lô — "his arm rules for him" — the strong arm of YHWH is the instrument of sovereign rule. hinnēh śĕkārô ʾittô ûpĕullātô lĕpānāyw — "his reward (śākār) is with him, and his recompense (pĕullâ) before him" — the formula repeated in 62:11 and Rev 22:12 where Christ comes with his recompense.
40.11: kĕrōʿeh ʿēḏrô yirʿeh bizrōʿô yĕqabbēṣ ṭĕlāʾîm — "like a shepherd he pastures his flock; with his arm he gathers the lambs." The contrast with v.10's powerful arm: the same arm that rules (v.10) is the arm that gathers (qābaṣ) the lambs. ûbĕḥêqô yiśśāʾ — "and carries them in his bosom/breast" — the image of divine tenderness in the context of sovereign power. The shepherd imagery here is taken up in John 10:11 and Luke 15:4-7. ʿālôt yĕnahēl — "he gently leads the nursing ewes."
40.12: mî-māddad bĕšāʿlô mayim — "who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand?" (šaʿal, the cupped palm — measuring water in the hollow of one's hand). wĕkāl baśśālîš ʿāpār hāʾāreṣ — "and marked off the heavens with a span" (šālîš, H8027, a third — the unit of the hand-span), wĕkāl baśśālîš ʿāpār hāʾāreṣ — "and enclosed the dust of the earth in a measure." The creation-scale measurements are expressed in human body-measure terms — the cosmic is rendered comprehensible through embodied metaphor.
40.13: mî-tikkēn ʾet-rûaḥ Yhwh — "who has measured/directed the Spirit of YHWH?" (tākan, H8505, to regulate, measure, direct). wĕʾîš ʿăṣātô yôdîʿennû — "and what counselor (ʾîš ʿăṣâ) made him know?" Paul quotes this in Rom 11:34 ("Who has known the mind of the Lord, or who has been his counselor?") and 1 Cor 2:16 ("who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him?"). The independence of divine wisdom from human instruction is absolute — YHWH was not taught by anyone.
40.14: ʾet-mî nôʿāṣ wayyĕbînēhû — "with whom did he take counsel, and who made him understand?" — the series of rhetorical questions continues. wayyĕlammdēhû bĕʾōraḥ mišpāṭ — "who taught him the path of justice?" None. The point is YHWH's absolute independence of mind — he was not mentored in wisdom, justice, or knowledge by any created being. His wisdom is not derived but original.
40.15: hēn gôyim kĕmar middĕlî — "behold, the nations are like a drop from a bucket" (mar, a drip/drop; dĕlî, a bucket/vessel for drawing water). ûkĕšaḥaq mōzĕnayim neḥšĕḇû — "reckoned as dust on the scales" (šaḥaq, fine dust, the kind too light to register on a balance). The political powers that overwhelm human experience are, from YHWH's perspective, negligible. The cosmic creator views the greatest empires as irrelevant weight.
40.16: ûlĕḇānôn ʾên dî bāʿēr — "Lebanon is not sufficient for fuel" — even the endless cedars of Lebanon would not produce enough wood for a sacrifice worthy of YHWH. wĕḥayyātô ʾên dî ʿôlâ — "and its animals are not sufficient for a burnt offering." The point is not that sacrifice is useless but that no created abundance is adequate to honor the Creator — the gap between creature and Creator is infinite.
40.17: kol-haggôyim kĕʾayim negdô — "all the nations are as nothing before him" — ʾayim (H657, H408) — nothingness, non-existence. mēʾepes wātōhû neḥšĕḇû-lô — "they are accounted as less than nothing (ʾepes, zero/end) and emptiness (tōhû, the same as Gen 1:2's primeval chaos) to him." The nations are not merely small — they are below the threshold of being. YHWH's perspective reduces all geopolitical significance to zero.
40.18: wĕʾel-mî tĕdammĕyûn ʾēl — "to whom then will you liken God (ʾēl)?" — the decisive anti-idol question. ûmah-dĕmût taʿărĕkû lô — "what likeness will you set up to compare with him?" The question opens the idol polemic that Isaiah 40-48 develops at length (cf. 44:9-20; 46:1-13). Any human representation of YHWH is by definition inadequate — not merely aesthetically limited but ontologically impossible.
40.19: The idol's manufacture: happesel nāsak ḥārāš — "the craftsman casts an idol" (pesel, carved/cast image; nāsak, to pour/cast molten metal). wĕṣōrēp bazzāhāb yĕraqqĕʿennû — "the goldsmith overlays it with gold" (rāqaʿ, to hammer thin, to plate). ûrĕtuqôt kesep ṣōrēp — "the silversmith casts chains of silver" — the finishing work of the idol production. The detailed manufacturing description makes the point: this thing was made by human hands, step by step. It is entirely a human product.
40.20: hamesukkān tĕrûmâ — "the one who is too poor for a dedicatory offering" (sukkān, H5539, impoverished — debated) selects wood that will not rot (ʿēṣ lōʾ-yirqāb). He seeks a skilled craftsman (ḥākām) to set up an idol that will not move (lōʾ-yimmôṭ). The irony is the goal: the idol's primary virtue is that it stays put — it does not move. YHWH's defining characteristic is that he acts, speaks, and intervenes. The static stability of the idol is the exact opposite of the dynamic activity of YHWH.
40.21: hălōʾ tēdĕʿû hălōʾ tišmāʿû — "do you not know? Do you not hear?" — four rhetorical questions challenging the idol-makers' ignorance. hălōʾ huggad lākem mērōʾš — "was it not told you from the beginning?" hălōʾ hĕḇînottem môsĕdôt hāʾāreṣ — "have you not understood from the foundations of the earth?" The knowledge of YHWH's creative sovereignty is not new revelation but primordial truth available to all — the idol-maker's error is culpable ignorance, not innocent confusion.
40.22: hayyōšēḇ ʿal-ḥûg hāʾāreṣ — "he who sits above the circle of the earth" (ḥûg, H2329, circle/vault/arc). wĕyōšĕḇêhā kĕḥăgāḇîm — "and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers" — the divine perspective on humanity. From YHWH's cosmic vantage point, the great nations and their inhabitants are as small and numerous as grasshoppers in a field. hannōṭeh kaḏōq šāmayim — "who stretches out the heavens like a curtain" (dōq, H1852, thin cloth/gauze).
40.23: hannōtēn rôzĕnîm lāʾayin — "who brings princes to nothing" — rôzĕnîm (H7336, rulers/princes, from rāzan, to be weighty/authoritative). šōpĕṭê ʾāreṣ kattōhû ʿāśâ — "makes the judges of the earth as nothing (tōhû)." The application of the creation vocabulary of Gen 1:2 (tōhû) to human rulers is pointed: the great political powers are returned to primeval formlessness by YHWH's action.
40.24: ʾap bal-niṭṭāʿû ʾap bal-zōrāʿû ʾap bal-šōrēš bāʾāreṣ gizʿām — "scarcely planted, scarcely sown, scarcely has their stem taken root in the earth" — the three ʾap bal ("hardly... not yet") constructions describe the briefness of a ruler's establishment. wayyiggaʿ bāhem wayyi bāšû — "he blows on them and they wither" — YHWH's breath (nāšap, blow) is all it takes to end a reign. ûsĕʿārâ kaqaš tiśśāʾēm — "and a whirlwind carries them off like stubble."
40.25: wĕʾel-mî tĕdammĕyûnî wĕʾeśweh — "to whom then will you compare me, that I should be like him?" — YHWH himself asks the anti-idol question (the same as v.18 but now in the first person). yōʾmar qādôš — "says the Holy One." The divine self-designation qādôš (Holy One — Isaiah's signature divine title: 1:4; 5:19,24; 6:3; 10:17; 12:6; 17:7; etc.) reasserts incomparability from YHWH's own mouth.
40.26: śĕʾû mārôm ʿênêkem ûrĕʾû mî-bārāʾ ʾēlleh — "lift up your eyes on high and see: who created these?" — the command to look at the stars as evidence for the Creator. hammôṣiʾ bĕmispār ṣĕbāʾām — "who brings out their host by number" (ṣĕbāʾ, army/host — the stars as YHWH's cosmic army mustered and reviewed). lĕkullām bĕšēm yiqrāʾ — "to all of them he calls by name" — the naming of each star by YHWH is both the assertion of sovereignty and of intimate knowledge. mērōb ʾônîm wĕʾammîṣ kōaḥ ʾîš lōʾ neʿĕdār — "because of his great might, and the strength of his power, not one is missing."
40.27: lāmmâ tōʾmar yaʿăqōb ûtĕdabbēr yiśrāʾēl nistarrâ darkî mēyhwh — "why do you say, O Jacob, and speak, O Israel, 'My way is hidden from YHWH, and my right/justice is passed over by my God'?" — the despairing complaint of exile: YHWH does not see our suffering; our case goes unheard. The questions are not accusations but pastoral address — YHWH engages the people's doubt, not dismissing it but answering it.
40.28: hălōʾ yāḏaʿtā ʾim-lōʾ šāmaʿtā — "Have you not known? Have you not heard?" — the same rhetorical questions as v.21, now applied to the Israelites themselves. ʾĕlōhê ʿôlām Yhwh bōrēʾ qĕṣôt hāʾāreṣ — "YHWH is the everlasting God (ʾĕlōhê ʿôlām), the Creator of the ends of the earth." lōʾ yîʿap wĕlōʾ yîgāʿ — "he does not faint or grow weary" — the same verbs used of the exhausted exiles in vv.29-31 are denied of YHWH. His inexhaustible energy is the source of renewed energy for the weary.
40.29: nōtēn lĕyāʿēp kōaḥ — "he gives power to the faint/exhausted" (yāʿēp, H3287, exhausted). ûlĕʾên ʾônîm ʿaṣmâ yarbeh — "and to him who has no might he increases strength" (ʾôn, H202, strength, vigor — "no strength" is the absolute exhaustion of exile). The divine pattern: YHWH gives to those who have nothing, increases to those who lack. This is not a reward for the strong but a gift to the weak — the theology of grace in concentrated form.
40.30: yîʿăpû nĕʿārîm wĕyîgāʿû — "even youths (nāʿar) will faint and grow weary" — the young, who should have the most energy, are not exempt from exhaustion. ûbaḥûrîm kāšôl yikkāšēlû — "and young men (bāḥûr, choice/selected young men, the military elite) will fall exhausted." Human strength at its peak cannot sustain itself indefinitely. The point is not pessimism but preparation for the contrast in v.31.
40.31: wĕqôwê Yhwh yaḥălîpû kōaḥ — "but those who wait on/hope in YHWH will renew/exchange strength" (qāwâ, H6960, to wait, to hope with expectant tension — cf. 25:9; 26:8; 33:2; 49:23). The verb ḥālaP (H2498, to exchange, to renew, to sprout fresh) suggests not merely restoration but transformation — new strength replacing old. yaʿălû ʾēḇer kannĕšārîm — "they will soar on wings like eagles" (nešer, the large bird of prey — eagle or vulture). yārûṣû wĕlōʾ yîgāʿû yēlĕkû wĕlōʾ yîʿāpû — "they will run and not grow weary, they will walk and not faint" — the progression moves from soaring (extraordinary) to running (normal effort) to walking (ordinary pace), all sustained by YHWH's energy.

52.13: hinneh yaskil avdi yarum venissa vegavah meod (hinnēh yašĕkîl ʿabdî yārûm wĕniśśāʾ wĕgābahh mĕʾōd): 'Behold, my servant shall act wisely; he shall be high and lifted up and shall be exalted.' The fourth Servant Song (52:13-53:12) opens with the Servant's triumph — yarum venissa vegavah (high, lifted up, exalted) — three verbs used for YHWH's own majesty in Isa 6:1 and 33:10. The Servant shares the divine exaltation-vocabulary. The shock of the Song: the path to this exaltation is through disfigurement so severe that people were appalled (52:14), and the means of exaltation is vicarious suffering (53:4-6). The Hebrew nasa (to bear/lift) runs through the Song: 52:13 (be lifted up), 53:4 (bore our griefs), 53:11 (bear their iniquities), 53:12 (bore the sin of many) — the lifting of the Servant and his bearing of sin are different sides of the same word.
52.7: noogesh vehu naaneh velo yiptach piv kesh latabach yuval vekrachel lifnei gozezeiha nelamah velo yiftach piv: 'He was oppressed and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter, and like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.' The lamb-image combines the Passover lamb (Exod 12) and the Day of Atonement offering — an intentional fusion of Israel's two great atoning sacrifices. The silence contrasts with every protest of innocence in biblical lament-psalms: the Servant has no claim to make, no defense to offer — or rather, his silence is itself the defense, the refusal to protect himself from the death that will accomplish others' salvation. Peter applies this silence to Christ's non-retaliation (1 Pet 2:23) and Luke applies the lamb-image to the Ethiopian eunuch's question about Isa 53:7 (Acts 8:32-33).
52.3: YHWH grounds the promise of redemption with a paradox: you were sold for nothing, and without silver you will be redeemed. Because the exile was not a legitimate sale — Babylon received nothing of real value, only YHWH permitted the transfer for discipline — there is no ransom price to be paid to Babylon. YHWH redeems his people not by paying Babylon off but by exercising his sovereign authority. This will become the pattern of the costless-yet-costly redemption of the Servant (53:4-6).
52.4: Two paradigmatic oppressions: Egypt (voluntary sojourn, then slavery) and Assyria (forced conquest, for nothing). In both cases YHWH ultimately intervened. The point is that YHWH has precedent for liberating his people from great powers — Babylon is simply the latest instance.
52.5: YHWH asks what stake he has in the current situation. His people are taken for nothing, their rulers howl with contempt, and his name is blasphemed. The blasphemy of YHWH's name among the nations is the trigger for action. YHWH's honor and his people's redemption are bound together.
52.6: Therefore my people will know my name — the exodus pattern: YHWH reveals his name through his acts of deliverance (cf. Exod 6:7). On that day they will know that I am the one who says, Hineni — Here I am. The self-disclosure formula is the same YHWH used at the burning bush and with the servant. Knowing the name means knowing the character revealed through act.
52.8: The watchmen of Zion raise their voices in united shout because they see eye to eye when YHWH returns to Zion. The watchman's job is to first sight the returning king; their unanimous rejoicing signals that the report is confirmed and unambiguous. The return of YHWH to Zion is the goal of the entire movement begun at 40:1.
52.9: Even the ruins of Jerusalem are commanded to burst into singing — inanimate rubble participates in the restoration. The ground: YHWH has comforted (niham) his people and redeemed Jerusalem. Comfort and redemption are the double foundation that the whole of chapters 40-55 has been building toward.
52.10: YHWH has bared his holy arm — made it visible, unsheathed his power — before all nations. The qodesh/holy arm (holiness, disp 2): the manifestation of YHWH's saving power is simultaneously the revelation of his holiness. All ends of the earth will see YHWH's salvation. The universal scope means Israel's redemption is the vehicle for worldwide revelation of who YHWH is.
52.11: The call to depart Babylon: Leave! Touch nothing unclean! The priestly logic — those who carry YHWH's vessels must be purified — echoes the Levitical purity of the wilderness tabernacle. The exilic priests will lead the return in a processional that is simultaneously an act of worship. The departure is both political liberation and cultic consecration.
52.12: You will not leave in a hurry or flee in panic — contrast with the original Exodus departure, which was hasty (Exod 12:11). The new exodus will be ordered, majestic, YHWH himself going before and also serving as rear guard. The rearguard imagery (cf. 58:8) shows YHWH encircling his people with protective presence, not merely leading from the front.
52.14: The counterpoint to exaltation: many were appalled at him because his appearance was so disfigured beyond any human likeness. The disfigurement is described in the strongest possible terms — beyond human. This prepares the reader for the Servant's suffering (53:2-9) while holding the exaltation (v 13) in tension: the same figure who will be lifted up is the one so marred that people turn away.
52.15: So he will sprinkle many nations — the verb nazah (sprinkle) is priestly, used of blood-sprinkling for purification and atonement (Lev 4, 14, 16). Kings will be struck silent before him, seeing what they had not been told, understanding what they had not heard. The reversal: those appalled (v 14) become those who comprehend and are silenced before the Servant's exaltation. Paul echoes this in Rom 15:21 as the pattern of Gentile gospel reception.
52.1: Wake up, wake up! Clothe yourself with strength, O Zion! Put on your beautiful garments, Jerusalem, holy city! The parallel to 51:9 (the exiles' cry for YHWH's arm to wake up) is now inverted: YHWH calls Zion to wake up and clothe herself in strength, just as his arm is clothing itself. The holiness vocabulary — qodesh (holiness, disp 2) city — designates Jerusalem as set apart for YHWH's purposes. The garments of strength and beauty replace the garments of mourning and dust (v 2). The uncircumcised and unclean will no longer enter: the restored city will be what the wilderness tabernacle was meant to be — a space ordered around YHWH's presence.
52.2: Shake off the dust, rise up, sit enthroned, Jerusalem! Loose yourself from the chains around your neck, captive daughter Zion! The seated-in-dust posture is the posture of a conquered city in mourning; sitting enthroned is the posture of a queen. The chains are the specific bonds of captivity in Babylon (cf. 51:23 — the tormentors who walked over Jerusalem). The command to self-liberate is paradoxical: the release is YHWH's act (vv 3-6), yet Zion is called to rise as if she were already free — the posture of faith anticipating the coming reversal.

1.1:
ḥăzôn (ḥāzôn, H2377) — "vision/revelation received." Isaiah identifies his entire book as a single integrated vision, not a collection of separate oracles. This is the preferred self-description of classical Hebrew prophecy as direct visual perception rather than merely verbal reception. Compare Obadiah 1:1 and Nahum 1:1. The book of Isaiah opens with this rarer term rather than the more common dāḇār (word of the LORD), signaling that these oracles were experienced as a unified prophetic seeing.

The four-king span (Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) dates the ministry to approximately 740–700 BCE — from the height of Judean prosperity through the Assyrian crisis. This temporal anchoring is not merely biographical; it situates the vision within the arc of covenant history.
1.2:
šimʿû šāmayim wĕhaʾăzînî ʾereṣ — "Hear, O heavens, and give ear, O earth." Two different Hebrew verbs: šāmaʿ (hear/obey) addressed to the heavens; ʾāzan (give ear/attend closely) to the earth. The distinction is stylistic but both are legal summons vocabulary. The cosmic witnesses (heavens and earth) are invoked as parties to the Sinaitic covenant (cf. Deut 30:19, 31:28; Mic 6:1-2), establishing the rîb (lawsuit) pattern that structures this entire chapter.

pāšĕʿû (pāšaʿ, H6586) — not merely "sinned" or "erred" but deliberate, willful breach of a covenant relationship; rebellion of a vassal against a sovereign. The MKT renders "rebelled," which captures this active agency. The children (bānîm) whom YHWH reared to maturity (giḏaltî) have committed pāšaʿ — the most serious category of covenant violation.
1.3:
yādaʿ (H3045) — relational, experiential knowing, not merely cognitive recognition. "Israel does not know" means Israel has failed to maintain the covenant bond that constitutes relationship, not merely that Israel is ignorant of facts. An ox "knows" its owner in the sense of having an established bond of recognition and dependence.

The ox/donkey proverb is a qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) argument: if even domesticated work animals maintain the primary bond of recognition with their owner, how much more should Israel, YHWH's covenant son, maintain theirs. ʾēbûs (H18) = "manger/feeding trough," not simply "master" — the donkey knows the source of its provision. Israel, by contrast, has stopped seeing YHWH as provider.
1.4:
hôy (H1945) — the exclamatory lament particle, used both as a funeral cry (alas!) and as a prophetic woe-oracle announcing judgment. Its combination with gôy ḥōṭēʾ (sinful nation) is striking: nations are never called "sinful" in Deuteronomic terms, only Israel — the one to whom covenant obligations applied. The entire verse has four accusatory phrases stacked asyndetically (without conjunctions), creating rhetorical urgency.

ʿāzĕbû = "have abandoned" (perfect, completed action); nāʾăṣû = "have spurned" (perfect). Both verbs describe completed covenant breach, not ongoing drift. nāzōrû (H2114) = "are estranged, turned away" — the third charge. The accumulation reinforces the totality of the abandonment.
1.5:
ʿal-māh — "Why?" / "For what?" — the rhetorical question implies that further punishment is pointless, not that YHWH is incapable; Israel has reached the point of hardened incorrigibility. The entire body-politic metaphor of vv. 5-6 presupposes a patient who has refused all treatment.

kol-rōʾš lāḥolî = "the whole head is sick/diseased (ḥolî)"; kol-lēḇāḇ dawwāy = "the whole heart is faint/failing (dāwway)." The pairing of head (leadership/mind) and heart (will/center) as both diseased signals comprehensive moral and social corruption from top to bottom.
1.6:
The body metaphor from v. 5 is extended with clinical specificity: pĕṣaʿ = wound/laceration; ḥabbûrâ = bruise/welt; makkâ ṭĕriyyâ = open/fresh sore. All three denote different stages or types of injury, suggesting the comprehensive and untreated nature of the wound. Not merely injured but unattended.

lōʾ-zōrû wĕlōʾ ḥubbāšû = "not pressed out, and not bound up" — wound treatment in ancient medicine involved squeezing out infected material, then bandaging. wĕlōʾ rukkeḵāh baššāmen = "and not softened with oil." All three remedial actions absent. The metaphor describes not the absence of suffering but the absence of any attempted cure.
1.7:
šĕmāmâ (H8077) — desolation, the word that names what is left after total devastation. Structurally related to the threat-language of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 (covenant curses for covenant breach). Isaiah is describing the covenant curses coming to pass, not merely a political situation.

kĕmaḥpēkat zārîm = "like the overthrow of strangers" — a disputed phrase; the MKT "a desolation like the ruin left by invaders" captures the comparison to devastation wrought by foreign armies. The land belongs to Israel but looks like it has been thoroughly colonized by outsiders.
1.8:
bat-ṣiyyôn — "daughter of Zion" — the city of Jerusalem personified as a daughter/young woman. This is a standard Hebrew personification idiom (cf. Lamentations 1-2). The emotional weight is grief for a beloved person, not just a place.

sûkkâ = "temporary shelter/booth" — the agricultural harvest booth (related to the Feast of Booths, sukkot), flimsy and impermanent; mĕlûnâ = watchman's hut in a cucumber field — equally temporary. nĕṣûrâ = "besieged city." The three images move from vineyard to field to military siege — Jerusalem is isolated, exposed, temporary, as if already under attack.
1.9:
śārîd (H8300) = "survivor, remnant" — a crucial theological term in Isaiah (occurs repeatedly in chs. 1, 4, 6, 10, 11, 28, 37). The "tiny surviving remnant" (kiṁʿāṭ = "almost," with śārîd) is the sole basis for Israel's continued existence. Theologically: remnant is not a consolation prize but the seed of continuity.

The Sodom-Gomorrah comparison is jarring: those cities were the canonical example of total divine destruction (Gen 18-19). That Israel "would have been like Sodom" but for the remnant — not that Israel was like Sodom in wickedness, but in deserved fate — is a statement of sheer grace. The condition of survival is not merit but mercy.
1.10: The address to Jerusalem's leaders as "rulers of Sodom" (qĕṣînê Sĕdôm) and the people as "people of Gomorrah" is a rhetorical shock tactic — v. 9 had just said Israel was almost like Sodom in its destruction; now Isaiah says the leaders ARE Sodom-like in their character. The rîb lawsuit continues: šimʿû (hear, imperative) and haʾăzînû (give ear, imperative) repeat v. 2's legal summons, now directed at Judah's leadership specifically.
1.11:
māh-lî rōb-ziḇḥêkem — "What to me is the multitude of your sacrifices?" — rhetorical question dismissing the cult. Māh-lî = "what is it to me?" — a formula of total disinterest (cf. Matt 8:29). The verse does not condemn sacrifice per se but sacrifice divorced from obedience (cf. 1 Sam 15:22; Hos 6:6; Amos 5:21-24). This is the prophetic critique: the sacrificial system was never intended to substitute for covenant loyalty.

śābaʿtî = "I am surfeited, I have had more than enough" (perfect of śāḇaʿ); lōʾ ḥāpaṣtî = "I do not desire/take pleasure in" (ḥāpēṣ = to delight in, take pleasure). YHWH's response to the temple cult is not theological interest but visceral revulsion — the strongest language of divine rejection.
1.12:
mî-biqēš zōʾt miyyĕdekem — "Who required this from your hands?" — bāqaš = to seek, require, demand. The question is devastating in its simplicity: no one asked for empty ritual. The rhetorical form implies the answer: YHWH himself did not require this; what he required was justice and righteousness (v. 17). The ritual came to substitute for what was actually demanded.

rĕmōs ḥăṣērāy = "to trample through my courts" — the temple precincts (ḥăṣēr) are being polluted rather than honored by feet that bring no matching moral state. The image of trampling (used for enemy armies destroying cities in other contexts) applied to worshipers is deliberately provocative.
1.13:
lōʾ tôsîpû hāḇîʾ minḥat-šāwĕʾ — "Stop bringing worthless offerings." Minḥat-šāwĕʾ = "offering of emptiness/vanity" — šāwĕʾ (H7723) means nothingness, emptiness, falsehood (the same root as the third commandment: "take the name of the LORD in vain"). The offerings are themselves defined as šāwĕʾ because they are offered by those whose lives contradict them.

qĕṭōret tôʿēḇâ = "incense is an abomination (tôʿēḇâ)" — tôʿēḇâ is the strongest ritual-disgust word in Hebrew, used for idolatry (Deut 7:25), sexual immorality (Lev 18:22), and unjust weights (Prov 11:1). Applying it to incense — prescribed in the law itself — is Isaiah's most radical statement: a commanded ritual can become an abomination when separated from covenant faithfulness.
1.14:
śānēʾtî (perfect of śānēʾ, to hate) — "I utterly hate." The perfective aspect emphasizes this as a settled state of divine disposition, not a momentary reaction. YHWH hates the festivals, new moons (ḥodšêkem), and appointments (môʿădêkem) — the full calendar of Levitically mandated gatherings. The word môʿēd (appointed time/festival) is the same word used in Leviticus 23 for the sacred calendar. The institutions are not condemned; their divorce from covenant life is.

hāyû ʿālay lĕṭōraḥ = "they have become a burden to me" (ṭōraḥ = burden, travail). The image is ironic: Israel was supposed to be YHWH's treasured possession (sĕgullâ); instead the relationship has become a burden. nillêtî nĕśōʾ = "I am weary of carrying them" — the festivals have become a burden YHWH must sustain rather than an offering of worship.
1.15:
bĕpārĕśkem kappêkem — "when you spread out your palms/hands" — the gesture of supplication, hands extended outward and upward. Kappayim = palms (the hollow of the hand). The image is of the universal gesture of prayer — yet YHWH responds by hiding his eyes (maʿlîm ʿênay = "I will hide my eyes, avert my gaze").

yĕdêkem dāmîm māĕʾû — "your hands are full of blood (dāmîm)." The plural dāmîm = bloodshed/bloodguilt (distinguished from dām = blood in general). The hands raised in prayer are the same hands covered in the blood of the innocent (social injustice, exploitation). This is the reason the prayers go unanswered: the posture of prayer and the posture of oppression cannot coexist.
1.16:
rāḥăṣû hizzakkû — "wash (rāḥaṣ) and make yourselves clean (zāḵāh)" — two imperatives, moving from external to internal: ritual washing and moral cleansing. Rāḥaṣ is the word for priestly ablutions; zāḵāh has broader moral-purity connotations. The sequence suggests that external ritual without internal transformation is the fundamental problem of vv. 11-15.

sûrû rōaʿ maʿalĕlêkem — "remove (sûr) your evil deeds from before my eyes" — sûr = to turn aside, remove; the deeds must be actively removed, not merely regretted. The phrase miggneged ʿênāy = "from before my eyes" reverses v. 15 where YHWH hid his eyes from their prayers; now removal of evil results in restored divine attention.
1.17:
lĭmdû hêṭêb = "learn to do good" — the positive form of v. 16's negative commands. Lāmad (to learn) implies that doing good requires instruction and practice, not mere intention. dĭršû mišpāṭ = "seek/pursue justice" (dāraš = to seek with urgency, to investigate thoroughly). ʾaššĕrû ḥāmôṣ = "correct the oppressor" — a difficult phrase; ʾāšar can mean "to make straight/correct" and ḥāmôṣ = "oppressor/extortioner."

šipṭû yātôm rîbû ʾalmānâ = "defend/judge the orphan, take up the widow's cause (rîb)." The yātôm (orphan) and ʾalmānâ (widow) are the canonical vulnerable pair in OT social law (Exod 22:22; Deut 10:18; 14:29; 24:17-21; Ps 68:5; 146:9). Their protection is the concrete test of covenant justice, not temple attendance.
1.18:
lĕkû-nāʾ wĕniwwākĕḥâ = "Come now and let us reason together/argue the case" — yākaḥ (H3198) is a legal-forensic term: to dispute a case, to bring a charge, to adjudicate. The divine invitation to "reason together" is a courtroom summons, not merely a friendly conversation. YHWH invites Israel to state their defense.

yihyû kaššāleg yalbînû = "they shall become white as snow (šeleg)." Šānî and tôlaʿat (scarlet/crimson) = the red dye from the scale insect (Kermes biblicus) — a costly, deep, essentially permanent colorfast dye. Against this: šeleg (snow) and ṣemer (wool) = the paradigm of whiteness. The contrast of maximum color saturation against maximum whiteness makes this the most vivid forgiveness image in the OT.
1.19:
ʾim-tōʾbû wĕšĕmaʿtem = "if you are willing (tōʾbû, from ʾābâ, to be willing/agree) and obey (šāmaʿ, to hear/obey)." Both ʾābâ and šāmaʿ are volitional: the first is the inner disposition, the second the outward compliance. The condition is both attitude and action.

ṭûb hāʾāreṣ tōʾkēlû = "the best/goodness of the land you shall eat." The blessing formula echoes Deuteronomy's prosperity language — the land as gift conditional on obedience (Deut 28:1-14). The obedience-blessing framework is operative throughout this pericope.
1.20:
ʾim-tĕmaʾănû wûmĕrîtem = "if you refuse (māʾēn) and resist (mārar)" — the two negatives matching the two positives of v. 19. Māʾēn = to refuse, decline (volitional); mārar = to be rebellious/bitter — the root of Miriam and Meribah.

ḥereḇ tĕʾukkĕlû = "by the sword you shall be eaten/devoured" — the same verb ʾākal (eat) used in both the blessing (v. 19: you shall eat) and the curse (v. 20: you shall be eaten). The covenant structure of blessing and curse is embedded in the very grammar. Pî YHWH dibbēr = "for the mouth of the LORD has spoken" — the oracular confirmation formula that seals the pronouncement.
1.21:
ʾêkâh hāyĕtâ lĕzônâ = "How has she become a harlot (zônâ)!" — the lament particle ʾêkâh (alas/how) opens the dirge form (cf. Lamentations 1:1, 2:1, 4:1 — same word). The prophetic move from lawsuit to lament is abrupt and emotionally powerful. The city that was "faithful" (neʾĕmānâ) has become a zônâ — spiritual prostitution, covenant infidelity in its most degraded form.

mišpāṭ/ṣedeq — the twin pillars of the ideal city: justice (right social order) and righteousness (conformity to divine standard). Their replacement by murderers (mĕrṣāḥîm) states the case with maximum compression: the city that was characterized by mišpāṭ and ṣedeq now has neither.
1.22:
kaspĕkā hāyâ lĕsîgîm = "your silver has become dross (sîgîm)." Sîgîm = slag, the waste product left after smelting — what is removed when refining silver, the opposite of the pure metal. The metaphor of metallurgical debasement (pure silver → dross; fine wine → diluted water) describes moral adulteration with precision: not ruined from outside but corrupted from within.

sōbĕʾăkā māhûl bammāyim = "your finest wine (sōbēʾ) is diluted (māhûl) with water." Māhûl from the root māhal = to dilute, to adulterate. The pairing of adulterated silver and adulterated wine introduces the theme of the next verses: corrupt leaders who have debased justice as thoroughly as a merchant adulterates a product.
1.23:
šōrĕrîm = "rebels/revolters" — from šārar (to be hostile, to be an adversary); ḥaḇrê gaḇḇāʾîm = "companions of thieves (gaḇ)." The rulers are characterized not merely as corrupt but as partners in crime — their position as judges makes their complicity in theft the more damning.

kullô ʾōhēḇ šōḥad = "every one loves a bribe (šōḥad)" — šōḥad is specifically the bribe that perverts legal judgment (cf. Deut 10:17; 16:19; Exod 23:8). wĕrōdēp šalmōnîm = "chasing after gifts/payoffs" (šalmōnîm = rewards/remunerations). The orphan (yātôm) and widow (ʾalmānâ) of v. 17 resurface: the same vulnerable groups YHWH commanded to protect are those the rulers actively fail.
1.24:
hāʾādôn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾăḇîr yiśrāʾēl — three divine titles stacked: hāʾādôn (the Lord, sovereign), YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt (LORD of armies/hosts), ʾăḇîr yiśrāʾēl (Mighty One/Champion of Israel — ʾaḇîr = bull/warrior). The threefold title makes this the most solemn divine pronouncement in the chapter; this is YHWH in his full covenant authority.

ʾinnāḥēm (hithpael of nāḥam) = "I will relieve/console myself" — the reflexive form: YHWH relieves himself of the burden of his enemies (cf. v. 14: the festivals have been a burden). The word can also mean "comfort" (YHWH takes comfort in acting against evil) — the irony: the consolation that comes from punishing oppressors mirrors Israel's need for consolation.
1.25:
bōr (H1253) = lye, potash, a strong alkali used in ancient smelting to separate dross from metal. The refining metaphor returns (cf. v. 22: silver → dross): YHWH will apply bōr to Israel as a metallurgist refines silver, removing impurities (sîgîm = dross). The judgment is purposeful, not merely punitive — the goal is purification.

wĕʾāsîrâ kol-ʾabddîlêk = "and I will remove all your alloy/impurities" — bĕdîl (H913) is specifically the tin or lead mixed into silver to create alloy; smelting removes it. The metaphor of smelting-as-judgment is pervasive in Hebrew prophecy (Jer 6:29; Ezek 22:18-22; Mal 3:2-3) and always implies a purified remnant on the other side of the furnace.
1.26:
wĕʾāšîḇâ šōpĕṭayik kĕḇārīʾšōnâ = "I will restore your judges as at the first" — the kĕ- (as/like) comparison points to the ideal period of Mosaic administration, not merely political stability. Šāpaṭ here points to the combined function of judge-deliverer (as in the book of Judges), not merely a juridical officer.

qirěyat haṣṣedeq hāʾîr hanneʾĕmānâ — "the City of Righteousness (ṣedeq), the Faithful City (neʾĕmānâ)" — the terms of the ideal city in v. 21 (where she was called faithful) are fully restored. The judgment of vv. 24-25 is not termination but restoration: refining achieves what punishment could not — the city's original character.
1.27: ṣiyyôn bĕmišpāṭ tiggāʾēl = "Zion will be redeemed through/by justice (bĕmišpāṭ)." The preposition bĕ here is instrumental: justice is the means of redemption, not merely its product. Gāʾal (H1350) = kinsman-redeemer action — the technical term for the near-kinsman who rescues family members from slavery, debt, or death (cf. Ruth 3-4; Lev 25). YHWH acting as kinsman-redeemer for Zion is one of Isaiah's major theological claims (cf. Isa 41:14; 43:14; 44:6; 47:4; 54:5).
1.28: wĕšeber pōšĕʿîm wĕḥaṭṭāʾîm yaḥdāw = "and the crushing/breaking (šeḇer) of rebels and sinners together." Pōšĕʿîm (rebels, those who commit pāšaʿ — deliberate covenant breach, v. 2) and ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners, those who miss the mark). Both categories are destroyed together. wĕʿōzĕḇê YHWH yiklû = "and those who forsake the LORD will be consumed (kālâ)." The judgment is total for those in the second category; the remnant (v. 9) is for those who respond to the invitation of vv. 16-19.
1.29:
kî yēḇōšû mēʾêlîm ʾăšer ḥămaḏtem = "for you will be put to shame (bôš) because of the sacred oaks (ʾêlîm) you desired (ḥāmad)." ʾÊlîm (H352 + H356) are sacred oak trees used in Canaanite fertility cult worship (cf. Hos 4:13; Ezek 6:13). ḥāmad = to covet, desire intensely — the same root as the tenth commandment (do not covet). The desire for sacred trees is covenant-violation in the mode of coveting.

gannôt = "ritual gardens" — gardens associated with fertility rites (cf. Isa 65:3; 66:17). The shame (bôš) will be commensurate with the desire: the things Israel pursued will become the occasion of their disgrace.
1.30:
kĕʾallâ nōḇelet ʿălêhā = "like an oak tree with falling/withering leaves (nōḇelet)" — nāḇal = to wilt, fade, fall (related to the word for corpse, the foolish person, and the wineskin that deflates). The sacred oak (ʾêlâ = terebinth/oak, v. 29) becomes the image of Israel's own fate — the tree they worshipped becomes the metaphor for their withering.

wĕkĕgannâ ʾăšer mayim ʾên lāh = "like a garden without water" — the ritual gardens of v. 29 now lack the water that gives life. The irony: Israel pursued fertility cult practices at watered sacred groves; their judgment is to become a waterless garden. The judgment mirrors the sin precisely.
1.31:
wĕhāyâ heḥāsôn lĕniṣôreṯ = "and the powerful one (ḥāsôn) will become tinder (niṣôreṯ)" — ḥāsôn (H2626) = strong, powerful; niṣôreṯ (H5369 or H5534) = tinder, something that easily catches fire. The most powerful person becomes the most combustible — power without covenant alignment becomes fuel for judgment.

û-pōʿălô lĕniṣôṣ = "and his work (pōʿal) a spark (niṣôṣ)." The person and their deeds burn together simultaneously. wĕšārĕpû šĕnêhem yaḥdāw wĕʾên mĕkaббeh = "and both will burn together with no one to put them out" — the self-consuming judgment: the oppressor and his works destroy each other.

2.1: haddāḇār ʾăšer ḥāzâ = "the word (dāḇār) that Isaiah... saw (ḥāzâ)" — the syntax of this superscription is noteworthy. Here dāḇār (word) replaces the ḥāzôn (vision) of 1:1, but the verb is still ḥāzâ (to see/behold). Isaiah "sees" a word — the word is something that can be visually perceived, not merely heard. This is distinct from other prophetic books that use ḥāyâ dĕḇar YHWH ʾel- ("the word of the LORD came to"), suggesting a different mode of reception for this oracle.
2.2:
bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm = "in the last days/at the end of days" — a fixed eschatological formula in Hebrew prophecy (Gen 49:1; Num 24:14; Deut 4:30; 31:29; Hos 3:5; Mic 4:1; Dan 2:28). This is not just "in the future" but a terminus — the decisive end-horizon toward which history is moving. The formula grounds the oracle in a different temporal register from the oracles of judgment in ch. 1.

nākôn yihyeh har-bêt YHWH = "the mountain of the house of the LORD will be established (nākôn, niphal of kûn)" — kûn (H3559) = to be fixed, established, prepared; the niphal passive means "will be firmly established/prepared." The mountain of YHWH's house is presently one mountain among many; it will be established as supreme — a complete inversion of current political geography. This oracle is verbatim repeated in Mic 4:1-3.
2.3:
yōrēnû miḏḏĕrākāyw = "he will instruct us in his ways" — yārâ (H3384) = to throw, shoot; in the hiphil, to direct, point, instruct; the noun tôrâ (teaching/law) derives from this root. The nations actively seek to be taught YHWH's tôrâ — the instruction that flows outward from Zion is not imposed by conquest but sought by willing learners.

kî miṣṣiyyôn tēṣēʾ tôrâ ûḏĕḇar YHWH mîrûšālāim = "for out of Zion will go forth Torah, and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem." The centripetal movement of v. 2 (nations flowing to Zion) reverses in v. 3 to centrifugal: tôrâ and dĕḇar YHWH going out from Zion to the nations. Both directions of movement are eschatological — not present reality but end-time promise.
2.4:
wĕšāpaṭ bên haggôyim = "and he will judge/arbitrate between the nations" — šāpaṭ here is judicial arbitration, not punitive condemnation. YHWH as universal judge settles disputes between nations, eliminating the need for warfare as a dispute-resolution mechanism.

wĕkitĕtû ḥarĕḇôtām lĕʾittôt ûḥănîtôtêhem lĕmazĕmērôt = "they will beat their swords (ḥereb) into plowshares (ʾēt) and their spears (ḥănît) into pruning hooks (mazmērâ)" — the versal pivot of the oracle. Kātat (to beat/pound) is the verb of metalworking — swords are hammered into agricultural tools, weapons into instruments of harvest. The reversal in Joel 3:10 (pruning hooks into spears) shows the motif can be read in either direction depending on the oracle.
2.5: bêt yaʿăqōb lĕkû wĕnēlĕkâ bĕʾôr YHWH = "O house of Jacob, come, let us walk in the light of the LORD." Following the vision of eschatological peace (vv. 2-4), Isaiah turns to a present exhortation. Bêt yaʿăqōb here = the covenant people as a whole. The call to "walk (hālaḵ) in the light (ʾôr) of the LORD" is the present-tense response to the eschatological promise: the nations will come to Zion's light in the future; Jacob should be walking in it now.
2.6:
kî nāṭaštā ʿamĕkā bêt yaʿăqōb = "for you have rejected (nāṭaš) your people, the house of Jacob" — nāṭaš (H5203) = to forsake, abandon, leave alone. The conjunction kî (for/because) grounds the call of v. 5 in the present reality: Israel is currently in a state of divine abandonment — the reason they need to be called back to YHWH's light.

miqqedem = "from the east" — specifically Eastern divinatory practices. qōsĕmîm = diviners/practitioners of divination (H7080, qāsam). yišpĕqû bĕyaldê nŏḵrîm = "and they clasp hands with the children of foreigners" — striking hands as a gesture of covenant or agreement (cf. Prov 11:21; 17:18); commercial or treaty alliances with foreign nations that involved adopting their religious practices.
2.7:
wattimmālēʾ ʾarṣô keseṕ wĕzāḥāḇ — "and his land was filled with silver and gold" — the three-fold wattimmālēʾ (was filled) structure of vv. 7-8 is a rhetorical indictment: land filled with treasures → land filled with horses → land filled with idols. Each filling is a violation of Deuteronomic kingship law (Deut 17:16-17: the king must not multiply horses, wives, or gold). Isaiah describes a nation that has done all three.

ûmišsûsāyw = "and their horses" — horses were associated specifically with Egyptian military power (Deut 17:16 prohibits the king from returning to Egypt to multiply horses). Israel's military trust has migrated from YHWH to cavalry.
2.8:
wattimmālēʾ ʾarṣô ʾĕlîlîm = "and his land was filled with idols (ʾĕlîlîm)" — ʾĕlîlîm (H457) is a derogatory diminutive from ʾēl (god): "nothings/worthless ones/non-gods." The term appears specifically in prophetic polemic and the psalms as a dismissal of idols as non-entities (cf. Ps 96:5).

lĕmaʿăśēh yādāyw yištaḥăwû = "they bow down to the work of their hands (maʿăśēh yādāyw)" — the manufacture-then-worship sequence is the central absurdity of idol polemic in Isaiah (developed more fully in Isa 44:9-20). The creator and the creation have been inverted: the maker worships what he made.
2.9:
wayyiššaḥ ʾādām wayyišpal ʾîš = "Mankind is bowed down and every person humbled" — šāḥâ and šāpēl are the two verbs of humiliation that run through ch. 2 as a refrain (vv. 11, 17). Here they describe human idolaters prostrating themselves; the irony is that the posture of idolatry is also the posture of divine judgment — those who bow to idols will be "brought low" by YHWH.

wĕʾal-tiśśāʾ lāhem = "do not lift them up/forgive them!" — the negative command addressed to YHWH (or to the prophet?). This is a petition against divine forgiveness, asking that the judgment not be stayed. The severity of idolatry (covenant violation at its most basic) is such that mercy itself is inappropriate.
2.10:
bôʾ baṣṣûr wĕhiṭṭāmēn bɛʿāpār = "Enter the rocks (ṣûr) and hide in the dust (ʿāpār)" — the two imperatives of terrified flight. ʿāpār (dust) recalls Gen 3:19 (return to the dust in death) — the flight into rock and dust is a death-impulse, a wish to become inanimate before the divine presence. The imagery is extended in Rev 6:15-17 (kings and great men hiding in rocks and mountains from the wrath of the Lamb).

mippenê paḥad YHWH ûmehādar gĕʾônô = "from before the terror (paḥad) of the LORD and the splendor (hāḏār) of his majesty (gĕʾônô)" — three weighty nouns: paḥad = dread/terror; hāḏār = glory/splendor/beauty; gĕʾôn = majesty/pride (when used of YHWH, always positive: the divine magnificence that reduces human pride to nothing). The refrain in vv. 10, 19, 21 marks the three movements of the oracle.
2.11:
ʿênê gaḇhût hāʾādām tišpālenāh = "the proud eyes (ʿênê gaḇhût) of mankind will be brought low (šāpēl)" — gaḇhût = height, altitude, pride; the "high eyes" are the haughty gaze that does not look up to YHWH but only across at peers. šāpēl (hiphil: to make low) is the verbal form of the great eschatological leveling.

wĕnišgāḇ YHWH lĕḇaddô bayyôm hahûʾ = "and the LORD alone will be exalted (nišgāḇ, niphal of śāḡaḇ) in that day." Śāḡaḇ = to be high, inaccessibly elevated. YHWH being "alone (lĕḇaddô) exalted" is the theological core of the oracle: the Day of the LORD is the day when no human or made thing shares YHWH's elevation.
2.12: kî yôm lĕYHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʿal kol-gāʾeh wĕrām = "for a day belongs to the LORD of hosts against everything proud and lofty." The "Day of the LORD" is not specified by timing but by character: it is YHWH's day of active assertion against everything that has usurped his position. Gāʾeh = proud/haughty (H1343); rām = exalted/lifted up (H7311). The language echoes Isaiah's commissioning vision (6:1 — "I saw the Lord... high and lofted up (rām wĕniśśāʾ)") — only YHWH is to be rām.
2.13:
ʿal kol-ʾarzê hallĕḇānôn hārāmîm wĕhanniśśāʾîm = "against all the cedars of Lebanon, lofty (hārāmîm) and lifted up (wĕhanniśśāʾîm)" — the two adjectives applied to the cedars are the vocabulary of divine elevation in 6:1 ("high and lifted up"). The trees that grow tallest and are most admired symbolize human pride at its most impressive. Cedars of Lebanon were the royal building material par excellence (Solomon's temple, Hiram's timber).

wĕʿal kol-ʾallônê habbāšān = "and all the oaks of Bashan" — Bashan (east of the Jordan, modern Golan) was famous for its oaks and fat cattle. Both Lebanon's cedars and Bashan's oaks = the tallest, most imposing trees of the ancient Near East.
2.14: wĕʿal kol-heheHārîm hārāmîm = "against all the high mountains" — shifting from trees to topography. The catalog of things brought low in vv. 13-16 moves from natural wonders (trees, mountains, hills) to human constructions (towers, walls, ships). Both creation and civilization are subjected to the divine leveling. Mountains and hills were also the sites of Canaanite high places and foreign cults (cf. 1:29).
2.15: wĕʿal kol-migdāl gābōhâ wĕʿal kol-ḥômâ bĕṣûrâ = "against every high tower (migdāl) and every fortified wall (ḥômâ bĕṣûrâ)" — the transition from natural features to military architecture. The tower (cf. the tower of Babel, Gen 11:4) and the fortified wall represent human military confidence — the trust in defensive engineering rather than in YHWH. These are the objects of both national pride and security planning.
2.16: wĕʿal kol-ʾoniyyôt taršîš = "against all the ships of Tarshish" — Tarshish was the farthest-known western destination (Spain? Sardinia?), associated with long-distance merchant trade and great wealth. The "ships of Tarshish" became a symbol for commercial power and luxury (1 Kgs 10:22; Ps 48:7; Ezek 27:25). ʿal kol-haśśĕkiyyôt haḥemdâ = "against all their desirable craft/vessels" — śĕkiyyôt (H7914) is rare; context suggests prized or coveted items (NJPS: "lovely craft"). The catalog ends with luxury commerce — the ultimate symbol of wealthy self-sufficiency.
2.17: The refrain of v. 11 is repeated verbatim, creating a rhetorical inclusio around the catalog of vv. 12-16: wĕšāḥ gĕbah hāʾādām and wĕnišgāḇ YHWH lĕḇaddô. The repetition is not redundancy but structural emphasis — the catalog of proud things (trees, mountains, towers, ships) is bracketed by the declaration of their humbling. Whatever is proudly elevated will be brought to the same level; YHWH alone will remain elevated.
2.18: wĕhāʾĕlîlîm kālîl yăḥălōp = "and the idols (ʾĕlîlîm) will completely (kālîl) vanish/pass away (ḥālaph)" — kālîl = wholly, completely (used elsewhere of whole burnt offerings); ḥālaph = to pass away, disappear, change. The terse sentence (four Hebrew words) is almost contemptuous in its brevity: the idols that dominate the land of v. 8 will simply disappear entirely. No battle, no polemic — they vanish.
2.19: The refrain of v. 10 expands here with new imagery: bimĕʿārôt ṣûrîm ûbimḥillôt ʿāpār = "into the caves of the rocks and into the holes of the dust/ground." The flight intensifies: v. 10 spoke of entering rocks and hiding in dust; v. 19 adds caves and underground holes — deeper concealment, greater terror. This is the cosmic-panic reversal of Amos 9:2-3 (where even hiding in the depths or under Carmel does not escape YHWH).
2.20:
bayyôm hahûʾ yašlîḵ hāʾādām ʾēt ʾĕlîlê kasppô wĕʾēt ʾĕlîlê zĕhāḇô = "In that day people will throw away (šālaḵ, to hurl/cast away) their silver idols and gold idols" — the same sequence as ch. 2's indictment (v. 7: silver and gold; v. 8: idols). In the day of divine terror, people abandon the very idols they made at great expense. ʾĕlîlê (worthless things) made of precious metals: the elaborate worthlessness of the idol is exactly proportional to the cost of its materials.

laḥpōr pêrôt wĕlāʿaṭallēp̄îm = "to the moles and the bats" — burrowing animals that live in darkness and underground spaces. The precious idols are thrown to creatures that live in the very places humans will be hiding. The silver and gold that gave the idols their grandeur are now discarded in the flight into darkness.
2.21: A third and final refrain of the hiding-in-rocks motif, with fuller elaboration: bĕniqrôt haṣṣûrîm ûbĕsĕʿîpê hassĕlāʿîm = "in the clefts of the rocky crags and in the fissures of the cliffs." The vocabulary escalates — more specific terms for rock formations, deeper concealment. The threefold repetition (vv. 10, 19, 21) mimics the rhythm of an eschatological drumbeat: again and again the same flight, the same terror, the same sovereign.
2.22: ḥidlû lākem min-hāʾādām ʾăšer nĕšāmâ bĕʾappô = "Stop trusting in mere mortals who have only a breath (nĕšāmâ) in their nostrils" — the chapter's practical conclusion. ḥāḏal (H2308) = to cease, stop, desist from. The breath in the nostrils recalls Gen 2:7 (nišmat ḥayyîm = breath of life breathed into Adam's nostrils) — it is the most contingent aspect of human existence, the most easily extinguished. Bammeh neʾĕśab-hûʾ = "what account is he really worth?" (neʾĕśab from ḥāšaḇ = to reckon, account). The rhetorical question demands the answer: nothing, measured against the divine majesty that has just been described.

3.1:
hāʾādôn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "the Lord (hāʾādôn), the LORD of hosts" — two distinct divine titles. Hāʾādôn (with definite article) emphasizes absolute sovereignty; YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = LORD of armies/heavenly hosts. The combination heightens the authority of the following announcement.

mašʿēn ûmašʿēnâ = "support (mašʿēn, masc.) and staff (mašʿēnâ, fem.)" — two forms of the same root (from šāʿan, to lean upon), one masculine, one feminine, creating a comprehensive "all supports" through gender variation. The double word means every kind of thing that can be relied upon. YHWH is removing from Jerusalem every prop on which the city leans — both physical (bread and water, v. 1b) and social (the leadership catalog of vv. 2-3).
3.2: The removal catalog begins: gibbôr = warrior/mighty man; ʾîš milḥāmâ = man of war (military professional); šōpēṭ = judge; nāḇî = prophet; qōsēm = diviner (included here alongside legitimate leaders — the catalog is sociological rather than theological; all leadership types disappear); zāqēn = elder. The list spans political, military, judicial, prophetic, and divinatory leadership — the entire social structure that holds a city together.
3.3: The catalog continues: śar-ḥămiššîm = "captain of fifty" — a military rank (cf. 2 Kgs 1:9-15); nĕśûʾ pānîm = "man of standing/person of distinction" (lit. "one whose face is lifted up" — someone with honor and social status); yôʿēṣ = counselor/advisor; ḥăkam ḥărāšîm = "skilled artisan/master craftsman" (ḥāraš = craftsman, especially metalworker or woodworker); nĕḇôn laḥaš = "expert in charms/enchantments" (lāḥaš = whispered charm, serpent charming). The inclusion of craftsmen and enchanters alongside military captains shows the comprehensive social disintegration: even technical and magical expertise disappears.
3.4:
wĕnātattî nĕʿārîm śārêhem = "I will make youths (nĕʿārîm) their rulers" — nāʿar = youth, boy (often with connotations of inexperience, rashness, or servile status). The word that in other contexts describes Joseph as a young servant (Gen 39:2) or Rehoboam's bad counselors (1 Kgs 12:8-11) now names the rulers of Jerusalem. The reversal of age and wisdom hierarchies is the judgment.

ûtaʿălûlîm yimšĕlû-ḇām = "and infants/capricious ones will govern them" — taʿălûl (H8586) is rare and disputed: "infants" (NIV/ESV), "capricious ones" (NJPS), "babes" (KJV). The root ʿālaʿ suggests wantonness or playful caprice. Either way: the city that once had Solomonic wisdom will be governed by those who have neither age nor discernment.
3.5:
wĕniggaś hāʿam ʾîš bĕʾîš = "The people will oppress each other, man against man" — the social contract collapses from horizontal solidarity into mutual exploitation. Nāgaś (H5065) = to press, drive, exacte (the same word used for Egyptian slave-drivers in Exod 3:7; 5:6). Every member of the community becomes a slave-driver to every other.

wĕhazzāqēn = "and the elder" — the reversal of the age hierarchy from v. 4 is now stated socially: youth is "arrogant against (yirgĕzôz)" the elder; the base (niqaleh, from qālal = to be light, contemptible) against the honorable (nikkābēd, from kāḇēḏ = heavy/weighty/honored). These are the honor-shame categories of the ancient world: kāḇēḏ (weighty) = honorable; qālal (light) = contemptible. Social disintegration is expressed through the inversion of these categories.
3.6:
kî-yiṯpōś ʾîš bĕʾāḥîw = "for a man will seize his own brother" — the seizure (tāpas = to grasp, catch) is coercive: grabbing an unwilling person for forced leadership. The desperation of the scenario: even the brother is the one seized, pointing to the complete breakdown of family solidarity as a social resource.

śalmâ lĕkā qāṣîn tihyeh-lānû = "you have a robe (śalmâ), be our leader/ruler (qāṣîn)!" The only qualification offered for leadership is possession of a garment — the minimum visible marker of social standing above the desperately poor. The depletion of all other social resources has made a robe sufficient credential for authority.
3.7:
yiśśāʾ bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr = "he will protest on that day, saying" — the naśāʾ qôl (lifting of the voice) is a formal response to the seizure of v. 6. The refusal of leadership is not cowardice but honest assessment: the designated leader has nothing to give.

lōʾ-ʾehyeh ḥōḇēš = "I am no healer/binder of wounds" — ḥōḇēš from ḥāḇaš = to bind up wounds (used in Isa 1:6; also Ezek 30:21). The word connects back to ch. 1's body-politic metaphor: the city's wounds are unbound (1:6), and now no one is willing to be the wound-binder. The social disintegration of ch. 3 is the direct consequence of the moral disintegration of ch. 1.
3.8:
kî kāšĕlâ yĕrûšālāim wîhûḏâ nāpāl = "for Jerusalem has stumbled (kāšal) and Judah has fallen (nāpal)" — the two verbs describe stages of collapse: stumbling (destabilization) followed by falling (collapse). Jerusalem precedes Judah (the capital before the province) — the failure begins at the center and extends outward.

lĕšônām wĕmaʿalĕlêhem ʾel-YHWH = "their tongue (lāšôn) and their deeds (maʿălāl) against the LORD" — both speech and action are directed against YHWH. lĕmārôt ʿênê kĕḇôdô = "defying/provoking (mārar) the eyes of his glory (kāḇôd)" — the divine kāḇôd (glory) is not merely abstract honor but a visual presence (Isa 6:3); defying "the eyes of his glory" means acting as if YHWH does not see and is not present.
3.9:
hakarāt pĕnêhem ʿānĕtâ bām = "the expression (hakārat) of their faces has testified against them" — hakkārāh = recognition, appearance; what the face reveals about the person. The moral state of Jerusalem is so manifest that the faces of its people are self-indicting testimony. They do not need to be charged — they accuse themselves.

wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām kĕsĕdôm higgîdû lōʾ kiḥēdû = "they parade (higgîd) their sin like Sodom and do not conceal (kāḥad) it." The Sodom comparison recurs from 1:9-10, but here the charge is specific: deliberate, shameless public display of what Sodom did. The sin is not hidden but flaunted — the opposite of the shame that might lead to repentance.
3.10: ʾimrû ṣaddîq kî-ṭôḇ = "Say of the righteous that it will go well (ṭôḇ) for him" — the call to proclaim good news to the righteous is a counter-note within the judgment oracle. Even in the midst of social chaos (vv. 1-9), there is a word for the righteous: they will eat the fruit of their actions. Kî pĕrî maʿalĕlêhem yōʾkēlû = "for the fruit of their deeds (maʿălāl) they will eat."
