The same maʿălāl (deeds) used pejoratively in vv. 8 serves here positively: righteous deeds produce righteous fruit.
3.11: ʾôy lĕrāšāʿ rāʿ = "Woe to the wicked — it will go badly (rāʿ)" — the second ôy (woe) in the book (cf. 1:4). The lex talionis principle stated: kîggĕmûl yādāyw yēʿāśeh-llô = "for what his hands have done will be done to him in return." Gāmal = to deal out, recompense; the reflexive return of deed to doer. This is the principle underlying both OT and NT eschatology — deeds return to their originator.
3.12:
ʿammî nōgĕśāyw mĕʿôlēl = "My people — their oppressors are children (mĕʿôlēl)" — mĕʿôlēl is disputed: "infants/children" (LXX) or "those who treat them like children" (i.e., capriciously). The connection to v. 4 (nĕʿārîm and taʿălûlîm) suggests "children" is correct — the judgment of immature rulers is now being experienced.

wĕnāšîm māšĕlû-ḇô = "and women rule over them" — read within its ANE context, not modern: female rule was the cultural marker of a society whose men had been killed, exiled, or disempowered. The point is the inversion of expected leadership structures, not a critique of women per se. mĕʾaššĕrêkā maṭʿîm = "your guides lead you astray" (lit. "those who call you blessed cause you to err") — the very people who provide reassurance are those leading into error.
3.13: niṣṣāḇ lārîḇ YHWH wĕʿōmēd lādîn ʿammîm = "the LORD has taken his stand (niṣṣāḇ, niphal of yāṣaḇ) to bring a charge (rîb) and stands (ʿōmēd) to judge (dîn) peoples." The rîb (lawsuit) pattern from ch. 1 is explicitly invoked: YHWH as both prosecutor and judge. The prophetic perfect (niphal of yāṣaḇ) treats the future judicial action as already accomplished in its certainty.
3.14: bĕziqnê ʿammô ûśārāyw yāḇôʾ bamišpāṭ = "He will enter into judgment (mišpāṭ) with the elders and rulers (śārîm) of his people" — specifically the leaders of Israel are the defendants, not nations in general. The charge against them: wĕʾattem biʿartĕm hakkārem = "you have plundered/burned (bāʿar) the vineyard." Hakkārem = "the vineyard" — the vineyard that is Israel (cf. 5:1-7, where the vineyard imagery is developed into a full allegory). The leaders have burned/pillaged what was entrusted to them.
3.15: mallĕkem tedakkĕʾû ʿammî = "What do you think you are doing, crushing (dākaʾ) my people?" — dākaʾ = to crush, grind to powder (used for grinding grain; also of the crushed Servant in Isa 53:5). The same verb will describe the Servant who is crushed for our sins. Here it describes what the leaders are doing to the people. ûpĕnê ʿăniyyîm tiṭḥānû = "and grinding the faces of the poor into the dust" — the milling metaphor: the poor are grain being ground under the millstone of economic and judicial exploitation.
3.16: bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "daughters of Zion" — the women of Jerusalem, addressed in contrast to the bat-tsion of 1:8 (which is the city personified). Here the daughters are the elite women who have absorbed the city's values. bĕgābah ʿênayim = "with haughty/lifted eyes" — gāḇah in eyes = arrogance (cf. 2:11); ûbĕṭāpheph tēlakhnâ = "and prancing/tripping (ṭāpheph) as they walk" — the verb imitates mincing steps; ûbĕraglêhem tĕʿakkasênāh = "and jingling with their feet (ʿākaś)" — ankle bracelets that make a sound as they walk, advertising their presence and wealth.
3.17: wĕšippaḥ ʾăḏōnāy qodqod bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "the Lord will afflict the scalps (qodqod) of the daughters of Zion with sores" — šippaḥ = to lay bare, to make scabby; qodqod = the crown of the head, the top. The proud head (v. 16: lifted eyes, styled hair) is specifically targeted. YHWH pitĕhen yĕʿāreh = "the LORD will expose their nakedness/shame (pōt)" — the revelation of nakedness is the ultimate reversal of the elaborate covering described in vv. 18-23.
3.18:
The catalog of ornaments removed in vv. 18-23 is the most detailed inventory of women's jewelry and clothing in the OT. Its comprehensiveness mirrors the comprehensiveness of the social catalog in vv. 2-3: just as every form of leadership is removed (vv. 2-3), every form of adornment is removed here. The parallel structure suggests the ornaments are a form of social-power display, not merely personal decoration.

ʿăkāsîm = anklets (cf. v. 16); šĕbîsîm = headbands/hairnets; haśśahărōnîm = crescent ornaments/moon-shaped pendants (from sahăr = moon; possibly related to moon-cult jewelry).
3.19: Continuing: hannĕṭîpôt = eardrops/pendants (from nāṭap̄ = to drip/hang); haššĕʾērôt = bracelets or chains (disputed: "chains" NIV; "bangles" ESV; from root meaning threaded items?); hārĕʿālôt = veils (of a fine, thin material — cf. Song 5:7).
3.20: happĕʾērîm = turbans/headdresses (pĕʾēr = ornament, glorify; cf. Isa 61:3 where YHWH gives the "crown of beauty" as a messianic gift — here it is removed); haṣṣĕʿādôt = armlets/ankle chains; haqqiššurîm = sashes/cinctures; bāttê hanneppeš = "houses of breath" = perfume bottles (lit. "houses of the soul/breath" — containers that hold aromatic spirits); haḥăḇāšîm = lucky charms/amulets (lit. "binding items").
3.21: haṭṭabbāʿôt = signet rings (ṭabbāʿat from ṭāḇaʿ = to dip/seal); signet rings bore the family insignia and were used to seal documents and transactions — both jewelry and instruments of social power and legal identity; nizmê hāʾāp̄ = nose rings/nose ornaments.
3.22: The catalog transitions to clothing: hammaḥălāṣôt = "holiday garments/festive robes" (from ḥālaṣ = to draw out, strip off — perhaps garments that were put on for festivals then removed); hammĕʿāṭāpôt = "capes/outer robes" (from ʿāṭap̄ = to wrap, cover); hammiṭpāḥôt = "wraps/shawls"; haḥārīṭîm = "purses/bags" (possibly the reticulated bags or purses carried by women of status).
3.23: haggîlyōnîm = "polished mirrors" (from gālâ = to uncover/reveal; mirrors that reveal appearance); hassĕdînîm = "linen garments/tunics" (fine linen underwear or outer garments); happenîpôt = "tiaras/turbans" (of a specific ceremonial type); harĕḏîdîm = "full-length veils/large wraps" (cf. Song 5:7). The exhaustive catalog concludes here — twenty-one items total, covering head to foot. The rhetorical effect is comprehensive: everything that adorned, defined, and signaled elite status is taken.
3.24:
wĕhāyâ taḥat-bōśem môqâ = "Instead of sweet perfume (bōśem), there will be a stench (môqâ)" — the four-fold taḥat ("instead of") refrain structures the reversals: perfume → stench; sash → rope; styled hair → shaved head; fine robe → sackcloth (with branded/scorched skin). Taḥat (under/instead of) is the word of covenant curse (Deut 28 uses it repeatedly for reversals of blessing into curse).

kî taḥat yōpî = "for instead of beauty (yōpî)" — the final and climactic reversal: beauty itself, the thing the entire catalog describes, becomes something else. The MKT's "instead of fine robe, a garment of grief (māḥăgōret śaq)" and "burning instead of beauty" captures the complete overturning.
3.25: mĕtayik bĕḥereḇ yippōlû = "Your men (mĕtê, pl. of met = mortal man) will fall by the sword (ḥereḇ)" — from adornment to battlefield. Wĕgĕḇûrātēk = "your warriors (gĕḇûrâ = strength/might, used for military prowess) in battle (milḥāmâ)." The military capacity that Judah trusted in (2:7: horses, chariots) will be destroyed in battle.
3.26: wĕʾāněllû wĕʾāḇĕlû pĕtāḥeyhā = "And her city gates will mourn (ʾānal) and grieve (ʾāḇal)" — the personification of Jerusalem as a woman continues from 1:8 (bat-tsion); now the city gates (the places of commerce, judgment, and social life) mourn. Pĕtāḥîm = gate-openings. wĕnuqqĕtâ lāʾāreṣ tēšēḇ = "she will sit desolate (nāqāh = emptied, stripped) on the ground" — sitting on the ground is the posture of mourning (Job 2:13; Lam 2:10; Ezek 26:16). Jerusalem, stripped of its adornments (vv. 16-23) and its men (v. 25), sits in mourning on the ground.

4.1:
wĕheḥĕzîqû šeḇaʿ nāšîm bĕʾîš ʾeḥāḏ = "And seven women will seize (ḥāzaq, to grasp firmly) one man" — the sequel to 3:25-26: so many men have died in battle that seven women compete for one surviving man. Šeḇaʿ (seven) is a round number signifying completion/totality — all the women; all the remaining men are overwhelmed by demand.

leḥmēnû nōʾkal ûśimlātēnû nilbaš = "we will provide our own food (leḥem) and clothing (simlâ)" — in ANE marriage the husband provided food and clothing as fundamental obligations (cf. Exod 21:10). The women offer to waive these obligations — a sign of social desperation so extreme that the normal order of economic provision within marriage is abandoned. raq yiqqārēʾ šimmĕkā ʿālênû = "just let your name be called upon us" — to bear a man's name was to be under his protection and identity. The women seek identity and honor (kherpa = disgrace/reproach removed) even without the economic basis of marriage.
4.2:
ṣemaḥ YHWH = "the Branch of the LORD" — a title of the utmost Messianic importance. Ṣemaḥ (H6780) = a sprout, growth, shoot from a root. In this context it is theophoric: "the Shoot of YHWH" — not merely a branch of the Davidic line but one whose origin is attributed directly to YHWH himself. The parallel texts that deploy ṣemaḥ as a title are: Jer 23:5 ("I will raise up for David a righteous ṣemaḥ"); Jer 33:15 (same); Zech 3:8 ("my servant, the Branch"); Zech 6:12 ("Here is the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple"). In all these texts ṣemaḥ is clearly Messianic.

liṣbî ûlĕkāḇôd wĕpĕrî hāʾāreṣ lĕgāʾôn ûlĕtipʾāret = "beautiful (ṣĕḇî) and glorious (kāḇôd), and the fruit of the earth will be the pride (gāʾôn) and splendor (tipʾāret) of those who survive Israel." The four positive words in this verse (beauty, glory, pride, splendor) are the exact reversal of the four-fold "instead of" in 3:24 (stench, rope, baldness, sackcloth). The Branch brings the reversal of judgment and the restoration of beauty.
4.3:
wĕhāyâ hannôṭār bĕṣiyyôn wĕhannôṭār bîrûšālāim qādôš yēʾāmer lô = "And whoever remains (nôṭār, niphal participle of yātar = to be left over, to remain) in Zion and whoever is left in Jerusalem will be called holy (qādôš) — everyone enrolled (kātûḇ = written/registered) among the living (ḥayyîm) in Jerusalem."

Two key terms: śārîd (remnant by survival) in 1:9 is now supplemented by nôṭār (remainder, survivor). More importantly: kātûḇ laḥayyîm = "written for/among the living" — this is the first clear reference in Isaiah to a heavenly register of the living (the "book of life" motif found in Exod 32:32-33; Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1; Mal 3:16-17; Rev 3:5; 13:8; 20:12). Enrollment in this register, not ethnic survival, defines the remnant as holy.
4.4:
ʾim-rāḥaṣ ʾăḏōnāy ʾet ṣōʾat bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "when the Lord has washed away (rāḥaṣ) the filth (ṣōʾâ = excrement/filth) of the daughters of Zion" — the vocabulary of vv. 18-24 (adornment of the daughters) is reversed: the judgment that stripped the women (3:16-24) becomes the cleansing of filth. Ṣōʾâ is a deliberately coarse word for moral impurity — the filth is the moral equivalent of the physical waste that defiles.

bĕrûaḥ mišpāṭ ûbĕrûaḥ bāʿēr = "by a spirit (rûaḥ) of judgment (mišpāṭ) and a spirit of burning/fire (bāʿar)." Two modes of divine cleansing: judicial (mišpāṭ — separating what belongs from what does not) and fiery refining (bāʿar — consuming the dross). The pairing anticipates the dual work of the Servant (purifying through justice) and the Spirit (baptism of fire — cf. Matt 3:11; Isa 44:3).
4.5:
wĕbārāʾ YHWH ʿal kol-mĕkôn har-ṣiyyôn = "Then the LORD will create (bārāʾ) over every dwelling-place on Mount Zion" — bārāʾ is the verb of divine creation from Genesis 1:1, reserved exclusively for divine creative acts with no human parallel. The new protective cloud over Zion is an act of creation in the same category as the original creation.

ʿānān wĕʿāšān yômām ûnōgah ʾēš lĕhābâ lāylâ = "a cloud by day and smoke with the glow of flaming fire at night" — the Exodus theophany re-enacted. Cloud by day / fire by night is the specific mode of divine accompaniment in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22; 40:38; Num 9:15-23). The eschatological Zion is not a return to pre-Exodus conditions but a new Exodus: YHWH re-inhabits his people with the visible presence that accompanied them from Egypt. Kî ʿal-kol-kāḇôd ḥuppâ = "for over all the glory there is a canopy (ḥuppâ)" — ḥuppâ = bridal canopy/wedding chamber; the image of YHWH's presence as a wedding canopy over his people is an extraordinary metaphor for covenant intimacy under divine protection.
4.6:
wĕsukkâ tihyeh lĕṣēl-yômām mēḥōreḇ = "There will be a shelter (sukkâ) for shade (ṣēl) from the heat (ḥōreḇ) of the day" — sukkâ here closes the frame opened in 1:8 (Jerusalem was "like a sukkah in a vineyard" — exposed and temporary). There the sukkâ was the image of vulnerability; here it is the image of divine protection. The same word, reversed in meaning: from the city exposed like a flimsy harvest booth to the eschatological community sheltered under a divine sukkah.

ûlĕmisteir wĕlĕmaḥseh mizzærem ûmimmāṭār = "and a refuge (mistār) and hiding place (māḥāseh) from storm (zérem) and rain (māṭār)." The shelter from storm is both physical and eschatological. Three nouns for shelter in this verse (sukkâ, mistār, māḥāseh) answer the three images of Jerusalem's exposure in 1:8 (sukkâ, mĕlûnâ, nĕṣûrâ). The precision of the reversal is Isaiah's hallmark: the exact vocabulary of the indictment becomes the vocabulary of the promise.

9.1: The opening verse reverses the darkness of chapter 8's closing: no more gloom where gloom once reigned. The MKT preserves the Hebrew contrast structure without softening it. The geographical references — Zebulun, Naphtali, the way of the sea — anchor the promise in the northern territories that fell first to Assyrian conquest, making the fulfillment in Matt 4:13-16 geographically precise.
9.2: The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light. The Hebrew haalekh (walking) is a participle indicating habitual action — they were characteristically darkness-walkers; the light-seeing is a prophetic perfect (seen as already accomplished). The MKT's past tense have seen captures this prophetic confidence. Matthew 4:16 cites this verse for Jesus' Galilean ministry.
9.3: The joy-multiplication imagery draws on harvest and battle-spoil celebrations — the two moments in Israelite life when communal joy was most intense. The textual note: some manuscripts read not multiplied the joy (lo = no), while the Qere reads to him (lo = to him). The MKT follows the Qere, reading an increase of joy, which fits the surrounding context of abundance.
9.4: The breaking of the yoke, staff, and rod evokes the Midianite defeat (Judg 7) where Gideon's 300 routed an overwhelming army. The MKT's the day of Midian is an allusion requiring the reader to know the Gideon narrative. The instrument of oppression — the rod — will become the instrument of the Messiah's rule in 9:6, but transformed from oppression into governance.
9.5: Every warrior's boot and blood-soaked garment will be burned as fuel — an image of warfare's complete abolition. The MKT renders sogar as soaked rather than rolled in — the former emphasizes the saturation, giving visceral weight to the contrast with what follows. The burning of war equipment appears again in Ezek 39:9-10 for the eschatological end of warfare.
9.6: Four throne-names in apposition: the MKT renders Pele-Yoetz as Wonderful Counselor (two words, not one name), following the majority reading. The name Mighty God (El Gibbor) uses El — the word for deity — applied to the royal child, a significant theological statement. Everlasting Father (Abi-Ad) means father of eternity/perpetuity — a protector-king role. Prince of Peace (Sar-Shalom) uses shalom in its full sense of well-being and wholeness, not merely absence of conflict.
9.7: Three key terms cluster here: mishpat (justice/right order), tsedaqah (righteousness with social-justice force), and shalom (peace). The MKT renders tsedaqah as righteousness — a defensible choice, though the prophetic context (justice for the poor, governance of the oppressed) suggests the social-legal dimension is primary. Olam is rendered everlasting — absolute timelessness rather than age-long duration — following the NT application to Christ's eternal kingdom (Luke 1:33). The YHWH-name note applies: the zeal of the LORD of hosts identifies the divine personal name as the guarantor of this promise.
9.8: The word (dabar) sent against Jacob — word as active agent of divine judgment, not mere communication. The MKT captures this with sent a word rather than spoken against: the word goes out and accomplishes its purpose (cf. Isa 55:11). The word-as-weapon motif will recur in 11:4 (the breath of his lips) and Rev 19:15.
9.9: Yada (know/acknowledge) is flagged as dispute-level 2. The verse says the people know — but in context it is a proud, self-sufficient knowing: we know what to do to rebuild. The MKT's will know it preserves ambiguity between simple awareness and the arrogant self-confidence Isaiah is critiquing.
9.10: The bricks-to-dressed-stone / sycamores-to-cedars contrast illustrates the arrogance of upgrading rather than repenting after judgment. The sycamore was a common cheap timber; the cedar was the premium timber of temples and palaces. The people respond to God's corrective discipline with a building program, not a return to the LORD — a pattern Paul warns against in treating divine patience as license (Rom 2:4).
9.11: The divine name YHWH is flagged here. The LORD raises up the adversaries of Rezin — the personal name of God as the one who orchestrates the nations is the MKT's way of maintaining the intimate, covenantal dimension of judgment. It is not an abstract deity but the covenant God who raises up enemies to discipline his people.
9.12: Devoured with open mouth — the image of Israel as prey surrounded on all sides (Syrians in front, Philistines behind). The refrain his hand is stretched out still (repeated in 9:17, 9:21, 10:4) creates a structural urgency: the divine hand of judgment does not withdraw because the people do not return. The hand stretched out in judgment will be stretched out in healing and deliverance in 11:11.
9.13: They have not returned to him who struck them, nor have they sought the LORD of hosts. The failure of judgment to produce repentance is a recurring diagnostic in Isaiah (cf. 1:5). The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth (LORD of hosts) is flagged — the military title for the God who commands the armies of heaven, a title implying that Israel's enemies are ultimately fighting against the divine Commander of all armies.
9.14: Head and tail, palm branch and reed — the Hebrew idiom for high and low, top and bottom of society. All ranks will be cut off on a single day (yom echad). The day of comprehensive judgment is a precursor to the Day of the LORD theme developed in chapter 13. The divine name YHWH is flagged — this cutting-off is an act of the personal covenant God, not fate.
9.15: The identification of the honored elder as the head and the lying prophet as the tail is a key hermeneutical moment: Isaiah is interpreting the metaphor. The false prophet who teaches lies is the most culpable part of the leadership structure — those who speak in God's name but do not speak God's truth bear the greatest responsibility for the people's misdirection (cf. Jer 23; Ezek 34).
9.16: Those whom they lead are swallowed up. The Hebrew mevulshalim means entangled, confused — the MKT's swallowed up is a stronger rendering that captures the total engulfment the false guidance produces. The young men and orphans and widows — the vulnerable who most need honest leadership — are specifically named as those the LORD will not have compassion on, a shocking reversal of divine mercy that underlines the severity of the crisis.
9.17: The divine withholding of compassion on the young men and orphans is one of the most jarring statements in Isaiah. The MKT preserves the shock: has no compassion — not a softening like cannot rejoice in. In the context of 9:13 (they have not returned), this is the logical consequence: persistent refusal of repentance eventually exhausts the patience of the compassionate God. The refrain appears again.
9.18: Wickedness burning like fire — an image that moves from individual wickedness to consuming societal conflagration. The briers and thorns of unproductive land catch first, then the dense forest, then the smoke rises in columns. The image of fire-as-judgment consuming first the worthless vegetation and then the great forest is developed in 10:17-19, where the Assyrian empire is the forest destroyed by the LORD's fire.
9.19: Through the wrath of the LORD of hosts the land is scorched, and the people are like fuel for the fire. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged again. Fuel for the fire — the Hebrew esh-oklah-esh is a striking pun: fire is being consumed by fire. Society in breakdown devours itself. The MKT's like fuel for the fire preserves the consuming image without losing the self-devouring element.
9.20: Snatch food on the right, eat on the left, still hungry — the insatiability of self-consuming sin. The tribal conflict that follows (Manasseh and Ephraim against each other and against Judah) is the domestic expression of the cosmic spiral of mutual destruction. When society forgets the LORD, it turns its violence inward. The refrain of the outstretched hand closes the chapter and opens it to 10:1.
9.21: The closing refrain — his hand is stretched out still — is the third occurrence, creating a drumbeat of unrelenting divine attention. The hand that is stretched out in judgment is the same hand that will be stretched out for the second time in 11:11 to reclaim the remnant. The outstretched hand is the posture of both judgment and salvation; which you encounter depends on whether you turn toward or away from the LORD.

10.1: Woe to those who enact unjust laws. The woe-oracle (hoy) opens chapter 10 with a direct address to the ruling class. The Hebrew chaqaq (enact/decree) is a technical legal term for official legislation — the critique is not of individual wrongdoing but of institutionalized injustice built into the legal system itself. This is system-level corruption, not personal sin alone.
10.2: Turning the poor away from justice and robbing the rights of the afflicted. Mishpat (justice/legal right) is the legal entitlement that the poor have by covenant law (Deut 24-25; Exod 22-23). The MKT's rights is strong — the poor have legal claims being actively suppressed. The oppressor is not merely neglecting the poor but perverting the legal system to strip them of what the Torah guarantees.
10.3: The day of reckoning (yom pekudah) — the day of visitation, inspection, or accounting. The MKT renders it day of reckoning, capturing the auditing metaphor: on that day the books will be opened and the corrupt rulers will have nowhere to put their unjustly acquired wealth. The rhetorical question — where will you flee? — parallels the Day of the LORD language of Amos 5:18-20.
10.4: Nothing left but to cower among captives or collapse among the dead. Two parallel fates: imprisoned or dead. The Hebrew presents the impossibility of escape from divine judgment. The refrain closes again: his hand is stretched out still — the fourth and final occurrence, showing that the woe-oracle against social injustice is the same divine hand at work as in the covenant judgments of chapter 9.
10.5: Woe, Assyria — you are the rod of my anger; the club in whose hand is my fury! A stunning reversal: Assyria, the most feared empire of the ancient Near East, is merely a tool in YHWH's hand. The matteh (rod/staff) and maqqel (club/stick) are implements — the metaphor fully worked out in 10:15 (the axe does not boast over the woodcutter). The MKT's rod of my anger and club of my fury maintain the double-object structure of the Hebrew.
10.6: The LORD sends Assyria against a godless nation — Isaiah's own people. The Hebrew chaneph (godless/profane) is the same word used for those who have desecrated themselves. The covenant people are called godless for their covenant infidelity — a prophetic indictment that would have been as shocking to Isaiah's audience as Paul's declaration that Jews as well as Gentiles are under sin (Rom 3:9).
10.7: That is not his intention. The Assyrian king does not know he is fulfilling divine purposes — he acts from imperial ambition, not divine calling. This establishes the principle of secondary causation: human actors pursue their own goals while unknowingly fulfilling God's providential purposes. The NT applies this to the crucifixion (Acts 4:27-28) — the rulers acted from their own motives while fulfilling what God had predestined.
10.8: Are not all my officers kings? The Assyrian boast of imperial hierarchy — officers who are equivalent to kings in their power — illustrates the pride the MKT is about to deconstruct. The word melech (king) is flagged as dispute-level 2; in this context it functions as a superlative: my officers are as powerful as other nations' kings, implying Assyria's officers outrank everyone else's rulers.
10.9: The list of conquered cities — Calno, Carchemish, Hamath, Arpad, Samaria, Damascus — is a catalogue of imperial conquests cited as precedents for the inevitable fall of Jerusalem. The rhetorical move is boastful induction: if all these have fallen, Jerusalem will too. Isaiah's point is that the boast contains its own refutation — Assyria's confidence in its track record is precisely the pride that will precede its fall.
10.10: Just as my hand reached kingdoms whose idols surpassed those of Jerusalem. The Assyrian king's idolatrous logic: if I could defeat nations with better idols, I can certainly defeat Jerusalem. He evaluates divine power by the quality of idol craftsmanship — the very category mistake Isaiah's idol polemic (44:9-20) deconstructs. The LORD is not an idol whose power can be assessed by comparison; he is the living God who raises up and tears down empires.
10.11: Shall I not treat Jerusalem and her idols just as I treated Samaria and her carved images? The Assyrian king's symmetry argument — Samaria fell; Jerusalem will fall by the same logic. The inclusion of idols in Jerusalem is historically accurate: under Ahaz and Manasseh, idolatry was practiced in the temple. Isaiah's response (v. 12) is that once YHWH has used Assyria, he will deal with Assyria's own arrogance.
10.12: Once the Lord has completed his work on Mount Zion. The melech (king) of Assyria is flagged — in context the proud heart and haughty eyes of the king. The fruit of the arrogant heart of the king of Assyria — the MKT renders this as the product/result of pride, making the king's internal attitude the target of divine judgment. The word kavod (glory/honor) is flagged in the surrounding verses — Assyria's glory is exactly what will be consumed (vv. 16-18).
10.13: It is by my own power and wisdom that I have done this. The Assyrian king's speech (vv. 13-14) is a masterpiece of self-aggrandizement: I erased boundaries, plundered treasuries, subdued populations as easily as taking eggs from an abandoned nest. The MKT preserves the first-person singulars throughout, which intensifies the arrogance: every verb is I did. The accumulation sets up the devastating axe-metaphor of v. 15.
10.14: No one fluttered a wing, or opened a beak, or chirped. The deserted nest metaphor — eggs taken without any resistance — is one of Isaiah's most vivid images of imperial ease. The three negatives (no wing-flutter, no beak-open, no chirp) emphasize total passivity of the conquered. The MKT preserves the avian vocabulary, which is essential for the rhetorical effect; scooped up riches captures the casual reach of empire.
10.15: Does an axe boast over the person who wields it? The tool-instrument metaphor: four instruments (axe, saw, rod, staff) and their absurdity if they claim agency over the person using them. The kavod (glory) in v. 16 is flagged — the Sovereign LORD will send a wasting disease on Assyria's stout warriors and will kindle a blazing fire under Assyria's glory. The MKT's glory captures the kabod's range from physical splendor to proud status.
10.16: A wasting disease among the Assyrian's stout warriors. The Hebrew mishmanav (stout ones/fat ones) is a description of the elite warrior class, well-fed and formidable. Kavod is flagged — rendered glory. Under the glory there will be kindled a fire like a burning flame. The image is of the brilliant exterior concealing the consuming fire that will hollow it out from within — divine judgment working from the inside.
10.17: The Light of Israel will become a fire, and his Holy One a flame. The divine titles here are striking: the LORD is Israel's Light (a positive presence-title, Ps 27:1) who becomes a consuming fire against Assyria (a judgment-title, Heb 12:29). Both attributes belong to the same God. The MKT preserves both titles without merging them into a generic deity. The fire consumes briers and thorns first — the worthless growth — then the great forest.
10.18: It will consume the glory of his forest. The kavod (glory) and nefesh (soul/life) are both flagged here. Nefesh in this context means the whole vitality — the MKT's both root and branch is a good rendering though it paraphrases. It will be as when a sick man wastes away — the Hebrew mesos nasas is a medical-deterioration image: the empire will not fall suddenly in battle but will waste away like a terminal illness.
10.19: So few that a child could write them down. From the dense impenetrable forest (the Assyrian army's metaphor) to a handful of trees a child can enumerate — total decimation described through the reversal of the forest imagery. The hyperbole (child counting) emphasizes the completeness of the reduction. This anticipates the remnant theology of 10:20-22: a small number surviving a massive catastrophe is both judgment and grace.
10.20: In that day the remnant of Israel will no longer rely on him who struck them. Emet (truth/faithfulness) is flagged — rendered in truth. The remnant will rely on the LORD in truth — in faithfulness, in genuine covenant loyalty rather than the formal religion that has characterized Judah. The turn from relying on Assyria (the instrument of discipline) to relying on the LORD who disciplines is the model of true conversion.
10.21: A remnant will return — the remnant of Jacob — to the Mighty God. El Gibbor (Mighty God) appears here — the same title used for the Messiah in 9:6. The remnant returns to the same Mighty God who is the Messiah's title, creating a theological echo: the one who rules on David's throne (9:7) and the one to whom the remnant returns (10:21) are identified by the same divine title.
10.22: Only a remnant will return. Tsedaqah is flagged — the decreed destruction is in righteousness. Paul cites this verse in Rom 9:27 as evidence that even in the OT, mere physical descent from Israel did not guarantee inclusion in the people of God — only the remnant is saved. The tension between numerical abundance (sand of the sea) and covenantal reality (only a remnant) is foundational for Paul's theology of election.
10.23: The Lord GOD of hosts is executing his decreed destruction throughout the whole land. The double title Adonai YHWH-Sabaoth emphasizes the triple sovereignty: master, covenant God, commander of armies. The destruction is decreed (kalah ve-necheretset) — cut, determined, inscribed in the divine plan. The MKT's his decreed destruction captures the irrevocability; this is not contingent judgment but fulfilled inevitability.
10.24: O my people who live in Zion, do not be afraid of the Assyrians. The same divine voice that decrees destruction now addresses his people with comfort — the same tension as the double edge of the outstretched hand. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth addresses Zion as my people — the intimate covenant address in the midst of the judgment speech. The rod will be lifted, as at Midian (v. 26).
10.25: For in a very little while my fury will be spent. The temporariness of divine anger is a key Isaianic theme — anger is an instrument, not an attribute. The wrath against Judah passes through Assyria to expire; then the wrath turns against Assyria's arrogance. This double movement — through instrument to target — structures much of chapters 7-37: the LORD uses and then judges the nations he uses.
10.26: The LORD of hosts will lash out against them as he defeated Midian at the rock of Oreb. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged. The Midian defeat (Gideon, Judg 7-8) and the Egyptian crossing are both cited as precedents for miraculous divine deliverance against impossible odds — the new Exodus typology that runs through Isaiah 40-55 begins here in chapter 10.
10.27: The yoke will be lifted from your shoulders because of the anointing. The Hebrew lifney-shamen is disputed: because of fatness (the yoke breaks because the ox's neck is too fat) or because of anointing oil (the Davidic anointing breaks the Assyrian yoke). The MKT's because of the anointing reads the messianic option, which connects to the anointed ruler of 9:6-7 and 11:1-5 — a defensible interpretation that makes the liberation messianic.
10.28: Verses 28-32 are a dramatic march of an advancing army through Benjaminite territory toward Jerusalem — town by town, with trembling spreading as the army advances. The place-names are listed not for geographical instruction but for dramatic effect: the reader feels the inexorable advance through familiar places. The MKT preserves the place-name list without paraphrase, respecting the dramatic structure.
10.29: At Geba they camp for the night; Ramah shakes with fear; Gibeah of Saul flees. The shaking and fleeing of these towns creates a wave of terror that anticipates the final image at Nob. Each town mentioned is within sight of Jerusalem — the proximity is the terror. The MKT's active verbs (shakes, flees) are direct; some versions soften these to past tense narration, losing the dramatic immediacy.
10.30: Cry out loudly, O daughter of Gallim! Listen, O Laishah! The feminine address — daughter of Gallim — is a personification device common in the prophets (daughter of Babylon, daughter of Zion). The call to listen is urgent; the towns are being called to witness what is coming toward them, perhaps even to warn each other. The MKT's imperative forms preserve the urgency.
10.31: Two more towns: Madmenah flees, Gebim's people scatter for cover. The escalating panic as the army approaches is Isaiah's way of making the reader feel the terror viscerally. The brevity of these verses — almost pure action verbs — mimics the speed and chaos of flight before an advancing army.
10.32: That same day he will halt at Nob and shake his fist toward the hill of Zion. Nob is within sight of Jerusalem — the military climax of the march. The gesture of shaking the fist is one of taunting threat, not action. At this precise moment (the next verse) the divine hand intervenes. The juxtaposition is dramatic: the Assyrian halts, threatens with a fist — and then the LORD lops off the branches with terrifying power.
10.33: The Sovereign LORD of hosts is about to lop off the branches with terrifying power. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged. The divine Lumberjack suddenly appears at the moment of the Assyrian's triumph. The tall trees (gavohim) fall — the imagery of the great forest being cut down will become the stump of Jesse in 11:1. Judgment clears the forest so a new shoot can grow.
10.34: He will slash down the dense forest with iron, and Lebanon will fall before a powerful one. The chapter ends with the forest cleared — the imperial forest of Assyria reduced to stumps. This clears the narrative space for the next chapter's entirely different image: not a dense threatening forest but a single tiny shoot (choter) emerging from a stump. The contrast between 10:34 and 11:1 is one of Isaiah's most deliberate structural juxtapositions.

11.1: A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots will bear fruit. The Hebrew choter (shoot) and netzer (branch) both denote small, unexpected new growth from what appears dead. Jesse (not David) is named — going back one generation to the pre-dynastic root, suggesting the Davidic dynasty will be cut to ground level and a new beginning made. Matt 2:23 cites netzer as a wordplay on Jesus' hometown of Nazareth (Nazarenos).
11.2: The Spirit of the LORD will rest on him. Ruach is flagged — the Spirit of wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and fear of the LORD. The sevenfold Spirit-endowment in the LXX became important for later theology (Rev 1:4's seven spirits). The MKT renders ruach as Spirit throughout, capitalizing it to indicate the divine Spirit. The verb rest (nuach) suggests settled, permanent resting — not a temporary charismatic endowment but an abiding presence (cf. John 1:32-33).
11.3: He will take delight in the fear of the LORD. Shaphat (judge) is flagged. The Messiah will not judge by appearances or rumors — by what he sees or hears — but by righteous judgment. YHWH is flagged as well. This inward, Spirit-driven judgment rather than surface-level assessment anticipates Jesus' refusal of external religious performance as the measure of righteousness (Matt 5:20; John 7:24).
11.4: With righteousness he will judge the poor and with fairness he will decide for the afflicted. Ruach is flagged — the breath of his lips will strike the wicked. The word (dabar) as weapon — spoken righteousness that executes judgment — is developed in NT as the sword from Christ's mouth (Rev 19:15) and the word of God sharper than any two-edged sword (Heb 4:12). Shaphat here carries forensic weight: legal decision for the poor as the beneficiaries of divine justice.
11.5: Righteousness will be the belt around his waist, and faithfulness the girdle around his loins. Clothing imagery for character — what girds the Messiah defines his identity. The MKT's belt and girdle distinguish the two parallel terms: tsedaqah (righteousness/justice) and emunah (faithfulness/steadiness). Paul's armor of God (Eph 6:14) draws on this: the belt of truth around the waist, developing the Isaianic image into the Christian life of warfare.
11.6: The wolf will live peacefully with the lamb. The Hebrew gar (sojourn as a guest/resident alien) — the wolf sojourns alongside the lamb as a guest-resident, not as a predator. The MKT's live peacefully captures the new relational status without importing full domestication. The reversal of predator-prey relationships is the eschatological sign of the age of the Spirit — creation renewed so that its enmities are healed.
11.7: The cow and the bear will graze together; their calves will lie down side by side; and the lion will eat straw like the ox. The dietary habits of the predators change — lion eating straw is the most radical transformation: the apex predator's nature is altered, not just its behavior. This is not merely peaceful coexistence but a change of what the creature fundamentally is — an image of thorough new-creation transformation.
11.8: The nursing infant will play at the viper's burrow. The most vulnerable human (infant) playing at the most dangerous animal (venomous serpent) without harm. The MKT's nursing infant and toddler make the human vulnerability concrete. The serpent-at-play image evokes the reversal of Eden (Gen 3:15): the enmity between the seed of the woman and the serpent is ended, but by the defeat of the serpent rather than a truce with it.
11.9: Nothing will harm or destroy anywhere on my holy mountain, for the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea. Two parallel images for comprehensive new-creation peace: (1) no harm anywhere on the holy mountain — the absence of violence; (2) universal knowledge of the LORD filling the earth as completely as water fills the ocean floor. YHWH is flagged — the divine name is the content of this universal knowledge. Hab 2:14 parallels this verse nearly exactly.
11.10: In that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples; the nations will seek him, and his resting place will be glorious. Kavod (glory) is flagged — his resting-place (menuchah) will be glory. The Root of Jesse (not branch from Jesse's root, but Root — identifying him with Jesse's origin, not just his descendant) standing as a banner for the peoples (plural — Gentile nations) anticipates the global mission. Paul cites this in Rom 15:12 for Gentile inclusion in the covenant.
11.11: The Lord will extend his hand a second time to reclaim the remnant of his people. The second-time reclamation frames the new Exodus: as the first exodus brought Israel out of Egypt, the second brings back scattered exiles. The list of nations (Assyria, Egypt, Pathros, Cush, Elam, Shinar, Hamath, coastlands) suggests the entire known world will be the diaspora from which Israel is gathered. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the agent of this second gathering.
11.12: He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiled of Israel. The banner (nes) of v. 10 is now raised by the Root of Jesse — the Messiah as the gathering-point for both Israel and the nations. The gathering imagery (gather, assemble) is the language of eschatological pilgrimage. In the NT, the cross becomes the banner that draws all people to Christ (John 12:32): lifted up, he draws all people to himself.
11.13: The tribal jealousy between Ephraim and Judah — the two rival kingdoms — will end. The cutting-off of Judah's antagonizers and the joint military action against surrounding peoples (Philistines, Edomites, Moabites, Ammonites) envisions national reunification. For NT readers this is spiritualized: the enmity between Jew and Gentile is the deeper division resolved in Christ (Eph 2:14-16).
11.14: The military imagery — swooping on the Philistines, plundering to the east — can be read as literally nationalistic or typologically as the spread of the gospel overcoming its adversaries. The MKT renders the geographical details accurately; the commentary note is that the NT spiritualizes the military conquest of Canaan into the spiritual warfare of 2 Cor 10:4-5.
11.15: The LORD will dry up the gulf of the Egyptian Sea. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the one who dries the sea and waves his hand over the Euphrates to divide it into streams for walking through in sandals. The new Exodus typology is explicit: as Moses divided the sea (Exod 14), the LORD will divide both the Nile delta and the Euphrates to bring the scattered people home. The ruach (breath/spirit/wind) is flagged — the same breath/wind that divided the Red Sea (Exod 14:21) will work the new Exodus.
11.16: There will be a highway from Assyria for the remnant of his people who survive, just as there was for Israel when they came up from Egypt. The explicit first-Exodus / second-Exodus comparison closes the chapter. The highway through the wilderness (cf. Isa 40:3-5) is the path of the new-covenant community. John the Baptist cites this highway imagery (Luke 3:4-6 citing Isa 40:3-5) as preparation for Jesus' coming. The MKT's highway preserves the road-building imagery central to Isaiah's eschatological vision.

12.1: I praise you, O LORD; though you were angry with me, your anger has turned away and you have comforted me. The YHWH-name is flagged — this is a personal address to the covenant God by name. The turning away of divine anger (shub) is the same verb for repentance — as Israel turns, God's anger turns away; as God's anger turns away, Israel can turn in praise. The MKT's though you were angry preserves the contrast that makes the praise meaningful: this is not cheap joy but joy after genuine judgment.
12.2: The LORD, the LORD himself, is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation. YHWH is flagged twice — the Hebrew reads Yah YHWH oz v'zimrat: the divine name appears in its short form Yah embedded in salvation (yeshuah) — this is the verbal root of the name Jesus (Yeshua). The MKT's rendering he has become my salvation preserves the verbal form. The verse is an almost verbatim quotation of the Song of Moses (Exod 15:2), framing the restoration as a new Exodus song.
12.3: With joy you will draw water from the springs of salvation. The springs of salvation (ma'ayanei hayeshuah) — salvation as water drawn from springs of divine life. The MKT preserves the Hebrew yeshuah (salvation) rather than substituting a synonym; the root of the name Jesus appears again. Jesus will develop this image: I am the living water (John 4:14; 7:38-39). Rev 7:17 closes the loop: the Lamb will lead them to springs of living water.
12.4: Give thanks to the LORD; call on his name; make his deeds known among the peoples. YHWH is flagged — the covenant name as the content of proclamation to the nations. Yada (know) is flagged — here make known (causative): Israel's calling is to publicize the divine name and deeds among the peoples. This missionary impulse — make God's deeds known to the nations — anticipates the Gentile mission of Acts (Acts 2:11; 13:47 citing Isa 49:6).
12.5: Sing praises to the LORD, for he has done glorious things; make this known throughout all the earth. Yada (know/make known) is flagged — again the causative: the knowledge of the LORD is to be caused to be known throughout the earth. The glorious things (ge'ut) are the specific acts of salvation — the new Exodus the previous chapter described. The MKT's glorious things is accurate; some versions use excellent things, but glorious better captures the weight of the Hebrew.
12.6: Cry aloud and shout for joy, you who dwell in Zion, for great is the Holy One of Israel in your midst. The chapter ends with the divine title Qadosh Yisrael (Holy One of Israel) — Isaiah's distinctive name for God, used 25 times in Isaiah, only 6 times in the rest of the OT. Great in your midst is the climax: the greatness of the Holy One is not an abstract attribute but a presence — he dwells in Zion. The NT equivalent is Immanuel: God with us.

13.1: The heading identifies this as an oracle (massa) — a word meaning both burden and lifting up/utterance. The double meaning is deliberate: the oracle against Babylon is a heavy burden on the prophet to carry, and a heavy word to pronounce. The MKT's oracle is standard; some preserve the weight dimension with burden. Isaiah the son of Amoz adds the prophetic credential — this is not speculative political analysis but divine revelation.
13.2: Raise a banner on the bare mountain! Lift your voice to them! Wave your hand, so that they may enter the gates of the nobles. The call to arms imagery — banner, voice, hand — is the language of mobilizing an army. The gates of the nobles are Babylon's famous fortified gates. The MKT preserves the military address without identifying the army — the effect is that the call comes before the caller is identified (v. 3: the LORD's consecrated troops).
13.3: I have given orders to my consecrated troops. The LORD speaks in the first person — the divine Commander addressing his mequddashim (consecrated/holy ones). This is not a standard army but a set-apart force — the same word used for the holy articles of the temple. The consecrated warriors become an instrument of divine judgment without themselves being righteous; they are set apart for the purpose of judgment as Babylon was once set apart for the purpose of disciplining Judah.
13.4: The clamour of kingdoms, of nations massing together! YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged — the LORD mustering the army of battle. The great noise of the mustering armies is described in terms of rumbling, roaring, clamor — all sound imagery that creates an auditory picture of overwhelming force assembling. The MKT's LORD of hosts preserves the military title; the Commander of heaven's armies is raising an earth-shaking force.
13.5: They are coming from a distant land, from the far end of the heavens — the LORD himself and the weapons of his wrath. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the one who comes at the head of the instruments of wrath. The geographical scope (far end of the heavens) suggests cosmic rather than merely regional judgment. The Day of the LORD as cosmic event rather than local military campaign is the key claim here.
13.6: Wail, for the day of the LORD is close at hand — it will come as devastation from the Almighty. YHWH is flagged. The Hebrew sound-play is famous: shod miShaddai yavo — devastation from the Devastator will come. The MKT's devastation from the Almighty preserves the Shaddai title but loses the sound-play — an acceptable trade-off in English. The Day of the LORD (yom YHWH) is its first major occurrence in Isaiah, framing the oracle as eschatological rather than merely historical.
13.7: At this every hand will go limp and every human heart will melt with fear. The physical effects of divine judgment — limp hands, melting hearts — are the opposite of the courage described in 9:4 (the yoke of burden broken, the rod of the oppressor). When the LORD comes in judgment on the oppressor, even the oppressor's strength fails. The MKT's go limp and melt with fear preserve the bodily images of collapse.
13.8: They will writhe like a woman in labor. Birth-pang imagery for the suffering of divine judgment — the same image Jesus uses for the beginning of eschatological woes (Matt 24:8: all these are the beginning of birth pains) and Paul uses for creation's groaning (Rom 8:22). The MKT's labor imagery is consistent with NT use; the pangs of judgment become the pangs of the new creation being born.
13.9: YHWH is flagged — the Day of the LORD described as relentless in wrath and burning anger. The day arrives with the purposefulness of a determined traveler; it cannot be deflected by the wicked's plans or preparations. The dual goal is stated: to turn the land into a desolation and to banish its sinners from it. Desolation of the land and banishment of sinners are the two coordinates of divine judgment in Isaiah's pattern.
13.10: The stars of heaven and their constellations will not give their light; the sun will be darkened when it rises, and the moon will not shine. The cosmic darkness of the Day of the LORD — astronomical signs that the ordering of creation is being shaken. Jesus cites the same cluster of images for the end of the age (Matt 24:29 citing Isa 13:10; also Joel 2:10-11; Amos 8:9). The MKT preserves the astronomical specifics (stars, constellations, sun, moon) without reduction to metaphor.
13.11: I will punish the world for its evil and the wicked for their guilt. The scope expands: the oracle against Babylon becomes an oracle against the world (tevel). Babylon is the world-empire; its judgment types the judgment of every world-empire that sets itself against God. The MKT's world (not earth alone) captures the universal scope of tevel — the inhabited world in its totality.
13.12: Human life will become rarer than pure gold — rarer even than the gold of Ophir. The scarcity of survivors expressed through the most precious metals. Ophir's gold was the gold of temple-construction quality (1 Kings 10:11) — the finest known. Human beings will become more rare than even this superlative standard of value. The MKT's human life (not merely man) is a good universalizing rendering.
13.13: YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged — the cosmic shaking is an act of the LORD of hosts. The heavens tremble, the earth is displaced from its foundations. This language of cosmic de-creation (undoing the ordering of Gen 1) appears in NT apocalyptic (Heb 12:26-27 citing Hag 2:6; Rev 6:12-17). The MKT's tremble and displaced from its foundations are literal and accurate.
13.14: Like a hunted gazelle or like sheep scattered with no one to round them up. The individual turns to his own people and flees to his own land — the cosmopolitan empire dissolves into ethnic flight. The sheep-without-a-shepherd image (Matt 9:36; Mark 6:34) resonates here: when the shepherd-king of Babylon fails, the flock scatters. The MKT's double simile (gazelle + sheep) preserves the Hebrew parallelism.
13.15: Anyone who is caught will be run through with a sword; anyone taken prisoner will fall by the sword. The comprehensive violence of conquest — caught or captured, the outcome is the same. The rhetoric of no escape is deliberate: this is divine judgment executed through human agents, and its thoroughness is the point. No ransom, no escape, no exception for the powerful.
13.16: Their children will be dashed to pieces in front of them. One of the most disturbing verses in the OT — and one that Ps 137:9 reflects in reverse (the imprecation against Babylon). The practice was historical and documented in ancient warfare; Isaiah describes it not approvingly but as the horror that will befall Babylon what Babylon inflicted on others. The MKT preserves this without softening, which is important for honest engagement with the text's historical realism.
13.17: I am rousing the Medes against Babylon — a people who care nothing for silver and feel no delight in gold. The Medes as divine instrument are identified before they are aware of their appointment — the same pattern as Assyria in chapter 10. The Medes' indifference to treasure means they will not be bought off; they are a pure instrument of destruction. Historically, the Medes and Persians under Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BC.
13.18: Their bows will cut the young men down; they will show no mercy on newborn infants. The reversal of the Babylonian victimization of Israel (2 Kings 24-25) by exactly the same methods. The oracle operates on the lex talionis principle applied at the national level: what Babylon did to others will be done to Babylon. The MKT's bows will cut down is literal — arrows cutting through the young men.
13.19: Babylon, the most glorious of kingdoms, the pride and glory of the Chaldeans, will be overthrown by God like Sodom and Gomorrah. The Elohim title is flagged — the grammatically plural divine name with singular reference. The comparison to Sodom and Gomorrah is the ultimate standard of divine judgment: sudden, total, irreversible. Rev 18:2 applies this oracle to spiritual Babylon (Rome). The MKT's God renders Elohim as singular, following standard convention.
13.20: It will never be inhabited or lived in through all generations. The permanent desolation of Babylon is the sign of complete divine judgment. Historically, Babylon declined over centuries but was not immediately or completely desolated after 539 BC — which is why this oracle functions typologically in Revelation as describing the eschatological end of the world-empire principle that Babylon represents, not merely the historical city.
13.21: Wild animals will rest there, and its houses will be full of howling creatures. The reversal of Babylon's splendor into a haunt for wilderness animals — jackals, ostriches, wild goats, hyenas — is the standard prophetic image of complete urban collapse (also Jer 50-51). The list of animals in the MKT follows the Hebrew; some are difficult to identify precisely (lilith in v. 22 is rendered night creature or screech owl). The MKT's howling creatures captures the desolation-ambience.
13.22: Her time is almost here and her days will not be prolonged. The oracle closes with the urgency of near fulfillment — almost here (karov). The temporal urgency of prophetic oracles operates on both historical and eschatological levels: historically the fall of Babylon was imminent for Isaiah's audience two centuries later; eschatologically the fall of the world-empire system is always near for those who live under its oppression. Rev 1:3 opens with the same temporal urgency: the time is near.

14.1: ḥāmal (H2550) — show compassion/pity; more visceral than rāḥam, often connoting reluctance to destroy. Here YHWH's compassion is ʿal-Yaʿăqōb — on Jacob — resuming the covenant bond despite judgment. bāḥar (H977) — elect/choose; paired with ḥāmal to form the twin poles of election theology: compassion motivates re-election. The re-choosing after exile is not a new covenant but the old one reasserted.
14.2: gēr (H1616) — sojourner/alien resident. The ironic reversal: nations who were Israel's oppressors now escort (lāqaḥ) them home and become their servants. The word šĕbî (captive) is repurposed — those who held Israel captive are now Israel's captives. This reversal structure pervades the return-from-exile oracles in Isaiah 40-55 and anticipates eschatological restoration.
14.3: nûaḥ (H5117) — give rest/cause to settle; the same root as Noah (Nōaḥ) and the Sabbath rest. The ʿāḇōdâ qāšâ (harsh servitude) echoes the language of Egyptian bondage in Exodus 1:14, deliberately connecting Babylonian exile to the Egypt paradigm. YHWH's liberation from Babylon is presented as a second Exodus.
14.4: māšāl (H4912) — proverb, taunt-song, parable; a literary genre that mocks through comparison. The taunt against the king of Babylon is formally introduced as a māšāl, signaling that what follows is satirical polemic, not straightforward historical description. nōgēś — taskmaster/oppressor; the same word used of Egyptian overseers in Exodus 3:7. The repetition is not accidental.
14.5: maṭṭeh (H4294) — rod/staff, especially of authority; the symbol of ruling power. šēbeṭ (H7626) — scepter/staff; a near synonym but with more specifically royal connotations. The pairing of these two words frames the king's domination as both administrative coercion (maṭṭeh) and royal claim (šēbeṭ), both of which YHWH shatters simultaneously.
14.6: ḥārâ (H2750) — fury/heat; describes the intensity of the king's blows. biltî ḥāsak — "without restraint/without relief" — the particle biltî negates ḥāśak (to hold back, spare). The Babylonian empire's characteristic is the unrelenting nature of its oppression — no mercy shown, no pause granted. This is the moral charge that justifies the divine judgment of vv.22-23.
14.7: The earth's response to Babylon's fall: nāpaḥ (H5117, a variant) — to rest/breathe freely; the verb conveys release of pent-up tension. The whole earth šāqaṭ (is quiet, at rest) and pāṣaḥ rinnâ (bursts into song). The passive rest of nature anthropomorphizes creation as a sympathetic audience whose relief mirrors the relief of the oppressed nations.
14.8: The cypress trees (bĕrôšîm) and cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê Lĕbānôn) are among the tallest and most valuable trees in the ANE. Their speech — mēʾāz šākabbĕtā, "since you lay down" (ironically echoing the language of sleep/death) — functions as a nature-poem celebrating that the lumberjack who felled them has himself been felled. This is a structural pun: the king who cut down trees is himself cut down.
14.9: šĕʾôl — Sheol, the underworld; personified here as an actor that stirs itself. rāgaz (H7264) — to tremble/stir up in excitement; the animation of Sheol at the tyrant's arrival is satirical. rĕpāʾîm (H7496) — the shades, the dead; a technical term for the inhabitants of the underworld. This is not the "giants" of Genesis 6 (different context) but the departed rulers of the earth who greet the king ironically.
14.10: The shades' speech — all of them saying gam-ʾattâ ḥullêtā kāmônû, "you too have become weak like us" — uses ḥālal (H2470, to be weak/sick/wounded) in its niphal form. The taunt is that the mightiest king has been reduced to the same helplessness as the powerless dead. The word ḥullêtā also puns on ḥālal as "profaned/desecrated" — a double shame.
14.11: gāʾôn (H1347) — pride, arrogance, majesty; the royal self-exaltation. nebel kinnôreykā — the noise of your harps/lyres; the royal music that accompanied the king's feasts in Babylon is silenced. rimmâ (H7415) — maggot/worm; tôlēʿâ — worm/grub. The descent from palace music to bed of maggots is deliberately extreme — the lowest point contrasted with the highest.
14.12: hêlēl ben-šāḥar — "shining one, son of the dawn/aurora" — the most debated phrase in the chapter. hêlēl (H1966) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible; it derives from hālal (to shine/praise) and refers to the planet Venus as morning star. The Latin Vulgate rendered it Lucifer (light-bearer), a translation that later became a proper name in Christian tradition. The text is using Babylonian royal mythology: the king compared himself to the dawn-star that outshines all other stars. nāpal (H5307) — fallen; gādaʿtā — cut down; the twin verbs describe complete reversal.
14.13: The five ʾeʾĕleh ("I will ascend/mount") statements: (1) šāmayim (heaven), (2) above kôkĕbê ʾēl (stars of God), (3) at the har môʿēd (mountain of assembly) in the far north, (4) above the bāmôt ʿāb (heights of the clouds), (5) be like ʿelyôn (Most High). The fifth is the culminating claim — not equality with God but likeness to the Most High, the ultimate sacrilege of self-deification.
14.14: ʿelyôn (H5945) — Most High; the divine title used especially in contexts of universal sovereignty (Gen 14:18-20; Num 24:16; Deut 32:8). The king does not claim to be God but to be like God — the same temptation as Gen 3:5 ("you will be like God"). The trajectory from ambition to Sheol in vv.13-15 forms a rhetorical arc that answers every claim with its opposite.
14.15: ʾak — "but/yet" — the adversative particle that punctures the five boasts. šĕʾôl — Sheol; yarkĕtê-bôr — the innermost depths of the pit/cistern. The reversal is total: from ascending to heaven to descending to the lowest pit. The word bôr (pit) is used for both cisterns and graves in the Hebrew Bible — the descent is spatial and permanent.
14.16: šātat (H7582) — to make tremble/cause to shake; the king who once made the earth shake is now himself an object of spectators' scrutiny. hittîr ʾāreṣ — "made the earth tremble" — his former power is now the ironic measure of his humiliation. The verb rāʾâ (gaze/look closely) implies the stunned contemplation of those who see what has become of him.
14.17: The indictment: nātaṣ (H5422) — to pull down/demolish; lōʾ-pitaḥ — "did not open" — he never released prisoners. The crime is not merely political but against the created order: the king closed what should have been opened (freedom for the captive), creating the disorder that necessitates divine reversal. This frames the oracle within the structure of Isaiah 61:1 (opening prison to captives).
14.18: kĕbôd (H3519) — glory/honor; all kings lie in glory in their own burial places. The contrast with the Babylonian king's fate is emphatic — every other king gets honorable burial, but not this one. qeber (H6913) — tomb/grave; each king in "his own house" (bêtô), his own dignified resting place.
14.19: neṣer niʾāṣ — "rejected/abhorred branch" — the most significant inversion in the chapter. neṣer (H5342) occurs in Isa 11:1 as the "shoot/branch" from Jesse's root (the messianic king). Here the Babylonian king is a neṣer but niʾāṣ (abhorred), contrasting directly with the Davidic branch who is honored. The linguistic connection is deliberate: the true king from Jesse's root stands against the false king who claims divine status.
14.20: zereʿ mĕrēʿîm — "seed of evildoers"; lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ lĕʿôlām — "shall never be named forever." The denial of a name (šēm) is the ultimate punishment in the ANE honor-shame culture — to be nameless is to be as if one never existed. Contrast with Isa 56:5 (those who obey will receive an everlasting name better than sons and daughters).
14.21: māṣṣēbâ lĕ-ʾābôt — "on account of their fathers' guilt/iniquity." The intergenerational judgment here is not arbitrary — it reflects the ANE understanding that dynasties and lineages perpetuate their founding character. The sons are executed so they do not fill the earth with cities (ʿārîm) — the Babylonian imperial project of building empire through conquest. This is the cancellation of the king's legacy.
14.22: Four terms for total annihilation: šēm (name), šĕʾār (remnant/survivor), nîn (offspring/posterity), nekeḏ (descendant). The fourfold formulation is a rhetorical device ensuring completeness — no continuation is possible in any dimension of identity. This is YHWH's declaration, not simply Cyrus's conquest; divine speech is indicated by the oracle formula that follows.
14.23: qipôd (H7090) — hedgehog or owl (species debated); ʾagmê-mayim — pools/stagnant marshes. The imagery of wild creatures and swamp reclaiming a great city reverses the civilizing project — Babylon built on the Euphrates becomes a wasteland. ṭāʾṭēʾ (H2894) — to sweep out; the broom of destruction is a vivid metaphor for total clearance.
14.24: The oath formula nišbaʿ Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "the LORD of hosts has sworn" — invokes the strongest possible divine guarantee. The parallel ʾim-lōʾ clause and the double "exactly as I planned... exactly as I purposed" (ken tihyeh... ken tāqûm) emphasize the inviolability of the divine ʿēṣâ (counsel/purpose). Divine sovereignty is the foundation of prophetic certainty.
14.25: ʾereṣ — "my own land" — YHWH claims territory: Assyria will be destroyed on YHWH's own ground, the land of Israel. mōʾes — "be crushed/trodden"; harāy — "on my mountains." The defeat of Assyria (fulfilled in 2 Kings 19:35) is presented as YHWH asserting territorial sovereignty. ʿul — yoke; sûbl — burden; the imagery of liberation from oppression.
14.26: ʿēṣâ (H6098) — counsel/plan/purpose; the term used throughout Isaiah for YHWH's sovereign governance of history (cf. 14:27; 19:17; 23:8-9; 25:1; 28:29; 46:10-11). The plan is kol-hāʾāreṣ — for the whole earth — not merely regional. YHWH's sovereignty over Babylon is simultaneously sovereignty over all nations; no empire is outside his jurisdiction.
14.27: The rhetorical questions mî yāpēr ("who can annul/frustrate?") and mî yāšîḇ ("who can turn it back?") are the basis for the radical confidence of prophetic proclamation. Isaiah 40:14 and 46:10 develop this theme fully. The extended hand of YHWH (yādô haneṭûyâ) is a standard Exodus image (cf. Deut 4:34; 26:8) applied now to universal judgment.
14.28: The superscription bišnat môt hammelek ʾāḥāz — "in the year King Ahaz died" (c. 715 BCE) — provides a historical anchor for the oracle against Philistia. The chronological precision is unusual; it may reflect a specific historical occasion when Philistian envoys came to Jerusalem after Ahaz's death to test Hezekiah's loyalty. Isaiah's response would then be this oracle.
14.29: šōreš nāḥāš — "root of the snake/serpent" (Assyria that beat Philistia); śerāp mĕʿôpēp — "fiery flying serpent" — a more dangerous successor. The escalating threat-imagery (snake → viper → fiery flying serpent) is deliberate: each Assyrian successor is more terrible. This is probably a warning to Philistia not to seek alliance with Judah's enemies, since Judah's "oppressor" will only be replaced by worse.
14.30: bĕkôrê dallîm — "firstborn of the poor" — an idiom for the poorest among the poor; or alternatively, the "chief poor" (most completely destitute). The verse contrasts the fate of the poor (they will have pasture and safety — lābeṭaḥ) with the fate of Philistia's root (destroyed by famine and remnant killed). The social reversal follows the Isaianic pattern established in 1:19-20 and developed in 25:4-5.
14.31: ʿāšān miṣṣāpôn — "column of smoke from the north" — Assyrian armies traditionally advanced from the north along the Mediterranean coast. The šāʾ môʿĕdāyw (none straggles/no deserter in his ranks) emphasizes the invader's total discipline and organization — Philistia faces a force without weakness. The gates and cities are addressed in apostrophe.
14.32: The concluding theological question: what answer to the malʾăkê-gôy (envoys of the nations)? The answer is twofold: (1) Yhwh yissad ṣiyyôn — "YHWH has established Zion" — a perfect tense with certainty; (2) the poor of his people will find refuge in her. This closes the oracle against Philistia by contrasting Philistia's doom with Zion's permanence — the theological crux of chapters 13-23.

15.1: māśśāʾ (H4853) — "oracle/burden" — from nāśāʾ (to lift/carry); used for prophetic pronouncements against foreign nations (cf. 13:1; 17:1; 19:1; 21:1; 22:1; 23:1). The word suggests both a pronouncement and a heavy load. layl (night) twice — "in a single night... in a single night" — emphasizes the suddenness and totality of Moab's fall. šāddad (H7703) — devastated/destroyed.
15.2: bāmôt (H1116) — high places, the hilltop shrines characteristic of Moabite (and Canaanite) worship. Dibon, Nebo, Medeba — specific Moabite cities east of the Dead Sea, each with its own sacred identity. yillîl (H3213) — to wail; the verb is onomatopoeic, suggesting the sound of mourning. The shaving of beard (kol-rōʾš qārēḥâ) and cutting of hair (kol-zāqān gĕruʿâ) are ancient mourning practices (cf. Isa 22:12).
15.3: The city squares (rĕḥōbôt) and rooftops are where public lamentation takes place — weeping is communal and visible. The word yārēd (going down) may describe both the procession to the valley and the descent into sorrow. yĕlîlâ — the howling/wailing sound; a vivid auditory image of a city in communal grief.
15.4: The cities of Moab cry out — Heshbon and Elealeh (ṣāʿăqû, raised their voice) — their voices reach even to Jahaz (nišmaʿ qôlām, their voice is heard). The Moabite warriors (ḥalûṣê Môʾāḇ, the armed men of Moab) themselves tremble (rāʿăšâ nap̄šô, his soul quivers/trembles). The prophet is describing a collapse of military nerve alongside civic grief.
15.5: Isaiah's own lament: libbî lĕMôʾāḇ yizʿāq — "my heart cries out for Moab." This is one of the most striking features of Isaiah's oracles against foreign nations — the prophet does not gloat but mourns. The fugitives flee to Zoar (cf. Gen 19:22, where Lot fled to Zoar) and to Eglath-shelishiyah. The road to Lûḥît and Ḥôrônayim — specific Moabite escape routes — suggests detailed geographical knowledge.
15.6: mê Nimrîm — the "waters of Nimrim"; unidentified but probably a fertile region now deserted. šāʾôt (desolate) and yābēš (dried up) — the drying of water sources signals ecological as well as social collapse. dešeʾ yārāq ʾênennu — "the green grass is no more" — the loss of vegetation in an arid region is catastrophic.
15.7: yitrāh — the "surplus" or "accumulated wealth"; what they have saved (pĕqudātô, their stored things). The refugees carry whatever they can over the wādî haʿărābîm (the Brook of the Willows) — probably the Wadi el-Hesa (Zered). The image is of a complete evacuation.
15.8: The weeping pervades the entire border of Moab — sāḇab (surrounds/goes all around) — from Eglaim to Beer-elim. Every named location emphasizes that no part of Moab escapes grief. This is a total national lamentation, not a local disaster.
15.9: mê Dîmôn — "the waters of Dimon"; possibly a pun on dām (blood), since māleʾ dāmîm (full of blood) follows. The wordplay intensifies the horror. ʾaryēh (lion) as the destroyer who pursues the survivors — a standard image for a conquering enemy (cf. Amos 3:12). The survivors of Moab face a final threat even after the initial destruction.

16.1: šilḥû-ʾayil ʾereṣ — "send the tribute ram/lamb of the land" — the tribute request to the ruler of the land (Jerusalem?). Sela — the rock city, probably Petra; the request goes from a Moabite place of safety through the desert (midbar) toward the mount of the daughter of Zion. Whether this is diplomatic tribute-seeking or a call for Moab to submit to Judah is debated.
16.2: ʿôp-nōdēd — "a fluttering/wandering bird" — the simile for Moabite women refugees. nāšîm Môʾāḇ — the women of Moab — are specifically mentioned, emphasizing the vulnerability of the civilian population. The fords (maʿbĕrôt) of the Arnon represent the escape route.
16.3: The appeal for help: ʿăśî ṣēl — "make shade/shadow" — protection from the midday heat; habbîʾî — "bring in/hide" — the verb for sheltering refugees. The request is addressed in the imperative to an unnamed figure (Jerusalem or its ruler). The phrase ṭallēlê niddāḥîm — "shelter the scattered/driven away ones" — echoes Deut 30:4 and the Isaiah 27:13 vision of the exiles gathered.
16.4: yāgûrû — "let the outcasts of Moab sojourn with you." The appeal for sanctuary uses the language of covenant hospitality (gûr, to sojourn). mēṣ — oppressor; šōd — devastation; rômēs — the one who tramples. All three are nouns for violence. When these pass (kāllâ), the oracle promises safety. The temporal condition is eschatological — "when violence is gone."
16.5: hûkan bĕḥesed kissēʾ — "a throne will be established in steadfast love/faithfulness" — the messianic note in the oracle. ḥesed (H2617) — the covenant faithfulness/loyalty/love of YHWH (cf. 55:3). The throne of ḥesed will be occupied by one who judges rightly, seeks justice (dōrēš mišpāṭ), and acts quickly in righteousness (mĕhîr ṣedeq). The phrase anticipates the Davidic king of Isa 9:7 and 11:1-5, and ultimately the messianic king of NT expectation.
16.6: gāʾôn (H1347) — pride; the root word for Moab's characteristic sin throughout the oracles (15:1-16:14). gēʾâ and gāʾôn — repeated synonyms for pride; ʿebrātô — his fury/wrath. Moab's pride is the cause of his downfall (cf. the Proverbs pattern: "Pride goes before destruction," Prov 16:18). badîm lōʾ kēn — his boastings are not reliable/true.
16.7: All Moab wails for its raisin-cakes (ʾăšîšê Qîr-ḥāreśet) — the specific ritual foods associated with Moabite festivals (cf. Hos 3:1 where raisin-cakes are associated with Canaanite worship). The grieving is communally focused on specific cultural practices that will be lost. mōgey neḵēʾîm — "stricken/crushed men" is the condition of Moab as she laments what was.
16.8: The fields of Heshbon and the vine of Sibmah (gephen Sibmâh) — Moabite viticulture is famous in antiquity; Numbers 32:38 and Jer 48:32 both reference Sibmah's vines. The great ones of the nations have beaten down (hālĕmû) the prime branches that extended to Jazer and spread toward the desert — a vivid image of destroyed agricultural prosperity.
16.9: Isaiah's continued lament — "I will weep as Jazer weeps" — is extraordinary: the prophet participates in the mourning of a foreign nation. šibbolê qayiṣ — "the summer fruit branches"; the harvest of the vineyard will not be celebrated. This compassionate stance toward a nation under judgment models an aspect of prophetic ministry that transcends nationalistic celebration of enemies' defeats.
16.10: śimḥâ wĕgîl — "joy and gladness" removed from the fruitful land. The wine-press treading (darāḵ) with its traditional shouting (hêdād) falls silent. hišbattî — "I (YHWH) have stopped" — a divine first-person action: the cessation of harvest joy is the direct act of the LORD, not merely a natural consequence. YHWH directly removes the song of the treader.
16.11: Isaiah's lament deepens: kinnôr (H3658) — lyre/harp. The prophet's inner being (meʿay, bowels/inner parts — the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology) resonates like a lyre for Moab. lĕQîr-ḥāreś — for Qir-hareseth (another name for a key Moabite city). The prophetic compassion here is deeply personal — not performed but felt in the body.
16.12: Despite Moab's weeping on the high places (bāmôt) and its prayers in the sanctuary (miqdāš), he will not prevail (lōʾ yûkal). The futility of religious activity in the face of divine judgment is a recurring Isaianic motif (cf. 1:11-15; 58:1-7). True worship cannot coexist with injustice and pride.
16.13: haddābār ʾăšer dibber Yhwh — "this is the word that YHWH spoke" — the authentication formula confirming the oracle's divine origin. mēʾāz — "long ago/from that time" — suggesting the Moab oracle is not new but a restatement of long-established divine intention. This is editorial framing that situates the oracle in Isaiah's larger collection.
16.14: bišlōš šānîm kišnê śākîr — "within three years, as the years of a hired worker" — the temporal precision is unusual. The comparison to a hired worker's years (śākîr) means counting exactly, without any rounding or grace period. The glory of Moab (kĕbôd Môʾāb) will be contemptible (nibzeh), and a very small and feeble remnant will remain. This temporal limit both threatens and limits — judgment is real but bounded.

17.1: māśśāʾ Dammeśeq — the oracle against Damascus (the capital of Aram/Syria). mēsîr mēʿîr — "removed from being a city"; mĕʿî rûpâ — "a ruin heap/pile of ruins." ʿî and māpēlâ — these two terms for ruin are both used for urban devastation. Damascus fell to the Assyrians under Tiglath-pileser III in 732 BCE, which provides the historical context for this oracle.
17.2: The cities of Aroer (ʿārê ʿĔrôʿēr) — probably a region in Aram rather than the Aroer in Moab — are abandoned (ʿăzûbôt) to flocks. The imagery of wild animals taking over abandoned human habitations is a standard devastation-oracle feature (cf. Isa 13:20-22; 34:11-15). ʿēder — flock; the pastoral present replaces the urban past.
17.3: The fortified city (miḇṣār) ceases from Ephraim; the kingdom from Damascus. The oracle addresses the Syria-Ephraim coalition that threatened Ahaz in 734-735 BCE (cf. Isa 7:1-9). The remnant of Aram (šĕʾār ʾărām) will be like the glory of Israelites — both reduced to the same diminished state. The parallel fate of Israel and Syria is the theological point.
17.4: kĕbôd Yaʿăqōb yiddal — "the glory of Jacob will be diminished" — the contrast between Jacob's glory now and its diminished future state. mišman bĕśārô — "the fat of his flesh"; the emaciation metaphor (glory going from full to wasted) appears in famine and siege contexts. This is the judgment on the northern kingdom for its alliance with Syria against Judah.
17.5: kĕqôṣēr — "like a harvester" — the simile that controls vv.5-6. When someone gleans grain in the valley of Rephaim (ʿēmeq rĕpāʾîm, a fertile agricultural valley southwest of Jerusalem), they sweep the arm through the standing grain. The divine harvester sweeps through the population, leaving only a few.
17.6: bôṣēr (H1219) — gleaning/stripping; the remainder after the main harvest. The olive tree simile: two or three berries on the topmost bough (qōṣ-ʾōmer bērōʾš ʾāmîr) and four or five on the fruitful boughs after thorough beating. This is the "šĕʾār" — the remnant theology of Isaiah: judgment is total but not absolute; a remnant always survives. Cf. the detailed remnant theology of Isaiah 10:20-23.
17.7: bayyôm hahûʾ — "in that day" — the eschatological marker that introduces a reversal. yišʿeh haʾādām — "man will look to his maker" (ʿāśâhû, "the one who made him"). The judgment produces a return to proper orientation — the remnant will look to their creator rather than to their idols. This is the positive purpose of the divine pruning.
17.8: The things they will not look to: hammizbēḥôt (altars), maʿăśēh yādāyw (the work of their hands), and what their fingers have made (ʿāśû ʾeṣbĕʿōtāyw). The specific objects: hāʾăšērîm (Asherah poles — fertility goddess worship) and hĕḥammānîm (incense altars — associated with solar worship). These represent the full spectrum of Canaanite/syncretic religion that Isaiah consistently condemns.
17.9: The forsaken cities will be like ruins in the forests (kĕʿăzûbat hāḥōreš wĕhāʾāmîr) — like the abandoned places of the Hivites and Amorites who fled before Israel (though the text is uncertain). The point is reversal: just as Canaan was once emptied before Israel, now Israel's cities will be emptied before Assyria.
17.10: šākaḥt ʾĕlōhê yišʿēk — "you have forgotten the God of your salvation" — the core accusation. The people have planted naʿamānîm (pleasant/lovely plants, perhaps a term for sacred gardens for pagan deities) and set out zemôrat zār (slips of a foreign vine). The garden imagery may refer to ritual gardens dedicated to Adonis or another fertility deity. To plant for a foreign god is to forget one's own creator.
17.11: Though the plants are established (bĕyôm tiṭṭāʿennâ tĕśaggēj), the harvest will be gone on the day of disease and incurable pain.
