The sowing and harvest image is inverted: you planted but will not harvest — the deferred judgment that nullifies one's own effort. This reflects the covenant curse structure (Deut 28:30-31).
17.12: hôy hĕmôn — "woe to the tumult" — the escalating international noise. The simile: like the roar (šāʾôn) of many waters, like the rushing (šĕʾôn) of mighty nations. The hôy oracle is turned not against Israel now but against the nations massed against Zion — the subject shifts from Israel's sin to the nations' threat.
17.13: The nations rush forward like the rush of many waters — but YHWH rebukes (gāʿar) them and they flee far away (driven like chaff before the wind, like a rolling thing before a storm). kĕgalgal — a tumbleweed; lippnê-rûaḥ — before the wind. The contrast between the nations' threatening noise (v.12) and their actual fragility (v.13) is the rhetorical climax of the oracle.
17.14: lĕpĕnôt ʿereḇ — "at evening time" — the time of maximum threat. bĕṭerem bōqer ʾênennû — "before morning he is no more" — the sudden overnight reversal. This one-verse summary captures the entire dynamic of the chapter: threat (evening), divine intervention (overnight), extinction (morning). ḥēleq šōsēʾnû — "the portion of those who plunder us" — the theological conclusion that interprets the oracle as the fate of those who attack God's people.

18.1: ṣilṣal kĕnāpayim — "whirring/buzzing of wings" — the phrase is unique and debated. ṣilṣal can refer to (a) the buzzing of insects in a hot climate, (b) the whirring of boat sails, or (c) the clicking of locust wings. "Beyond the rivers of Cush" (ʿēḇer nǎharê-Kûš) locates the oracle in the region of modern Ethiopia/Sudan — the land of the Nile headwaters, known to Israelites as the southernmost of nations.
18.2: kĕlê-gōmeʾ — "vessels/boats of papyrus/bulrush" — the Nile papyrus reed boats, known from Egyptian imagery. ṣîrîm — ambassadors/envoys (root: to go on a mission). The envoys are sent to "a people tall and smooth-skinned" (gôy mĕmušāk ûmôrāṭ) — the Cushites are described by physical characteristics, with a sense of awe at their distinctive appearance. All nations and all land is addressed — the oracle has a universal scope.
18.3: The signal-raising (nes nissâ) and the trumpet-blowing (šôpār tĕqāʿû) are calls to attention for the whole world. kāl-yōšĕbê ṯēḇēl — "all inhabitants of the world" — tēḇēl is the inhabited, ordered earth (as opposed to chaos). The universalism of the oracle means it is not merely about the fate of Cush but about YHWH's governance of all nations.
18.4: YHWH's deliberate inaction before action: ʾeštĕqôṭâ wĕʾaббîṭâ — "I will rest/be quiet and I will look" — divine patience modeled. The simile: like clear heat (ḥōm ʾôr) over sunshine (ʿal-ʾôr) and like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest — natural images of something shimmering, visible but not yet destructive. YHWH watches before he acts; his watching is itself a form of sovereign presence.
18.5: The vine pruning (kārat hazzalzallîm) before harvest — cutting off the new shoots, removing the spreading branches (nĕṭišôt). The agricultural metaphor shows YHWH intervening at a specific moment — just when the fruit appears but before it ripens. The cut branches are left to birds of prey (ʿayiṭ hehārîm) and wild animals (bĕhēmôt hāʾāreṣ) — the unclaimed carcasses become food for scavengers.
18.6: The summer birds (ʿêṭ qayiṣ) and all the wild animals winter on the remains. This verse completes the agricultural/natural metaphor: the cut-off branches that served as food for birds in summer become a resting place for animals in winter. The two seasons are covered — the oracle describes the total consumption of what was pruned. There is no harvest for Cush to bring against Zion.
18.7: bāʿēt hahîʾ — "at that time" — the eschatological conclusion. A tribute (šay, gift/tribute) will be brought to YHWH of hosts from "a people tall and smooth-skinned" — the very people described in v.2 as threatening or challenging. The reversal: what appeared as a threatening nation sending envoys is transformed into a worshiping nation bringing tribute to Zion. har-ṣiyyôn — Mount Zion; the oracle ends with all nations oriented toward YHWH's dwelling place, the eschatological gathering point of Isaiah's theology.

5.1: šîrat dôdî = "song of my beloved/love-song." Šîr (H7892) = song; dôd (H1730) = beloved, loved one (the word of the Song of Solomon). The prophecy opens as a love poem, luring the audience before the legal indictment unfolds. karmô = "his vineyard" — kerem (H3754) always implies cultivated care and investment. bĕqeren ben-šāmen = "on a horn of a son of oil/fertility" — the most fertile hillside, a sunny south-facing slope. Every advantage was given.
5.2:
wayyĕʿazkĕqēhû — Piel of ʿāzaq (H5823) = to dig/cultivate thoroughly, a rare verb found only here. wayyĕsaqqĕlēhû — Piel of sāqal = to clear of stones — a major labor investment. šôrēq (H8321) = the finest variety of red grape vine. migdāl = watchtower; yāqeḇ = wine vat hewn in rock. Every element — clearing, planting, tower, vat — demonstrates total preparatory commitment.

wayyĕqaw laʿăśôt ʿănāḇîm wayyaʿaś bĕʾušîm = "he waited (qāwâ = hope-laden waiting) for grapes, but it produced wild grapes (bĕʾušîm)." Bĕʾušîm (H891) = worthless, stinking fruit — the antithesis of šôrēq. The disproportion between investment and return is the legal complaint.
5.3: šipṭû-nāʾ bênî ûḇên karmî = "judge (šāpaṭ, imperative), please, between me and my vineyard." The audience is summoned as jury in the rîb (lawsuit). The rhetorical brilliance: they render judgment before realizing they are judging themselves.
5.4: mah laʿăśôt ʿôd lĕkarmî wĕlōʾ ʿāśîtî bô = "What more was there to do for my vineyard that I have not done in it?" The question presupposes the answer: nothing. Every possible investment was made. The failure is entirely in the vineyard. This is the theological logic of prevenient grace preceding covenant failure.
5.5: wĕʿattâ ʾôdîʿâ-nāʾ ʾetkem ʾēt ʾăšer-ʾănî ʿōśeh lĕkarmî = "And now let me tell you what I am about to do to my vineyard." The verdict: since the vineyard failed despite total care, the protection (mĕśûkâ = hedge; gādēr = wall) will be removed. Without the hedge, it becomes trampling-ground (mirmās).
5.6:
waʾăśîtēhû bātâ = "I will make it a waste." lōʾ yizzāmēr wĕlōʾ yēʿādēr = "it will not be pruned and not be hoed" — abandonment. wĕʿālu šāmîr wāšît = "thorns and briers shall come up" — šāmîr wāšît is Isaiah's repeated pair for curse-reversed-fruitfulness (7:23-25; 9:17; 10:17; 27:4; 32:13).

wĕʿal heʿābîm ʾăṣawweh = "I will command the clouds not to rain on it" — YHWH commands the weather, unmasking his control of the cosmos. Israel's land productivity depends on covenant relationship with the one who controls rain (cf. Deut 11:14; 28:12,24).
5.7: kî kerem YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bêt yiśrāʾēl — the allegory decoded: vineyard = Israel, choice vine = Judah. wayyĕqaw lĕmišpāṭ wĕhinnēh miśpāḥ — the devastating Hebrew wordplay: mišpāṭ (justice) → miśpāḥ (bloodshed); ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) → ṣĕʿāqâ (cry of the oppressed). The near-rhyme makes the contrast both memorable and cutting: YHWH expected justice, found bloodshed; expected righteousness, heard the shriek of the oppressed.
5.8: hôy (H1945) = the woe-oracle particle, originally a funeral cry, used by prophets to pronounce coming judgment as though it were already death. First woe targets land accumulation: maggîšê bayit bĕbayit = "those who join house to house." Land consolidation violated Mosaic land law (Lev 25; Num 27:1-11), under which each family held inalienable tribal allotment. ʿad ʾepem māqôm = "until there is no more place" — economic monopoly described as spatial displacement of the poor.
5.9: The divine oath formula: bĕʾoznāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "in my ears, LORD of armies" — Isaiah hears YHWH swear. bāttîm rabbîm lĕšammâ yihyû = "many houses shall become a desolation (šammâ)" — the accumulated houses become the judgment. The collector of houses will find them all empty; the "great and fine" houses (gĕdōlîm wĕṭōḇîm) become great vacancies.
5.10: kî ʿăśeret ṣimĕdê kerem yaʿăśû bat ʾeḥāt = "ten yokes of vineyard will produce one bath." A ṣemed = the area one yoke of oxen plows in a day. A single bath (ca. 6 gallons) from ten plots is catastrophic failure — a single plot should produce 300+ liters. wĕzeraʿ ḥōmer yaʿăśeh ʾêpâ = "a homer of seed will produce an ephah" — one-tenth yield. They hoarded land for agricultural dominance; the land itself will resist them.
5.11: Second woe: early-rising drinkers. maškîmê babbōqer šēkār yirdōpû = "those who rise early to pursue (rādap = chase after) strong drink (šēkār)." The urgency of maškîmê (to rise early, H7925) applied to alcohol consumption rather than prayer or work satirizes misplaced devotion. mĕʾaḥărê bannešep = "who tarry late in the evening" — bookending the day with drunkenness.
5.12: wĕhāyâ kinnôr wāneḇel tōp wĕḥālîl wāyyin mištêhem = "the harp, lute, tambourine, flute, and wine are at their feasts." The full musical catalog — kinnôr (lyre), neḇel (harp), tōp (tambourine), ḥālîl (flute) — is not condemned as such but as a substitute for attending to YHWH's work in history. wĕʾēt pōʿal YHWH lōʾ yaḇbiṭû = "but they do not regard the work (pōʿal) of YHWH" — to miss God's work in history is spiritual blindness at the deepest level.
5.13: lākēn gālâ ʿammî mibbelî-dāʿat = "Therefore my people will go into exile for lack of knowledge (bĕlî-dāʿat)." Gālâ (H1540) = to go into exile — the technical term for Assyrian/Babylonian deportations. The cause: not factual ignorance but failure of relational daʿat (knowing YHWH, cf. 1:3; Hos 4:6). wĕkaḇĕdô mĕtê rāʿāb = "their honored ones will die of famine" — social reversal: wealthy feasters will starve.
5.14: lākēn hirḥîḇâ šĕʾôl napšāh = "therefore Sheol has enlarged its throat (napšāh, lit. its soul/throat)." Sheol is personified as an insatiable monster opening its gullet (cf. Hab 2:5). The death-as-consuming-beast motif appears in Canaanite mythology (Mot); Isaiah deploys it to describe the scale of coming judgment where even the honored (v13) are swallowed.
5.15: wayyišaḥ ʾādām wayyišpal-ʾîš = "mankind will be humbled (šāḥâ) and people brought low (šāpal)" — both verbs describe descending from height, reversing the exaltation sought by the land-grabbers and revelers. wĕʿênê gĕḇōhîm tišpalnâ = "and the eyes of the haughty will be brought low" — the proud gaze that looked down at the poor is itself brought down. This reversal-of-pride theology is central to Isaiah's theodicy (cf. Isa 2:12-17).
5.16: wayyigbaĥ YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bammišpāṭ = "but YHWH of hosts is exalted (gāḇah) in justice (mišpāṭ)." Ironic reversal: man descends, YHWH ascends — in the very act of judgment. wĕhāʾēl haqqādôš niqdaš biṣĕdāqâ = "the Holy God shows himself holy (niqdaš, niphal reflexive of qādaš) in righteousness." Holiness is not merely a status; it is demonstrated in righteous action against injustice.
5.17: wĕrāʿû ḵĕḇāśîm kĕdoḇrām = "and lambs shall graze as in their pasture." After human judgment, nature reclaims what was over-cultivated by the greedy. wĕḥurāḇôt mēʾîm gārîm yōʾkēlû = "and the ruins of the fat ones (mēʾîm) will be eaten by migrants (gārîm)" — the accumulated wealth of the wealthy becomes food/pasture for those they displaced. Poetic justice in agricultural terms.
5.18: Third woe: those who drag sin behind them like a cart. hôy môšĕkê heʿāwōn bĕḥaḇlê haššāwĕʾ = "woe to those who drag iniquity (ʿāwōn) with cords (ḥeḇel) of falsehood (šāwĕʾ)" — sin is a burden requiring effort to pull. ʿĀwōn = guilt/iniquity/punishment; ḥaḇlê šāwĕʾ = ropes of nothingness. wĕkaʿăḇôt hāʿăgālâ ḥaṭṭāʾâ = "and sin as with cart (ʿăgālâ) ropes (ʿăḇôt)" — cart ropes are thicker, heavier; sin has become structural, load-bearing.
5.19: hāʾōmĕrîm yĕmaher yāḥîšâ maʿăśēhû = "who say: let him hurry, let him hasten his work" — they mock YHWH's slowness to judge, daring him to act. wĕtiqraḇ wĕtāḇôʾ ʿăṣat qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "and let the plan (ʿēṣâ) of the Holy One of Israel draw near." Qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "Holy One of Israel" — one of Isaiah's signature divine epithets (25+ occurrences in Isaiah vs. 6 in all other OT books), linking holiness to the divine plan that the mockers challenge.
5.20: Fourth woe — the most conceptually radical. hôy hāʾōmĕrîm lārāʿ ṭôḇ wĕlaṭṭôḇ rāʿ = "woe to those who call evil good and good evil." Not merely moral confusion but category inversion — deliberate redefinition of moral categories. śāmîm ḥōšek lĕʾôr wĕʾôr lĕḥōšek = "who put darkness for light and light for darkness" — cosmological inversion. śāmîm mar lĕmāṯôq ûmāṭôq lĕmar = "who put bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter." The reversal descends from moral (good/evil) to cosmic (light/dark) to sensory (bitter/sweet): total perversion of perceivable reality.
5.21: Fifth woe: intellectual pride. hôy ḥăkāmîm bĕʿênêhem ûneḡed pĕnêhem nĕḇōnîm = "woe to those who are wise in their own eyes (bĕʿênêhem) and shrewd in their own sight." The reflexive self-certification: wisdom that only looks at itself cannot correct itself. Compare Prov 26:12 ("there is more hope for a fool than for a man wise in his own eyes") and Rom 12:16 ("do not be wise in your own sight").
5.22: Sixth woe: judicial corruption by those who pride themselves on drinking capacity. hôy gibbôrê lišĕtôt yāyin wĕʾanšê-ḥayil limSōk šēkār = "woe to those who are heroes (gibbôrê) at drinking wine and champions (ʾanšê-ḥāyil) at mixing strong drink." The irony: gibbôr = mighty man/military hero — the highest honor title now applied to drinking prowess. The warriors who should defend justice are drunk.
5.23: maṣdîqê rāšāʿ ʿēqeḇ šōḥad = "who acquit (ṣādaq, hiphil) the wicked for a bribe (šōḥad)" — hiphil of ṣādaq = to declare righteous, to acquit; here of the perversion where payment purchases legal vindication of the guilty. wĕṣidqat ṣaddîqîm yāsîrû mimmennû = "and take away the righteousness of the righteous" — the innocent condemned while the guilty are acquitted. This concrete charge explains all six woes: drunken leaders sell verdicts.
5.24: lākēn kiʾekol-qāš lĕšôn ʾēš = "therefore as the tongue of fire devours stubble" — judgment metaphors: fire on qāš (stubble) and ḥāšaš (chaff); rot in the root. Simultaneous destruction from above (fire on blossoms) and below (rot in root). kî māʾăsû ʾet-tôrat YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "because they have rejected the tôrâ of YHWH of hosts" — the summary charge. Nāʾaṣ = to spurn, treat with contempt. All six woes rest on this single root: refusal of YHWH's tôrâ.
5.25: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bĕʿammô = "and the anger (ʾap, lit. "nose/nostril" = anger by synecdoche) of YHWH burned against his people." wayyēṭ yādô ʿālāw wayyakkēhû = "he stretched out his hand against them and struck them" — the same hand-extended formula as the Exodus plagues (Exod 7:5; 9:15), now turned against Israel. bĕkol-zōʾt lōʾ-šāḇ ʾappô = "for all this, his anger has not turned away" — the refrain repeated in 9:12,17,21; 10:4, marking that judgment has not yet exhausted itself. This verse prepares the extended Assyrian-invasion section (chs. 7-12).
5.26: wĕnāśāʾ-nēs laggôyim mērāḥôq = "and he will raise a signal/banner (nēs) for nations from far away." Nēs = the military signal banner raised to summon troops. ûšĕraq lô miqqĕṣēh hāʾāreṣ = "and he will whistle (šārak) for it from the ends of the earth" — the shepherd's call or beekeeper's call to swarm. YHWH calls Assyria with the ease of summoning animals. wĕhinnēh mĕhērâ qal yāḇôʾ = "and behold, it comes swiftly" — the response is instant.
5.27: Military description of the Assyrian army: ʾên-ʿāyēp wĕʾên-kôšēl bô = "none is weary and none stumbles" — a formidable, disciplined machine. lōʾ yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān = "it does not slumber and does not sleep" — the ironically god-like quality of the invader (cf. Ps 121:4 where only YHWH never sleeps). The enemy that never sleeps is YHWH's instrument, not a divine rival.
5.28: ʾăšer ḥiṣṣāyw šĕnûnîm = "whose arrows (ḥēṣ) are sharpened." kol-qăšĕtōtāw dĕrûkôt = "all his bows are bent/strung" — drawn and ready. pārāšāyw = cavalry; sus = horse. The description builds an impression of a fully armed, mechanically precise war machine — opposite of the drunken, sleepy leaders of the woe oracles.
5.29: šĕʾāgâ lô kaллābîʾ = "its roaring is like a lion (laḇîʾ, a lion in its prime)" — šāʾag = the triumph-roar of the successful hunter. wayyiśʾ kĕkĕpîrîm wayyinhōm = "it growls like young lions" — not one lion but the pride. wayyiśʾ tereṗ wĕyaʿăḇōr = "it seizes prey and carries it off, and none rescues." YHWH himself has become the lion that hunts through his Assyrian instrument.
5.30: wĕyinham ʿālāw bayyôm hahûʾ kĕhēmôt yām = "it will growl over them on that day like the roaring of the sea" — the sea as chaos-power. wĕnibbaṭ lāʾāreṣ wĕhinnēh-ḥōšek ṣar = "and if one looks to the earth, behold darkness and distress." The chapter ends at the lowest point: ḥōšek (H2822) and ʿărāpel (H6205) = thick/deep darkness. Yet the darkness gives way (cf. 9:1-2): the chapter ends before the Immanuel oracle begins.

8.1: qah-lĕkā gillāyôn gādôl = "take a large tablet/scroll (gillāyôn, H1549)" — a polished writing board; large for public visibility. wĕktoḇ ʿālāyw bĕḥereṭ ʾĕnôš = "write on it with an ordinary stylus (ḥereṭ ʾĕnôš)" — in common/plain handwriting that anyone can read. The public inscription māhēr šālāl ḥāš baz = "swift is plunder, speedy is prey" — the prophetic summary of the Syro-Ephraimitic campaign's outcome.
8.2: wĕʾāʿîdâ lî ʿēdîm neʾĕmānîm = "I will take for myself faithful witnesses (ʿēdîm neʾĕmānîm)" — ʿēd (H5707) = legal witness; the inscription is formally testified before fulfillment. ʾet-Ûriyyâ hakkohen wĕʾet-Zĕkaryāhû ben-Yĕḇerekĕyāhû = Uriah the priest (probably the high priest who collaborated with Ahaz, 2 Kgs 16:10-16) and Zechariah son of Jeberechiah — credible court witnesses who validate the prophecy.
8.3: wāʾeqraḇ ʾel-hannĕḇîʾâ wattahar watteledabbēn = "I approached the prophetess and she conceived and bore a son" — the prophetess (nĕḇîʾâ) = Isaiah's wife (titled by her husband's prophetic role). The birth fulfills the Immanuel sign's near dimension: a young woman of Isaiah's household bears a son as a sign-child. wayyōʾmer YHWH qĕrāʾ šĕmô māhēr šālāl ḥāš baz = the name is YHWH's command — the child is a living sign-oracle.
8.4: kî bĕṭerem yēdaʿ hannaʿar qĕrōʾ ʾāḇî wĕʾimmî = "for before the child knows to call 'my father' and 'my mother'" — the linguistic-developmental marker: pre-speech, the earliest possible span. yiśśāʾ ʾet-ḥêl Dammāśeq wĕʾēt šĕlal Šōmrôn lipnê melek ʾAššûr = "the wealth of Damascus and the spoil of Samaria will be carried away before the king of Assyria." Near-fulfillment: Damascus fell 732 BCE, Samaria 722 BCE — within infancy of Maher-shalal-hash-baz. The child's infancy is the countdown clock.
8.5: wayyôsep YHWH dabbēr ʾēlay ʿôd = "and YHWH continued speaking to me again" — a new oracle appended, expanding judgment beyond the two northern kingdoms to include Judah itself, since Ahaz chose Assyria over YHWH.
8.6: yaʿan kî māʾas hāʿām hazzeh ʾet mê haššilōaḥ hahōlĕḵîm lĕʾaṭ = "because this people has rejected the waters of Shiloah that flow gently (lĕʾaṭ)." Shiloah (šilōaḥ) = the slow channel supplying Jerusalem's water from the Gihon spring (cf. John 9:7 Siloam). Its gently flowing water = YHWH's quiet, sufficient provision. Rejected in favor of the roaring Assyrian flood. wĕśimḥâ ʾet-Reṣîn ûben-Rĕmalyāhû = "and rejoices over Rezin and the son of Remaliah" — Judah's sympathy with the coalition rather than YHWH's plan.
8.7: hinnēh ʾădōnāy maʿăleh ʿălêhem ʾet-mê hannāhār hāʿăṣûmîm wĕhārabbîm = "the Lord is bringing up the waters of the River (hannāhār = the Euphrates), mighty and many." The contrast: mê haššilōaḥ hahōlĕkîm lĕʾaṭ (Shiloah's gentle stream) vs. mê hannāhār hāʿăṣûmîm (the Euphrates flood). Reject the quiet water; receive the flood. wĕʿālâ ʿal-kol-ʾăpîqāyw wĕhālak ʿal-kol-gĕḏōtāyw = "rising over all its channels and going over all its banks."
8.8: wĕḥālap bîhûḏâ šāṭap wĕʿāḇar ʿad-ṣawwāʾr yagîaʿ = "it sweeps into Judah, overflows and passes through, reaching even to the neck" — the flood reaches Judah's neck (just short of drowning, cf. 30:28). Not completely submerged: judgment but not annihilation. wĕhāyâ muttôt kĕnāpāyw mĕlōʾ rōḥaḇ ʾarṣĕkā ʿimmānûʾēl = "and the outspread of his wings will fill the breadth of your land, O Immanuel." Even in the flood-oracle, the Immanuel name resounds: the land belongs to Immanuel, not to Ahaz.
8.9: rōʿû ʿammîm wĕḥōttû = "Be broken (rāʿaʿ) O peoples, and be shattered (ḥātat)!" — the taunt to the nations: YHWH mocks their pretension. ʾizzĕrû wĕḥōttû ʾizzĕrû wĕḥōttû = "gird yourselves and be shattered; gird yourselves and be shattered" — the anaphora of futile military preparation (girding for battle only to be shattered).
8.10: ûṣû ʿēṣâ wĕtûpar daḇĕrû dāḇār wĕlōʾ yāqûm kî ʿimmānû ʾēl = "Take counsel and it will be frustrated; speak a word and it will not stand, for God is with us (ʿimmānû ʾēl)." The nations' plans are futile because of Immanuel. The name becomes a war-cry against the coalition. Note: ʿimmānû ʾēl here is YHWH's presence with Judah; in Matt 1:23 the same declaration is the name of the incarnate Son.
8.11: kî kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾēlay kĕḥizqat hayyād wĕyisĕrēnî = "For thus YHWH said to me, with strong hand (kĕḥizqat hayyād)" — the grip of prophetic compulsion; the same phrase used of Ezekiel's prophetic experiences (Ezek 3:14; 8:1). lĕbiltî lēket bĕdereḵ hāʿām hazzeh = "not to walk in the way of this people" — Isaiah warned not to adopt the political fearfulness of the people. The prophet must operate from a different epistemology.
8.12: lōʾ-tōʾmrûn qešer lĕkōl ʾăšer-yōʾmar hāʿām hazzeh qāšer = "Do not call conspiracy (qeśer) everything that this people calls a conspiracy" — the people's fear-driven conspiracism. wĕʾet-môrāʾô lōʾ-tîrāʾû wĕlōʾ taʿărîṣû = "do not fear what they fear, do not be in dread (ʿāraṣ)" — two different words for dread and terror. The prophet must not be shaped by collective political anxiety.
8.13: ʾet-YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾōtô taqddîšû = "YHWH of armies — him you shall sanctify (qiddaš, piel)." wĕhûʾ môrāʾăkem wĕhûʾ maʿărîṣăkem = "he shall be your fear and he shall be your dread" — the same two words for terror (v12) are now redirected: YHWH becomes the object of the awe that displaces political fear. 1 Pet 3:14-15 cites this verse: "Do not fear their threats... but sanctify Christ as Lord."
8.14:
wĕhāyâ lĕmiqdāš ûlĕʾeben negeṗ ûlĕṣûr mikšôl = "he will become a sanctuary (miqdāš) and a stone of stumbling (ʾeben negeṗ) and a rock of offense (ṣûr mikšôl)" — the same divine presence is both sanctuary (refuge) and stumbling stone (occasion of fall). ûlĕpāḥ ûlĕmôqēš lĕyôšēḇ Yĕrûšālaim = "and a trap and a snare for the inhabitants of Jerusalem."

The stone imagery is absorbed into NT Christology: Rom 9:33 ("a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense"); 1 Pet 2:8 ("a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense"); Jesus cites Ps 118:22 (the rejected stone becoming cornerstone, Luke 20:17-18) in the same framework. The stumbling stone that is also a sanctuary is the tension resolved only in Christ: those who trust him find refuge; those who reject him stumble on him.
8.15: wĕkāšĕlû bām rabbîm wĕnāpĕlû wĕniśbārû wĕnôqĕšû wĕnilkādû = "and many shall stumble (kāšal) on them and fall and be broken and be snared and be taken" — five verbs cascading: fall → breaking → ensnaring → capture. Total disaster for those who treat YHWH's word as an obstacle. Paul applies this to Israel's stumbling over the gospel (Rom 9:32-33); Peter applies it to those who reject the cornerstone (1 Pet 2:8).
8.16: ṣôr tĕʿûḏâ ḥătôm tôrâ bĕlimmûdāy = "Bind up the testimony (tĕʿûḏâ), seal the teaching (tôrâ) among my disciples (limmûdāy)." Limmûdāy = those who have been taught/disciples. This is the earliest use of a "disciples" concept in the prophets: Isaiah has a circle of learners who will preserve the prophetic word when the general population rejects it. The sealing is for safekeeping until fulfillment demonstrates its truth.
8.17: wĕḥikkîtî laYHWH hammastîr pānāyw mibbayt yaʿăqōb = "and I will wait for YHWH who is hiding his face (hammastîr pānāyw) from the house of Jacob." Ḥāḵâ (H2442) = to wait with earnest expectation; wĕqiwwîtî lô = "and I will hope in him" — qāwâ (H6960) = the wait of expectation (same word as 40:31). Heb 2:13 directly cites this verse ("I will put my trust in him") as words of Christ — the Servant/Son trusting the Father in the hiddenness of incarnation/passion.
8.18: hinnēh ʾānōkî wĕhayĕlādîm ʾăšer nātan-lî YHWH lĕʾōtôt ûlĕmôpĕtîm bĕyiśrāʾēl = "Behold, I and the children whom YHWH has given me are signs (ʾōtôt) and portents (mōpĕtîm) in Israel." Isaiah and his sign-children (Shear-jashub = "a remnant shall return"; Maher-shalal-hash-baz = "swift is plunder") are living oracles. mēʿim YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt haššōkēn bĕhar-ṣiyyôn = "from YHWH of armies, who dwells on Mount Zion." Heb 2:13 directly quotes this verse, placing it in Christ's mouth: "Behold, I and the children God has given me" — the children = those who share Christ's flesh and blood, those he calls brothers (Heb 2:11-14).
8.19: wĕkî yōʾmĕrû ʾălêkem dirĕšû ʾel-hāʾōḇôt wĕʾel-hayyiḏdĕʿōnîm = "when they say: Consult the mediums (ʾōḇôt) and the spiritists (yiḏdĕʿōnîm)." ʾĀḇôt = "ghost-pits" or the spirits summoned through them; yiḏdĕʿōnîm = "knowing ones," familiar spirits. hameṣapṣĕpîm wĕhammĕhagîm = "who chirp and mutter" — the sound-description of spirit-communication (cf. Matt 12:43-45 where Jesus uses spirit-language in a related context). hălôʾ ʿam ʾel-ʾĕlōhāyw yiḏrōš = "should not a people consult their God?" — the obvious counter: YHWH is the living God who speaks through prophets.
8.20: lĕtôrâ wĕlitĕʿûḏâ = "To the tôrâ and to the testimony!" — the standard is YHWH's revealed word, the tôrâ-tĕʿûḏâ pair = the whole prophetic deposit (cf. v16). ʾim-lōʾ yōʾmĕrû kaḏḏāḇār hazzeh ʾăšer ʾên-lô šāḥar = "if they do not speak according to this word, there is no dawn (šāḥar) for them." The test: those who speak against tôrâ have no light. The darkness they inhabit is their judgment — until the dawn of ch9 breaks.
8.21: wĕʿāḇar bāh niqšeh wĕrāʿēḇ wĕhāyâ kî-yirʿaḇ wĕhitqaṣṣap = "they will pass through the land, hard pressed and hungry; when they are hungry they will be enraged (qāṣap)." The social unraveling of judgment: hunger and desperation. wĕqillēl bĕmalkô ûḇĕʾĕlōhāyw ûpānâ lĕmāʿĕlâ = "and will curse their king and their God, looking upward" — blame God and king in anger rather than turning to God in prayer.
8.22: wĕʾel-ʾereṣ yabiṭ wĕhinnēh ṣārâ waḥăšēḵâ = "and they will look to the earth, and behold, distress (ṣārâ) and darkness (ḥăšēḵâ)." Looking up: cursing; looking down: anguish. No direction offers relief. ûʾăpēlâ mĕnuddāḥ = "and into thick darkness (ʾăpēlâ) they are driven away (nāḏaḥ)" — ends at the darkest point. But this is the pivot: ch9:1-7 opens immediately with the people who walked in darkness have seen a great light — the Immanuel oracle finds its completion in the Light that breaks from this very darkness.

19.1: The oracle of Egypt. Massa (oracle/burden) opens this unit as it opened ch 13. YHWH is flagged -- the divine personal name is the one coming on a swift cloud to Egypt. The cloud-rider imagery evokes Ps 68:4 and 104:3, where the LORD rides the clouds; that title belongs to YHWH alone. Idols of Egypt (elilim) -- worthless things, cognate with nothing -- tremble before the LORD coming in theophanic judgment.
19.2: I will stir up Egyptians against Egyptians. Divine sovereignty over internal Egyptian strife -- the LORD is the active agent behind civil conflict that appears merely political. The Hebrew yasseti (I will stir up) makes YHWH the prime mover. The structure moves from the high-level announcement (v1) to the specific mechanisms of judgment (vv2-4).
19.3: Egypt's spirit will drain away within it. Ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- rendered spirit. Here, ruach refers to Egypt's national capacity for wisdom, courage, and governance; it is being emptied out (vavuqqah: drained). The MKT's drain away captures the emptying image. Egypt turns to enchanters, mediums, and spiritists -- replacing the LORD's counsel with occult substitutes. Idols (v1) and spirit (v3) both fail simultaneously.
19.4: I will hand Egypt over to a harsh master; a cruel king will rule over them. YHWH is flagged as the one making this pronouncement. Melek (king, disp 2) -- the cruel king is deliberately unnamed, keeping the oracle at the level of type (Assyrian kings -- Esarhaddon, Ashurbanipal -- are historical referents). The MKT preserves the Hebrew pair adon qasheh (harsh master) and melek az (cruel king).
19.5: The Nile's drying is the centerpiece of Egypt's agricultural judgment -- Egypt is the gift of the Nile, and without it there is no harvest. The MKT's parched and drained captures the two stages of water loss. The LORD does not need armies when he can remove the water: judgment works through the withdrawal of created goods.
19.6: The channels and branches of the Nile system are specified. Reeds and rushes -- the papyrus-producing plants, Egypt's primary writing material and export commodity -- wither and rot. Judgment works from the main channel outward, drying up the entire delta system.
19.7: The papyrus reeds are the most economically significant detail: Egypt's papyrus trade supplied the ancient world's writing materials. The MKT's crops planted along its banks broadens the picture from papyrus to the full agricultural system. When the Nile withdraws, everything planted along it dies in sequence from the main channel outward.
19.8: The fishermen mourn -- hook, net, and seine fishermen whose livelihood depends on the Nile's abundance. The mourning (avelu) is the formal vocabulary of communal lamentation; this is more than economic loss. The MKT distinguishes lament (for hook fishermen) and languish (for net fishermen), following the Hebrew verbal distinctions.
19.9: Linen workers and weavers -- Egypt's other major export industry after papyrus. Egyptian fine linen was famous throughout the ancient world. Both the raw material workers (combed flax) and the finished-goods manufacturers (weavers) are put to shame -- the economic ripple from water loss reaches every major industry.
19.10: Egypt's foundations will be crushed; all who work for hire will be sick at heart. Nefesh is flagged (disp 3) -- the MKT renders the workers as sick at heart. The Hebrew kol-sachire nefesh is literally all the hired-ones of soul -- those who hire out their whole vital selves for wages. Sick at heart captures not just economic distress but deep existential anguish when life-defining work is destroyed.
19.11: The wisest of Pharaoh's counselors give worthless advice. The confrontation between Egypt's legendary wisdom and the word of the LORD. Egyptian wisdom was renowned (1 Kings 4:30); Isaiah ironically dismantles it. The princes of Zoan (Tanis, capital of the Nile delta) and their wise men cannot decode what the LORD is doing -- wisdom that lacks the fear of the LORD is hollow at the critical moment.
19.12: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name is the one whose plan the Egyptian wise men cannot decode. The rhetorical challenge -- where are they now? -- is devastating: the wisdom tradition collapses when confronted with genuine divine action. Yada (know, disp 2) appears in make known (causative): the wise men cannot cause-to-be-known what the LORD has decreed.
19.13: The leaders of Egypt have led her astray. Those who should be the anchor of Egyptian governance have become stumbling-blocks. The princes of Zoan are treated as fools (nivalu) and Memphis's princes are deluded (hitvu). The geographic diversity of failed leadership (Zoan in the delta, Memphis in the center) suggests all of Egypt's governing centers have collapsed simultaneously.
19.14: YHWH is flagged; ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- a second occurrence, now a spirit the LORD actively pours in (massakh: mixed/poured). This is judicial deception -- when a people persistently refuse truth, the LORD gives them over to confusion (cf. 1 Kings 22:22; Rom 1:24-28). The MKT's spirit of confusion distinguishes this active divine gift of disorientation from the natural political chaos of v2.
19.15: There will be nothing Egypt can accomplish -- neither head nor tail, neither palm branch nor reed. The head-tail and palm branch-reed idioms (already used in 9:14-15 for Judah) reappear for Egypt -- the judgment vocabulary is transferable across nations. From top to bottom, from the honorable to the worthless, nothing remains capable of action.
19.16: YHWH is flagged -- the hand of the LORD is the source of Egypt's terror. The comparison to frightened women is a stock ancient Near Eastern image of military collapse -- the reversal of martial confidence into panic. The phrase in that day (repeated five times in vv16-23) marks this section as eschatological: a future period when the LORD's redemptive purposes for Egypt unfold.
19.17: YHWH is flagged -- the land of Judah becomes a source of dread to Egypt because of the LORD's purposes (etzah: counsel/plan). The mention of Judah is startling: tiny Judah, perennially Egypt's dependency, becomes frightening to Egypt because it carries the LORD's counsel. Whoever stands with the LORD of hosts becomes formidable to all who oppose him.
19.18: YHWH is flagged. In that day five cities in Egypt will speak the language of Canaan -- Hebrew, the language of the covenant people -- and swear loyalty to the LORD of hosts. Five Egyptian cities speaking Hebrew and swearing allegiance to YHWH is the oracle's most extraordinary future-hope element. One city is called the City of Destruction (Hebrew heres) or the City of the Sun (Heliopolis) in a manuscript variant -- the textual ambiguity may be deliberate wordplay. The MKT follows the heres reading.
19.19: YHWH is flagged twice -- an altar dedicated to the LORD at Egypt's center, and a standing stone at Egypt's border dedicated to the LORD. Both altar and matsevah (standing stone) are covenant-marker forms. In the Torah, standing stones mark patriarchal covenant events (Gen 28:18); here Egypt erects its own. The MKT's in the heart of Egypt emphasizes that this is not a peripheral border cult but a central worship practice.
19.20: YHWH is flagged twice -- the altar and pillar serve as sign and witness to the LORD of hosts in the land of Egypt. The covenant-legal language is applied to Egypt's worship. When Egypt cries out to the LORD, he will send a savior (moshia) and champion -- the same word used for the judges who delivered Israel (Judg 3:9,15). The MKT's deliver and rescue them preserves the doubled Hebrew verb of salvation.
19.21: YHWH is flagged three times. The LORD will make himself known to Egypt, and the Egyptians will acknowledge the LORD in that day. Yada (know, disp 2) appears twice -- the LORD making himself known and the Egyptians knowing/acknowledging him. The second yada is covenantal acknowledgment-knowing, not mere intellectual awareness. The Egyptians will worship with sacrifice, grain offering, vows fulfilled -- the full vocabulary of covenant worship applied to the former oppressors of Israel.
19.22: YHWH is flagged twice. The LORD will strike Egypt -- striking and healing. The paradox of divine chastisement as ultimately remedial. The repeated striking-and-healing is not cruelty but covenantal discipline that produces repentance. The Egyptians will shuv (return/repent) to the LORD -- the core word for covenant restoration -- and the LORD will heal them with active therapeutic care.
19.23: In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria. The highway connecting the scattered (11:16; 40:3) now connects Egypt and Assyria -- Israel's two great enemies -- in shared worship. The three nations together avad (serve/worship) the LORD. The MKT's will worship together captures the shared cultic life the highway enables. The nations who enslaved and threatened Israel are now co-worshippers.
19.24: In that day Israel will be the third alongside Egypt and Assyria, a blessing in the midst of the earth. The three-in-a-row structure -- Egypt, Assyria, Israel -- is itself remarkable: Israel in the middle, no longer defined by opposition to these nations but by shared membership in the LORD's community of blessing. The blessing in the midst of the earth echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) now extended to include Israel's former oppressors as co-recipients.
19.25: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name as the blesser. Egypt my people -- the covenant term ami (my people), used exclusively in the OT for Israel, is here applied to Egypt. Assyria the work of my hands -- creator-language. Israel my inheritance -- land-gift language. Three equally honorific divine titles for three nations. This verse is the zenith of Gentile-inclusion prophecy in the first half of Isaiah (chs 1-39), surpassing even 2:2-4.

20.1: The historical anchor: Sargon king of Assyria -- the only occurrence of Sargon's name in the Bible, confirmed by Assyrian annals for the capture of Ashdod in 711 BC. The commander-in-chief (tartan) is an Assyrian military title preserved in Hebrew transliteration. Melek (king, disp 2) -- Sargon as king is the flagged usage; the MKT's king of Assyria is standard. The precision of the date-formula is a claim of specific historical grounding for what follows as prophetic sign-act.
20.2: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name as the one who speaks through (beyad: by the hand of) Isaiah. The command to Isaiah is stark: remove sackcloth and sandals and walk naked. Sackcloth was the prophetic garment, identifying Isaiah's public prophetic identity. The command strips him of his identity-garb to embody the stripping of Egypt and Cush. The MKT's he obeyed makes Isaiah's compliance explicit from the Hebrew verbal form.
20.3: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name confirms the command and its duration: three years as a sign and portent (ot u-mofet). Sign and portent is the paired phrase of prophetic symbolic action (Deut 28:46; Ezek 4-5). Three years of nakedness is an extraordinary commitment to prophetic embodiment. The word portent (mofet) means wonder or omen -- Isaiah's naked body is an omen of what is coming to Egypt and Cush.
20.4: Melek (king, disp 2) -- the king of Assyria who will lead away the Egyptian captives. The sign is interpreted: naked and barefoot Isaiah represents the captives who will be stripped and marched out. The Hebrew erom (naked) is intensified by specifying buttocks bared -- the humiliation of captivity described with anatomical frankness the oracle requires. The MKT preserves this without euphemism.
20.5: Nations that had relied on Cush and boasted about Egypt will find their guarantor stripped and captive. The language of hope shifted to terror and shame -- the standard prophetic reversal of misplaced confidence. Egypt trusted in idols (ch 19); nations trusted in Egypt; now both the idols and Egypt fail together.
20.6: Melek (king, disp 2) -- the coastland peoples realize their flight to Egypt as protection from the king of Assyria has failed. The oracle closes with the question: if Egypt -- the one we escaped to -- has been captured, how shall we ourselves escape? The sign-act (three years of nakedness) has compressed a multi-year geopolitical process into bodily demonstration.

21.1: The oracle of the Desert of the Sea -- paradoxical title that has puzzled commentators. The oracle concerns Babylon (identified in v9), and the Babylonian plain between the Tigris and Euphrates -- flat, semi-arid, seasonally flooded -- could be described as both desert and sea-flat. The MKT preserves the paradoxical title. Whirlwinds sweeping through the Negeb create visceral urgency: swift, uncontrollable, and desiccating.
21.2: The grim vision identifies the agents: Elam and Media will besiege Babylon. Historically, the Medo-Persian coalition under Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BC -- this oracle anticipates it by generations. The Hebrew uses the same root word for both agent and action (boged yibgod, shoded yishod): the traitor practices his trade, the plunderer plunders thoroughly. Repetition creates the inescapability of the judgment.
21.3: Isaiah's physical response to the vision -- racked with anguish, labor pains, staggering with terror. The prophet becomes the receiver of what is shown in a way that involves bodily suffering. This is not detached prediction but participatory revelation: the prophet's body registers the weight of what is coming. The evening of longing turned into dread -- the watchman waiting for morning (v11) finds the night extending.
21.4: The reeling mind and shuddering terror continue. The prophet had been waiting for the collapse of Babylon as a moment of relief for God's people; instead the vision is more terrible than expected. This prophetic ambivalence -- longing for judgment on the wicked but being shattered by its horror when it arrives -- is a mark of authentic prophetic sensibility (cf. Jeremiah's laments, Hab 3:16).
21.5: The feast scene: Set the table, spread the rugs, eat and drink! The Babylonian feasting party is interrupted by the sudden call to arms -- Rise up, O princes, and oil the shields. Oiling leather shields was preparation for battle. The juxtaposition of feast and military emergency is historically grounded in Daniel 5, where Belshazzar's feast is interrupted by the Medo-Persian conquest. The MKT preserves the abruptness of the transition.
21.6: The Lord (Adonai) commissions the watchman -- a different divine title here, not YHWH. The commissioning of a watchman is a formal military-prophetic action. The watchman imagery runs through Ezekiel (3:17; 33:2-7) and becomes the model for the prophetic role: stationed to report what he sees, responsible for faithfully conveying the divine warning.
21.7: The watchman sees a procession -- cavalry pairs, donkey caravans, camel caravans -- the movement of a large siege force. The instruction to listen diligently, very diligently repeats the adverb for urgency. The MKT's doubled adverb follows the Hebrew. The mixed procession (donkeys + camels + cavalry) suggests a siege train: supplies and military force moving together.
21.8: The lookout calls from the watchtower: I stand continually by day, and I keep my watch post every night. The faithfulness of the watchman (continually, every night) contrasts with the coming suddenness of the announcement. The MKT follows the lookout reading rather than the lion (aryeh) variant of some manuscripts.
21.9: Fallen, fallen is Babylon! All the carved images of her gods are shattered to the ground! Elohim is flagged (disp 4) -- the plural divine name used for Babylon's gods. The dual fallen, fallen creates the repetition that becomes the signature of the eschatological Babylon announcements in Rev 14:8 and 18:2, which quote this verse verbatim. The MKT's exact phrasing preserves the doubled announcement that Revelation cites.
21.10: O my people, crushed and winnowed. Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- the God of Israel, the God of hosts, has spoken. The address my threshing, son of my threshing floor is one of Isaiah's most tender images for the suffering of God's people: they are grain that has been threshed by the events of history. The announcement is not triumphalism but pastoral assurance: what the LORD of hosts reported, Isaiah has now passed on.
21.11: The oracle of Dumah -- possibly Edom (edom and dumah share consonants; Seir is Edomite territory). The night-watchman cry: Watchman, what is left of the night? Night carries symbolic weight of judgment and uncertainty; morning represents deliverance. The doubled question suggests the inquirer's anxiety: the night feels interminable, and they need assurance that it will end.
21.12: The watchman's answer is enigmatic: morning comes, but so does night -- if you want to inquire, inquire; come back again. For Edom specifically (if Dumah = Edom), the oracle offers no final resolution -- a partial morning, not an eschatological dawn. The invitation to come back leaves a door open for further inquiry, which is itself a form of grace extended to those outside the covenant.
21.13: The oracle concerning Arabia -- the Dedanite caravans taking shelter in the thickets of Arabia. Dedanites were a trading people from northwestern Arabia (cf. Ezek 27:20); their caravans were famous long-distance trade routes. The taking-shelter image suggests the disruption of normal trade and the flight of merchants into the wilderness to escape approaching armies.
21.14: The people of Tema -- another Arabian tribe east of the Dedanites -- are called to bring water and food to the fugitives. The humanitarian appeal within the oracle of judgment: even amid warfare, the obligation to care for the displaced persists. The ancient Near Eastern hospitality ethic applied to a refugee crisis.
21.15: The fugitives have fled from swords, drawn swords, bent bows, and the fury of battle. The four instruments of war -- sword (sheathed/unsheathed), bow (bent/aimed), general battlefield fury -- enumerate the full range of military threat. The cascading list of dangers gives the sense of enemies on all sides with no direction of safety.
21.16: Within a year, reckoned as a hired worker counts a year. The temporal precision -- a hired worker's year is a full twelve months, not an approximation -- makes the oracle specific and verifiable. All the kavod (glory, disp 2) of Kedar (a major Arabian tribal confederation) will fail. The MKT renders kavod as glory -- the honor and splendor of the Kedarite warriors is what the oracle targets.
21.17: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- the LORD, the God of Israel is the speaker who decrees the diminishing of Kedar's warriors. The double divine title (YHWH + Elohim of Israel) emphasizes the covenant-specific character: the God who is Israel's particular God is also the one who sets the limits of Arabia's power. The chapter closes with this covenantal note: Kedar's archers are the remnant left after the LORD's word runs its course.

22.1: The burden of the Valley of Vision. Massa -- the oracle-burden, now applied to Jerusalem itself. The Valley of Vision is associated with Jerusalem (the Hinnom Valley or the broader depression around the city). The irony is acute: Jerusalem, the place of divine vision, has become the object of the oracle of judgment. The scene of the people on rooftops celebrating while surrounded by siege fits either the Assyrian threat (701 BC) or a later Babylonian siege.
22.2: You are a city full of uproar and celebration! The Hebrew mehuma (tumult) and aliza (exultant) depict a city in festive mood despite military crisis. The shocking note: your slain were not killed by the sword -- they died not heroically in battle but from famine, disease, and flight. Not a warrior's death but the disgrace of non-combat death in siege conditions. The MKT preserves the distinction.
22.3: All your commanders fled together. Military leadership's flight without battle -- captured without a bow being drawn. The bow (keshet) as synecdoche for military resistance: not a single bow was drawn in defense. The commanders were found and taken by archers in their flight -- captured, not defeated. The dishonor of this kind of surrender is what Isaiah mourns in v4.
22.4: Isaiah refuses to be comforted: Turn away from me; let me weep bitterly. The prophet personally mourns over Jerusalem's ruin -- he is not detached from the people he indicts. Pastoral lamentation alongside prophetic judgment: both are authentic responses to the same reality. The personal voice (I said, let me weep bitterly) makes this more than oracle; it is private grief at covenant failure.
22.5: The double divine title Adonai YHWH-Sabaoth -- Master + covenant name + army commander -- governs the day of panic. The three nouns for what fills the Valley: mehuma (panic), mirmas (trampling), mevukha (confusion) -- alliterative in Hebrew. The battering of walls and crying to the mountains fills the day with disaster from every direction.
22.6: Elam and Kir -- two nations used as Assyrian auxiliaries -- contribute to the siege. Elam's quiver and Kir's shield represent different military units: archery and close-combat infantry. The specificity of military equipment (quiver, chariot, cavalry, shield) gives the oracle historical density. Jerusalem is surrounded by the composite forces of the Assyrian imperial army.
22.7: The finest valleys choked with chariots and cavalry posted at the gates -- the siege of the city from the surrounding terrain. The finest valleys are productive agricultural land now turned into a military staging area. Cavalry at the city gates means no one enters or exits -- the classic siege cut-off. The MKT's choked with chariots captures the sense of overwhelming density.
22.8: He stripped away Judah's defenses. The masculine singular agent -- the LORD himself -- removed Judah's covering on that day. The people's response was to look to the human arsenal (Solomon's armory, the House of the Forest, 1 Kings 7:2) rather than to the LORD. When divine protection is withdrawn, the people's instinct is to trust human military resources rather than to return to the one who withdrew the protection.
22.9: The technical details of Jerusalem's water system -- the gaps in the wall, the lower pool -- are consistent with Hezekiah's Siloam tunnel (2 Kings 20:20; 2 Chron 32:30). The preparations are practically competent. But v11 will indict the failure behind all this competent preparation: they looked to the human water system and not to the one who fashioned it long ago.
22.10: Taking inventory of the houses and demolishing buildings to shore up the wall -- a standard siege-preparation practice. Jerusalem's response to the threat is technically rational: census the population, mobilize the housing, strengthen the walls. The problem is not the tactics but the theology: all this human preparation occurs without returning to the LORD whose word determines the outcome.
22.11: But you did not look to him who made it. The accusation is not against the practical preparations but against the direction of trust. The two pools, the wall, the cistern -- all created by the same LORD who fashioned the land long ago. The MKT's you did not look to him who made it and you did not see him who planned it long ago preserves the two Hebrew verbs of spiritual perception that Jerusalem failed to exercise.
22.12: YHWH-Sabaoth called for weeping, mourning, shaved heads, and sackcloth -- the standard signs of grief and repentance in a crisis. The divine expectation was penitential return. What actually happened (v13) is the opposite: celebration and feasting. The contrast between what the LORD called for and what the people chose is the moral center of the oracle against Jerusalem.
22.13: Instead there was only celebration -- slaughtering oxen, killing sheep, eating meat and drinking wine. Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die. The feast of denial: present-tense verbs of consuming, then the fatalistic refrain. Paul cites this verse in 1 Cor 15:32 as the logical conclusion of a theology without resurrection: if the dead are not raised, hedonistic fatalism is the rational response. The MKT's exact phrasing matches the form Paul cites.
22.14: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD revealed this directly in the prophet's hearing: This guilt will never be atoned for while you live. The irrevocability is unusual in Isaiah, where most oracles of judgment are conditional or reversible. The phrase niglah be-aznai (uncovered/revealed in my ears) conveys intimate auditory revelation. The oracle carries the weight of a final refusal of repentance, beyond which further intercession is pointless.
22.15: The oracle shifts from the people collectively to Shebna, the palace administrator (asher al-habayit: the one over the house -- the chief steward). The abrupt targeting of a specific bureaucrat is unusual prophetic practice. Shebna is accused of carving a grand tomb for himself -- claiming the honor of the royal burial ground for a man of no distinguished lineage. The divine commissioning formula (Go) makes this a direct divine charge.
22.16: What gives you the right to be here? Who do you think you are? The blunt rhetorical challenge -- not even naming Shebna, addressing him as this man -- drips with prophetic contempt. The tomb was carved in the rock on a height, the most prestigious burial location. The MKT's what gives you the right captures the Hebrew mah-lekha poh literally: what is to you here? The implied answer: nothing you have earned.
22.17: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD is about to throw Shebna away violently. The metaphor of hurling -- tavhevkha: whirl you around and throw you -- is physical and humiliating. The violent throw of a large man from a high position. The MKT's hurl you away with a violent throw captures the forceful Hebrew. The promised honor of a grand tomb is inverted: Shebna will die in a foreign land.
22.18: YHWH is flagged; kavod (glory, disp 2) -- your glorious chariots will be the shame of your master's house. The kavod that Shebna sought through his grand tomb and ceremonial chariots becomes the very vehicle of his shame. The MKT's glorious chariots renders kavod's range from physical splendor to proud status. Outward display of honor becomes the marker of disgrace when expelled.
22.19: The removal oracle is stated plainly: I will remove you from your position, and you will be pulled down from your office. The MKT uses the pair remove / pulled down to translate the Hebrew double verb of displacement. Both the status-role and the functional-role are taken away. The displacement creates the vacancy that Eliakim (v20) is called to fill.
22.20: Eliakim son of Hilkiah is summoned. The contrast with Shebna is implicit: Shebna's family is unspecified; Eliakim's father Hilkiah is named. The divine first-person commissioning (I will summon my servant Eliakim) makes this a divine appointment, not a political promotion. The word servant (avdi: my servant) is the title of honor given to Moses, David, and the Servant of the LORD in chs 40-55 -- Eliakim participates in this honorific vocabulary.
22.21: The investiture of Eliakim with Shebna's robe, sash, and authority is a formal succession ceremony. Eliakim becomes a father to the residents of Jerusalem and to the house of Judah -- the pastoral-paternal role of the chief steward. The MKT's hand your authority over to him is appropriately formal for an act of official investiture, and the paternal title underscores the protective, not merely administrative, dimension of the office.
22.22: I will lay on his shoulder the key of the house of David; what he opens no one will shut, and what he shuts no one will open. The key of the house of David is the chief steward's authority over royal access. Rev 3:7 quotes this verse directly for Christ: who holds the key of David, what he opens no one can shut, and what he shuts no one can open. The MKT's exact phrasing closely follows the Hebrew and is quotable for Rev 3:7 without adjustment.
22.23: I will drive him in like a peg into a solid place, and he will become a throne of glory to his ancestral household. Aman (disp 2) is the root of solid place -- the same root as emet (truth/faithfulness) and amen. The maqom ne'eman (solid/faithful/reliable place) secures the peg in something genuinely trustworthy. Kavod (glory, disp 2) -- Eliakim will become a throne of glory. The MKT's solid place captures aman's reliability sense without importing the belief sense.
22.24: Everything of the household will hang on Eliakim -- all the kavod (honor/glory, disp 2), from the largest vessels to the smallest bowls. The MKT's all the honour of his father's household captures the kavod that transfers to Eliakim's peg. The list of vessels hanging on the peg illustrates how much weight a single trustworthy appointment can bear -- and therefore how catastrophic its failure would be (v25).
22.25: In that day the peg that was driven into the solid place will fail. Aman (disp 2) appears again -- the solid/faithful place gives way and the peg is cut down and falls, and everything hanging on it falls with it. YHWH is flagged twice. Even a divinely appointed steward is temporary. Only the one who holds the key of David permanently (Rev 3:7 -- Christ) is the true and unbreakable peg -- the fulfillment that makes all human stewards types and shadows.

23.1: The oracle against Tyre -- the greatest commercial city of the ancient world. Massa again. The ships of Tarshish (the farthest western trading destination) are addressed: wail, for Tyre is destroyed. The news travels the trade routes faster than armies: ships at sea hear of Tyre's fall and lose their home port. The MKT's no house left standing, no harbor captures the total collapse of Tyre's infrastructure.
23.2: The coastal peoples and Sidon's merchants are addressed in shock. Sidon was Tyre's sister-city in Phoenicia; together they dominated Mediterranean trade. The Hebrew milhati-yam (traders of the sea) -- the MKT's sailed the seas is a dynamic rendering of what is literally crossers of the sea. The merchants who made the coastal peoples wealthy now have no livelihood.
23.3: The grain of Shihor -- the Nile delta harvest -- was the cargo that made Tyre the grain distribution center for the Mediterranean. The great waters of the sea are Tyre's commercial highway. The MKT's trade-flow rendering is accurate: Egyptian grain crossed the Mediterranean and became Tyre's income. Tyre was the world's trading floor, and nations were enriched by her commerce.
23.4: The sea itself -- personified as Sidon's fortress -- disowns Tyre and Sidon. The sea's declaration that it has not labored nor brought up sons and daughters creates a strange maternal metaphor: the sea had been the mother-womb of Tyre's civilization, and now it disavows the relationship. The MKT's the sea's own fortress declares preserves the personification -- the sea, created by YHWH, refuses to be the foundation of Tyre's pride.
23.5: The news spreads to Egypt -- Egypt writhes with anguish (chul: writhe, the labor-pain verb) at the news. Egypt's economic dependence on Tyre as its trading partner means Tyre's fall causes Egypt real economic pain. The sequence of grief (Sidon, vv2-4; Egypt, v5) traces the economic web along which Tyre's collapse ripples outward.
23.6: The command to sail to Tarshish -- flee to the far west, to the end of the known world. With no home market to return to, the displaced merchants must sail as far as possible. The repetition of Tarshish (vv1, 6, 10, 14) frames the oracle with the image of the farthest trading post now receiving all the displaced commerce of a collapsed Tyre.
23.7: The rhetorical question: Is this the city that you once celebrated? The contrast between Tyre's ancient glory and its present ruin is the oracle's central irony. Ancient in its origins -- Tyre claimed great antiquity. A city whose people sailed out to settle distant shores -- Tyre's colonial expansion into Carthage, Gades, and the western Mediterranean was a source of pride. All of it is now past.
23.8: Who planned this against Tyre? The rhetorical question sets up v9. The crown-bestowing city, whose traders were princes, whose merchants were the honored of the earth -- the superlatives of Tyrian commercial glory pile up before the answer falls. Tyre issued trade charters and commercial rights as a monarch issues crowns: hence crown-bestowing city.
23.9: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD of hosts planned it. The answer to v8's question: the LORD of hosts is the planner of Tyre's fall. The purpose: to strip the pride from all magnificence and to humiliate all the famous of the earth. The divine motive is not commercial jealousy but the systematic humiliation of human pride -- the same theme as 2:12-17. The universality (all the famous of the earth) makes Tyre's judgment paradigmatic, not merely local.
23.10: The daughter of Tarshish is told to flow through the land like the Nile -- with Tyre gone, Tarshish can trade freely across the sea without the Tyrian commercial monopoly that previously restricted her. The liberation of Tarshish through Tyre's fall: judgment against one creates freedom for others. The MKT's flow through your land like the Nile preserves the river-metaphor of commercial freedom.
23.11: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD stretched his hand out over the sea and shook the kingdoms. The divine hand over the sea evokes the Exodus (Exod 14:21): as the LORD stretched out his hand over the Red Sea to divide it, now he stretches it over the Mediterranean to shatter Tyre. The order against Canaan (Phoenicia) is pronounced through the same gesture that once opened the sea for Israel.
23.12: The crushed virgin daughter of Sidon -- the virgin-daughter personification for a city that had maintained her independence and was now violated by conquest. The command to cross to Cyprus (Kittim) offers no real refuge -- even there there will be no rest. The geographic expansion of the flight westward maps the exhaustion of escape options.
23.13: The Chaldeans were originally a people-of-nothing, raised up by Assyria, who then themselves conquered the empire that raised them -- a compressed historical sketch of how empires rise and fall. The MKT's people who were nothing until Assyria raised them up captures the irony: Assyria created the instrument of its own replacement. No empire is final; each is provisional.
23.14: The refrain of the ships of Tarshish (v1 repeated) closes the oracle's body: your fortress harbour is destroyed. The refrain-structure creates the sense of inevitable completeness -- what was announced at the beginning has been traced through its causes and is now confirmed in the repeated wail. The Hebrew inclusio (ships of Tarshish opening and closing) is a deliberate literary frame.
23.15: In that day Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years -- the span of one king's lifetime. Melek (king, disp 2) -- the king's lifetime (seventy years) gives the oracle a specific temporal horizon. After seventy years (the same period as the Babylonian exile, Jer 25:11-12; 29:10), Tyre will be remembered again. The MKT's span of one king's lifetime renders kime melek echad: like the days of one king.
23.16: The forgotten prostitute's song -- a haunting image: the city-as-prostitute must go through the streets playing her harp, making sweet music so people will remember her again. The city-as-harlot metaphor will be developed extensively in Ezekiel (16; 23) for Jerusalem and Samaria, and by Revelation (17-18) for Babylon. Tyre is the commercial precedent: a city that sold itself to every trading partner.
23.17: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD will attend to Tyre after seventy years. She will return to her trading -- playing the harlot with all the kingdoms of the world. The prostitute metaphor is sustained: Tyre's commercial promiscuity is her nature, and after the seventy-year interruption it resumes. The MKT preserves the harlot metaphor without euphemism, as the Hebrew requires for the theological statement to register.
23.18: YHWH is flagged twice; qodesh (holiness, disp 2) is flagged -- her merchandise and her earnings will be set apart as holy to the LORD. The eschatological reversal is extraordinary: Tyre the harlot-city's income, previously consecrated to herself and her commercial partners, will become qodesh YHWH -- holy to the LORD. The MKT's set apart as holy captures the consecration language. The profits will supply those who live before the LORD -- a transformation of commercial wealth into covenant-community support that anticipates the eschatological gathering of all nations' wealth into Zion (60:5-7; Rev 21:24-26).

24.1: bōqēq (H1238) — to empty, drain, lay waste; from a root meaning to pour out completely. The verb conveys total depletion rather than mere destruction. bôlēq (H1110) — to lay waste, make desolate; the near-synonym in the same verse intensifies the completeness. YHWH empties the earth of its human order and then distorts its surface — the cosmic judgment is both demographic and physical.
24.2: The social leveling formula: priest/people, master/servant, mistress/maid, buyer/seller, lender/borrower, creditor/debtor — six pairs spanning the entire social hierarchy. The reversal is grammatical equality: kāʿam kakkōhēn ("as with the people, so with the priest"). Social structures that organize human community are dissolved by the coming judgment — no status exempts.
24.3: The repetition of the root bwq twice in one verse — hēbôq tibboq — is an infinitive absolute construction that intensifies: "will be completely/utterly emptied." Similarly hbz tibbaz — "plundered, it will be plundered." The double infinitive absolute construction is Isaiah's way of expressing absolute certainty with rhetorical force. YHWH has spoken this word (peh Yhwh dibber).
24.4: ʾābelâ nābĕlâ hāʾāreṣ — "the earth mourns/dries up, the world withers"; ʾābal (H56) can mean both to mourn (as people do) and to dry up (as plants do) — the deliberate double meaning personifies the earth as a mourner while describing its literal ecological collapse. mārôm — the height/heavens; the cosmic mourning reaches vertically through all creation.
24.5: ḥillĕpû tôrâ — "transgressed the laws/instruction" (tôrâ without the article, suggesting law in general, not specifically Moses); ḥālĕfû ḥōq — "violated the statute/decree"; hēpĕrû bĕrît ʿôlām — "broken the everlasting covenant." The bĕrît ʿôlām most likely refers to the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:16), which is with "all flesh" and "the earth" — explaining why creation itself is cursed. Violation of the universal covenant produces universal ecological judgment.
24.6: ʾālâ (H423) — oath/curse; the covenant curse (qĕlālâ) has consumed (ʾāklâ) the earth. Those who live on it are pronounced guilty (nĕʾāšĕmû, held to account). The diminishing of the inhabitants — wayyiśrāpû yōšĕbê-ʾāreṣ ("the inhabitants of the earth are scorched/burned") — may be the result of drought or plague, both covenant curse instruments (Deut 28:22-24).
24.7: ʾābel tîrôš — "the new wine mourns/withers" — the same ʾābal double meaning as v.4 (mourns/withers). The festive grape harvest becomes an occasion of grief. ʾumllâ gephen — "the vine languishes/is sick" (ʾāmal, H535 — to be faint, sick, languid). The joy of the wine culture is eliminated as a specific sign of covenant curse — wine gladdens the heart (Ps 104:15); its absence means cursed desolation.
24.8: šābat (H7673) — ceased/stopped; the root of Sabbath. The joyful tambourines (tōp), the noisy revelers (šaʾôn ʿallîzîm), and the joyful harp (kinnôr) all fall silent. In the ancient world, music, drinking, and celebration were inseparable from civic and cultic life — their cessation signals total social collapse.
24.9: Strong drink (šēkār) becomes bitter to those who drink it — the reversal of the expected pleasure. They no longer sing to wine (lōʾ yāšîrû layyāyin). The drinking-song culture of the ANE was part of how people celebrated life, harvest, and victory; its silencing is both literal (no harvest) and metaphorical (nothing to celebrate).
24.10: qiryat-tōhû — "city of chaos/formlessness" — the word tōhû (H8414) is the same root as in Gen 1:2 (the primeval chaos before creation: tōhû wābōhû). The city, which represents the height of human civilizational achievement, is reduced to primeval formlessness. šubar kol-bayit — "every house is broken/shut up" — the city is physically impassable.
24.11: The crying in the streets is for wine (ʿal-hayyāyin) — the same wine that mourned in v.7. The cry is specifically the absence of the expected substance of celebration. ʿārbâ kol-śimḥâ — "all joy has grown dark/has set" (ʿārab, to grow dark, as the sun at evening). gālâ mĕśôś hāʾāreṣ — "the joy of the earth has gone into exile."
24.12: šûʾâ (H7722) — desolation/ruin; the city is left as a wasteland (nišʾar bāʿîr šammâ). The city gates (šaʿar) are battered to ruins (yuškat šaʾar lĕtōhû). Gates in the ANE were the judicial and commercial center of city life — their destruction means the end of all public order and commerce.
24.13: The remnant imagery: kĕnōqep zayit — "like the gleaning of the olive" (nōqep, the beating/shaking of the olive tree to dislodge the last olives); kĕʿôlēlôt — "like gleanings/after-pickings" after the grape harvest. The survival of a remnant is compared to the few olives or grapes left after thorough harvesting — the remnant is intentionally left, not forgotten. Compare 17:6.
24.14: The remnant raises its voice — yiśśĕʾû qôlām — and sings for joy (yārōnnû). The geographical direction is significant: they sing "from the west" (mimmāʿărāḇ). The cosmic scope of judgment (vv.1-13) produces a cosmic scope of praise — the remnant in the distant west responds to YHWH's majesty (gĕʾôn Yhwh). This is not a nationalistic but a universal gathering.
24.15: bāʾûrîm — "in the east/in the fires" — highly debated. LXX reads "in the light" (ʾôr); MT likely means "in the regions of light" (east). bĕʾiyyê hayyām — "in the coastlands of the sea." The universal praise comes from east and west simultaneously — the people of YHWH are scattered and gathered from every direction.
24.16: mikkĕnap hāʾāreṣ — "from the wing/edge of the earth" — the extremities of the inhabited world. The songs of praise are for the ṣaddîq ("Righteous One" — may be a title for YHWH or the messianic agent). The prophet's response: rāzî lî — "my secret/my leanness to me!" — a cry of personal grief or exhaustion at what he has seen. bōgedîm bāgādû — "the treacherous have been treacherous" — the world's faithlessness continues even as judgment falls.
24.17: paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ — the three "p" words: terror, pit, and snare — a rhetorical triple alliteration in Hebrew that is untranslatable but memorable. Flight from one danger leads directly to another (the one who flees the terror falls into the pit; the one who escapes the pit is caught in the snare). This is the inescapability of the cosmic judgment.
24.18: ʾarûbbôt mimmārôm niptāḥû — "the windows of heaven are opened" — the language of the Flood (Gen 7:11). The cosmic catastrophe reactivates the Flood imagery, confirming that the bĕrît ʿôlām of v.5 is indeed the Noahic covenant. The foundations of the earth (môsĕdê ʾāreṣ) shake (yirʿāšû) simultaneously — judgment attacks both from above and below.
24.19: The threefold verb assault: rōʿâ rōʿāʿâ hāʾāreṣ ("the earth is utterly broken") — rāʿaʿ (H7489) means to be shattered/shaken violently; pôr hitpōrĕrâ ("completely dissolved/split apart"); môṭ hitmoṭĕṭâ ("moved violently/shaken to the core"). Three different verbs of earth-shaking each intensified by their own infinitive absolute — the rhetorical force mimics the seismic event.
24.20: nûaʿ tānûaʿ hāʾāreṣ kaššikkôr — "the earth staggers like a drunkard" — the same verb nûaʿ (H5128) used for swaying/staggering in intoxication. The earth is compared to a drunk man (šikkôr) and to a hammock (mĕlûnâ, temporary shelter) — both images convey instability. Its transgression (pišʿāh) weighs it down; it falls and will not rise again.
24.21: yipqōd Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "YHWH of hosts will visit in judgment" (pāqad, H6485 — to attend to, to visit for judgment or blessing depending on context). The object is twofold: the "host of the exalted ones on high" (ṣĕbāʾ hammārôm bammārôm — probably celestial/spiritual powers) and the kings of the earth (malkê hāʾădāmâ). The cosmic dimension of the judgment includes both supernatural and human authority structures.
24.22: yē ʾaspû ʾăsēpâ — "they will be gathered, gathered together" (double infinitive absolute for certainty) — like prisoners into a pit (bôr) and shut up (sāgar) in a prison (kelēʾ). After many days they will be visited (yuppāqēdû) — the same pāqad as in v.21, now for release or further judgment. The delay suggests a temporary holding before final judgment.
24.23: bôš halbānâ ûpĕrâ hahammâ — "the moon (lĕbānâ, the white one) will be ashamed and the sun (ḥammâ, the hot one) will be abashed" — when YHWH reigns on Mount Zion (mālak Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt bĕhar ṣiyyôn) and in Jerusalem before his elders (ziqnāyw). The cosmic lights are eclipsed by the glory of YHWH's royal presence — the image of divine enthronement that renders all other light secondary.

25.1: ʾělōhay ʾattâ — "you are my God" — the intimate possessive framing the entire psalm. ʾēśāʾ ʾĕrômmĕmkā — "I will exalt you." The basis: YHWH did wondrous things (pālaʾ ʿēṣôt mērāḥôq) — "wonderful counsels from afar," i.e., plans laid long ago now fulfilled. ʿēṣôt (plans/counsels) is the same word as 14:26-27 — YHWH's sovereign governance of history proved by events. ʾĕmûnâ ʾōmen — "faithfulness/firmness of old/truth."
25.2: śamtā mēʿîr lĕgal — "you have made a city into a heap of rubble" (gal, a heap of stones); mibṣār lĕmappēlâ — "a fortified city into a ruin." The unidentified city (probably representative of all hostile powers rather than a specific city) is contrasted with YHWH's eternal city Zion. The city of foreigners (qiryat zārîm) will never be rebuilt (lōʾ tîbbāneh lĕʿôlām).
25.3: ʿam ʿāz — "a strong/fierce people" will honor YHWH; qiryat gôyîm ʿārîṣîm — "city of ruthless/terrible nations" will fear him. The reversal: the nations that trusted in military strength now honor YHWH. This is the eschatological conversion of the gentile nations — not their destruction but their submission and worship, which is the hope of vv.6-8.
25.4: kî-hāyîtā māʿôz laddāl māʿôz lāʾebyôn — "you have been a stronghold to the poor/needy" (dāl + ʾebyôn, the two key Hebrew terms for the economically marginal). maḥăseh mizzārem — "refuge from the storm." The image of YHWH as shelter specifically for the vulnerable is one of Isaiah's persistent themes (cf. 14:30; 32:2) and a defining characteristic of his eschatological king.
25.5: kĕḥōreb bĕṣāyôn — "like heat in a dry place" — the powerful nations are reduced like excessive heat moderated by a cloud's shadow (ṣēl ʿāb). zammîr ʿārîṣîm yaʿăneh — "the song of the terrible ones is silenced/humbled" — the victory songs of the conquering nations go quiet before YHWH's intervention. The powerful are not merely defeated but shamed into silence.
25.6: mišteh šĕmānîm — "feast of fat things/rich food"; mišteh šĕmārîm — "feast of well-aged wine" (šĕmārîm, from šāmar, to keep/preserve — aged wine kept on its dregs). The eschatological banquet on Mount Zion is for kol-hāʿammîm — "all peoples" — not Israel alone. The double qualification šĕmānîm mĕmullāḥîm ("marrow-rich"), šĕmārîm mĕzuqqāqîm ("well-refined") emphasizes the superlative quality of what YHWH provides.
25.7: hallôṭ hallûṭ — "the covering that is spread/cast" (lûṭ, H3875, wrapped/veiled); hamsĕkâ hamsûkâ — "the veil that is woven over" (māsak, to weave/cover). The double image of a shroud/veil covering all peoples is the metaphor for death and mourning that YHWH removes. The removal is not merely comforting but cosmic — death as a regime is dismantled.
25.8: billaʿ hammāwet lāneṣaḥ — "he will swallow up death forever" (bālaʿ, H1104, to swallow, consume). Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 15:54 at the climax of his resurrection argument: "Death is swallowed up in victory." The word lāneṣaḥ (forever/in victory — the same word the NT reads as "victory") makes the swallowing definitive and permanent. māḥâ ʾădōnāy Yhwh dimʿâ mēʿal kol-pānîm — "Lord YHWH will wipe away tears from all faces" — quoted directly in Rev 21:4.
25.9: The response of the saved remnant: hinnēh ʾĕlōhênû zeh — "behold, this is our God" — the climactic identification of the God who acts is made in the moment of salvation. qiwwînû lô — "we waited for him/hoped in him"; yôšîʿēnû — "he will save us." The waiting (qāwâ, H6960) is the posture that characterizes faith in Isaiah's theology (cf. 8:17; 30:18; 40:31).
25.10: kî tānûaḥ yad Yhwh bāhar hazzeh — "for the hand of YHWH will rest on this mountain" — Mount Zion as the place of YHWH's settled presence. The contrast: Moab is trampled (yûddaš) like straw in a dunghill (mô madmēnâ) — the trampling imagery for judgment. pāraś yādāyw bĕqirbô — "he spreads his hands in the midst of it" like a swimmer spreading his arms to swim.
25.11: The swimmer comparison: one who swims extends his arms (yiśḥeh lišĕḥôt) — so YHWH humbles (šippal) Moab's pride (gāʾôn) and the skill/craft of his hands (ʿim miśgab yādāyw). The precise mechanism of humiliation — the very extension of hands that is skillful swimming becomes the gesture of defeat — turns Moab's effort into the means of its own downfall.
25.12: ûmiḇṣar māʿûzzê ḥômōtêkā — "the fortress and the high walls of your fortifications" — Moab's military security. higgîaʿ hišpîl hĭggiyaʿ lāʾāreṣ — "he brings them low, he brings them down to the ground" — the threefold verb descent mirrors the cosmic descent imagery of 14:11-15. Even the most fortified human security has no defense against YHWH's cosmic judgment.

26.1: bayyôm hahûʾ — "in that day" — the eschatological day of YHWH. ʿîr ʿāz lānû — "we have a strong city" — in contrast to 24:10's qiryat-tōhû (city of chaos). yĕšûʿâ yāšît ḥômôt wāḥēl — "he sets salvation as walls and ramparts" — the city's security is not military but theological; yĕšûʿâ (salvation) is the structural material of the city's defense.
26.2: pitḥû šĕʿārîm — "Open the gates!" — the imperative of welcome. gôy ṣaddîq šōmēr ʾĕmûnîm — "a righteous nation that keeps faith/faithfulness" (ṣaddîq, H6662; ʾĕmûnîm, plural of faithfulness). The city is open to those who are righteous and faithful — the entry requirement is character shaped by YHWH's covenant, not ethnic identity.
26.3: yēṣer sāmûk tiṣṣōr šālôm šālôm — "the steadfast mind you keep in perfect peace" (yēṣer, H3336, formed/framed inclination or formed mind; sāmûk, H5564, upheld/leaned upon). The doubled šālôm šālôm (peace, peace) is an intensified form — "complete peace" or "deep peace." The basis is trust (bāṭûaḥ bĕkā, trusting in you) — the orientation of the mind determines the quality of peace received.
26.4: biṭḥû ḇYhwh ʿăḏê-ʿaḏ — "trust in YHWH forever and ever" — ʿăḏê-ʿaḏ is the strongest Hebrew expression of perpetual duration. The reason: kî bĕYāh Yhwh ṣûr ʿôlāmîm — "for in Yah YHWH is the Rock of Ages/everlasting Rock." The title ṣûr ʿôlāmîm combines the rock-refuge imagery (Ps 18:2; 31:3) with the eternal duration of divine character — the reliability of YHWH is permanent by definition.
26.5: kî hišpîl yōšĕbê mārom — "for he brings low those who dwell on high" — the divine reversal pattern. The lofty city (qiryâ niśśāʾâ, the exalted city) is brought down; it is laid low to the ground (lāʾāreṣ yiggāʿennāh), brought even to the dust (ʿad-ʿāpār). The vocabulary of v.5 reverses the vocabulary of self-exaltation from 14:13-14 — pride goes to dust.
26.6: The foot of those who were afflicted (regelyānî ûpăʿămê ḏallîm) treads on what was the proud city. The inversion: those at the bottom of the social order now walk on the ruins of what oppressed them. This is not mere social revolution but the specific reversal that characterizes YHWH's governance — the poor (ḏal) and the needy are elevated precisely as the powerful are cast down.
26.7: ʾōraḥ laṣṣaddîq mĕšārîm — "the path of the righteous is level/smooth" (mĕšārîm, H4339, from yāšar, straight/even). mêšārîm tipal-maʿgal ṣaddîq — "you make smooth/level the track of the righteous." YHWH is the one who clears the path — the righteous do not walk on their own strength but on a way prepared for them.
