26.8: ʾap bĕderek mišpāṭêkā Yhwh qiwwînûkā — "in the path of your judgments/justice, YHWH, we wait for you" — the waiting (qāwâ, the same root as 25:9) is done on the path of divine justice, not in withdrawal from the world. lĕšimkā ûlĕzikrekā taʾăwat nāpeš — "for your name and your memory/remembrance is the desire of the soul" — the ultimate object of longing is the name and presence of YHWH.
26.9: napšî ʾiwwitîkā ballaylâ — "with my soul I have desired you in the night" — the night as the time of intense spiritual seeking (cf. Ps 63:6). ʾap rûḥî bĕqirbî ʾăšaḥărêkā — "even with my spirit within me I seek you earnestly" (the šaḥar root means to seek at dawn). kî ʾăšer mišpāṭêkā lāʾāreṣ ṣedeq lāmĕdû yōšĕbê ṯēḇēl — "for when your judgments fall on the earth, the inhabitants of the world learn righteousness" — divine judgment has a pedagogical purpose.
26.10: yûḥan rāšāʿ bal-lāmad ṣedeq — "when grace is shown to the wicked, he does not learn righteousness" — the inverse pedagogical principle: unmerited favor does not reform character the way discipline does. The wicked continue to deal wrongly even in an upright land (bĕʾereṣ nĕkōḥôt yĕʿawwēl) and do not see YHWH's majesty (gĕʾût Yhwh bal-yirʾeh).
26.11: Yhwh rāmâ yādĕkā — "YHWH, your hand is lifted high" — the extended hand as the instrument of judgment (cf. 5:25; 9:12, 17, 21; 10:4). The adversaries do not see it (bal-yĕḥĕzāyûn) but will be ashamed. ʾēš ṣārêkā tōʾkĕlēm — "fire shall consume your adversaries" — the fire of divine jealousy/zeal (qinʾat ʿamkā) is what destroys the enemies of YHWH's people.
26.12: Yhwh tišpāt šālôm lānû — "YHWH, you will ordain/appoint peace for us" — the passive reception of peace as YHWH's gift. kî gam kol-maʿăśênû pāʿaltā llānû — "for indeed all our works you have accomplished for us" — the acknowledgment that even the good works of the community are ultimately YHWH's doing; the monergistic theology of grace here anticipates Paul's formulation in Phil 2:13.
26.13: ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû bĕʿālûnû ʾădōnîm zûlatekā — "O YHWH our God, lords other than you have ruled over us" — the historical confession of unfaithfulness and subjugation to foreign powers. raq-bĕkā nazkîr šĕmekā — "but through you alone do we acclaim your name" — the exclusive focus on YHWH as the only name worth calling upon emerges from the history of failed alternatives.
26.14: mētîm bal-yiḥyû rĕpāʾîm bal-yāqûmû — "the dead will not live; the shades/departed spirits will not rise" — a specific group (the oppressive lords of v.13) will not experience resurrection; their memory is destroyed (lakēn pāqadtā wattašmîdēm). This is in deliberate contrast with v.19 where YHWH's dead will rise — the distinction between those under divine judgment (no resurrection) and those under divine covenant (resurrection).
26.15: yāsaptā laggôy Yhwh yāsaptā laggôy — "you have added to the nation, O YHWH, you have added to the nation" — the repeated verb emphasizes the extension of Israel through the ingathering of exiles and gentile converts. YHWH is glorified (nikbadtā) and has extended all the borders of the land. The double accent on increase/addition is the opposite of the diminishment of the corrupt city (vv.5-6).
26.16: Yhwh bĕṣar pĕqādûkā — "YHWH, in distress they sought you" (pāqad, to seek out, attend to — here the people attend to God precisely when in trouble). ṣāqûn laḥaš mûsar — "they poured out a whispered/quiet prayer for discipline" — the word laḥaš (H3908) connotes whispering, quiet prayer, perhaps muttered under duress. Distress drives the community to YHWH when prosperity does not.
26.17: kĕmô hārâ taqrîḇ lālēdet tāḥîl tizʿaq bĕḥeblêhā — "like a pregnant woman approaching delivery who writhes and cries out in her labor pains" — the simile for the community's condition before YHWH. The birth metaphor is carefully developed: the pain is real, the outcome is expected, but the next verse reveals the tragic surprise.
26.18: The tragedy: hārînû ḥalnû kĕmô yālādnû rûaḥ — "we were with child, we writhed in pain, but we gave birth to wind/nothing" (rûaḥ here means wind, emptiness). No salvation has been accomplished on earth (lōʾ naʿăśeh ṣĕdāqôt bāʾāreṣ); the inhabitants of the world have not fallen (wĕbal-yippĕlû yōšĕbê ṯēḇēl). Human striving and even prophetic promise has produced nothing — only YHWH's intervention (v.19) can bring the dead to life.
26.19: yiḥyû mētêkā nĕbēlātî yāqûmûn — "your dead will live; my corpses/body (nĕbēlāh) will arise" — an explicit resurrection declaration, directly countering vv.14,18. hāqîṣû wĕrannĕnû šōkĕnê ʿāpār — "awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust" — the reversal of death-sleep into singing. ṭal ʾôrōt ṭallekā — "for your dew is a dew of light" — resurrection is connected to the dew that preserves life in arid seasons; ṭal ʾôrōt (dew of lights) may image the resurrection glory.
26.20: lĕkā ʿammî bōʾ bĕḥăḏāreykā ûsĕgōr dĕlatōtêkā baʿăḏekā — "come, my people, enter your rooms/chambers, and shut your doors behind you" — the hiding of God's people during the moment of judgment echoes the Passover (Exod 12:22-23: stay inside, blood on the doorposts) and the Flood (Noah shut in the ark). ḥăbî kimaṭ-rageʿ ʿad-yaʿăbōr-zāʿam — "hide for a little while until the wrath has passed."
26.21: kî-hinnēh Yhwh yōṣēʾ mimĕqōmô — "for behold, YHWH is coming out of his place" — the decisive divine self-disclosure in judgment. lipqōd ʿăwōn yōšēb hāʾāreṣ ʿālāyw — "to punish the iniquity of the earth's inhabitants against him." The earth will disclose (tigalleh) its blood — the unjust blood shed throughout history will be exposed and the earth will no longer cover (wĕlōʾ tĕkasseh ʿôd) it. Justice will be complete.

27.1: liwyātān nāḥāš bārîaḥ — "Leviathan, the fleeing serpent"; liwyātān nāḥāš ʿăqallātôn — "Leviathan, the twisting/coiling serpent"; hattannîn ʾăšer bayyām — "the dragon that is in the sea." These are the three names of the cosmic chaos-monster from Canaanite mythology (attested in Ugaritic texts as Lotan). YHWH's ḥereb qāšâ ûgĕdôlâ ûḥăzāqâ (hard, great, and strong sword) will slay it. The Day of the LORD inaugurates the defeat of cosmic chaos.
27.2: kerem ḥemed — "vineyard of delight/desire" (ḥemed, H2532, desirable, lovely). This vineyard is presented positively — in contrast to 5:1-7's vineyard that produced wild grapes and was destroyed. The shift is significant: the same vineyard imagery now pictures YHWH's relationship to his people as one of delight rather than disappointment. ʿannû lāh — "answer/sing about it" — the song is one of celebration.
27.3: ʾănî Yhwh nōṣĕrāh — "I, YHWH, am its keeper/guardian" (nāṣar, H5341, to guard, watch, preserve). The comparison with the withered vineyard of ch. 5 is deliberate: there YHWH removed his protection; here he personally provides continuous irrigation (lirgāʿîm ʾašqennāh, "moment by moment I water it") and guards it day and night (pen yipqōd ʿāleyhā).
27.4: ḥēmâ ʾên lî — "I have no wrath" — YHWH's posture toward his now-restored vineyard is one from which anger has departed. The only thing that might provoke his engagement is šāmîr wāšayit (briers and thorns, which represent external enemies or internal sin) — but even then he would march (ʾemdēhā) through them and burn them together (yāḥad ʾaṣṣîtennāh).
27.5: ô yĕḥāzeq bĕmaʿûzzî — "or let him take hold of my fortress/protection" — an alternative to being burned: seeking YHWH's protection by making peace (šālôm yaʿăśeh lî). The offer is open: yaʿăśeh šālôm lî — "let him make peace with me." This unexpected clemency in the middle of an oracle against the enemies of the vineyard models YHWH's willingness to receive any who turn to him.
27.6: hayyābāʾîm — "in coming days" (the plural of bāʾ, the ones coming/what is to come — a temporal expression for the eschatological future). Israel will blossom (yiprāḥ), sprout (ûparāḥ), and fill the face of the world (ûmillĕʾû pĕnê-ṯēḇēl tĕnûʾâ) with fruit. The agricultural imagery carries the eschatological restoration — the people of YHWH become fruitful in a way that blesses the entire inhabited world.
27.7: The rhetorical question: hakĕmakkat makkēhû hikkāhû — "has he (YHWH) struck him (Israel) as he struck those who struck him?" The answer is implied: no — YHWH's discipline of Israel, though real, is not the same as his judgment on Israel's enemies. ʾim-kĕhereḡ hărûgāyw hōrāg — "has he killed as those who killed him were killed?" The graduated distinction in severity of punishment is a statement of divine mercy within judgment.
27.8: bĕsaʾssēʾâ bĕšallĕḥāh tĕrîḇennāh — "by driving her away, by sending her away, you contended with her" (rîḇ, to contend at law, to dispute). The exile is framed as a legal dispute in which YHWH argues his case against his people. hāgâ bĕrûḥô haqqāšâ bĕyôm qādîm — "he drove her away with his harsh/east wind on a day of the east wind" — the east wind (qādîm) is the hot, destructive desert wind from the east, used as a metaphor for divine judgment (cf. Jer 18:17; Hos 13:15).
27.9: lākēn bĕzōʾt yĕkuppār ʿăwōn-yaʿăqōb — "therefore by this will the iniquity of Jacob be covered/atoned for" (kāpar, H3722, to cover, to make atonement). The instrument of atonement is the removal of sin — specifically the destruction of the altars and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) and incense altars (ḥammānîm) that represented the sin. The atonement is enacted through the exile's purification of idolatry from the people.
27.10: The fortified city (ʿîr bĕṣûrâ) is desolate (bāḏāḏ) — an image of Samaria or Jerusalem after conquest. nĕwēh mĕšullāḥ — "an abandoned/deserted habitation" — the city that expelled its inhabitants is now itself deserted, its land serving only as pasture (wirʿâ šām ʿēgel). The calf grazing on the ruins of a great city is the ultimate image of civilization reduced to wilderness.
27.11: biyḇôš qĕṣîrāh tišbarnâ — "when its boughs are dry, they are broken off" (yāḇēš, dry/withered; šāḇar, break). The women come and kindle fire with the branches — the ruins become fuel. ʿam lōʾ-bînôt hûʾ — "it is a people without understanding/discernment" — the theological diagnosis that makes judgment inevitable. Without understanding of YHWH's ways, there is no path to blessing.
27.12: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ yāḥbōṭ Yhwh — "on that day YHWH will beat out/thresh" (ḥābaṭ, H2251, to beat grain from stalks) — the gathering image. From the Euphrates River (nahar) to the Brook of Egypt (naḥal miṣrayim), YHWH will glean the scattered Israelites one by one (ʾeḥāḏ ʾeḥāḏ). The careful individual gathering is the opposite of mass deportation — YHWH brings his people home one at a time.
27.13: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ yiqqāʿēʾ bĕšôpār gāḏôl — "on that day a great trumpet will be blown" (šôpār gāḏôl, the great ram's horn). The lost ones (hāʾōbĕḏîm) in Assyria and the scattered ones (hanniddāḥîm) in Egypt will come and worship (hištaḥăwû lYhwh) on the holy mountain in Jerusalem. The great trumpet of eschatological ingathering is developed in Matt 24:31 (the Son of Man's angels with a great trumpet) and 1 Cor 15:52 (the last trumpet at resurrection).

28.1: hôy ʿăṭeret gēʾût šikkōrê ʾeprayim — "Woe to the proud crown of the drunkards of Ephraim!" The northern capital Samaria is described as a crown (ʿăṭeret) perched on the head of a fertile valley (gēʾ šĕmānîm). The crown is simultaneously their pride and their symbol of power. ṣîṣ nōḇēl ṣĕḇî tipʾartô — "a fading flower of his glorious beauty" — the ephemeral crown will wither like a flower. Historically: Samaria fell to Assyria in 722 BCE.
28.2: hinnēh ḥāzāq wĕʾammîṣ lāʾāḏōnāy — "the LORD has one who is mighty and strong" — Assyria is YHWH's instrument. The imagery: a driving storm of hail (zerem bārāḏ saʿar qāṭeb) and flooding waters (zerem mayim kabbîrîm šōṭĕpîm). The natural forces are weaponized as divine judgment. The proud crown of Ephraim (Samaria) will be overwhelmed by the very forces that seemed to surround it safely.
28.3: bĕreglāyim tĕrûmmanâ ʿăṭeret gēʾût šikkōrê ʾeprayim — "the proud crown of the drunkards of Ephraim will be trampled underfoot" — the literal trampling fulfills the metaphor of vv.1-2. The crown of pride (ʿăṭeret gēʾût) becomes refuse underfoot — the reversal from head-covering to foot-contact is total humiliation.
28.4: wĕhāyĕtâ ṣîṣat nōḇēl ṣĕḇî tipʾartô — "the fading flower of his glorious beauty" repeats v.1's image but adds the resolution: it will be like a first-ripe fig (bikkûrâ) before the summer, which the one who sees it immediately swallows (biltĕʿennāh). The first fig of the season is devoured on sight — the eagerness of the destroyer matches the sudden vulnerability of the city.
28.5: bayyôm hahûʾ yihyeh Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt lĕʿăṭeret ṣĕbî — "on that day YHWH of hosts will be a crown of beauty" (ʿăṭeret ṣĕbî) — the divine substitute for the human proud crown of Ephraim. YHWH himself becomes the crown, the glory, and the diadem (ṣĕpîrat tipʾārâ) for the remnant of his people (lišĕʾār ʿammô). The same imagery used to describe Samaria's pride is redirected to describe YHWH's presence with the faithful remnant.
28.6: rûaḥ mišpāṭ lĕyôšēḇ hammišpāṭ — "a spirit of justice to the one who sits in judgment" — the judicial function rightly discharged when YHWH is the crown; ûgĕḇûrâ lĕmĕšîḇê milḥāmâ šāʿrâ — "strength to those who turn back the battle at the gate." The gate was both the court and the last line of defense — YHWH's presence enables both true justice and effective defense.
28.7: Now Isaiah turns to Judah and Jerusalem: wĕgam-ʾēlleh bayyāyin šāgû — "but even these stagger with wine" (šāgâ, H7686, to go astray, err, stagger). The same drunkard imagery applied to Ephraim now indicts Jerusalem. The priest (kōhēn) and the prophet (nāḇîʾ) specifically are drunk — those responsible for justice and revelation have compromised their office through intoxication and thus error in vision (bāreʾâ pāqû) and judgment (kāšĕlû bammišpāṭ).
28.8: kî kol-šulḥānôt māleʾû qîʾ ṣōʾâ bĕlî māqôm — "for all tables are full of vomit and filth" — the graphic image of a banquet hall defiled by the drunkenness of those who ate there. The prophetic indictment uses the physical reality of the consequence of drunkenness to symbolize the moral-spiritual state of the leadership: what should be the table of holy counsel and sacred meal has become a site of degradation.
28.9: The mocking response of the drunken leaders to Isaiah: ʾet-mî yôreh ḏēʿâ — "whom will he teach knowledge?" and ʾet-mî yāḇîn šĕmûʿâ — "to whom will he explain the message?" — the sarcastic questions imply: does he think we are children, just weaned from milk (gĕmûlê mēḥālāḇ ʿattîqê miššādāyim)? The leaders reject the simplicity of YHWH's instruction as beneath their sophistication.
28.10: kî ṣaw lāṣaw ṣaw lāṣaw qaw lāqaw qaw lāqaw — the debated phrase: either (a) the leadership mocks Isaiah's teaching as baby-talk (ṣaw and qaw as meaningless syllables), or (b) Isaiah describes YHWH's patient repetitive instruction (line upon line, precept upon precept — zĕʿēr šām zĕʿēr šām). The ambiguity may be intentional: what the leaders mock as childish repetition is actually YHWH's gracious teaching method.
28.11: kî bĕlaʿăgê śāpâ ûblĕšôn ʾaḥeret yĕdabbēr ʾel hāʿam hazzeh — "for by people of strange lips and a foreign tongue he will speak to this people" — YHWH will use the incomprehensible language of the Assyrian army as his word to the people who would not hear clear Hebrew instruction. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 14:21 as a text about tongues (glossolalia) — the sign of tongues addressed to those who do not believe.
28.12: ʾăšer ʾāmar ʾălêhem zōʾt hammĕnûḥâ hānîḥû lĕʿāyēp — "who said to them, 'This is the resting place; let the weary rest'" — YHWH offered rest (mĕnûḥâ) through his instruction, but they would not hear. wĕlōʾ ʾāḇû šĕmôaʿ — "and they were not willing to listen." The refusal of offered rest is a persistent Isaianic motif (cf. 30:15: "in returning and rest you shall be saved") that Hebrews 3-4 develops as the warning against refusing the Sabbath-rest in Christ.
28.13: The consequence: the word of YHWH (dĕbar Yhwh) that they refused now comes to them as ṣaw lāṣaw ṣaw lāṣaw — the very thing they mocked becomes the instrument of their stumbling (kāšĕlû wĕnāplû), being broken (wĕnišbārû), trapped (wĕnôqĕšû), and taken (wĕnillākĕdû). The four verbs of destruction progressively increase in finality.
28.14: lākēn šimʿû dĕbar Yhwh ʾanšê lāṣôn — "therefore hear the word of YHWH, you scoffers (lāṣôn, H3944, those who mock/deride)" — the address to the leaders of Jerusalem who have struck a deal with Egypt against Assyria. The term ʾanšê lāṣôn (men of scoffing/mockery) identifies the ruling class's defining posture toward prophetic warning.
28.15: kāratnû bĕrît ʾet-māwet wĕʿim-šĕʾôl ʿāśînû ḥōzeh — "we have made a covenant with death, and with Sheol we have an agreement (ḥōzeh)." The Jerusalem leaders' claim — that they have a deal that will protect them from the coming "overflowing scourge" (šôṭ šôṭēp) by hiding in lies (šeqer maḥsēnû) and falsehood (bāšāqer nistaranû). The political alliance with Egypt is metaphorized as a covenant with death itself.
28.16: hinnēh yissaḏ bĕṣiyyôn ʾāben ʾeben bōḥan pinnāt yiqrat mûssāḏ mûssāḏ — "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a tested/proven stone, a precious cornerstone as a sure foundation." The cornerstone that YHWH lays contrasts with the false foundation the leaders have built on lies. hammaʾămîn lōʾ yāḥîš — "the one who believes/trusts will not flee/be in haste." This verse is cited in Rom 9:33; 1 Pet 2:6-8; Matt 21:42 — the NT identifies Christ as this tested cornerstone.
28.17: wĕśamtî mišpāṭ lĕqāw ûṣĕdāqâ lĕmišqālet — "I will make justice the measuring line (qāw) and righteousness the plumb line (mišqālet)" — the precise measurement of mišpāṭ and ṣĕdāqâ will expose the false foundation. The same qāw word used mockingly in vv.10,13 now becomes the divine measuring instrument. yissaḥ bārāḏ māḥăseh kāzāb — "hail will sweep away the refuge of lies" — the covenant with death is undone.
28.18: wĕkuppar bĕrîtĕkem ʾet-māwet — "your covenant with death will be annulled/covered" (kāpar, the atonement/covering verb used here for cancellation). The overflowing scourge (šôṭ šôṭēp) will come and they will be trampled by it (wahyîtem lô lĕmirmās). The false security is stripped away: every time the scourge passes it will take them (yiqqaḥ ʾetkem).
28.19: miggap ʿôbĕrô yiqqaḥ ʾetkem — "each time it passes, it will take you" — the repeated assault. kî-baqqer baqqer yaʿăbōr bayyôm wāballaylâ — "morning by morning it will pass, by day and by night." The relentlessness of the judgment is its defining quality. raq šĕmûʿâ hăḇîn ʾet-haššĕmûʿâ — "it will be nothing but terror to understand the message" — the very understanding of what YHWH has said will itself be terrifying.
28.20: kî qāṣar hammittâ mihistārēaʿ — "for the bed is too short to stretch out on" — the proverbial saying (too short to lie in, too narrow to wrap in) describes the inadequacy of the false security. The Egyptian alliance is like a bed that is too small and a blanket that is too narrow — it cannot actually cover the danger or provide the promised rest.
28.21: kî kĕhar pĕrāṣîm yāqûm Yhwh — "for YHWH will rise up as at Mount Perazim" (2 Sam 5:20, where YHWH broke out against the Philistines for David); kĕʿēmeq bĕgiḇʿôn yirgeʿ — "as in the valley of Gibeon he will storm" (probably Jos 10:10-11). maʿăśēhû zār maʿăśēhû — "his work is strange/alien, his deed is foreign" — judgment against his own people Israel/Judah is unnatural to YHWH; mercy and restoration are his characteristic work. Discipline is the exception, not the norm.
28.22: wĕʿattâ ʾal-titṣaḥăqû — "therefore do not scoff" — the direct warning to the scoffers addressed in v.14. The restraint from scoffing is grounded in the fact that YHWH of hosts has told him (kī kālâ wĕneḥĕrāṣâ šāmaʿtî mēʾēt ʾăḏōnāy Yhwh ṣĕḇāʾôt ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ) "a decreed end/destruction upon the whole earth." The cosmic scope of what has been determined makes mockery both foolish and dangerous.
28.23: haʾăzînû ûšimʿû qôlî haqšîḇû ûšimʿû ʾimrātî — "give ear and hear my voice; be attentive and hear what I say" — the introduction to the agricultural parable (vv.24-29). The call to attentive listening (haʾăzîn, šāmaʿ, hiqšîḇ — three hearing verbs) signals that what follows requires careful thought, not surface reading.
28.24: The farmer does not plow all day to prepare the ground — he plows in order to plant (yiphattaḥ wîśadded ʾaḏmātô, he opens and breaks up his ground). hal kol-hayyôm yaḥărōš haḥōrēš — "does the plowman plow all day?" The rhetorical question expects "no" — preparation serves a purpose beyond itself. The parable will draw an analogy to divine action: YHWH's destructive work (plowing) serves a constructive purpose (harvest).
28.25: After preparing the ground, the farmer sows in order: qeṣaḥ (dill/black cumin) and kammōn (cumin) are scattered; ḥiṭṭâ (wheat) and śĕʿōrâ (barley) are planted in rows; kussemeth (spelt/emmer) at the border. Each seed has its own proper place (nismān śĕʿōrâ ûkussemeth gebûlātô) — the farmer arranges according to the nature of each crop. The specificity of agricultural knowledge here reflects real Palestinian farming practice.
28.26: wĕyissĕrô lammišpāṭ — "and he instructs him for the right order/justice" — YHWH is the source of the farmer's knowledge. ʾĕlōhāyw yôrennû — "his God teaches him." The agricultural wisdom of the skilled farmer is itself a form of divine instruction — wisdom about the natural world and wisdom about YHWH's governance share the same source.
28.27: Different crops require different threshing methods: qeṣaḥ is not threshed with a threshing sledge (bĕḥārûṣ) — it is beaten out with a stick (bĕmaṭṭeh); kammōn not with a wagon wheel (ʾôpan ʿăgālâ) — with a rod (bĕšāḇeṭ). The delicate seeds require delicate methods. The implication for divine action: YHWH applies graduated, appropriate judgment, not indiscriminate force.
28.28: Grain is crushed but not infinitely — the thresher does not thresh it forever (lōʾ lāneṣaḥ yādûš); when he drives the wagon wheel and horses over it, he does not grind it (lōʾ yĕšōḥeqennû). The grain is threshed to free the kernel, not to destroy it. galgal ʿeglātô ûpārāšāyw lōʾ yĕdaqqennû — "he does not powder it with his wagon wheel and horses." The restraint of the divine thresher mirrors the farmer's wisdom.
28.29: gam-zōʾt mēʾim Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt yāṣāʾâ — "this also comes from YHWH of hosts" — the agricultural wisdom of the parable is explicitly claimed as coming from YHWH. hipliʾ ʿēṣâ hiḡdîl tûšiyyâ — "he is wonderful in counsel (pālāʾ, the same root as in 9:6's pele-yôʿēṣ, Wonderful Counselor), and excellent in wisdom (hiḡdîl tûšiyyâ, made great the sound wisdom)." The agricultural parable thus closes with a title that ties Isaiah 28 to Isaiah 9 — YHWH's wisdom in governing history is as careful and purposeful as a skilled farmer's wisdom in managing his harvest.

33.1: The woe-oracle (hoy) targets the anonymous destroyer and betrayer -- likely Assyria, which has used other nations and will be discarded. The oracle operates on a law-of-retaliation principle: when you finish destroying, you will be destroyed; when you stop betraying, you will be betrayed. The MKT preserves the passive-future forms that make the reversal feel structural rather than merely retributive.
33.2: YHWH is flagged -- the direct address to the covenant God: LORD, be gracious to us; we wait for you. The arm of strength every morning is a daily-renewal image of divine help; not a crisis intervention but a covenantal sustenance renewed each day. The waiting (qivvinu) is active expectation, not passive resignation -- the same word for hope used in Isa 40:31 (those who wait on the LORD).
33.3: At the sound of the LORD's voice the peoples flee -- a theophanic motif where the divine voice itself causes panic and scattering. The distinction between the voice (which causes flight) and the rising (which causes scattering) is a two-stage theophany: first auditory, then visible. The nations' flight at divine speech anticipates the NT pattern of demons fleeing at Jesus' word.
33.4: The spoil is gathered like locusts stripping a field -- the simile runs both ways. The victor gathers the Assyrian's abandoned spoil as efficiently as a locust swarm strips crops: nothing is left. The locusts and grasshoppers evoke the plague imagery -- as Egypt was stripped by locusts (Exod 10:14-15), Assyria will be stripped by a similar comprehensive plundering.
33.5: YHWH is flagged; tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is flagged -- he has filled Zion with justice and righteousness. The MKT renders tsedaqah as righteousness alongside mishpat (justice). The filling of Zion with these qualities is an act of the exalted LORD dwelling on high -- divine attributes descend into the city from the transcendent LORD. The MKT's pairing preserves both the social-legal (mishpat) and the moral-relational (tsedaqah) dimensions.
33.6: YHWH is flagged -- the fear of the LORD is Zion's treasure. The stability of times is an unusual phrase -- emunat ittekha: the faithfulness/steadiness of your times. The MKT's stability of your times renders emunah as stability, capturing the firmness and reliability of the covenantal era. The list of treasures -- salvation, wisdom, knowledge, fear of the LORD -- is comprehensive; the fear of the LORD (the beginning of wisdom, Prov 9:10) is listed last as the crown of the sequence.
33.7: Shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- the envoys of peace are weeping bitterly. The MKT renders shalom as peace -- the covenant-wholeness word. The envoys of peace represent whatever diplomatic and negotiating process Judah attempted with Assyria; they failed. The brave warriors (erelam) crying out and the peace-envoys weeping mark the total failure of both military and diplomatic options, driving the people to the prayer of v2.
33.8: Berit (covenant, disp 3) is flagged -- he has broken the covenant. The covenant broken is likely the treaty between Hezekiah and Sennacherib (2 Kings 18:14-17). The MKT's covenant carries the full OT legal-relational weight: this is not merely a violated political agreement but a sworn commitment that creates legal and moral obligations. The cities despised and men regarded as nothing -- the comprehensive destruction of civil society by the Assyrian advance.
33.9: The earth mourning (avlah) is a double entendre: the land fades/mourns as if in grief. Lebanon, Sharon, Bashan, and Carmel -- the four most fertile and productive regions of the land -- are listed as the regions now shaking off and wilting. These are not arbitrary place-names; they are the places of greatest natural abundance, now described as dried up (shaken off as in harvest). The MKT's shakes off for Carmel captures the image of a vine stripped bare.
33.10: YHWH is flagged -- the divine self-declaration of rising (equm) and being exalted (eromam) and lifted up (etnasse): three synonymous verbs of divine elevation corresponding to the Assyrian's arrogance of v8. The Assyrian despised everyone; now the LORD declares his counter-elevation. The triple rise-declaration is the structural inversion of the triple woe (v1).
33.11: Ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- your own breath, like fire, will consume you. The Assyrian's breath/spirit that has powered his pride will become the fire that destroys him. The MKT's breath captures the Hebrew ruach in its wind/breath sense here -- not the Spirit of God but the Assyrian's own vital force turned against him. Chaff and stubble are the Assyrian's works; fire is the Assyrian's own spirit.
33.12: The peoples burned like limestone -- the image is of thorough calcination: limestone burned until it becomes quicklime, destroyed to its chemical structure. Thornbushes set ablaze -- the briefest and most complete combustion. The two images together (rock reduced to powder, thornbush gone in a flash) represent comprehensive judgment that leaves nothing behind, working on both the enduring and the ephemeral.
33.13: YHWH is flagged (yada, know, disp 2) -- acknowledge my power. The call goes to both the far and the near: those who are distant witnesses and those who have seen the events close up. The MKT's acknowledge translates yada in its acknowledgment sense: not merely recognizing facts but giving appropriate recognition to divine power. The covenantal knowing-acknowledgment is the expected response to YHWH's acts.
33.14: Olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged -- who among us can live with the devouring fire? Who among us can dwell with everlasting burnings? The sinners in Zion ask the question that v15-16 will answer. Olam is rendered everlasting -- absolute rather than age-long -- because the consuming fire of divine holiness is being described in its eternal, unchanging quality. The terror of Zion's sinners before the holy God in their midst is the starting point for the righteous-path description that follows.
33.15: Tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is flagged -- the one who walks righteously. The answer to v14's question: the person who walks tsedaqot (righteousness-paths) and speaks mesharim (uprightly). The MKT's righteously and honestly cover both the moral and the verbal dimensions. The list of negatives (refusing unjust gain, shaking hands free of bribes, stopping ears against murder-plots, closing eyes against evil) delineates righteousness by what it refuses.
33.16: Aman (believe/reliable, disp 2) is flagged -- rendered in the phrase his bread will be provided (nitan: given/provided) and his water will be reliable (ne'emanim: faithful/sure). The righteous person dwells on the heights in a fortress of rock -- both images of security beyond the reach of siege and famine. Ne'emanim is the adjective from the aman root: reliable, trustworthy water that does not dry up or fail. The MKT's reliable is a good rendering of this faithfulness-dimension.
33.17: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged -- your eyes will see the king in his beauty. The eyes that were closed against evil (v15) will now see the king. The king in his beauty is the eschatological king -- the righteous ruler of ch 9 and 11 revealed in his full glory. The MKT's in his beauty is literal: beyofi means in his loveliness/handsomeness. The land stretching far away extends the vision beyond the siege-compressed walls of Jerusalem to a spacious future.
33.18: The heart will meditate on the terror -- looking back from the eschatological peace to remember how threatening the Assyrian bureaucracy was. The questions -- where is the scribe, the tribute-tallier, the tower-counter? -- are the questions of relief: all those oppressive mechanisms of empire are gone. The MKT's tallied the tribute is a vivid rendering of the Assyrian fiscal administration that demanded payment from conquered peoples.
33.19: The fierce people with incomprehensible speech -- the Assyrian administrators whose language (Akkadian) was foreign and whose contemptuous tone was alien. The deep speech and strange tongue describe a language too guttural to understand, used by people who mocked and dominated (scorned) those they controlled. The MKT's too deep to make out is a good rendering of the unintelligibility -- not just different but deliberately opaque.
33.20: Zion as the city of appointed festivals (moed) -- the three annual pilgrimage feasts of Israel. The festival-city becomes the stable tent: not one of its pegs will be pulled out, not one of its ropes broken. The tent-metaphor is striking for a permanent city -- Jerusalem is imagined as the ideal dwelling, combining the stability of a city with the intimate presence of a tent. The MKT's dwelling at peace captures naveh shanan: a peaceful pasture-home.
33.21: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD in his glory will be for us a place of broad rivers and streams. The divine name himself is the river -- YHWH replacing the geographical feature. Instead of the Nile giving Egypt its fertility or the Euphrates giving Babylon its wealth, the LORD himself will be the river of life for Jerusalem. No galley or warship can sail these rivers -- they are beyond the reach of military power. The MKT's in his glory preserves the kavod dimension of the LORD's presence as Jerusalem's waterway.
33.22: YHWH is flagged three times; melek (king, disp 2) is flagged; shaphat (judge, disp 2) is flagged. The triple identification -- the LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king -- is the eschatological fulfillment of Israel's governance structure. All three offices (judicial, legislative, executive) are held by the single divine person, the LORD. The MKT preserves all three divine titles applied to the same subject. He will save us -- the savior-role implicit in all three offices is made explicit.
33.23: The rigging metaphor -- applied to the Assyrian war-machine described as a ship (or to Jerusalem's enemies) -- suggests a vessel unable to sail: slack ropes, unstable mast, no sail. In the context of v21 (no warship on the LORD's rivers), the slack-rigged enemy ship cannot even float on the divine river. Then the plunder will be so great that even the lame will share in it -- no one will be excluded from the eschatological bounty.
33.24: No inhabitant of Zion will say I am sick -- the physical healing of the city's people accompanies the spiritual healing: the iniquity of the people who dwell there will be forgiven (nissa avon: lifted/carried, using the Servant's bearing-language of 53:4,12). The pair of healing -- bodily sickness removed, guilt lifted -- anticipates Jesus' question in Mark 2:9 (which is easier, to forgive sins or to heal?) by showing that the eschatological state has both as simultaneous gifts.

34.1: The summons to the nations (goyim) and peoples (leumim) is a universal call to witness -- come near and hear. The scope expands: not just nations but the earth and everything in it, the world and all that comes from it. The four concentric expansions (nations, peoples, earth, world) make the oracle explicitly universal in its audience even before the specific target (Edom, v5) is named. This is divine judgment that the entire creation must witness.
34.2: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD is enraged against all the nations. The divine wrath is directed at all (kol) nations and all their armies -- the universality matched by the comprehensive judgment: he has devoted them to destruction (heherimam: placed them under the ban of holy war), given them over to slaughter. The cherem (devoted to destruction) is the most absolute form of divine judgment -- everything under cherem is given entirely to the LORD and cannot be retained.
34.3: The physical consequences of cherem: the slain cast out unburied, the stench of decomposing corpses rising, the mountains soaked with blood. The scale of judgment is matched by the scale of the physical aftermath. The mountains drinking blood is a hyperbole that captures the comprehensive character of the slaughter -- even the landscape is saturated with the results of divine judgment.
34.4: The cosmic-dissolution language: all the host of heaven dissolving, the heavens rolled up like a scroll, the stars falling like leaves or figs. Rev 6:13-14 echoes this exactly: stars falling, sky rolling back like a scroll. The MKT's rolled up like a scroll preserves the exact image that Revelation cites. The creaturely heavens (not the divine throne) are described as temporary -- the cosmos can be un-created as decisively as it was created.
34.5: YHWH's sword bathed in heaven -- the divine sword is described as already prepared in the heavenly realm before it descends on Edom. The people devoted to destruction (cherem) are specifically identified as Edom. The sword descending from heaven to earth for judgment is the pattern NT apocalyptic uses for Christ's return with the sword from his mouth (Rev 19:15). The MKT's bathed in heaven is literal -- the sword is dripping from its heavenly preparation.
34.6: YHWH is flagged twice -- the sword is the LORD's, filled with blood from Edomite sacrificial animals. The sacrifice imagery (lambs, goats, rams, fat, kidneys) makes the Edomite slaughter a divine sacrifice on a massive scale -- as if Edom were being offered to the LORD in the same way Israel offered sacrifices. The LORD has a sacrifice in Bozrah (the Edomite capital). This sacrificial-slaughter language recurs in Rev 19:17-18 for the eschatological battle.
34.7: Wild oxen, young bulls, and mighty bulls falling alongside the Edom armies -- the wild animals of the land participate in the judgment, soaked with blood along with the human armies. The fertility of the land (fat and rich soil) is inverted: instead of grain from productive soil, blood is absorbed into the soil. The MKT's soaked with blood and enriched with fat follow the Hebrew's agricultural inversion.
34.8: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Zion. The double time-span (day, year) indicates both the decisive moment and the extended period of the LORD's judgment. Cause of Zion (riv Tzion: the legal case/quarrel of Zion) introduces the forensic dimension: this is not arbitrary violence but legal retribution for what was done to Zion. The LORD acts as Zion's legal advocate and enforcer.
34.9: The environmental transformation of Edom's territory -- streams become tar, soil becomes sulfur, land becomes burning pitch. The Sodom-and-Gomorrah pattern (fire and sulfur, Gen 19:24-25) is applied to Edom, making Edom's judgment a second Sodom. The MKT's tar and burning pitch preserve the combustible-liquid imagery that makes the land itself flammable and permanently hostile to life.
34.10: Olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged -- its smoke will rise forever. The MKT renders olam as forever -- absolute permanent duration. Rev 14:11 applies this exactly to the smoke of torment rising forever and ever for those who worship the beast. The permanent desolation of Edom -- from generation to generation it will lie desolate -- functions in Revelation as the type for permanent eschatological judgment.
34.11: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD stretches over Edom the measuring line of tohu (formlessness) and the plumb line of bohu (emptiness). These are the exact words of Gen 1:2 (the earth was tohu vabohu -- formless and void) before creation. The LORD is un-creating Edom, measuring it out with the tools of Genesis but using them to produce pre-creation chaos rather than ordered creation. The MKT's measuring line of chaos and plumb line of desolation captures this de-creation inversion.
34.12: They will call it No Kingdom There, and all its princes will come to nothing. The renaming of the land as No Kingdom There is the linguistic embodiment of the un-creation: the political reality (kingdom, princes) is declared non-existent. The nothingness (efes) that characterizes the princes is the same word for zero/nothing used for the idols and nations in chs 40-41.
34.13: Thorns overtaking citadels, nettles and brambles in fortresses -- the reversal of human architectural achievement by wild vegetation. Haunt for jackals and home for owls: the wilderness animals that inhabit desolate places replace the human inhabitants. The citadels and fortresses that were Edom's pride become nesting grounds for scavengers.
34.14: The night owl (lilit) will settle there and find herself a resting place. The lilit is a creature of the desert and desolation -- variously rendered as screech owl, night creature, or (in later tradition) a demonic figure. The MKT's night owl is consistent with the surrounding list of desert animals and is a defensible naturalistic rendering. The settling and finding of rest is ironic: the desolate land provides rest for the creatures of desolation.
34.15: The great owl makes her nest, lays eggs, hatches, gathers young -- the complete reproductive cycle happening in the former citadel. The arrows-gather metaphor (dayyah, possibly a falcon or kite) gathering her kind adds another predatory bird. The thoroughness of the animal colonization -- nesting, breeding, gathering -- shows that the desolation is not temporary but has become the permanent habitat of wilderness creatures.
34.16: YHWH is flagged; ruach (Spirit, disp 4) is flagged -- for my mouth has commanded it, and his Spirit has gathered them. The book of the LORD is searched for verification that each animal of vv11-15 is present -- not one will be absent, not one will lack its mate. His Spirit gathered them connects the creative-gathering work of the Spirit (Gen 1:2) to the de-creative gathering of desolation-animals. The same Spirit who gathered creation now gathers the un-creation. The MKT capitalizes Spirit here, reflecting the divine agent.
34.17: Olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged twice -- they will possess it forever; from generation to generation they will dwell in it. The animals' permanent possession of the desolated land is expressed with two time-spanning phrases. The MKT's forever and from generation to generation follow the Hebrew's paired temporal expressions. The lot-casting and measuring-line division (v17) mirrors the conquest-era land distribution of Israel -- but now used for the permanent allotment of wilderness to wilderness-creatures.

35.1: The dramatic reversal of ch 34's desolation: the wilderness and the dry land will rejoice; the desert will exult and blossom like the crocus. The same creation-vocabulary used for de-creation in ch 34 is now used for re-creation: the dry land (tziyah) and desert (aravah) that were places of desolation are now the subjects of joy and blossoming. The crocus (chavatzelet) is a spring flower -- the image of new growth in the most barren place.
35.2: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged twice; Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon. They will see the glory of the LORD, the splendor of our God. Two occurrences of kavod: first the glory of Lebanon/Carmel/Sharon given to the desert; then the glory of the LORD and the splendor of Elohim seen by the new-creation inhabitants. The MKT's glory is consistent -- the kavod that was Lebanon's is transferred to the wilderness, and the kavod that is the LORD's is then seen directly.
35.3: Strengthen the weak hands, and steady the shaking knees. The pastoral-exhortation embedded in the eschatological vision: before the final revelation of glory, the discouraged people must be strengthened. Heb 12:12 quotes this verse directly: strengthen the feeble arms and weak knees -- applied to the Christian who is running with endurance, encouraged by the example of Jesus who endured the cross for the coming joy.
35.4: Elohim is flagged twice -- your God will come with vengeance; God will come with divine retribution -- he will come and save you. The three-part divine-coming announcement: (1) with vengeance (naqam), (2) with retribution (gemul Elohim: the recompense of God), (3) to save (yishakhem). The first two are toward enemies; the third is toward God's people. Jesus begins to fulfill this in his healing ministry (vv5-6), while the full vengeance-retribution aspect awaits the second coming.
35.5: The eyes of the blind opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped -- the two sensory-healing miracles that mark the arrival of the eschatological age. Matt 11:5 and Luke 7:22 list these exactly when Jesus answers John's question (are you the one who is to come?): the blind receive sight, the deaf hear, the lame walk, the mute speak. The MKT's rendering is close to the LXX form that the NT Gospels cite, which is important for the connection to Jesus' answer to John.
35.6: The lame leaping like a deer and the mute tongue bursting into song -- the kinetic and vocal miracles. The waters breaking out in the wilderness connects the physical healing to the landscape transformation of v1-2: as the desert blossoms, so the wilderness acquires streams. The healing of the human body and the healing of the land are presented as parallel acts of the same divine restoration. The MKT's burst into song is vivid for the Hebrew teranen: shout/sing with joy.
35.7: The burning sand becoming a pool and thirsty ground becoming springs -- the inversion of the desert's drought. The haunt of jackals (where only scavengers lived, cf. 34:13) becomes a place of grass, reeds, and papyrus. The reed and papyrus are specifically writing-material plants -- the association may be with the book of the LORD (34:16) being fulfilled. The MKT's burning sand for sharav is the correct identification of the desert mirage-sand.
35.8: Qodesh (holiness, disp 2) is flagged -- a highway will be there, called the Way of Holiness. The Way of Holiness (derech haqodesh) is the consecrated road of the returning exiles. The unclean will not travel on it -- the holy way is protected from defilement. It is for those who walk on that Way; fools will not wander onto it -- the protective exclusivity of the holy highway. The MKT's Way of Holiness is the standard rendering; the highway links back to 11:16 (highway from Assyria) and forward to 40:3 (prepare the way of the LORD).
35.9: No lion on the Way -- the predator-free road for the redeemed. The lions absent from the highway contrast with the lions of 34:7 (wild oxen alongside Edom's armies) and recall the peaceful coexistence of 11:6-9 (lion lying down with the lamb). But the redeemed (geulim) walk there -- they are specifically the ones this road is for. The exclusion of the lion (predator) and the inclusion of the redeemed walker is the shape of the new-creation safety.
35.10: YHWH is flagged; olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged -- the ransomed of the LORD will return; they will come to Zion with singing, with everlasting joy on their heads. The geulim (ransomed/redeemed, from the kinsman-redeemer root goel) come back with singing and simchat olam (everlasting/age-long joy) on their heads. The MKT's everlasting joy renders olam in the absolute sense, not the age-long sense -- the joy is permanent, not merely long-lasting. This verse is quoted in Isaiah 51:11 almost verbatim, making it an Isaianic refrain of the return-promise.

36.1: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged twice -- King Hezekiah and Sennacherib king of Assyria. The historical narrative begins with a precise date (fourteenth year of Hezekiah = 701 BC) and two kings placed in deliberate contrast. Chapter 36 is nearly verbatim from 2 Kings 18:13-37 -- the narrative appears in both books, with minor variations. The MKT follows the Isaiah version. The great king title that Rabshakeh uses for Sennacherib (v4) contrasts with the LORD as the true king (33:22).
36.2: Melek is flagged twice -- the king of Assyria sending Rabshakeh and King Hezekiah receiving the challenge. Rabshakeh (the field commander / title, not personal name) arrives at the conduit of the Upper Pool on the fuller's field road -- the exact location where Isaiah had earlier confronted Ahaz (7:3). The geography is significant: the crisis point of the Assyrian confrontation is the same location as the earlier Syro-Ephraimite crisis, creating a structural echo across Isaiah's career.
36.3: Eliakim son of Hilkiah -- the steward installed in Shebna's place (22:20-22) -- comes out to meet Rabshakeh alongside Shebna (now the secretary, having been demoted) and Joah. The dramatic irony is visible: Shebna, against whom the woe-oracle was pronounced in ch 22, is still present but reduced to a secondary role. The cast of characters makes explicit the connection between the oracle against Shebna and the historical crisis now unfolding.
36.4: Melek is flagged twice -- the Assyrian self-designation the great king, the king of Assyria is Sennacherib's formal imperial title, contrasted with Hezekiah addressed simply as Hezekiah without royal title. The rhetorical stripping of Hezekiah's royal title while inflating Sennacherib's is deliberate diplomacy-as-domination. The question -- what confidence is this in which you trust? -- frames the entire speech as an interrogation of Judah's source of security.
36.5: The Rabshakeh's argument: words without military strategy or battle strength are empty. The translation issue: the Assyrian is mocking the Judeans' verbal confidence (perhaps their recitation of the LORD's promises) as inadequate substitute for actual military capacity. The MKT's mere words are not strategy or battle strength captures the reductio ad militarem -- the Assyrian worldview that reduces all confidence to military calculation.
36.6: Melek is flagged -- the king of Assyria in the Rabshakeh's mouth. Egypt as a cracked and broken staff of reed -- if you lean on it, it pierces your hand. The Egypt-as-useless-ally taunt is historically accurate: Egypt had proved unable or unwilling to help Israel and Judah against Assyria (cf. 30:1-5). The MKT's cracked and broken reed is vivid for the Hebrew kaneh haratsuts: the split/cracked stick of a reed.
36.7: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- the Rabshakeh's twisted argument: even if Judah trusts the LORD their God, hasn't Hezekiah just removed his high places and altars? The Assyrian misunderstands Hezekiah's reform (2 Kings 18:4) as impiety against the LORD, when in fact removing the high places was covenantal fidelity. The argument exposes how foreign the Assyrian framework is: he cannot distinguish between multiplying worship sites and faithful centralized worship.
36.8: Melek is flagged -- my master the king of Assyria. The wager of two thousand horses -- even if Sennacherib gives Judah cavalry, they cannot find enough riders. The taunt reveals the military asymmetry: Judah is not even capable of fielding the horses that Assyria could give them. The MKT's make a wager is a good idiomatic rendering of the Hebrew hitarev-na: engage/bet with my master.
36.9: The rhetorical escalation: not even one of the least of Sennacherib's junior officers could be resisted by Judah with Egyptian cavalry. The argument moves from the impossible (Judah can't use horses, v8) to the absurd (yet Judah relies on Egypt for chariots and cavalry). The MKT's junior officers is accurate for the Hebrew pechot: governors/deputies, the lowest level of Assyrian command.
36.10: YHWH is flagged twice -- the Rabshakeh's most audacious claim: The LORD himself said to me: Go up against this land and destroy it. Whether the Rabshakeh genuinely believed this (based on the Isaianic oracles about Assyria as the LORD's instrument, ch 10) or was purely bluffing, the claim uses YHWH's own name to authorize the invasion. It is both theologically perceptive (Assyria was the LORD's instrument) and spiritually presumptuous (assuming that instrument-status meant permission for this specific act).
36.11: Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah ask Rabshakeh to switch to Aramaic (the diplomatic lingua franca) rather than Hebrew, which the general population on the wall can understand. The request for Aramaic is a containment strategy: keep the demoralizing speech from the ears of the people who might panic or defect. The three officials demonstrate awareness of the psychological warfare dimension of Rabshakeh's speech.
36.12: Rabshakeh refuses and escalates: the speech is precisely for the people on the wall, to whom it is now addressed directly. The graphic description of famine conditions -- eating their own dung and drinking their own urine -- is the Rabshakeh's depiction of what a siege will bring. The graphic content is an attempt to break the people's will before the siege begins. The MKT preserves the graphic language as the text requires.
36.13: Melek is flagged twice -- the Rabshakeh stands up and calls in Hebrew at full volume to the people on the wall. The public proclamation using the covenant people's own language is a deliberate move to bypass the official channels and appeal directly to the population. Hear the words of the great king, the king of Assyria -- the imperial title repeated as rhetorical thunder.
36.14: Melek is flagged -- this is the king's word. The Rabshakeh attributes the speech to Sennacherib himself: do not let Hezekiah mislead you -- he is not able to deliver you. The word deliver (natsil) is the key word of the chapter: Rabshakeh is disputing Hezekiah's claim that the LORD will deliver, by substituting the Assyrian king as the only effective agency. The theological claim embedded in the political speech is the real confrontation.
36.15: YHWH is flagged twice -- do not let Hezekiah lead you to trust in the LORD by saying, The LORD will certainly deliver us -- this city will not be handed over to the king of Assyria. The Rabshakeh quotes what Hezekiah is presumably saying to the people -- the Isaianic promise-formula that the city is under the LORD's protection. The Rabshakeh frames this faith as a deception. Melek is flagged -- the contest is between Hezekiah's LORD and Sennacherib's claim. The king of Assyria is used as a title of overwhelming power that the LORD cannot resist.
36.16: Melek is flagged -- the Rabshakeh speaks on behalf of the king of Assyria. The offer of terms -- make terms with me and surrender, eat from your own vine and fig tree -- is a parody of the Solomonic peace-picture (1 Kings 4:25; Micah 4:4). The good land promised (a land of grain, new wine, bread, vineyards, olive oil, honey) echoes the Deuteronomic promised-land formula (Deut 8:8) -- Sennacherib is offering an alternative promised land in deportation. The MKT's make terms with me and surrender preserves the condition of surrender embedded in the offer.
36.17: The promised deportation land is described in terms deliberately echoing the Promised Land -- grain and new wine, bread and vineyards. The Rabshakeh is rhetorically reversing the Exodus narrative: instead of the LORD bringing Israel out of Egypt into the Promised Land, Sennacherib offers to bring them out of Jerusalem into a replacement promised land in Assyria. The MKT preserves the agricultural abundance vocabulary that makes the parallel to the Promised Land explicit.
36.18: Elohim, YHWH, and melek are flagged -- Has any god of the nations ever delivered his land from my power? What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem? The Rabshakeh's comparative-religion argument: the gods of the nations (Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim) could not deliver their cities from Assyria. The argument treats YHWH as one deity among others, subject to the same evaluation by military track record. The flaw in the argument is precisely its comparatist framework: the LORD is not one deity among comparable deities.
36.19: Elohim is flagged twice -- where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did they deliver Samaria from my power? The historical examples are accurate: Hamath, Arpad, and Sepharvaim had all fallen to Assyria. Samaria (the northern kingdom) had fallen in 722 BC -- within living memory of the Judeans hearing Rabshakeh's speech. The inclusion of Samaria is the most painful point: Israel's fall was supposed to be a warning to Judah, and Rabshakeh turns it into a threat.
36.20: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- Which of all the gods of these lands has ever delivered his land from my power? What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem? The speech closes with the summary argument: universal Assyrian supremacy over all the gods of all the nations. The Rabshakeh's theological claim is that divine power is measurable by military outcome -- and by that measure, Sennacherib wins. The flaw is the measure, not the data. The LORD's answer will be not a battle but an angel (37:36).
36.21: Melek is flagged -- the people's silence: they did not answer the Rabshakeh a word, for the king (Hezekiah) had commanded, do not answer him. The silence is disciplined obedience, not defeat. Hezekiah's command not to engage with Rabshakeh's speech reflects wisdom: engaging the speech on its own terms would be to accept its framework. Silence withdraws the legitimacy that a response would confer. The MKT's remained silent and said nothing in reply preserves the completeness of the non-engagement.
36.22: The three officials -- Eliakim the administrator, Shebna the secretary, Joah the recorder -- return to Hezekiah with their clothes torn in the sign of mourning and grief. The torn clothes are the appropriate response to blasphemous speech against the LORD: the same response Caiaphas will make at Jesus' claim to be the Son of Man (Matt 26:65). The officials' report to the king completes the chapter's narrative arc from Rabshakeh's challenge to the delegation's return -- and prepares for Hezekiah's response in ch 37.

29.1: hôy ʾAriʾēl ʾAriʾēl qiryat ḥānâ Dāwid = "Woe, Ariel, Ariel, the city where David camped." ʾAriʾēl (H740) carries a double meaning: (1) "lion of God" (ʾărî + ʾēl) — the city as a powerful beast; (2) "hearth of God" or "altar-hearth" (ʾar-ʾēl, cf. Ezek 43:15-16 where the altar hearth is called haʾăriʾēl) — Jerusalem as the city of the divine altar. The irony: the city that performs sacrifices will itself become a sacrificial hearth surrounded by siege works (v3). The double meaning is sustained throughout the oracle.
29.2: waăṣartî ʿal-ʾAriʾēl wĕhāyĕtâ tĕʾăniyyâ waʾĕniyyâ = "I will press hard upon Ariel (ṣûr, to besiege/distress), and she will be full of lamentation (tĕʾăniyyâ) and moaning (ʾăniyyâ)." The wordplay on ʾAriʾēl continues: wĕhāyĕtâ lî kĕʾAriʾēl = "and she shall be to me like an Ariel" — the city becomes the thing its name signifies. The siege transforms Jerusalem into the hearth/altar of divine judgment, just as its altar was the hearth of sacrifice.
29.3: wĕḥānîtî ʿālayik dûr = "I will encamp against you as a circle (dûr)" — the siege works surrounding Jerusalem. wĕṣartî ʿālayik māṣâb = "and I will besiege you with a siege mound (māṣâb)." wăhăqîmōtî ʿālayik mĕṣurōt = "and I will raise up siege towers (mĕṣurōt) against you." Three different siege-work terms — the vocabulary of ancient military engineering — applied to YHWH attacking his own city. The enemy, whoever it is militarily, is YHWH's instrument.
29.4: wĕšāpalt mittĕreṣ tĕdabbērî = "you will be brought low, you will speak from the ground (ʾereṣ)" — humiliation described as speaking from the earth rather than upright. ûmēʿāpār tiŝpeh ʾimrātek = "and your speech (ʾimrâ) will come muffled from the dust." kĕʾôḇ mēʾereṣ qôlēk = "like a ghost/spirit-medium (ʾôḇ) from the ground, your voice" — the defeated city speaks like a necromantic spirit, a murmur from below ground. The city that pursued the dead (cf. 8:19) will speak like the dead.
29.5: wĕhāyâ kĕʾāḇāq dak hămôn zārāyik = "and the multitude (hāmôn) of your foes will become like fine dust (ʾāḇāq dak)" — the sudden reversal: the siege armies that reduced Jerusalem to a whisper will themselves become dust. ûkĕmōṣ ʿōḇēr hămôn ʿārîṣîm = "and the multitude of the ruthless like blowing chaff (môṣ ʿōḇēr)" — the judgment works both ways: Jerusalem humbled, then enemies scattered. The eschatological double reversal.
29.6: mēʿim YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt tiPPāqēd = "from YHWH of armies she will be visited (pāqad)" — pāqad (H6485) = to visit in attention, either for blessing or judgment; here YHWH's visitation brings sudden reversal. bĕraʿam ûbĕraʿaš wĕqôl gādôl sûpâ ûsĕʿārâ wĕlahab ʾēš ʾōkēlâ = "with thunder, earthquake, great noise, storm, tempest, and devouring flame" — the full theophanic toolkit (cf. Exod 19:16-18; 1 Kgs 19:11-12). The reversal from siege to salvation comes through direct divine intervention in creation.
29.7: wĕhāyâ kĕḥălôm ḥăzôn laylâ hămôn kol-haggôyim = "and the multitude of all the nations that fight against Ariel will be like a dream, a night vision." The dreamer wakes and the threat is gone. wĕkol-ṣōbĕhêhā ûmĕṣādôtêhā wĕhammaṣîq lāh = "all who fight against her and her fortification and those who press her hard." Three classes of attacker — all become a vanished dream at YHWH's intervention.
29.8: wĕhāyâ kaʾăšer yaḥălōm hārāʿēḇ wĕhinnēh ʾōkēl = "as when a hungry man dreams he is eating, and awakens with his hunger unsatisfied (rêqâ napšô, lit. his soul is empty)." The enemy armies that dreamed of conquering Jerusalem will wake to find their campaign was illusory. The simile of the thirsty man who dreams of drinking (second image) reinforces: the apparent victory over Jerusalem will evaporate like a dream at sunrise.
29.9: hitmahmĕhû wĕtĕmāhû hiššāʿăšĕʿû wĕšōʿû = "Be dull and be amazed, blind yourselves and be blind" — four imperatives of stupor, possibly ironic: Isaiah commands the people to do what they are already doing. šāḵrû wĕlōʾ yāyin nāʿû wĕlōʾ šēkār = "they are drunk but not with wine, they stagger but not with strong drink" — moral-spiritual intoxication without physical cause. The dullness is theological, not physical; it is the stupor that comes from sustained rejection of prophetic truth.
29.10: kî-nāsak ʿălêkem YHWH rûaḥ tardēmâ = "For YHWH has poured out upon you a spirit of deep sleep (tardēmâ, H8639)." Tardēmâ is the word used for Adam's sleep (Gen 2:21) and Abraham's visionary trance (Gen 15:12) — a divinely induced stupor. wayĕʿaṣṣēm ʾet-ʿênêkem ʾet-hannĕḇîʾîm = "and he has closed your eyes, the prophets" — the very people whose eyes were opened for vision have their eyes shut as judgment for spurning the vision. Paul cites this verse in Rom 11:8 ("God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear").
29.11: wĕhāyâ lākem ḥăzôt hakkōl kidibĕrê hassēper heḥātûm = "And the vision of all this has become to you like the words of a sealed (ḥātam) scroll." The paradox: the prophetic revelation is inaccessible — either literally sealed (requiring the literate who say "I cannot read it, it is sealed," v11) or through illiteracy (v12). The word of God is present but unavailable to those in the stupor of v10.
29.12: wĕnātan hassēper ʾet ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿ sēper = "and the scroll is given to one who cannot read (lōʾ-yādaʿ sēper, lit. who does not know the scroll)." The illiterate man's response: "I cannot read." Both classes — the literate who say "it is sealed" and the illiterate who cannot read — are equally excluded from the prophetic vision. The seal is internal (the stupor of v10), not merely external (the wax seal of v11).
29.13:
yaʿan kî niggaš hāʿām hazzeh bĕpîw ûbĕśĕpātāyw kibbĕdûnî ûlibbô riḥaq mimmennî = "because this people draws near with their mouth (bĕpîw) and honors me with their lips (śĕpāhayim), but their heart (lēḇ) is far from me." Niggaš = to draw near (liturgically, as in priestly approach); the form of worship is present, the substance absent.

Jesus quotes this verse in Matt 15:8-9 and Mark 7:6-7 as a direct critique of Pharisaic tradition that substitutes human precepts for the command of God. The precise Hebrew contrast (bĕpîw / libbô = mouth vs. heart) is the heart of Jesus's accusation: Corban practice honored God verbally while dishonoring parents practically. Isaiah's critique of empty cult becomes Jesus's critique of legal formalism — the structural problem is identical.
29.14:
lākēn hinnĕnî yôsip lĕhapliʾ ʾet-hāʿām hazzeh haplēʾ wāpele = "Therefore behold, I will again do wonderful things (hiplîʾ) with this people, wonderful and marvelous (haplēʾ wāpele)" — the divine wonder is not blessing here but judgment: the wisdom of the wise will perish. wĕʾābĕdâ ḥokmàt ḥăkāmāyw ûbînâ nĕḇōnāyw tistattār = "the wisdom (ḥokmâ) of its wise men will perish (ʾāḇad) and the understanding (bînâ) of its understanding men will be hidden (sātar)."

Paul directly quotes this verse in 1 Cor 1:19 ("I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart") — applying it to the cross, which appears as foolishness to human wisdom but is the wisdom of God. The divine reversal of Isa 29:14 is enacted in the crucified Messiah: the very moment wisdom would declare the cross absurd, wisdom itself is overthrown.
29.15: hôy hammaʿămîqîm meYHWH lastar ʿēṣâ = "Woe to those who hide deep (māʿaq) from YHWH their plan (ʿēṣâ)" — plotting in secret as though the hidden God cannot see. wĕhāyâ bĕmaḥšāk maʿăśêhem = "and their deeds are in the dark" — the political machinations of the Judean leadership forming unauthorized alliances (with Egypt) while claiming to serve YHWH. wayyōʾmĕrû mî rōʾênû ûmî yôdĕʿēnû = "and they say, who sees us? who knows us?" — the epistemological arrogance of those who deny divine omniscience.
29.16: hăpkĕkem ʾim-kĕḥōmer hayôṣēr yeḥāšēḇ = "You turn things upside down! Shall the potter (yôṣēr) be regarded as the clay (ḥōmer)?" — the reversal of creator-creature distinction. kî-yōʾmar maʿăśeh lĕʿōśēhû lōʾ ʿāśānî = "as if the thing made (maʿăśeh) said of its maker (ʿōśeh): he did not make me." The potter/clay metaphor is the standard OT image of divine sovereignty over human creatures (cf. Jer 18; Rom 9:21). Paul cites Isa 29:16 and Isa 45:9 together in Rom 9:20-21 in his defense of God's sovereign election.
29.17: hălôʾ-ʿôd miʿaṭ mĕʿāṭ wĕšāḇ lĕḇānôn lakkarmel = "Is it not yet a very little while until Lebanon shall be turned into a fruitful field (karmel)?" — the eschatological reversal: the mighty forest becomes a garden, the garden becomes wild forest. wĕhakarmel layaʿar yeḥāšēḇ = "and the fruitful field shall be regarded as a forest" — when YHWH acts, conventional categories of abundance and wilderness exchange. The vision moves from judgment (vv1-16) toward restoration (vv17-24).
29.18: wĕšāmĕʿû bayyôm hahûʾ ḥĕrĕšîm diḇrê sēper = "And in that day the deaf (ḥĕrĕšîm) will hear the words of a scroll" — reversal of the sealed scroll (v11): what was inaccessible because of the stupor becomes accessible in the day of restoration. ûmēʾōpel ûmiḥōšek ʿênê ʿiwwĕrîm tirʾênâ = "and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see" — the messianic signs that Jesus cited in Matt 11:5 as proof of his identity (Isa 35:5-6; Isa 42:7) are anticipated here as eschatological markers.
29.19: wĕyāsĕpû ʿănāwîm bĕYHWH śimḥâ = "And the humble (ʿănāwîm) will increase their joy (śimḥâ) in YHWH." ʿĂnāwîm (H6041) = the poor, meek, lowly — the same word that appears in the Beatitudes context (Matt 5:5: "blessed are the meek/humble"). wĕʾebyônê ʾādām biqĕdôš yiśrāʾēl yāgîlû = "and the needy (ʾebyôn) among mankind will exult in the Holy One of Israel." The eschatological reversal: the humbled become the joyful.
29.20: kî ʾāpes ʿārîṣ wĕkālâ lēṣ = "For the tyrant (ʿārîṣ) will be no more and the scoffer (lēṣ) will cease" — the two social enemies of the ʿănāwîm (the violent oppressor and the religious mocker) eliminated in the restoration. wĕnikrĕtû kol-šōqĕdê ʾāwen = "and all who watch for (šāqad) iniquity will be cut off" — the third category: the legalistic inquisitors who scrutinize others for violations they can exploit.
29.21: maḥăṭîʾê ʾādām bĕdāḇār = "those who cause a person to sin by a word (dāḇār)" — litigation tactics that trap people in verbal admission. ûlĕmôkîaḥ baššaʿar yĕqōšûn = "and who lay a snare for the one who reproves at the gate" — the gate (šaʿar) was the public legal forum where elders held court; targeting the judge/reprover there was a perversion of justice. wayyaṭṭû bĕtōhû ṣaddîq = "and by empty argument (tōhû) they turn aside the righteous" — tōhû = chaos/emptiness/nothingness; using insubstantial legal arguments to corrupt just decisions.
29.22: lākēn kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾel-bêt yaʿăqōb = "Therefore thus says YHWH to the house of Jacob" — the oracle of assurance that follows the reversal descriptions (vv17-21). ʾăšer pādâ ʾet-ʾAḇrāhām = "who redeemed (pādâ) Abraham" — an unusual application: Abraham as the one YHWH redeemed, usually applied to the Exodus. The connection of Abraham to redemption may relate to the Akedah (Gen 22) or to God's deliverance of Abraham from Ur. The point: the God who redeemed the father will restore the children. lōʾ-ʿattâ yēḇôš yaʿăqōb wĕlōʾ-ʿattâ pānāyw yeḥěwwārû = "Jacob shall not now be ashamed, nor shall his face now grow pale."
29.23: kî bĕrĕʾôtô yĕlādāyw maʿăśēh yādāy bĕqirbô yaqĕdîšû šĕmî = "For when he sees his children, the work of my hands, in his midst, they will sanctify (qiddaš) my name." Qiddaš (piel) = to treat as holy, to recognize and declare holiness. wĕhiqdîšû ʾet-qĕdôš yaʿăqōb wĕʾet-ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yaʿărîṣû = "and they will sanctify the Holy One of Jacob and will stand in awe of (ʿāraṣ) the God of Israel" — the eschatological goal of divine redemption: a community that recognizes and declares the holiness of God.
29.24: wĕyādĕʿû tōʿê-rûaḥ bînâ = "and those who err in spirit (tōʿê-rûaḥ) will come to understanding (bînâ)" — the restoration of the bînâ that was hidden (v14). wĕrōgĕnîm yilmĕdû lĕqāḥ = "and those who murmur (rāgan) will accept instruction (lāqaḥ)." Rāgan = to murmur, grumble against authority (used of Israel in the wilderness; cf. Num 14:2; Ps 106:25); the murmurers who rejected prophetic instruction will receive it in the day of restoration. The spiritual inversion is complete: stupor → understanding; grumbling → instruction.

30.1: hôy bānîm sôrĕrîm = "Woe, rebellious children (sôrĕrîm)" — sārar (H5637) = to be stubborn, rebellious, the root of the "stubborn and rebellious son" of Deut 21:18-21. neʾum YHWH laʿăśôt ʿēṣâ wĕlōʾ minnî = "declares YHWH, to carry out a plan but not from me" — the sin: independent policy-making that bypasses prophetic consultation of YHWH. wĕlinsōk massēkâ wĕlōʾ rûḥî = "and to make an alliance (massēkâ, lit. a libation/poured treaty) but not of my Spirit." The covenant negotiation with Egypt bypasses YHWH's Spirit who speaks through prophets.
30.2: hallĕkîm lāredet miṣrayim = "who set out to go down to Egypt" — yārad (to go down) is the standard direction for Egypt in Hebrew geography; "going down" to Egypt reverses the Exodus's "coming up." ûpî lōʾ šāʾālû = "and who did not ask my mouth (peh)" — the prophetic oracle was the legitimate means of determining YHWH's will; bypassing it means bypassing YHWH himself. laʿōz bĕmaʿôz parʿōh ûlaḥăsôt bĕṣel miṣrayim = "to seek refuge in Pharaoh's protection and shelter in Egypt's shadow" — ṣel (shadow) is an ironic image: Egypt's "shadow" provides no real shade, just an illusion of protection.
30.3: wĕhāyâ lākem maʿôz parʿōh lĕḇōšet = "and Pharaoh's protection (maʿôz) will become for you shame (bōšet)." The very thing sought for security becomes the occasion of disgrace. wĕhaḥăsûtyâ bĕṣel miṣrayim liklimmâ = "and the shelter (ḥāsâ) of Egypt's shadow will become humiliation (kĕlimmâ)." Both security words — maʿôz (stronghold) and ḥāsâ (shelter) — reverse to their opposites: shame and humiliation.
30.4: kî-hāyû bĕZôʿan śārāyw = "For his officials are at Zoan (Ṣoʿan)" — Tanis, a major Egyptian delta city, the meeting place of the Egyptian court. wĕmalʾākāyw ḥānēs yagîʿû = "and his envoys reach Hanes" — another Egyptian city (possibly Heracleopolis); the diplomatic mission has traveled deep into Egypt. The verse describes the actual journey the Judean envoys have taken — Isaiah is not speaking abstractly but about a real diplomatic mission underway.
30.5: kōl hikĕlîm ʿal-ʿam lōʾ yôʿîlû lāhem = "Everyone will be put to shame because of a people that cannot profit them (yāʿal)" — Egypt will not benefit Judah at all. lōʾ lĕʿezrâ wĕlōʾ lĕhôʿîl kî lĕḇōšet wĕgam lĕḥerpâ = "not for help and not for profit, but for shame and also for disgrace (ḥerpâ)." The oracle is categorical: no benefit whatsoever, only dishonor. ḥerpâ = the shame that comes from a failed partnership, worse than having sought no help at all.
30.6: maśśāʾ bahămôt negeḇ = "The oracle (maśśāʾ) concerning the beasts of the Negeb" — the oracle describes the caravan route through the Negeb wilderness to Egypt. bĕʾereṣ ṣārâ wĕṣûqâ lābiʾ ûlayiš mēhem ʾepʿeh ûśārāp mĕʿôpēp = "through the land of trouble and anguish, of lioness and lion, of viper and flying serpent (śārāp mĕʿôpēp)" — the wilderness through which the treasure-laden caravan travels is dangerous (cf. Isa 6:2 śārāp = the burning serpent-beings). yiśśĕʾû ʿal-ketep ʿayirim ḥêlām = "they carry their wealth on donkeys' backs" — the gifts for Egypt, the bribe for military protection. Going to enormous effort and expense for an alliance that will be worthless (v5).
30.7: wĕmiṣrayim hebel wĕrîq yaʿzōrû = "Egypt's help is worthless (hebel) and empty (rîq)" — hebel is Qohelet's word for vanity/vapor; here applied to Egyptian military reliability. lākēn qārāʾtî lāzōʾt Rahab hēm šāḇet = "therefore I have called this: Rahab who sits still." Rahab (H7294) = the chaos-sea-monster of OT mythology (cf. Job 9:13; 26:12; Ps 87:4; 89:10), also used as a symbolic name for Egypt. "Rahab who sits still" = the chaos-beast that does nothing — a powerless monster, all reputation and no action. The ironic title deflates Egypt's pretension.
30.8: ʿattâ bôʾ kĕtāḇāh ʿal-lûaḥ ʾittām wĕʿal-sēper ḥuqqĕhā = "Now go, write it before them on a tablet (lûaḥ) and inscribe it in a scroll (sēper)" — the act of writing preserves the prophecy for future vindication. wĕtihî lĕyôm ʾaḥărôn lĕʿad ʿad-ʿôlām = "that it may be for a later day a witness forever" — the prophecy is sealed into permanence (cf. 8:16). When Egypt proves useless and the Assyrian crisis comes, the written testimony will vindicate the prophet. The physical act of writing is the prophetic claim to predictive revelation.
30.9: kî ʿam mĕrî hû bānîm kĕḥāšîm = "For they are a rebellious people (mĕrî), lying children (kĕḥāšîm)" — mĕrî = rebellion (the noun from the same root as Miriam and Meribah); kĕḥāšîm = those who deal falsely (kāḥaš = to lie/deny). bānîm lōʾ-ʾāḇû šĕmôaʿ tôrat YHWH = "children who were not willing to hear (šāmaʿ) the tôrâ of YHWH." The diagnosis: the Egyptian alliance is not merely a political miscalculation but a symptom of refusing prophetic instruction.
30.10: ʾăšer ʾāmĕrû lārōʾîm lōʾ tirʾû = "who say to the seers (rōʾîm): Do not see!" — the demand to suppress prophetic vision. wĕlannĕḇîʾîm lōʾ tihzû-lānû nĕkōḥôt = "and to the prophets: Do not prophesy to us what is right (nĕkōḥôt)!" daḇĕrû-lānû ḥălāqôt = "speak to us smooth things (ḥălāqôt)" — flattering, comfortable words rather than true words. ḥězû mĕhaTtāʾôt = "prophesy illusions (mĕhaTtāʾôt)" — the demand for comfortable falsehood. 2 Tim 4:3 echoes this: "the time is coming when people will not endure sound teaching, but having itching ears they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own passions."
30.11: sûrû min-hadderek haTTû min-haʾōraḥ = "turn aside from the way, leave the path (ʾōraḥ)" — the demand to abandon the prophetic way entirely. haššibû mippānênû ʾet qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "remove from our presence the Holy One of Israel" — the most extreme demand: banish YHWH's presence from prophetic encounter. The "Holy One of Israel" is Isaiah's signature divine title (from the throne vision of ch6); to reject the prophet's message is to reject YHWH's holiness.
30.12: lākēn kōh-ʾāmar qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "Therefore thus says the Holy One of Israel" — the rejection of the title (v11) is answered by YHWH speaking with that very title. yaʿan miʾāsĕkem baḏḏāḇār hazzeh = "because you have despised (māʾas) this word" — the word māʾas (to despise, reject) is the key accusation; rejecting the prophetic word = rejecting YHWH. wattibṭĕḥû bĕʿōšeq ûlĕzût = "and you have trusted in oppression and deceit (lĕzût)" — the Egyptian alliance is characterized not just as politically unwise but as morally corrupt: built on oppression and crookedness.
30.13: lākēn yihyeh lākem heʿāwōn hazzeh kĕpereṣ nōpēl = "Therefore this iniquity will be to you like a bulging wall, broken and falling" — the metaphor of structural failure: pereṣ nōpēl = a breach/crack that leads to collapse. nibzĕqet bĕḥômat gāḇohâ = "a bulge in a high wall" — the crack in a high wall that gives suddenly without warning. ʾăšer pitʾōm lĕpetaʿ yāḇôʾ šibbĕrāh = "whose shattering (šāḇar) comes suddenly, in an instant (petaʿ)" — no warning, no gradual weakening visible; the collapse is sudden. The iniquity that appeared stable (the Egyptian alliance) will shatter catastrophically.
30.14: ûšĕḇārāh kĕšeḇer nĕḇel yôṣĕrîm kātût = "and its breaking (šiḇrāh) will be like the breaking of a potter's vessel (kĕlî yôṣĕr) smashed ruthlessly (kātût)" — the metaphor shifts from structural (wall) to ceramic (pot): irreparable destruction. wĕlōʾ-yimmāṣēʾ ḇĕmitṣāṭāyw ḥereś = "and among its fragments (ḥereś) not a shard is found" — the breaking is so complete that not even a piece large enough to carry coals or scoop water remains. Total uselessness: the alliance that was sought for survival becomes an unredeemable wreck.
30.15: kî-kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy YHWH qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl bĕšûḇâ wānāḥat tiwwāšēʿûn = "For thus says the Lord YHWH, the Holy One of Israel: In returning (šûḇâ) and rest (naḥat) you will be saved." Šûḇâ = repentance/return; naḥat = rest, settling/quietness. The paired terms are the spiritual equivalent of the military-political alliance they sought: instead of Egyptian cavalry, trust in YHWH's quiet provision. bĕhašqēṭ ûḇeḇiṭḥâ tihyeh gĕḇûratkĕm = "in quietness (hašqēṭ) and in trust (biṭḥâ) is your strength (gĕḇûrâ)" — the counter-intuitive logic of prophetic faith: the military strength (gĕḇûrâ) they sought through Egyptian cavalry is available through divine trust. wĕlōʾ ʾăḇîtem = "but you were not willing (ʾābâ)" — the volitional refusal.
30.16: wattōʾmĕrû lōʾ kî-ʿal-sûs nānûs = "and you said: No! for we will flee on horses" — the ironic response: they seek military mobility (horses = cavalry, speed = strategic advantage) rather than divine rest. ʿal-kēn tānûsûn = "therefore you will flee" — the wordplay: they will flee (nûs) on horses from the enemy, not toward victory. wĕʿal-qal nirkaḇ = "and we will ride swift (qal) horses" — qal = swift, light (the word of Gen 1:2 for "over the face of the waters"). ʿal-kēn yiqallû rōdĕpêkem = "therefore your pursuers will be swift" — the speed they sought in horses will be outdone by the speed of enemies pursuing them.
30.17: ʾelep miPPĕnê gaʿărat ʾeḥād = "a thousand before the rebuke (gaʿărâ) of one" — one enemy warrior's battle-cry will rout a thousand Judeans. miPPĕnê gaʿărat ḥămišşâ tānûsû = "before the rebuke of five, all of you will flee" — the expected ratio reverses: instead of one Israelite chasing a thousand (Deut 32:30; Lev 26:8), the equation flips. ʿad ʾim-nôtartem kĕtōren ʿal-rōʾš hāhār = "until you are left like a flagstaff (tōren) on a mountaintop" — the isolated, single flagpole on a hilltop: alone, exposed, conspicuous. The remnant reduced to a solitary marker.
30.18: wĕlākēn yaḥakkeh YHWH laḥankĕkem wĕlākēn yārûm lĕraḥămĕkem = "Therefore YHWH waits (ḥāḵâ) to be gracious to you (ḥānan), and therefore he exalts himself to show you mercy (raḥam)." The "therefore" is unexpected after the judgment oracles of vv15-17 — but this is YHWH's character even in judgment: he waits, ready to be gracious. kî ʾĕlōhê mišpāṭ YHWH ʾašrê kol-ḥôkê lô = "for YHWH is a God of justice (mišpāṭ); blessed (ʾašrê) are all who wait for him." The Beatitude-form (ʾašrê = blessed, the OT equivalent of NT makários) applied to those who wait for YHWH — the spiritual posture of 8:17.
30.19: kî-ʿam bĕṣiyyôn yēšēḇ bîrûšālāim = "for the people in Zion who dwell in Jerusalem" — the address shifts to a restored community. bākô lōʾ-tiḇkeh ḥānnôn yāḥannekā lĕqôl zaʿăqekā = "you will weep no more; he will surely be gracious to you at the sound of your cry (zaʿăqā)." Ḥānan (to be gracious) appears twice in one verse in the emphatic infinitive absolute construction (ḥānnôn yāḥannekā). The certainty of divine grace is reinforced by the grammar: "he will be-gracious-indeed gracious to you." kĕšomĕʿô ʾēleykā ʿānākā = "as soon as he hears it, he will answer you."
