30.20: wĕnātan lākem ʾădōnāy lehem ṣar ûmayim lāḥaṣ = "And the Lord will give you the bread of adversity (ṣar) and the water of affliction (lāḥaṣ)" — even in the coming hardship, YHWH provides. wĕlōʾ-yikkānēp ʿôd môrêkā = "and your Teacher (môreh) will no longer hide himself (kānaṗ)" — the Teacher who was hidden (cf. 8:17: hiding his face) will be revealed. wĕhāyû ʿênêkā rōʾôt ʾet-môrêkā = "and your eyes will see your Teacher." The restoration of prophetic vision: the sealed scroll (29:11) reopened, the Teacher visible.
30.21: wĕʾoznêkā tišmaʿnâ dāḇār mēʾaḥărêkā lēʾmōr = "and your ears will hear a word from behind you saying" — the prophetic voice returning from behind, like a guide directing the traveler. zeh hadderek lĕkû-ḇô = "this is the way, walk in it." The restoration of prophetic direction: the people who demanded "speak smooth things" (30:10) will finally receive and follow true guidance. kî taʾămînû wĕkî taśmĕʾîlû = "when you turn to the right or when you turn to the left" — comprehensive direction for life, not just crisis moments.
30.22: wĕṭimmēʾtem ʾet-ṣippûy pĕsîlê kaspekā = "and you will defile (ṭāmēʾ) the silver overlay (ṣippûy) of your graven images." Ṭāmēʾ (to declare unclean) applied by choice to idols: the restored community will voluntarily defile their own idols — declaring them as unclean as a corpse. wĕʾet-ʾăpuddâ massēkāt zāhābekā = "and the gold plating of your cast images" — the costly metal idols are defiled. tizrēm kĕmô dāwâ ṣēʾ tōmar lô = "you will scatter them like a menstrual cloth (dāwâ), saying to them: Out!" — the most powerful image of revulsion: the idols reduced to a used sanitary rag. The reformed covenant people's total rejection of idolatry.
30.23: wĕnātan mĕṭar zarʿekā ʾăšer tizraʿ ʾet-hāʾădāmâ = "and he will give rain for the seed (zereʿ) with which you sow the ground" — the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy restored: rain, abundant harvest, fat pastureland. wĕlāḥem tĕḇûʾat hāʾădāmâ wĕhāyâ dāšēn wĕšāmēn = "and the bread of the produce of the ground will be rich and plentiful (šāmēn)" — the land gives back to Israel what it withheld in judgment (cf. 5:10: the vineyard yielding almost nothing).
30.24: wĕhāʾĕlāpîm wĕhaʿăyārîm ʿōbĕdê hāʾădāmâ = "and the oxen and donkeys that work the soil" — working animals fed well on the threshed feed of restoration. bĕlîl ḥāmîṣ yōʾkēlû = "will eat salted/seasoned feed (bĕlîl ḥāmîṣ)" — the best feed for animals. Restoration extends to the humblest element of the agricultural economy: the well-fed working animal is the sign of a well-functioning covenant land.
30.25: wĕhāyâ ʿal-kol-har-gāḇohah ûʿal-kol-gibʿâ nĕśuʾâ pĕlāgîm yĕʿārôt mayim = "and on every high mountain and every exalted hill there will be streams of water." The inversion of the wilderness curse: bare heights that had no water (cf. 41:18) will flow with streams. The image is of a thoroughly watered, abundantly productive landscape — a reversal of the drought-judgment. bĕyôm hereḡ rāḇ bĕnepol migdālôt = "on the day of great slaughter, when towers fall" — this abundance follows a great military judgment; the restoration comes through and after the decisive defeat of the enemy.
30.26: wĕhāyâ ʾôr hallĕḇānâ kĕʾôr haḥammâ = "and the light (ʾôr) of the moon (lĕḇānâ) will be like the light of the sun (ḥammâ)" — the eschatological intensification of creation's light: lunar light (secondary) equals what was solar (primary). wĕʾôr haḥammâ yihyeh šiḇʿātayim kĕʾôr šiḇʿat hayyāmîm = "and the light of the sun will be sevenfold, like the light of seven days." The new creation's luminosity exceeds all prior calculation — light multiplied beyond its present capacity. Cf. Rev 21:23-25 where the new Jerusalem needs no sun or moon because God's glory illuminates it.
30.27: hinnēh šēm YHWH bāʾ mimmerhāq = "Behold, the Name (šēm) of YHWH comes from afar" — a theophany in which YHWH's character and presence (his šēm = Name = all that he is) approaches from a distance. bōʿēr ʾappô wĕkaḇĕd maśśāʾâ = "his anger (ʾap) burning and heavy is the burden (maśśāʾ)" — the theophany is not peaceful but judicial. śĕpātāyw māleʾû zaʿam wĕlĕšônô kĕʾēš ʾōkelet = "his lips are full of indignation (zaʿam) and his tongue is like a consuming fire (ʾēš ʾōkelet)" — the divine word as fire, consuming the enemy.
30.28: wĕrûḥô kĕnaḥal šôṭēp ʿad-ṣawwāʾr yaḥeṣeh = "and his breath (rûaḥ) is like an overflowing stream (naḥal šôṭēp) that reaches up to the neck" — the same flood imagery as 8:8 (the Assyrian reaching Judah's neck), now applied to YHWH's own breath/Spirit against Assyria. lāhānîp gôyim bĕnapâ šāwĕʾ = "to sift the nations with the sieve (nāpâ) of emptiness (šāwĕʾ)" — YHWH's breath as the refiner's sieve. wĕresen maṭʿeh ʿal lĕḥêy ʿammîm = "and a bridle that leads astray upon the jaws (lĕḥî) of the peoples" — YHWH bridles the nations as a rider bridles a horse, directing them to destruction.
30.29: haššîr yihyeh lākem kĕlêl hithaqqdĕšû-ḥāg = "The song will be for you as in the night when a feast is hallowed (hitqaddaš)" — the worship of the night before a sacred festival, probably Passover. wĕśimḥat lēḇāḇ kĕhôlēk bĕḥālîl = "and gladness of heart like one who marches with a flute (ḥālîl)" — the processional music of pilgrimage. lāḇôʾ bĕhar YHWH ʾel-ṣûr yiśrāʾēl = "to go to the mountain of YHWH, to the Rock (ṣûr) of Israel." In the midst of the judgment-oracle, this verse inserts a liturgical note of joy: the divine judgment of Assyria will be celebrated with the same joy as a Passover night.
30.30: wĕhišmîaʿ YHWH ʾet-qôl hadāratô = "and YHWH will cause the majestic voice (qôl hadāratô) to be heard" — the theophanic voice that judges. wĕnāḥat zĕrôʿô yirʾeh bĕzaʿam ʾap wĕlahab ʾēš ʾōkēlâ = "and the descent of his arm will be seen with furious anger (zaʿam), with devouring flame (lahab ʾēš ʾōkēlâ)" — the divine arm descending = direct intervention in battle. nepheṣ wāzerem ûʾeḇen bārād = "with cloudburst (zerem) and rainstorm and stones of hail" — the full arsenal of theophanic storm-weapons (cf. Josh 10:11 where YHWH fights with hailstones).
30.31: kî miqPĕnê YHWH yêḥat ʾAššûr = "For Assyria will be terrified (yêḥat, niphal of ḥātat) before the voice of YHWH" — the world empire that reduced nations to nothing is itself shattered by YHWH's voice alone. baššēḇeṭ yakkeh = "with a rod (šēḇeṭ) he will strike" — cf. 10:5 where Assyria was itself called YHWH's rod (šēḇeṭ); now YHWH's rod strikes the rod. The instrument of judgment is itself judged.
30.32: wĕhāyâ kol-maʿăḇar matteh hāmûsādâ = "and every stroke of the appointed staff" — mûsādâ = the staff of the appointed judgment, the Assyrian rod now redirected. wĕheyniîaḥ YHWH ʿālāyw bĕtupîm ûḇĕkinnōrôt = "YHWH will cause it to fall on them with tambourines and lyres" — the defeat of Assyria will be accompanied by worship music. ûḇĕmilḥamôt tĕnûpâ nilḥam bāh = "and in battles of shaking he will fight against them" — YHWH personally fighting against Assyria.
30.33: kî-ʿārûk mēʾetemôl Topteh = "For Topheth has long been prepared" — Tōpet = the altar of Molech in the Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna), where children were burned as offerings (Jer 7:31-32). Here Isaiah uses Topheth as a metaphor for the burning judgment prepared for the king of Assyria. gam-hûʾ lammelek hûkān = "even for the king it has been prepared." heʿĕmîq hirḥîḇ mĕdûrātāh ʾēš wĕʿēṣîm harbenû = "he has made the pyre deep and wide, with fire and wood in abundance" — the funeral pyre is outsized: for the most powerful king on earth, YHWH prepares a correspondingly massive burning. nišmat YHWH kĕnaḥal goprit bōʿărâ bāh = "and the breath (nišmat) of YHWH kindles it like a stream of burning sulfur (goprit)" — God's breath (the same word as Gen 2:7 where YHWH breathes life into Adam) now breathes death into Assyria's king. Topheth/Gehenna as eternal judgment appears in NT as the term Jesus uses for final destruction (Matt 5:22; Mark 9:43-48).

31.1: hôy hayyōrĕdîm miṣrayim lĕʿezrâ = "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help (ʿezrâ)" — parallel to 30:1-2 but now focused specifically on military hardware. ʿal-sûsîm yiššāʿēnû = "who rely on horses (sûs)" — Egyptian cavalry was the most prized military commodity in the ancient Near East (cf. Deut 17:16, where the king is forbidden to multiply horses). kî rāḇ meʾōd ḥêlô ʿāṣûm mĕʾōd pārāšāyw = "for their army is very large, their cavalry very strong" — the human logic of military alliance: numbers and cavalry = security. wĕlōʾ šāʿû ʿal-qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-YHWH lōʾ dāraš û = "but they do not look to the Holy One of Israel, and they do not seek YHWH" — the structural failure: military calculation without theological consultation.
31.2: wĕgam-hûʾ ḥāḵām wayyāḇēʾ rāʿ = "yet he too is wise and will bring disaster" — the sarcastic concession: YHWH is also "wise," but his wisdom produces judgment rather than deliverance for those who bypassed him. wĕlōʾ hēsîr dĕḇārāyw = "and he has not withdrawn his words" — YHWH's previous oracles of judgment remain in force; they are not canceled by the Egyptian alliance. wĕqām ʿal-bêt mĕrēʿîm wĕʿal-ʿezrat pōʿălê ʾāwen = "and he will rise against the house of evildoers and against the helpers of those who do iniquity." YHWH fights both against Israel (who sought the evil alliance) and against Egypt (who provided it).
31.3: wĕmiṣrayim ʾādām wĕlōʾ-ʾēl wĕsûsêhem bāśār wĕlōʾ-rûaḥ = "Egypt is human (ʾādām) and not God (ʾēl), their horses are flesh (bāśār) and not spirit (rûaḥ)" — the decisive theological contrast: the alliance partners (Egypt, horses) are located entirely in the category of the creaturely (ʾādām, bāśār) rather than the divine (ʾēl, rûaḥ). The contrast is ontological, not merely pragmatic. wĕYHWH yiṭṭeh yādô wĕkāšal ʿōzēr wĕnāpāl ʿāzûr wĕyaḥdāw kullām yikleyû = "and when YHWH stretches out his hand, the helper will stumble and the helped will fall, and all of them will perish together" — both Egypt and Judah collapse together when YHWH acts. The alliance that was supposed to be saving proves mutually destructive.
31.4: kî-kōh ʾāmar-lî YHWH kaʾăšer yehgeh hāʾaryēh wĕhakepir ʿal-ṭarpô = "For thus YHWH said to me: as the lion (ʾaryēh) growls (hāgâ) and the young lion (kepir) over his prey (ṭerep)" — YHWH is the lion standing over Jerusalem, not threatening it but protecting it. kî yiqqārēʾ ʿālāyw mĕlōʾ rōʿîm miqQôlam lōʾ yēḥāt = "when a multitude of shepherds is called out against him, he is not frightened (ḥātat) by their voice" — the lion is undaunted by the noise of human armies. YHWH as lion = both protector of Jerusalem (positive) and threat to enemies.
31.5: kĕṣipporîm ʿāpôt kēn yāgēn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʿal-yĕrûšālaim = "Like hovering birds (ṣipporîm ʿāpôt), so YHWH of hosts will protect (gānan) Jerusalem" — two incompatible images in consecutive verses: lion (v4) and hovering birds (v5). The lion = fierce protection; the birds = delicate, attentive protection (cf. Deut 32:11: the eagle hovering over its young). Together they describe divine protection that is both powerful and tender. gānôn wĕhiṣṣîl pāsôaḥ wĕhimliṭ = "protecting and delivering, sparing and rescuing" — four verbs of deliverance paired asyndetically, creating urgency and comprehensiveness.
31.6: šûḇû lĕʾăšer heeʿemîqû sārâ bĕnê yiśrāʾēl = "Return (šûḇ) to the one from whom they have deeply revolted (heeʿămîqû sārâ)" — the call to repentance in the middle of the judgment-oracle. Heeʿămîqû = they have gone deep in revolt (the word of depth applied to their apostasy). The lion who protects is also the one from whom they fled; returning (šûḇ) to the protector is the only path to safety.
31.7: kî bĕyôm hahûʾ yimʾăsûn ʾîš ʾĕlîlêyw kesep wĕʾĕlîlêyw zāhāḇ = "For in that day everyone will reject (māʾas) his silver idols and golden idols" — the repentance expressed in concrete terms: abandonment of material religion. ʾăšer ʿāśû lākem yĕdêkem ḥēṭĕʾ = "which your own hands have made for you, sinfully" — the idols are the work of human hands (yĕdêkem), not the work of God; the distinction of 31:3 (human vs. divine, ʾādām vs. ʾēl) is applied to worship objects as well as military allies.
31.8: wĕnāpal ʾAššûr bĕḥereḇ lōʾ-ʾîš wĕḥereḇ lōʾ-ʾādām tōʾklennû = "And Assyria will fall by the sword, but not of man (ʾîš); a sword, but not of man (ʾādām), will devour him" — the double negation is crucial: Assyria's defeat will not come through Egyptian cavalry or human military power (the very thing Judah sought) but through direct divine action. wĕnās mippĕnê-ḥāreḇ ûbaḥûrāyw lāmas yihyû = "and he will flee from the sword and his young men will become forced labor (mas)" — the military elite that was Assyria's pride reduced to corvée workers.
31.9: wĕsalʿô mimmāgôr yaʿăḇōr = "and his rock (sela) will pass away in terror (māgôr)" — Assyria's stronghold (either a deity or military fortress) will crumble in fear. wĕḥaṭṭāʾû ʾet-nisSô miyyîrāʾ = "and his officers will be terrified at the battle standard" — the commanders who rallied troops to the standard (nēs) will flee from it. nĕʾum YHWH ʾăšer-ûr lô bĕṣiyyôn wĕtannûr lô bîrûšālaim = "declares YHWH, whose fire (ʾûr) is in Zion and whose furnace (tannûr) is in Jerusalem" — the closing declaration: the fire of divine judgment is located in Zion. It destroys the enemy (Assyria) while purifying the city (Jerusalem).

32.1: hēn lĕṣedeq yimlōk melek = "Behold, a king (melek) will reign (mālaḵ) in righteousness (ṣedeq)" — the messianic-political ideal: a king who is characterized by ṣedeq (righteousness = conformity to divine standard of justice). wĕlĕmišpāṭ śārîm yāśōrû = "and princes will rule (śārar) in justice (mišpāṭ)." The pairing ṣedeq/mišpāṭ = the twin pillars of the restored kingdom (cf. 1:26-27; 9:7; 11:4-5; 33:5). The righteous king anticipates the Davidic Messiah who will embody both.
32.2: wĕhāyâ hāʾîš kĕmastēr-rûaḥ ûmahseh zerem = "And that man will be like a shelter from the wind (rûaḥ) and a refuge from the rainstorm (zerem)" — four natural refuge images: shelter from wind, shelter from storm, streams of water in a dry place, shadow of a great rock in a parched land. Each describes the protective function of the righteous king for the vulnerable. The rock in a weary land recalls the water-from-the-rock of the wilderness (Exod 17; Num 20) and anticipates Paul's identification of that rock as Christ (1 Cor 10:4). kĕpalgê-mayim bĕṣāyôn kĕṣēl sela kāḇēd bĕʾereṣ ʿāyēpâ = "like streams of water in a dry place, like the shadow of a great rock in a weary land."
32.3: wĕlōʾ tišʿênâ ʿênê rōʾîm = "and the eyes of those who see will not be closed (šāʿâ)" — the reversal of the hardening commission of 6:9-10 (where eyes were blinded and ears made heavy). In the kingdom of the righteous king, the perceptual faculties that were sealed in judgment are reopened. wĕʾoznê šōmĕʿîm tiqšābnâ = "and the ears of those who hear will pay attention (qāšaḇ)" — qāšaḇ = to incline toward, to attend carefully. The attentiveness that Israel refused (1:2; 28:23) will characterize the restored community.
32.4: wĕlēḇāḇ mahîrîm yāḇîn lādaʿat = "and the heart of the hasty (mahîrîm) will understand to know (yāḇîn lādaʿat)" — those who were rash in judgment will gain measured understanding. ûlĕšôn ʿilgîm tĕmahēr lĕdabbēr ṣāḥôt = "and the tongue of the stammerers (ʿilgîm) will speak clearly (ṣāḥôt)" — the stammerer whose speech was unclear will speak plainly and readily. All four verses (vv3-4) describe the restoration of communicative capacity — seeing, hearing, understanding, speaking — in the kingdom of the righteous king.
32.5: lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ ʿôd lĕnāḇāl nāḏîḇ = "the fool (nāḇāl) will no longer be called noble (nāḏîḇ)" — the social reversal of titles: the morally bankrupt person who held elite status will no longer be honored. ûlĕkilay lōʾ yēʾāmer šôaʿ = "and the deceiver (kilay) will not be called generous (šôaʿ)" — the exploitation labeled as generosity will be exposed. The righteous king's reign brings truth in naming: people will be called what they are, not what they have fraudulently claimed.
32.6: kî nāḇāl nĕḇālâ yĕdabbēr = "for the fool speaks folly (nĕḇālâ)" — nāḇāl (H5036) = the fool as a moral category (cf. Ps 14:1: "the fool says in his heart, there is no God"), not merely an intellectual classification. wĕlibbô yaʿăśeh-ʾāwen lĕʿăśôt ḥānēp = "and his heart practices iniquity (ʾāwen), to practice godlessness (ḥānep)." wĕdabbēr ʾel-YHWH tôʿâ = "and he speaks error concerning YHWH" — theological confusion at the root of social evil. lĕhārēq nepeš rāʿēḇ = "to leave the hungry soul (nepeš) empty" — the practical consequence of foolishness: the hungry go unfed. The fool's theological error produces social neglect.
32.7: wĕkilay kĕlāyw rāʿîm = "and the deceiver — his weapons (kĕlîm) are evil" — kĕlîm literally = implements/weapons; the deceiver's instruments of action are evil from the start. hûʾ zim-môt yaʿaṣ lĕḥabbēl ʿănāwîm bĕʾimrê-šāqer = "he devises wicked schemes to ruin the poor (ʿănāwîm) with false words (ʾimrê-šāqer)" — the target of the deceiver's evil is consistently the vulnerable (ʿănāwîm, the lowly/poor). wĕʾeḇyôn bĕdabbēr mišpāṭ = "and the needy (ʾeḇyôn) when he speaks what is right" — the needy person seeking legal justice is the particular victim of the deceiver's false words.
32.8: wĕnāḏîḇ nĕḏîḇôt yaʿăṣ wĕhûʾ ʿal-nĕḏîḇôt yāqûm = "but the noble person (nāḏîḇ) devises noble things (nĕḏîḇôt), and by noble things he stands." The contrast to the fool (vv6-7) and the deceiver: the truly noble person is characterized by noble intentions that match their noble status. Nāḏîḇ in the restored kingdom means what it should mean: those with elevated status use it for elevated purposes, not for personal enrichment at the expense of the poor.
32.9: šaʾănannôt bānôt šimʿû qôlî bānôt bôṭĕḥôt haʾăzinnâ ʾimrātî = "Rise up, you women who are at ease (šaʾănannôt), hear my voice; you complacent daughters, listen to my speech" — the address to women of leisure echoes 3:16-4:1 (the indictment of the daughters of Zion). Here the complacent women represent the ease of those who assume security without righteousness. The prophetic address to women signals a particular social class: those insulated from the consequences of political decisions by wealth.
32.10: yāmîm ʿal-šānâ tirgazânâ bôṭĕḥôt = "within a year and some days (yāmîm ʿal-šānâ), you will be in anguish, complacent ones" — a specific time indicator, unusual in prophetic oracles: the complacency will be shattered within a year. kî-kālâ bāṣîr ʾāsip bĕlî yāḇôʾ = "for the vintage (bāṣîr) will fail, the harvest (ʾāsip) will not come" — the agricultural metaphor for the economic collapse that will follow Assyrian invasion. The women's comfortable life depends on agricultural abundance that is about to end.
32.11: ḥirĕdû šaʾănannôt rĕgāzû bôṭĕḥôt = "Tremble, you women who are at ease; quake, complacent ones" — the reversal: the at-ease women will tremble (ḥārad), the self-secure will quake (rāgaz). Pitšĕṭû wĕʿôr ûḥigîrû ʿal-ḥălāṣāyim = "strip yourselves bare and put on sackcloth (śaq) on your loins" — the movement from fine clothing (3:16-24) to sackcloth is the outward sign of mourning, the reversal of the adornments described in 3:18-23.
32.12: ʿal-šādayim sōpĕdîm = "beat your breasts (šādayim)" — the gesture of grief: beating the chest is the universal mourning posture in the ancient Near East (cf. Luke 18:13 where the tax collector beats his breast; Luke 23:48 where crowds beat their breasts after the crucifixion). ʿal śĕdê ḥemdâ ʿal-gepen pōriyyâ = "for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vine" — the mourning is specifically over lost agricultural abundance: the good land destroyed by invasion.
32.13: ʿal ʾadmat ʿammî qôṣ šāmîr taʿăleh = "Over the soil (ʾadmâ) of my people thorns (qôṣ) and briars (šāmîr) shall come up" — the šāmîr wāšît pair (5:6; 7:23-25) signals the curse-reversal of the land; the cultivated soil returns to wilderness. kî ʿal-kol-bāttê māśôś qiryat ʿallîzâ = "over all the joyful houses in the exultant city" — Jerusalem, characterized by festive houses and joyful celebration, will become a desolation of thorns.
32.14: kî-ʾarmon nuṭṭāš hāmôn ʿîr ʿuzZāḇ = "for the palace (ʾarmôn) will be forsaken (nāṭaš), the noisy city (hāmôn ʿîr) abandoned (ʿāzaḇ)" — the architectural symbols of urban civilization — palace and city — emptied. ʿōpel ûḇaḥan hāyû bĕʿad mĕʿārâ ʿad-ʿôlām = "the hill and the watchtower will become dens (mĕʿārâ) forever (ʿad-ʿôlām)" — permanent reversal: the defensive infrastructure becomes animal shelter. śimḥat pĕrāʾîm = "the delight of wild donkeys" — the city that was a delight to humans becomes a pasture for feral animals.
32.15: ʿad-yiqqāreh ʿālênû rûaḥ mimārome = "until the Spirit (rûaḥ) is poured upon us from on high (mimārome)" — the great turning point: the Spirit poured from above. Yiqqāreh (niphal of ʿārâ) = to be poured out. This is one of the great OT Spirit-promise texts: the Spirit poured from heaven transforming the earth (cf. Ezek 36:26-27; 37:14; Joel 2:28-29). wĕhāyâ midbar lakarmel wĕhakarmel layaʿar yeḥāšēḇ = "and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field (karmel) and the fruitful field is regarded as a forest." The outpouring of the Spirit produces the agricultural reversal: desert → garden → forest. Acts 2 (the Spirit poured at Pentecost) is the initial fulfillment; the consummation remains eschatological.
32.16: wĕšākan bammidbar mišpāṭ = "then justice (mišpāṭ) will dwell (šākan) in the wilderness" — šākan = to tabernacle, dwell as a resident; the same root as the Mishkan (tabernacle) and the divine dwelling. Justice tabernacles in what was previously wilderness — the Spirit's transforming work is both ecological and social. ûṣĕdāqâ bakkarmel tēšēḇ = "and righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ) will abide (yāšaḇ) in the fruitful field." Both pillars of the ideal kingdom (v1: ṣedeq/mišpāṭ) are now embedded in the transformed landscape: the Spirit-created ecology is also the Spirit-created social order.
32.17: wĕhāyâ maʿăśēh haṣṣĕdāqâ šālôm = "and the work (maʿăśeh) of righteousness will be peace (šālôm)" — righteousness produces peace as its necessary fruit. waʿăḇōdat haṣṣĕdāqâ hašqēṭ ûḇeṭaḥ ʿad-ʿôlām = "and the service of righteousness will be quietness and trust (beṭaḥ) forever." Hašqēṭ ûḇeṭaḥ echoes 30:15 ("in quietness and trust is your strength") — the faith-rest that Israel refused becomes the permanent condition of the restored community under the Spirit. Peace as the fruit of righteousness, not its precondition.
32.18: wĕyāšaḇ ʿammî bĕnĕwēh šālôm = "and my people will dwell in a peaceful habitation (nĕwēh šālôm)" — nĕwēh = pasture, home, settled dwelling place; the image of the flock in its safe pasture applied to the human community. ûḇĕmiškĕnôt miṭṭāḥôt = "and in secure dwellings (miškĕnôt miṭṭāḥôt)" — miškān (tabernacle-root) = dwelling place; miṭṭāḥ = spreading out, relaxed security. ûḇimnûḥôt šaʾănannôt = "and in quiet resting places" — the three terms stack up into a comprehensive image of shalom: peaceful place, secure dwelling, quiet rest. The "complacent women" (vv9-12) will have true security rather than false security.
32.19: ûbārad bĕredet hāyāʿar = "and hail will fall (bārad) when the forest is brought low" — the judgment on the proud (the forest as symbol of arrogant power, cf. 10:18-19; 29:17) comes through hail, a weapon of theophanic warfare (cf. 30:30). ûšiplâ hāʿîr tišpāl = "and the city (hāʿîr) will be utterly humbled (šāpēl)" — the city of human pride is brought low by divine hail. The verse interrupts the peace-oracle (vv15-18) with a reminder: the shalom follows judgment, not instead of it.
32.20: ʾašrêkem zorĕʿê ʿal-kol-māyim = "Blessed (ʾašrê) are you who sow beside all waters" — the final beatitude of the chapter: those who farm the Spirit-watered land are blessed. mĕšallĕḥê regel-hašŝôr wĕhaḥămôr = "who let the feet of the ox and donkey roam free" — the animals graze freely in the restored land. The chapter that began with the righteous king (v1) ends with the blessed farmer: the kingdom of righteousness produces not only justice in the courts but abundance in the fields. The Spirit poured from above (v15) results in land, livestock, and human flourishing.

41.1: The great courtroom scene opens: coastlands summoned to silence, nations called to renew their strength, and then to come near to speak. The silence first -- no speech from the defendant until the court is formally assembled. The forensic framework (lawsuit, case, arguments) will run through ch 43. The MKT's let them come near and then speak captures the procedural sequence: assemble, then present your case. Together (yachdav) they are to draw near for judgment.
41.2: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged -- he delivers nations before him and treads down kings. The conqueror from the east is Cyrus of Persia, the LORD's anointed instrument (44:28; 45:1), who came from the east and defeated kings with remarkable speed. The MKT renders the kings as the objects of Cyrus's conquest. The key translation question is whether this is primarily historical (Cyrus) or typological (the ultimate Conqueror who defeats death and its principalities) -- the MKT allows both.
41.3: Shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- the MKT renders traveling paths his feet have never trodden before. The Hebrew shalom here means safely/unharmed rather than covenantal peace -- be-shalom means in safety, in wholeness, without being pursued successfully. The conqueror passes through without hindrance, as if the resistance simply melts away. The MKT's safely is the correct sense in this military-transit context.
41.4: YHWH is flagged -- the self-revelation formula: I, the LORD -- I was first, and I am with the last. The Hebrew ani rishon ve-et-acharonim ani-hu is the foundational divine I-am formula of Second Isaiah, claiming to be first and last simultaneously. Rev 1:17 and 22:13 apply this formula to the risen Christ: I am the first and the last. The MKT's rendering is close to the Hebrew word order, preserving the double I (the subject and the predicate) that creates the formula's weight.
41.5: The coastlands saw the conqueror's advance and were afraid -- the ends of the earth trembled. The reaction of fear at divine action is the expected theophanic response. But their fear drives them not to YHWH but to their own mutual encouragement (v6) and idol-making (v7) -- the same fear that should produce repentance instead produces religious panic that generates more idolatry.
41.6: The nations encourage each other -- take courage! The mutual reinforcement of the idol-manufacturing culture: craftsman to goldsmith, craftsman to craftsman. The social dimension of idolatry -- it is communally maintained and mutually reinforced -- is Isaiah's consistent observation. No one idol-maker works alone; the system is collaborative. The encouragement (chazaq: be strong!) is the same word used for YHWH's strengthening of Israel (v10); both the idolater and the LORD use it, to opposite ends.
41.7: The craftsman encourages the goldsmith; the one who smooths with the hammer encourages the one striking the anvil. The manufacturing process is described step by step: the technological competence is real -- the nails are good, the image will not fall -- but the product is nothing. The competent manufacture of nothing is Isaiah's ironic point. The MKT's joins the soldering captures the final stage of metalwork idol-production.
41.8: But you, Israel, my servant, Jacob, whom I have chosen, offspring of Abraham my friend. The contrast after vv6-7 is deliberate: while the nations encourage each other to make nothing, Israel is addressed in terms of covenantal relationship. Three titles: my servant, my chosen one, offspring of Abraham my friend. The word friend (ohavi: the one I love) for Abraham is unique here in the OT -- 2 Chron 20:7 and James 2:23 use it. The covenant history grounds the present promise.
41.9: YHWH's election of Israel from the ends of the earth and calling from the farthest regions. The you are my servant; I have chosen you and not rejected you language is the anti-rejection formula: the fear that YHWH has cast off his people is explicitly countered. The MKT preserves the geographic scope of the calling: from the ends of the earth -- not from somewhere nearby but from the farthest possible distance, showing the reach of divine calling.
41.10: Elohim is flagged -- do not be dismayed, for I am your God. The four divine actions: I will strengthen you (amatzekha), I will help you (azartikha), I will uphold you (tamakhti) with my righteous right hand. The pattern is past-guarantee-for-future: the same God who called you from the ends of the earth will continue to strengthen, help, and uphold. The right hand of righteousness is the hand that acts in covenant faithfulness.
41.11: All who are enraged against Israel will be put to shame and disgraced; those who oppose will come to nothing. The reversal formula: the shaming of the shamers, the nothing-ing of those who tried to destroy. The MKT's will come to nothing renders ke'efes (like nothing/zero): not just defeated but reduced to non-being, using the same word for the nothingness of idols (v24, 29).
41.12: You will seek your adversaries and not find them -- the enemies will simply be absent, not just defeated. The MKT's will be as nothing at all (ke'ayin) is the nothing-formula: total absence rather than merely military defeat. The enemies who once dominated Israel will not be findable -- not because they fled but because they have ceased to exist as a threat.
41.13: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- For I, the LORD your God, hold your right hand; I am the one who says to you, Do not fear; I will help you. The divine hand holding the human right hand is an intimate protection-image: the same posture as a parent holding a child's hand across a dangerous road. The MKT's hold your right hand is literal; the personal address (I am the one who says to you) makes the divine help personal and direct.
41.14: YHWH is flagged -- Do not be afraid, O Jacob, you worm; you few men of Israel! The address you worm (tola'at) is remarkable: Israel is not described as a great nation but as a worm, a creature of weakness and smallness. This is not contemptuous but protective -- the divine help is specifically for the weak. The MKT's you worm preserves the shock of the address. The Redeemer (go'el: kinsman-redeemer) is the LORD -- Ps 22:6 uses the same word for the Messiah's self-description in abandonment.
41.15: The worm-to-threshing-sledge transformation: I will make you a new, sharp threshing sledge with many cutting edges. The transformation is from weakness to instrument -- but the instrument serves YHWH's purposes, not Israel's self-aggrandizement. The mountains threshed and the hills reduced to chaff: the cosmic scope of the renewed Israel's function as YHWH's instrument in the world. The MKT's new, sharp threshing sledge with many cutting edges is literal and vivid.
41.16: YHWH is flagged; ruach (spirit, disp 4) is flagged -- the wind will carry them away and the gale will scatter them; you will rejoice in the LORD. The ruach here is the natural wind/gale that disperses the threshed chaff -- not the divine Spirit but atmospheric wind. The MKT's wind and gale are correct for this physical context. Then the promised response: you will rejoice in the LORD, glory in the Holy One of Israel -- the covenant joy that follows the divine act.
41.17: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- I, the LORD their God, will answer them; I, the God of Israel, will not forsake them. The double divine self-identification (LORD their God / God of Israel) for answering the poor and needy seeking water. The divine answer to thirst is the transformation of the landscape (vv18-19): rivers on bare heights, fountains in valleys, wilderness into pools. The material provision is real, not merely spiritual metaphor.
41.18: Rivers on bare heights, fountains in the middle of valleys, wilderness into pools, dry land into springs of water -- the comprehensive transformation of the arid landscape into a well-watered land. The MKT preserves the environmental specificity: the heights (normally dry), the valleys (normally prone to flooding but here fountains), the wilderness (arid zone), the dry land. Each inhospitable feature becomes hospitable by divine action.
41.19: The seven trees planted in the wilderness and desert: cedar, acacia, myrtle, olive, cypress, plane, and pine. The seven-tree list is a kind of paradise-restoration catalogue -- the most valuable timber and shade trees of the ancient Near East, now planted in the places of desolation. The MKT's precision in tree identification follows the Hebrew list; some identification is uncertain but the cumulative point is clear: where nothing grew, everything grows.
41.20: YHWH is flagged; yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- so that they may see and know, consider and understand together, that the hand of the LORD has done this. The four verbs of apprehension (see, know, consider, understand) all point toward the same recognition: this transformation is the LORD's doing, not natural or human process. The MKT's together (yachdav) preserves the communal dimension of this recognition -- it is not private but shared.
41.21: YHWH and melek (king, disp 2) are flagged -- the courtroom resumes: Present your case, says the LORD; bring forward your strongest arguments, says the King of Jacob. The idols are called to give evidence. The divine title King of Jacob applies the royal title to the covenantal relationship: YHWH is not merely a universal sovereign but specifically Israel's King. The MKT's King of Jacob is literal, consistent with the election-language of vv8-9.
41.22: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- the challenge to the idols: tell us what the former things were, what they mean, so that we may know their outcome. The idol is challenged to prove its knowledge of past events and future events -- the two realms where divine foreknowledge would demonstrate genuine divinity. The MKT's so we can consider them renders the reflective purpose: the knowledge is sought for evaluation, not mere curiosity.
41.23: Elohim is flagged; yada is flagged -- Tell us what is to come in the future, so that we may know you are gods. The test of divinity: predictive knowledge of the future. The MKT renders yeda (know) as know you are gods -- the knowing is recognition of divine status. The invitation to do good or do evil -- do something is the desperation of an unjust judge allowing anything: if the idols cannot predict, can they at least act? The answer is no.
41.24: The verdict: you are nothing and your work amounts to nothing; whoever chooses you is an abomination. The nothing-verdict (efes: zero, nothingness) applied to the idols and their makers' work. Choosing idols is an abomination (to'evah) -- the same word used for the Canaanite practices forbidden in Deuteronomy. The courtroom verdict against idolatry is total: not just ineffective but morally offensive to YHWH.
41.25: I have stirred up one from the north and one from the east -- Cyrus of Persia (from the east/northeast) who will come calling on YHWH's name. The trampling of rulers as on mortar, like a potter treading clay, is the image of the victorious king reducing his enemies to raw material. The MKT preserves the potter-clay image that will be developed in 45:9 and 64:8 for divine sovereignty over creation.
41.26: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- Who declared this from the beginning, so we could know? The repeated rhetorical question exposes the silence of the idols: no one among them declared it, no one foretold it, no one heard their words. The knowledge-through-prediction test has been failed by all the idols and their advocates. The MKT's so we could know preserves the purpose clause that makes the prediction a knowledge-event.
41.27: YHWH's self-claim: I was the first to declare to Zion, look -- here they are! The divine announcement-priority -- the LORD was first to announce the good news to Jerusalem. The messenger of good news (mevasser) is the word that becomes euangelion in Greek -- the gospel herald. This verse anticipates the great herald-announcement of 52:7 and the NT's appropriation of Isaianic herald-language for the gospel proclamation.
41.28: I looked and there was no one -- among all the idols and nations, no counselor, no one who could answer. The silence of the idols in the courtroom is complete: not even a single spokesman. The MKT's when I asked them, not one could answer captures the total absence of divine speech from the idols. The contrast with the LORD who speaks throughout this courtroom scene is the structural argument.
41.29: Ruach (spirit/wind, disp 4) is flagged -- their cast images are just wind and futility. Here ruach means wind/emptiness -- not the divine Spirit but the hollow breath of nothing. The MKT's wind is correct for this pejorative use: the images have no more substance than wind. The three parallel nothings (emptiness, comes to nothing, wind/futility) create a total verdict of non-existence for the idol system.

42.1: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) and ruach (Spirit, disp 4) are flagged -- Here is my servant...my chosen one, in whom I delight; I have placed my Spirit on him. The MKT renders ratseta nafshi as in whom I delight -- using soul as the idiom for the deepest personal pleasure (my soul delights = I fully delight). This is the idiom of deep personal satisfaction, not a clinical description. Matt 3:16-17 and 12:18 quote this verse for Jesus' baptism and healing ministry -- the Spirit descending and the divine voice saying this is my Son in whom I am well pleased draws directly on this verse.
42.2: He will not shout or raise his voice, or make himself heard in the streets. The quietness of the Servant's ministry is the anti-triumphalist claim: the one on whom the Spirit rests does not announce himself through the normal channels of power and publicity. The MKT's make himself heard in the streets captures the Hebrew velo-yashmi in-hachutz qolo: will not cause his voice to be heard outside/in the street. The Servant's work is done without imperial self-promotion.
42.3: Emet (truth/faithfulness, disp 2) is flagged -- he will bring forth justice faithfully. The MKT renders emet as faithfully rather than truly -- capturing the reliability/steadiness dimension of emet rather than the propositional-truth dimension. The bruised reed not broken and the smoldering wick not snuffed: the Servant's tenderness toward the weak and nearly-extinguished. Matt 12:20 quotes this verse for Jesus' non-coercive healing ministry.
42.4: He will not grow faint or be discouraged until he has established justice in the earth. The perseverance of the Servant mirrors the gentleness of v3: the same one who will not break a bruised reed will not quit until the whole earth has been reached with justice. The coastlands will wait for his instruction -- the global scope of the Servant's mission (all the way to the coastlands) is established before the specific commissioning of vv5-7.
42.5: YHWH and ruach (spirit, disp 4) are flagged -- God the LORD (ha-El YHWH) who created the heavens, stretched them out, spread out the earth, who gives neshamah (breath) to the people on it and ruach (spirit) to those who walk on it. The MKT distinguishes neshamah (breath, the animating breath of life, Gen 2:7) from ruach (the vitalizing spirit that animates walking creatures). Both together describe the comprehensive life-giving of the Creator God who is speaking to the Servant.
42.6: YHWH and berit (covenant, disp 3) are flagged -- I, the LORD, have called you in righteousness...I will make you a covenant for the people. The Servant as berit-am (covenant of/for the people) -- the Servant embodies the covenant rather than merely receiving it. The MKT's a covenant for the people is the correct translation: the Servant is the instrument through which the covenant relationship is established with the people. The pairing with a light for the nations expands the scope to universal. Heb 8:6-13 develops the new covenant the Servant mediates.
42.7: The Servant's mission: open blind eyes, bring prisoners from the dungeon, release those in darkness. The three acts of liberation -- sight, freedom, and light -- become the vocabulary of Jesus' mission statement (Luke 4:18-19 citing Isa 61:1-2, but the same liberatory cluster). The MKT's dungeon and prison are parallel -- different words for the same confinement. The Servant's mission is physical and spiritual liberation.
42.8: YHWH and kavod (glory, disp 2) are flagged -- I am the LORD; that is my name; I will not give my glory to another nor my praise to carved idols. The divine-name assertion and the refusal to share glory is one of the most theologically dense statements in Isaiah. The divine name YHWH is what makes this refusal of shared glory non-arbitrary: the covenant name defines a unique divine identity that cannot be distributed to other claimants. The glory (kavod) is the weight/honor that belongs to the one true God. The NT applies divine glory to Christ (John 17:5) without contradiction, since Christ shares the divine name.
42.9: The former things have come to pass -- YHWH's track record of fulfilled prediction. New things are now declared, announced before they spring up. The pattern of prediction-then-fulfillment is YHWH's way of demonstrating divine reliability and distinction from idols (who could predict nothing, ch 41). The MKT's before they spring up I announce them captures the temporal precedence of divine announcement over historical event.
42.10: YHWH is flagged -- Sing to the LORD a new song. The new song (shir chadash) called for by the new things: as YHWH does a new thing, the appropriate response is a new song, not the recycling of old thanksgiving forms. The scope of the singing -- from sea-travelers to desert-towns (Kedar) to mountain-tops (Sela) -- is universal. The MKT's ends of the earth matches the scope of the Servant's mission (v4: the coastlands).
42.11: Kedar (Arabian) and Sela (Edomite) raise their voices -- the non-Israelite peoples of the region joining the new song. The geographical specificity is important: these are not abstractions but named peoples with their own histories and territories, invited into the universal praise. The MKT's villages where Kedar dwells and inhabitants of Sela preserve the historical particularity.
42.12: YHWH and kavod (glory, disp 2) are flagged -- let them ascribe glory to the LORD and proclaim his praise in the coastlands. The ascription of glory to the LORD rather than to Cyrus (the human instrument) or to the nations themselves is the corrective to the imperial tendency to self-glorify. The coastlands who were terrified (41:5) are now called to praise -- fear transformed into worship.
42.13: YHWH is flagged -- The LORD marches out like a warrior, like a man of war he arouses his zeal. The divine warrior image: YHWH as a warrior who cries out, shouts, and triumphs over his enemies. This is the same image as Exod 15:3 (the LORD is a warrior) developed in a new context. The zeal (qinah: jealous burning passion) aroused in YHWH is the covenantal-love dimension of divine warfare: he fights for his people because he loves them jealously.
42.14: Olam (ancient time, disp 3) is flagged -- for a long time I have kept silent; I have been still and held myself back. The MKT renders me-olam as for a long time -- the age-long duration rather than absolute eternity. The period of divine silence during exile (olam) is the duration of the people's suffering. Now I will cry out like a woman in labor -- the breaking of divine silence with the urgency and pain of childbirth. The woman-in-labor image for YHWH's speaking is remarkable and maternal.
42.15: I will lay waste mountains and hills, dry up vegetation, turn rivers to dry land -- the scale of divine action is creation-wide. But this destructive action is in service of the restoration of vv16-17: the clearing away of obstacles to enable the blind to be led. The environmental disruption of v15 creates the leveled path of v16. The MKT preserves the active divine verbs (I will lay waste, I will dry up, I will turn) throughout.
42.16: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged twice -- I will lead the blind along a road they have not known; I will guide them on paths they have never walked. I will turn the darkness into light before them and the rough ground into a smooth place. The double yada (never known / never walked) emphasizes total unfamiliarity -- the road is completely new, not a familiar path with better lighting. The MKT's never known and never walked are both from the same root, showing the consistency of the Hebrew's emphasis on newness.
42.17: Elohim is flagged -- those who trust in carved idols and say to cast images, You are our gods. The word Elohim in the mouths of the idol-worshippers: they call their images elohim, the plural word for God. The divine name itself is transferred to false objects. Those who do this will be turned back and completely put to shame (bosh yevoshu: double shame-verb) -- the intensity of the shame matches the intensity of the misidentification.
42.18: The summons to the deaf and blind to hear and see. The deaf and blind of v18 are specifically Israel (as v19 clarifies) -- not the nations' idolaters but the covenant people who have eyes but don't see, ears but don't hear. The command to the blind to look and see is the same paradox as Ezek 12:2: a people who have the capacity for perception but have not used it. The MKT's imperative forms (Hear! Look! See!) match the Hebrew's urgency.
42.19: YHWH is flagged -- the paradox: Who is blind but my servant, or deaf like my messenger whom I send? Israel is YHWH's servant and messenger -- but the servant is blind, the messenger is deaf. This is the sharpest critique in the chapter: the one commissioned to carry divine sight and hearing to others cannot see or hear. The MKT's my dedicated one (meshullam: the one at peace, completed) maintains the irony of the sealed/complete one being blind.
42.20: He sees many things but does not take them in; his ears are open but he does not hear. The distinction between seeing and perceiving, hearing and registering -- the physical faculties are present but the responsive capacity is closed. This failure of perception is what the Servant of 42:1-9 will cure in others (vv6-7) but which describes the corporate servant Israel's own condition. The MKT's does not take them in is a good rendering of lo yishmor: does not keep/guard what is seen.
42.21: YHWH is flagged -- it pleased the LORD, for the sake of his righteousness, to make his law great and glorious. The magnification of the Torah is YHWH's own pleasure and his righteous purpose. The glorious (addir: magnificent, majestic) Torah is the object of the LORD's pleasure. This contrasts with the people's failure to hear (v20): the Torah is magnificent, but the servant Israel has not listened to it. The law is not the problem; the hearing is.
42.22: The consequence of not hearing: Israel robbed and plundered, trapped in holes, hidden in dungeons, taken as prey, with no rescuer. The prison imagery reverses v7 (bringing prisoners out of dungeons) -- Israel is now in the very dungeon the Servant would release them from. The ones who should carry liberation are themselves imprisoned. The MKT's trapped in holes and hidden in dungeons preserves the double imprisonment-imagery.
42.23: The rhetorical summons to attention: Who among you will pay attention to this? Who will listen and hear for what is to come? The call to hear (v18) now becomes a question about whether anyone will. The hearing for what is to come means hearing with prospective orientation -- not just registering past events but being formed by divine word to face the future. The MKT's pay attention renders yashev: who will sit/give ear to this?
42.24: YHWH is flagged -- Who handed Jacob over to plunderers...Was it not the LORD, against whom we have sinned? The theodicy question answered: the LORD handed Israel over. But the reason given is not arbitrary -- it was because they sinned, refused to walk in his ways, and did not obey his law. The acknowledgment of divine agency in the exile (the LORD handed over) is paired with the acknowledgment of Israel's responsibility (against whom we have sinned). Both are necessary for the repentance the text calls for.
42.25: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- it set him ablaze all around, but he did not understand; it burned against him, but he did not take it to heart. The yada here is rendered understand: Israel did not know/understand the divine message encoded in the suffering of the burning anger and the battle poured out. The failure to learn from judgment is the same failure as the failure to learn from blessing (vv18-20): both forms of divine communication are unheard.

43.1: YHWH is flagged -- But now, this is what the LORD says -- he who created you, Jacob, and formed you, Israel: Do not be afraid, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name; you are mine. The but now (ve-attah) after the judgment of 42:24-25 is the hinge of grace. Three foundational acts: created, formed, redeemed. The personal claiming -- I have called you by name; you are mine -- is the covenant-ownership declaration. John 10:3 (the shepherd calls his own sheep by name) draws on this ownership-by-naming language.
43.2: When you pass through the waters, I will be with you -- the promise of divine presence through danger, not the promise of danger-free passage. The waters will not overwhelm, the rivers will not sweep away, the fire will not burn -- but the passing-through is real. The MKT's when (not if) is correct: these are the conditions of life in a fallen world, and the promise is company, not immunity. The water/fire pair creates a merism: through every kind of danger.
43.3: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior. I give Egypt as your ransom, Ethiopia and Seba in your place. The three divine titles (LORD your God, Holy One of Israel, your Savior) cluster around the ransom-declaration. The nations given as ransom for Israel is a stunning claim: the divine economy values Israel above the great nations. The ransom language is picked up by Jesus (Mark 10:45: the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many).
43.4: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) is flagged -- I will give people in exchange for you and nations in exchange for your life (nafshekha). The nefesh here is Israel's life/being -- YHWH gives nations as the equivalent value. The logic: because you are precious and honored in my eyes and because I love you -- the triple ground of divine action is valuation, honor, and love. The MKT renders nafshekha as your life, correctly -- this is the vital-self, not merely the soul as inner spiritual component.
43.5: Do not be afraid, for I am with you. The same promise as 41:10 now frames the gathering from all four compass directions: east, west, north, south. The geographical comprehensiveness of the gathering mirrors the comprehensiveness of the diaspora -- wherever the exiles were scattered, the LORD will bring them back. The MKT's from the east...from the west and the corresponding north/south commands follow the Hebrew's deliberate exhaustiveness.
43.6: The command to the four directions to release and return the exiles: Give them back! Do not hold them back. The divine imperative directed at the compass points treats the whole created order as subject to YHWH's commands. My sons from far away, my daughters from the ends of the earth -- the parental address for the returning community. The MKT's from far away and from the ends of the earth create the widest possible scope.
43.7: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged -- everyone who bears my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made. Three creation-verbs (created, formed, made) building on the foundational claim of v1. The creation for glory (likhvodi) makes human existence teleologically oriented toward divine glory -- as Paul develops in 1 Cor 6:20 (glorify God in your body). The MKT's created for my glory preserves this teleological dimension.
43.8: The paradoxical command: bring out the people who are blind -- though they have eyes -- and those who are deaf -- though they have ears. The theological blindness and deafness of Israel is the topic of the legal proceeding that follows: Israel is called as witness but is impaired as a witness because of its own spiritual incapacity. The though they have eyes / ears parentheses are the MKT's rendering of the Hebrew's concessive clause structure.
43.9: Emet (truth, disp 2) is flagged -- who among them declared this, or foretold us what is true? The nations are gathered for the lawsuit; they are challenged to produce witnesses who can declare the former things and announce what is true. Emet as the truth of predictive speech: a true prediction is one that comes to pass. The legal-epistemological framework treats truth as verifiable by historical fulfillment.
43.10: Aman (believe/trust, disp 2) and yada (know, disp 2) are flagged; YHWH is flagged -- You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, along with my servant whom I have chosen -- so that you may know and believe me and understand that I am he. The triple cognitive-response (know, believe/trust, understand) is the goal of the witness-vocation. Aman here means believe/trust -- the covenantal faith-response that follows knowing. The declaration I am he (ani-hu) is the divine identity formula of Second Isaiah, applied in John 8:24,28,58 to Christ.
43.11: YHWH is flagged -- I, I am the LORD, and apart from me there is no savior. The emphatic double first-person (I, I am) intensifies the divine self-identification. The exclusivity claim -- no savior apart from YHWH -- is the theological conclusion of the preceding witness-structure. Peter quotes this exclusivity language for Christ (Acts 4:12: no other name under heaven...by which we must be saved), applying the Isaianic uniqueness-of-YHWH formula to the risen Jesus.
43.12: YHWH is flagged -- I declared, I saved, and I proclaimed. The three divine past-actions that constitute the evidence for the claim of v11. Then: You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, and I am God. The witness-declaration closes the section: Israel has witnessed the declaring, saving, and proclaiming; therefore Israel is qualified to testify. The MKT's and I am God is a rendering of ve-ani El (and I am God/El) -- the generic term for deity rather than the personal name, emphasizing the theological claim.
43.13: Even from eternity I am he. The mi-yom (from the day / from ever) anticipates the eternal self-identification formula. No one can deliver from my hand. When I act, who can reverse it? The irreversibility of divine action is the guarantor of the salvation promises: what YHWH saves cannot be unsaved by any other power. The MKT's who can reverse it renders yeshivenah: who can turn it back?
43.14: YHWH is flagged -- your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel. The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) title applied to YHWH: the one who has the family obligation to redeem is the LORD himself. The redemption is specifically from Babylon -- YHWH sends against Babylon and brings the Chaldeans down as fugitives. The Redeemer-from-Babylon is the new Exodus figure: as YHWH redeemed from Egypt, so from Babylon.
43.15: YHWH and melek (king, disp 2) are flagged -- I am the LORD, your Holy One, Israel's Creator, your King. The four divine titles in sequence: LORD (covenant name), Holy One (transcendence), Creator (cosmological sovereignty), King (political/covenantal authority). The MKT renders all four, with your King as the closing title -- making YHWH's kingship over Israel personal and direct. This is the answer to the foreign kings of ch 36 who claimed sovereignty.
43.16: YHWH is flagged -- This is what the LORD says -- he who makes a way through the sea and a path through the mighty waters. The Exodus-defining act (splitting the Red Sea) is cited as the credential for what follows. The divine track record of sea-path making is the guarantee of the new path through the wilderness (v19). The MKT's mighty waters preserves the Hebrew mayim azim: strong/powerful waters.
43.17: The Exodus reversal: chariot and horse, army and warrior brought out -- then they lay down, never to rise. The Egyptian army's drowning (Exod 14:27-28) is recalled as the paradigm case of YHWH's military action on Israel's behalf. The MKT's lay down and could not rise; they were extinguished, snuffed out like a wick uses the same wick-imagery as 42:3 (the smoldering wick the Servant will not snuff out) -- but reversed for the enemies: they are snuffed out.
43.18: Do not remember the former things, or consider the events of long ago. The command to forget the Exodus is startling -- Isaiah is consistently Exodus-minded. But the point is: the new thing about to be done (v19) will so surpass the Exodus that clinging to the former thing as the supreme category of divine action would prevent recognizing the greater thing. The MKT's events of long ago renders qadmoniot: ancient/former things.
43.19: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- Do you not see it? The new thing: the LORD is doing a new thing even now, springing up. The way through the wilderness and rivers in the desert are the material shape of the new Exodus. Do you not know/perceive it? -- the yada is the same perception-knowing as throughout the chapter. The new thing is already beginning and the question is whether anyone is perceiving it. The MKT's do you not see it renders the rhetorical invitation to perceive the divine action already in progress.
43.20: Wild animals will honor YHWH -- jackals and ostriches -- because the LORD provides water in the wilderness and rivers in the desert for the chosen people to drink. The wild animals' response is the creation-praise that C.S. Lewis develops in The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe as Narnia responding to Aslan. Even the inhospitable creatures of desolation acknowledge the water-giving LORD. The MKT preserves the specificity: jackals and ostriches, both desert-haunt animals (cf. 34:13-15).
43.21: The people I formed for myself, that they might declare my praise. The teleological statement: Israel was formed for the purpose of divine praise-declaration. This is the positive counterpart to the failure of vv22-24: the purpose is praise, the reality is silence and unfaithfulness. The MKT's formed for myself renders yatzarti-li: formed for my own sake -- the creation is purposive, not incidental.
43.22: Yet, Jacob, you have not called on me; you have grown weary of me, O Israel. The contrast with v21 is sharp: formed to declare praise but not calling on the LORD. The weariness (yagata) is the opposite of the renewal-of-strength promised for those who wait on the LORD (40:31 uses the same root). The covenant people are weary of the covenant God -- the deep failure of the relationship.
43.23: You have not brought sheep for burnt offerings -- the ritual accusations: the sacrificial system has been neglected, but not because YHWH was demanding too much (v23b: I have not burdened you with grain offerings or wearied you with frankincense). The problem is not the burden of the requirements but the people's failure to fulfill them. The MKT's not honored me renders lo kabadtani: not glorified/honored me -- the kavod that belongs to the LORD has not been given.
43.24: You have not bought me fragrant calamus with money, nor satisfied me with the fat of your sacrifices. Instead you have burdened me with your sins and wearied me with your iniquities. The reversal: the people have not offered their required gifts, but they have given YHWH their sins as an unwanted gift. The MKT's burdened me and wearied me (heevadtani...helo'tani) use the same language as the acceptable worship vocabulary -- inverted. The worship-absence has been filled with sin-presence.
43.25: YHWH is flagged -- I, I am the one who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins. The emphatic double first-person (anoki anoki hu) makes the forgiveness an act of divine self-motivation: the LORD blots out sins for my own sake (lema'ani), not because Israel deserves it or has offered adequate atonement. The formula I will not remember your sins is the basis of the new covenant in Jer 31:34 and Heb 10:17. The MKT's rendering preserves the emphatic self-referentiality of the forgiveness.
43.26: Shaphat (contend, disp 2) is flagged -- Let us argue the matter together. Present your case, that you may be proved innocent. The courtroom resumes after the accusation of vv22-24 and the forgiveness of v25: can you prove your innocence? The MKT's argue the matter together renders nishpatah yachdav: let us contend/judge together. The invitation to argue your case is a form of grace -- the accused is given the opportunity to speak.
43.27: Your first ancestor sinned -- either Adam (the universal ancestor) or Abraham/Jacob (the specific covenant ancestor). The intermediaries (melakim: interpreters or go-betweens, possibly the prophets or priests who should have mediated covenant faithfulness) have rebelled. The long history of covenant failure extends from the first ancestor to every intermediary -- the people cannot claim their case has been defended.
43.28: Qodesh (holiness, disp 2) is flagged -- I allowed the sanctuary's leaders to be defiled, and I gave Jacob over to destruction and Israel to scorn. The holy princes (sarei qodesh) -- the priestly/levitical leaders -- defiled and the people given over to destruction. The qodesh applied to the leaders who were supposed to embody holiness but instead led to defilement. The MKT's sanctuary's leaders renders sarei qodesh: the holy/set-apart ones who lead. The chapter ends at the point of judgment -- the restoration promise of 44:1ff will reverse it.

45.1: lĕmāšîaḥô lĕkôreš — to his anointed, to Cyrus. The term māšîaḥ (מָשִׁיחַ, anointed) is applied to a foreign king — a uniquely bold claim. YHWH grasps Cyrus's right hand (ḥāzaqtî bîmînô), the posture of divine empowerment. The verse opens with the oracular messenger formula angled toward a pagan ruler, asserting that YHWH is the hidden sovereign behind Persian conquest.
45.2: wāhădûrîm 'ăyaššēr — and the rugged places I will make level. The Pi'el of יָשַׁר (yeyyōšēr) denotes active straightening. Doors of bronze (daltôt nĕḥušet) and bars of iron (bĕrîḥê barzèl) are broken — Herodotus records Cyrus entering Babylon via the Euphrates channel under bronze gates. The verse uses *yiqtol* verbs throughout, framing YHWH's actions as imminent and certain.
45.3: maṭmônê ḥōšek — treasures of darkness and hidden riches of secret places. These are material spoils granted so Cyrus may know (tēda') the personal name YHWH. The clause qōrĕ'akā bišmekā — who calls you by your name — signals intimate foreknowledge; Cyrus is named prophetically before his birth.
45.4: lĕma'an 'abdî ya'ăqōb — for the sake of my servant Jacob. The cosmic military campaign of Cyrus has a theological purpose: Israel's redemption. The phrase wĕ'epĕsinnĕkā — though you have not known me — makes divine sovereignty explicit: YHWH acts through unknowing instruments. The verb ekannĕkā (Pi'el of כָּנָה) — I surname/title you — indicates honorific designation.
45.5: 'ănî YHWH wĕ'ên 'ôd — I am YHWH and there is none else. The foundational monotheistic formula ('ên 'ôd) recurs through vv. 5–6, 14, 18, 21–22. Even where Cyrus does not acknowledge YHWH, YHWH girds him — the verb wa'a'azzĕrĕkā (Pi'el of אָזַר, to gird/equip) — underscoring sovereignty over those outside covenant.
45.6: lĕma'an yēdĕ'û — so that they may know. The universal scope (mimmizraḥ šemeš wûmimma'ărabô, from east to west) is stated: all nations are to recognize YHWH's uniqueness. The infinitive of purpose drives the oracle — revelation of divine identity is the telos of historical events.
45.7: yôṣēr 'ôr ûbôrē' ḥōšek — forming light and creating darkness. Two Qal participles (yôṣēr from יָצַר; bôrē' from בָּרָא) assert continuous creative activity. The climax is the theologically sharp bôrē' rā' — creating evil/disaster. This is not moral evil but calamity (rā' as adversity) — asserting YHWH's sovereignty over all reality, countering Persian dualism (Ahura Mazda vs. Angra Mainyu).
45.8: har'îpû šāmayim — drip/rain down, O heavens. The verb rā'ap̄ (Hiphil) means to let fall in drops; heavens and earth are called to release righteousness (ṣedeq) and salvation (yešû'āh). The imagery is agricultural (rain bringing forth crops) applied to cosmic justice: salvation sprouts from the earth (tiṣmaḥ).
45.9: hôy rāb̠ 'et-yōṣrô — woe to one who strives with his Maker. The hôy oracle (woe cry) opens a lawsuit metaphor. ḥèreś (potsherd) among the potsherds of the ground — the creature is earthenware arguing with the potter. The rhetorical questions (hăyō'mar ḥōmer lĕyōṣĕrô) make the absurdity concrete. The Qal imperfect yārib̠ — to contend, bring legal suit — frames the creature's complaint as legal overreach.
45.10: hôy 'ōmēr lĕ'āb̠ — woe to one who says to a father, 'What are you begetting?'. The paired woe oracle extends the analogy from potter/clay to parent/child. Both challenge the prerogative of the creator/parent. The point is that creatures cannot interrogate the purposes of their origin.
45.11: šā'ălûnî — ask me of things to come. The form is a Qal imperative plural with suffix — YHWH invites interrogation of his purposes regarding his children and the works of his hands (pō'al yādāy). This is not sarcasm but genuine openness: YHWH will explain — through his word to prophets — what he is doing.
45.12: 'ănōkî 'āśîtî 'èreṣ — I made the earth and created man upon it. Creation credentials undergird the political claims of vv. 1–7. The series of *qatal* perfects ('āśîtî, bārā'tî, nāṭîtî, ṣiwwêtî) — made, created, stretched, commanded — establishes unassailable authority to commission Cyrus.
45.13: hā'îrtî bĕ-ṣedeq — I have stirred him up in righteousness. The verb 'ûr (Hiphil, hā'îrtî) means to arouse/awaken — Cyrus is YHWH's awakened instrument. The purpose is double: to build the city (yibĕneh 'îrî) and release the exiles (gālûtî yĕšallēaḥ). The clause lō' bĕmḥîr wĕlō' bĕšōḥad — not for price, not for bribe — emphasizes sheer sovereign grace.
45.14: yĕgî'a mĭṣrayim — the wealth of Egypt. Three nations — Egypt, Cush, Sabeans — will transfer their wealth and come in chains (bazzîqqîm) and bow, confessing 'ak bĕkā 'ēl wĕ'ên 'ôd — Surely God is in you, and there is no other. The chains here are voluntary submission, not conquest. This is the reversal of exodus: former oppressor nations becoming worshippers.
45.15: 'ēl mistattēr — a God who hides himself. The Hithpa'el participle of סָתַר — who conceals himself — describes YHWH's mysterious self-veiling in history, even as he is confessed as 'ĕlōhê yiśrā'ēl môšîa' — God of Israel, Savior. The tension between hiddenness and saving action is a motif throughout Isaiah.
45.16: bōšû wĕgam-niklĕmû — they are put to shame and also confounded. The idol-makers (ḥārāšê ṣîrîm, craftsmen of idols) face corporate humiliation. The Pi'el yēlĕkû — they go off together — in shame. The contrast with v. 17 (Israel saved with eternal salvation) is rhetorically sharp.
45.17: niôša' yiśrā'ēl — Israel is saved by YHWH with an everlasting salvation. The Niphal of יָשַׁע — passive, Israel as recipient of salvation. The modifier tĕšû'at 'ôlāmîm — everlasting salvation — is unique in Isaiah; temporal qualifiers emphasize the finality and permanence of what YHWH accomplishes.
45.18: lō'-tōhû bĕrā'āh — not in chaos did he form it. YHWH created earth to be inhabited (lāšèbet yĕṣārāh) — yāšab̠ (to dwell) is the telos of creation. The use of tōhû (formless void, cf. Gen 1:2) asserts that chaos was not the intended state. Monotheistic purpose underlies all creation. The repetition of 'ănî YHWH wĕ'ên 'ôd anchors the theology.
45.19: lō'-bassēter dibbartî — I have not spoken in secret, in a land of darkness. YHWH's word is public, given in the open and declared in advance — contrasting with the oracular obscurities of pagan divination. The verb 'āmartî — I said — and dibbartî — I spoke — are *qatal* perfects of completed, dependable revelation.
45.20: hiqqābĕṣû ûbō'û — assemble yourselves and come. The call is universal: survivors of the nations (pĕlîṭê haggôyim). The irony — those who pray to a wooden god (wĕmitpallĕlîm 'el-'ēl lō' yôšîa') have no knowledge — sets up the contrast with YHWH who saves. The verb yôšîa' (Hiphil of ישׁע) — cannot save — deliberately echoes môšîa' of v. 15.
45.21: haggîdû wĕhaggîšû — declare and present your case. Legal language: YHWH challenges the nations to produce counsel (yā'ṣû) that predicted events. The rhetorical question ûmî hiš-mî'a — who made this heard? — anticipates the answer: YHWH alone. The divine title 'ēl ṣaddîq ûmôšîa' — righteous God and Savior — combines covenantal justice with redemptive action.
45.22: pĕnû 'ēlay wĕhiwwāšĕ'û — Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth!. The Qal imperative pĕnû (turn) and Niphal imperative hiwwāšĕ'û (be saved) form a paired command: turning and salvation are simultaneous. The universal scope (kol-'apĕsê 'āreṣ) is maximally inclusive. The theological ground: 'ănî 'ēl wĕ'ên 'ôd.
45.23: bî niš-ba'tî — By myself I have sworn. Divine oath formula (cf. Gen 22:16). The word that has gone from YHWH's mouth (yāṣā' mippî ṣĕdāqāh) will not return empty. The prediction: kol bèrek tikra' — every knee shall bow — and kol lāšôn tiššābaʿ — every tongue shall swear. The *yiqtol* verbs convey inevitable future reality. NT: Phil 2:10–11; Rom 14:11 cite this verse for Christ's universal lordship.
45.24: 'ak bYHWH lî 'āmar — Only in YHWH are righteousness and strength. The particle 'ak (only, surely) introduces the exclusive claim. The phrase 'ēlāyw yāb̠ô' wĕyēb̠ōšû — all who were angry at him will come in shame (yēb̠ōšû, Niphal of בּוֹשׁ) — reversal of the shaming of Israel's enemies.
45.25: bĕYHWH yiṣdĕqû — in YHWH all the offspring of Israel will be declared righteous. The Qal *yiqtol* yiṣdĕqû — to be in right standing, to be justified — closes the chapter on the widest possible note: all the seed of Israel vindicated and glorying (yit-hallālû, Hithpa'el of הָלַל). This is the eschatological endpoint of the Cyrus oracle: political liberation serves theological vindication.

37.1: wayyiqrāʿ ʾet-bigdāyw — "he tore his garments" — the standard mourning rite. wayyitkas bassāq — "covered himself with sackcloth" (śaq, H8242, coarse cloth worn in grief and penitence). wayyābōʾ bêt Yhwh — "went into the house of YHWH" — Hezekiah's response to the Assyrian threat is first liturgical: to the temple, not the war council. The pairing of mourning ritual and temple approach models the appropriate response to existential crisis in the Isaianic framework.
37.2: Hezekiah sends ʾelyāqîm (household administrator), šebnâ (secretary), and the senior priests (ziqnê hakkohanîm) — a delegation of his court's highest officials — to Isaiah. The phrase mekussîm bassāq — "clothed in sackcloth" — indicates the officials also come in mourning dress. The symbolism of sending the sackcloth-wearing court to the prophet signals dependence on YHWH's word rather than military counsel.
37.3: yôm ṣārâ wĕtôkēḥâ ûnĕʾāṣâ — "a day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace" — three nouns stacking the severity of the moment. The crucial idiom: bāʾû bānîm ʿad-mašbēr wĕkōaḥ ʾayin lĕlēdâ — "children have come to the point of birth but there is no strength to deliver" — a medical metaphor for crisis at the point of maximum tension with no capacity to resolve it. The labor-without-delivery image is a Hebrew idiom for futile anguish.
37.4: yišmaʿ Yhwh ʾĕlōheykā — "perhaps YHWH your God will hear" — the tentative hope expressed through the envoys. The key phrase: bĕʿad šĕʾērit hannimṣāʾâ — "on behalf of the remnant that is still found" — the šĕʾērît (remnant) motif that is central to Isaiah's theology from ch. 7 onward. Hezekiah's appeal is not for himself but for the surviving remnant — the theological category that anchors YHWH's covenant faithfulness.
37.5: Isaiah's initial response comes quickly — the servants arrive, and he immediately gives a word (wayyōmer ʾălêhem Yĕšaʿyāhû). This contrasts with other prophets who require time or ritual preparation. The quickness of Isaiah's response reflects his continuous engagement with YHWH's word — he is already prepared to speak.
37.6: ʾal-tîrāʾ — "do not be afraid" — the standard oracle of salvation formula (cf. 35:4; 41:10; 43:1). middĕbrê hannaʿărîm — "from the words of the young men/servants" — YHWH characterizes the Assyrian blasphemy as coming from insignificant underlings. The divine perspective reframes the crisis: what appeared as an overwhelming imperial army is dismissed as the bragging of naʿărîm (young men, attendants).
37.7: hinnî nōtēn bô rûaḥ — "behold, I am putting a spirit in him" — the divine intervention will be internal to Sennacherib, not external military defeat alone. The spirit (rûaḥ) that YHWH puts in him will cause him to hear a rumor (šĕmûʿâ) and return to his land, where he will fall by the sword. The prophetic word is unconditional and specific — both the how (rumor) and the where (his own land) are named.
37.8: Sennacherib's field commander returns to find the king fighting against Libnah (Libnâ) — a Shephelah city — having already withdrawn from Lachish. The military narrative confirms the geopolitical instability that will make the king vulnerable to rumors and distraction. Isaiah's prophetic word (v.7) is already beginning to unfold through natural events.
37.9: wayyišmaʿ ʿal-Tîrhāqâ melek-Kûš — "he heard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush" — the Ethiopian pharaoh of the 25th Dynasty who was advancing against Sennacherib. This is the "rumor" of v.7. The military intelligence about Tirhakah forces Sennacherib to send a letter to Hezekiah rather than attacking personally — the shift from army to letter marks a psychological retreat disguised as threat.
37.10: The letter's argument: ʾal-yaššîʾakā ʾĕlōheykā — "do not let your God deceive you" — a theological attack on Hezekiah's trust in YHWH. The claim is that Sennacherib's pattern of victories proves the futility of any divine protection. The gods of the nations have consistently failed; YHWH will be no different. This is the blasphemy that triggers Hezekiah's temple prayer.
37.11: The catalogue of Assyrian conquests: the Assyrian king addresses Hezekiah with direct reference to what Assyrian kings (malkê ʾaššûr) did to all lands — hōḥărimûm, "devoted to destruction/utterly destroyed" (ḥāram, H2763, the verb of total destruction/holy war). The claim is that herem (total destruction) has been applied universally and YHWH's Jerusalem will be no exception.
37.12: The defeated nations named: Gozan (Gôzān), Haran (Ḥārān), Rezeph (Reṣep), and the people of Eden in Telassar (Ṭelʾaśśar) — all Aramean/Mesopotamian kingdoms already absorbed by Assyria. The list is designed to overwhelm with precedent. The rhetorical question mî hên ʾăšer hiṣṣîlûm ("where are the gods who delivered them?") challenges Hezekiah to produce a single counter-example.
37.13: The catalogue continues with Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah — all cities and regions conquered. The roll-call of defeated gods and nations functions as a liturgy of despair designed to produce surrender. What makes Hezekiah's prayer (vv.15-20) so powerful is that it directly engages this theology of comparative divine power.
37.14: wayyiqqaḥ Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾet-hassĕpārîm — "Hezekiah took the letters" (sĕpārîm, plural — perhaps multiple documents or a letter and accompanying threats). wayyipprōśēm lipnê Yhwh — "spread them out before YHWH." The physical act of spreading the letter in the temple is unprecedented — Hezekiah literally brings the human document into the divine court, making YHWH the judge of Sennacherib's claims. This is prayer as legal action.
37.15: The prayer begins with the address formula: Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yōšēb hakĕrûḇîm — "YHWH of hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim" — the full cultic title. ʾattâ hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm lĕḇaddĕkā lĕkol mamlĕkôt hāʾāreṣ — "you alone are God over all kingdoms of the earth." The prayer begins with the claim Sennacherib denied — YHWH's universal sovereignty — stated as direct address to YHWH himself.
37.16: yōšēb hakĕrûḇîm — "enthroned above the cherubim" — the technical formula for YHWH's presence above the ark (1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; Ps 80:1). The kĕrûḇîm are the heavenly attendants whose wings form YHWH's throne; they are not the storm/war-deity cherubim of Babylonian iconography but the covenant guardians of YHWH's holiness. Hezekiah appeals to YHWH as the transcendent creator who made heaven and earth (ʿāśîtā ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ).
37.17: haṭṭēh Yhwh ʾoznĕkā ûšmaʿ — "incline your ear and hear" — the petitionary formula characteristic of royal psalms (Ps 17:6; 31:2; 86:1). pĕqaḥ Yhwh ʿêneykā ûrʾēh — "open your eyes and see" — the request for divine attention activates the anthropomorphic divine perception imagery. dibĕrê Sannĕḥērîḇ ʾăšer šālaḥ lĕḥārēp ʾĕlōhîm ḥāy — "the words of Sennacherib who sent to taunt the living God" — ḥāy (living) is the decisive contrast with the dead idols of the nations.
37.18: ʾākēn Yhwh — "it is true, YHWH" — Hezekiah acknowledges the historical facts Sennacherib cited. hḥărîbû malkê ʾaššûr — "the Assyrian kings have laid waste all the lands" — the prayer is not naive. Hezekiah admits the military reality before making the theological argument. This intellectual honesty in prayer is itself a form of faith — praying with eyes open to the real situation.
37.19: wĕnātĕnû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem bāʾēš — "they threw their gods into the fire and destroyed them" — Hezekiah now makes the theological argument: those gods were not gods (kî lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm hēmmâ) but the work of human hands (maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām), wood and stone (ʿēṣ wāʾeben). They could be burned because they were merely material objects. This is the Isaianic idol-polemic at its sharpest (cf. 44:9-20).
37.20: wĕʿattâ Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû hôšîʿēnû miyyādô — "and now, O YHWH our God, save us from his hand" — the petition itself is brief after the long theological setup. The purpose clause is striking: lĕmaʿan yĕdĕʿû kol-mamlĕkôt hāʾāreṣ kî ʾattâ Yhwh lĕḇaddekā — "so that all kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone are YHWH." Salvation is requested not merely for Israel's sake but for YHWH's universal glory — a missionary motivation embedded in a prayer of crisis.
37.21: Isaiah sends Hezekiah a word — šālaḥ Yĕšaʿyāhû ben-ʾĀmôṣ — confirming that his prayer was heard (yaʿan ʾăšer hitpallaltā, "because you prayed"). The word of YHWH is addressed specifically against (ʿālāyw) Sennacherib. The prayer's success is explicitly linked to its content — Hezekiah prayed, therefore YHWH speaks. The connection between prayer and divine speech is direct and immediate.
37.22: bĕtûlat bat-ṣiyyôn — "the virgin daughter of Zion" — the personification of Jerusalem as a young woman. lāzâ lēz bāz lēz ʾaḥărêkā nūdâ rōʾšāh bat-yĕrûšālaim — "she despises you, she scorns you; behind your back she wags her head, O daughter of Jerusalem." The image of the humiliated Assyrian king being mocked by the city he could not conquer is one of Isaiah's most vivid rhetorical reversals — the greatest military power taunted by a city-girl.
37.23: ʾet-mî ḥēraptā ûgiddaptā — "whom have you taunted and reviled?" — the rhetorical question reveals the ultimate target of Sennacherib's words. He has not merely insulted Judah but the Holy One of Israel (ʾet-qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl). The elevation of the conflict from Judah-versus-Assyria to YHWH-versus-Assyria reframes the entire narrative.
