37.24: Sennacherib's arrogance described: cutting down Lebanon's cedars and choice firs (kōrĕtî qômat ʾărāzāyw mibḥar bĕrōšāyw), entering the innermost height (mārôm qiṣṣô), the forest of his Carmel. The cedar-cutting is both military metaphor (cutting down armies) and ecological reality (the Assyrian army consumed massive timber). The self-exaltation of the empire is mirrored in its consumption of creation.
37.25: ʾănî qartî wĕšātîtî mêm zarîm — "I dug and drank water from strange wells" — the Assyrian king's claim to have conquered waterless wilderness and survived by force of imperial will. wĕʾaḥărîb bĕkap pĕʿāmay kol yĕʾōrê māṣôr — "I will dry up with the soles of my feet all the rivers of Egypt" — the boast reaches cosmic proportions. The drying of rivers was YHWH's act at the Exodus; Sennacherib usurps this divine prerogative.
37.26: hălōʾ šāmaʿtā — "Have you not heard?" — YHWH speaks, now directly addressing Sennacherib. lĕmērāḥôq ʾōtāh ʿāśîtî — "from long ago I made it (the plan)." Everything Assyria has done — the conquests, the devastation — was YHWH's instrument (ʿēṣōt qedem, "plans of old"). The empire did not act autonomously; it fulfilled YHWH's predetermined purpose. The ʿēṣâ of YHWH (14:26-27; 25:1) governs even its enemies.
37.27: wĕyōšĕbêhem qĕṣar-yāḏ — "their inhabitants were short of hand" — an idiom for powerlessness/helplessness. The defeated cities were like ʿēśeḇ śādeh ûyereq deshe (grass of the field and green vegetation) — weak plants, not through Assyria's strength but through YHWH's preordained limitation of them. The demolished cities were before dismayed — their devastation was already determined.
37.28: šibteḵā wĕṣēʾtĕkā ûḇōʾăkā yāḏaʿtî — "your sitting and going out and coming in I know" — YHWH's comprehensive surveillance of Sennacherib. The three pairs (sitting/rising, going out/coming in) together represent the totality of human activity — YHWH monitors all of it. wĕʾēt hithraggĕzĕkā ʾēlay — "and your raging against me" — the surveillance is specifically interested in the rage directed at YHWH.
37.29: yaʿan hithraggĕzĕkā ʾēlay wĕšaʾănānĕkā ʿālâ bĕʾoznāy — "because your raging against me and your arrogance have come to my ears." The consequence: wĕśamtî ḥaqqî bĕʾapkā ûmaśĕgarî bĕśĕpātêkā — "I will put my hook in your nose and my bit in your lips." The animal-capture imagery (ḥāq, hook through nose; maśgēr, bit in lips — used to control horses and led animals) reduces the conqueror of nations to a led beast. YHWH will return him by the way he came.
37.30: The sign for Hezekiah: ʾākôl haššānâ hazzōʾt sĕpîaḥ ûbaššānâ haššĕnît śāḥîš — "eat this year what grows by itself, and in the second year what springs from that; but in the third year sow and reap, plant vineyards and eat their fruit." The three-year agricultural restoration sign confirms that YHWH's word to Hezekiah will be verified by the land's recovery — the sign is specifically agricultural, addressing the normal cycle of planting and harvest disrupted by siege.
37.31: wĕyāspâ pĕlêṭat bêt-yĕhûdâ hannišʾārt šōreš lĕmattâ wĕʿāśâ pĕrî lĕmaʿlâ — "the surviving remnant of the house of Judah will again take root downward and bear fruit upward" — the classic remnant-restoration metaphor combining botanical imagery (root/fruit) with the vertical movement of spiritual vitality. This is the Isaiah 6:13 "holy seed" in its positive form — the remnant as the seedbed of restoration.
37.32: kî miYĕrûšālaim tēṣēʾ šĕʾērît ûpĕlêṭâ mēhar ṣiyyôn — "for out of Jerusalem a remnant will go forth, and out of Mount Zion a band of survivors" — the city is the source from which the remnant disperses and from which covenant life is renewed. qinʾat Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt taʿăśeh zōʾt — "the zeal of YHWH of hosts will accomplish this" — the same formula as 9:7 (the Davidic ruler's kingdom) — divine zeal (qinʾâ) is the engine of both promises.
37.33: The unconditional protection oracle for Jerusalem: lōʾ yāḇōʾ ʾel-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "he will not enter this city." The Assyrian will not shoot an arrow (lōʾ-yidreh šām ḥēṣ), come before it with a shield (lōʾ-yĕqaddĕmennāh māgēn), or cast up a siege ramp (lōʾ-yišpōk ʿāleyhā sōlĕlâ). The three negated military actions specify the complete defeat of the Assyrian siege strategy.
37.34: baddereḵ ʾăšer yāḇōʾ bāh yāšûḇ — "by the way that he came, by that he will return" — the return formula that matches v.29's hook-and-bit imagery. The direction reversal — advance replaced by retreat on the same road — is the sign of YHWH's control over the campaign's trajectory. YHWH declares this in advance (nĕʾum-Yhwh, oracle of YHWH) to prove that the event is his action, not chance.
37.35: ûgannôtî ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt lĕhôšîʿāh — "I will defend this city to save it" — the double motivation: lĕmaʿanî ûlĕmaʿan dāwid ʿabdî — "for my own sake and for the sake of David my servant." The salvation is not based on Hezekiah's righteousness alone but on YHWH's self-commitment (divine honor) and his Davidic covenant (bĕrît dāwid). The two grounds are inseparable: YHWH's name and David's covenant.
37.36: wayyēṣēʾ malaʾk Yhwh wayyak bĕmaḥănēh ʾaššûr — "the angel of YHWH went out and struck down 185,000 in the Assyrian camp." The single agent, the single night, the massive number — the miracle is narrated with stark economy. wayyaškîmû babbōqer wĕhinnēh kullām pĕgarîm mētîm — "in the morning, they arose and behold, all of them were dead corpses." The contrast between the sleeping soldiers' expectation of victory and the morning discovery of mass death is the narrative climax of the Assyrian crisis.
37.37: Sennacherib departs — wayyisaʿ wayyēlek wayyāšāb Sannĕḥērîḇ — "he set out, departed, and returned" — and dwelt in Nineveh. The fulfilment of Isaiah's prophecy (v.7: "he will return to his land") is stated without elaboration. The brevity is rhetorical: the great conqueror's retreat is dismissed in three verbs.
37.38: wayhî hûʾ mištaḥăweh bêt nisrōk ʾĕlōhāyw — "he was worshiping in the house of Nisroch his god" — the final irony: the king who mocked YHWH is killed while worshiping his own god, in his own temple, by his own sons. wĕʾadrammeleḵ wĕśarʾeṣer bānāyw hikkuhû ḇaḥereb — "his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer struck him with the sword." The prophecy (v.7) is completely fulfilled. YHWH's word through Isaiah produced Sennacherib's defeat, retreat, and assassination.

38.1: ṣaw lĕbêtekā — "put your house in order/command your household" (ṣāwâ, to command; literally "command to your house"). This is the standard phrase for dying preparations — making arrangements for succession, inheritance, and burial. kî mēt ʾattâ wĕlōʾ tiḥyeh — "for you shall die and not live" — the death sentence is absolute and conditional-free. YHWH speaks it through the prophet without softening.
38.2: wayyāsseb Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾet-pānāyw ʾel-haqqîr — "Hezekiah turned his face to the wall" — the physical posture of private, intense prayer. The turn away from the sickroom audience toward the wall is both practical (privacy) and symbolic (orientation toward YHWH, away from human society). wayyitpallel ʾel-Yhwh — "and prayed to YHWH" — the prayer follows immediately.
38.3: hithallaktî lĕpāneykā beʾĕmet — "I have walked before you in truth/faithfulness" (ʾemet, H571, truth, reliability, fidelity — one of the covenant character terms). ûbĕlēḇ šālēm — "with a wholehearted/complete heart" (šālēm, complete, undivided). wĕhaṭṭôb bĕʿêneykā ʿāśîtî — "I have done what is good in your eyes." Hezekiah's prayer appeals to covenant fidelity, not merely divine mercy — he presents his life as evidence of faithfulness. wayyibkĕ Ḥizqiyyāhû bĕkî gādôl — "Hezekiah wept with great weeping."
38.4: The divine response comes wayhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾel-Yĕšaʿyāhû — "the word of YHWH came to Isaiah" — before the prophet has even left the middle court (ḥāṣēr hattîkônâ). The speed of divine response is emphasized: the prayer was heard immediately. Isaiah had delivered a death sentence (v.1) and now receives a reversal from YHWH — both words are YHWH's word, showing that divine pronouncements can be conditional on human response.
38.5: šûb wĕʾāmartā ʾel-Ḥizqiyyāhû — "return and say to Hezekiah" — Isaiah is sent back. šāmaʿtî ʾet-tĕpillatekā rāʾîtî ʾet-dimʿāteykā — "I have heard your prayer, I have seen your tears" — the two verbs address the two modes of Hezekiah's petition: spoken prayer and wept tears. Both were received. hinnî yōsip ʿal-yāmeykā ḥāmēš ʿeśrēh šānâ — "I will add fifteen years to your days." The specific number makes the promise verifiable.
38.6: ûmikkaP melek ʾaššûr ʾaṣṣîlĕkā wĕʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "and I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria" — the healing is coupled with the deliverance of Jerusalem, connecting Hezekiah's personal crisis to the national crisis of ch. 37. The two promises are simultaneous: king healed + city defended. wĕgannōtî ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "I will defend this city" — repeating 37:35.
38.7: The sign: wĕzeh-lĕkā hāʾôt mēʾēt Yhwh — "and this is the sign for you from YHWH." The sign authenticates the promise before it is fulfilled — it is evidence designed to produce faith, not just information. The reversal of the sun's shadow on the sundial of Ahaz (maʿălôt ʾāḥāz) — moving backward rather than forward — is an extraordinary astronomical sign. The shadow going backward (šāḇâ hāṣēl ʿeśer maʿălôt) is a temporal reversal that corresponds to the temporal gift of fifteen added years.
38.8: hinnî mēšîḇ ʾet-ṣel hammaʿălôt — "behold, I will make the shadow retreat on the steps" — the causative of šûḇ (return). The sign is explicitly YHWH's action (ʾănî, first person), not a natural phenomenon misinterpreted. The sun returning ten steps on the sundial of Ahaz (maʿălôt ʾāḥāz) is a specific, localized event that authenticates a specific, localized promise. The sign does not announce cosmic reversal but personal restoration.
38.9: miktāb lĕḥizqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ bĕḥalōtô wayyeḥî mēḥolyô — "the writing of Hezekiah king of Judah when he was ill and recovered from his illness" — the superscription of the Song of Hezekiah (vv.10-20). miktāb (H4385) can mean a written text, inscription, or poem — a form used for composed literary pieces (cf. Ps 16:1; 56:1 miktam). The psalm is a thanksgiving composed in retrospect from the perspective of survival.
38.10: ʾănî ʾāmartî bidmî yāmay — "I said in the noontide/prime of my days" — dĕmî (H1824) suggests the still point, the midpoint — cut off in the middle of his years. ʾēlĕkâ bĕšaʿărê šĕʾôl — "I will go to the gates of Sheol" — the image of Sheol as a city with gates (cf. Ps 107:18; Job 38:17; Matt 16:18). puqqadtî yeter šĕnōtāy — "I am deprived of the rest of my years" (pāqad, here in the pual — to be visited in deprivation, counted off, assigned to loss).
38.11: ʾāmartî lōʾ-ʾerʾeh Yāh Yāh bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm — "I said I will not see Yah, Yah, in the land of the living" — the doubled Yāh (the short form of YHWH) intensifies the loss: not just life but the vision of YHWH in the assembly is what Hezekiah mourns. lōʾ-ʾabbîṭ ʾādām ʿôd ʿim-yôšĕbê ḥāḏel — "I shall look no more upon man among the inhabitants of the world" (ḥāḏel, H2309, the cessation/world of the deceased — an unusual term for the underworld).
38.12: dôrî nissaʿ wĕniglâ minnî kĕʾōhel rōʿî — "my dwelling/generation is pulled up and removed from me like a shepherd's tent" (dôr, H1755, here in its rare meaning of "circle/dwelling/generation"). The tent imagery: a nomad's temporary shelter that can be struck and moved — death is the collapse and removal of one's dwelling. qippadtî kāʾōrēg ḥayyay — "like a weaver I roll up my life" (qāpad, to roll up or draw together — the life as a finished web being rolled up from the loom).
38.13: šiwitî ʿad-bōqer — "I waited until morning" — the long night of illness. kaʾărî kēn yĕšabbēr kol-ʿaṣmōtāy — "like a lion he breaks all my bones" — the illness personified as a lion crushing bones. The identification of YHWH as the source of the illness (the implied subject of "he breaks") reflects the ancient Hebrew theology that sickness was within YHWH's sovereign permission, not outside it — which is precisely why prayer to YHWH can heal.
38.14: kĕsûs ʿāgûr kēn ʾăṣappṣēp — "like a swallow or crane (sûs ʿāgûr) I chirp" — the plaintive bird-cry as a metaphor for weak, inarticulate prayer. The species identification of sûs and ʿāgûr is debated: possibly "swallow and crane" or "swift and turtledove." ʾehgeh kĕyônâ — "I moan like a dove." Three bird-sounds (chirping, twittering, moaning) describe the wordless quality of extreme illness prayer. dallû ʿênay lammārôm — "my eyes are weary looking upward" — exhausted, persistent upward orientation.
38.15: mā-ʾaddabbēr wĕʾāmar lî — "what shall I say, since he has both spoken and acted?" — Hezekiah is silenced by the reality of what YHWH has done. ʾeddaddeh kol-šĕnôtay ʿal-mar napšî — "I must walk carefully all my years because of the bitterness of my soul" — the surviving years are now lived under the shadow of the near-death, with a chastened and careful step (dādâ, to walk slowly/carefully).
38.16: ʾădōnāy ʿălêhem yiḥyû — "O Lord, by these things men live, and in all these is the life of my spirit." The "things" refer to the divine words, promises, and judgments that filled the psalm. Human life is sustained not merely by physical health but by the content of YHWH's address to his people. wĕtaḥălîmēnî wĕhaḥăyēnî — "restore me to health and give me life" — the prayer for healing is embedded within the broader theological vision of life as YHWH's gift.
38.17: hinnēh lĕšālôm mar-lî mar — "behold, it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness" — the retrospective theology of suffering. The bitterness proved to be the instrument of peace (šālôm). wĕʾattâ ḥāšaqtā napšî miššaḥat bĕlî — "but you have loved/desired (ḥāšaq, H2836, to delight in, to be attached to) my soul from the pit of destruction." kî hišlaḵtā ʾaḥărê gēwĕkā kol-ḥăṭāʾāy — "you have cast all my sins behind your back" — the forgiveness of sins coupled with the healing of the body.
38.18: kî lōʾ šĕʾôl tôdekkā māwet yĕhallĕlekkā — "for Sheol does not thank you, death does not praise you" — the theological argument for healing from the perspective of worship. Those who go down to the pit (yôrĕdê bôr) do not hope for YHWH's faithfulness (ʾel-ʾămittekā). Hezekiah's argument is not that he deserves to live but that the dead cannot praise YHWH — and YHWH desires the praise of his creatures. This praise-theology argument for healing is unique in the Hebrew Bible.
38.19: ḥāy ḥāy hûʾ yôdekā kāmônî hayyôm — "the living, the living, they praise you, as I do today" — the doubled ḥāy (living, living) mirrors the doubled Yāh Yāh of v.11 — two anguished calls to YHWH now become two declarations of living praise. ʾāḇ lĕḇānîm yôdîaʿ ʾel-ʾămittekā — "a father makes known to his children your faithfulness" — intergenerational transmission of praise is only possible through continued life. Hezekiah's argument climaxes here.
38.20: Yhwh lĕhôšîʿēnî — "YHWH will save me" — the concluding confidence statement. ûnĕgînôtay nĕnaggen kol-yĕmê ḥayyênû ʿal-bêt Yhwh — "we will play my songs on stringed instruments all the days of our life at the house of YHWH." The plural nĕnaggen (we will play) and ḥayyênû (our life) suggest communal temple worship — Hezekiah's individual healing becomes the occasion for shared liturgical music. The temple is the site of realized salvation.
38.21: wayyōʾmer Yĕšaʿyāhû — "Isaiah said" — a surprising appendix after the psalm. yiśśĕʾû dĕbelet tĕʾēnîm — "let them take a cake of figs" (debelet, H1690, a fig-cake, a pressed lump of dried figs). The medical instruction — applying a poultice of figs to the boil (šĕḥîn) — reflects ancient Near Eastern medical practice where figs were used therapeutically. The miraculous healing and the natural medical treatment coexist without tension in the narrative.
38.22: wayyōʾmer Ḥizqiyyāhû mah ʾôt kî ʾeʿĕleh bêt Yhwh — "Hezekiah had said, 'What is the sign that I shall go up to the house of YHWH?'" — this retrospective verse appears to have been displaced from its logical position (after v.7, where the sign is given). Its placement here may be an editorial note showing that Hezekiah's recovery was validated by his ability to return to the temple — the ultimate mark of restored communion with YHWH.

39.1: bĕrōdak balʾăḏān ben-balʾăḏān melek bābel — Merodach-Baladan (Marduk-apla-iddina II), the Chaldean king of Babylon who twice ruled Babylon (722-710 BCE and briefly 703 BCE) and consistently opposed Assyrian power. His envoys with a letter and a gift (sĕpārîm ûminḥâ) come to Hezekiah because he has heard of his illness. The diplomatic context: Babylon was seeking anti-Assyrian alliances. Hezekiah's reception of these envoys becomes the seed of the Babylonian exile prophesied in vv.5-7.
39.2: wayyiśmaḥ ʿălêhem Ḥizqiyyāhû — "Hezekiah was pleased/glad with them" — the dangerous delight. wayyarʾēm ʾet-bêt nĕkōtôh — "showed them his house of treasures" (nĕkōt, H5238, spice/treasure; bêt nĕkōt, the treasury). ʾet-hakkesef wĕʾet-hazzāhāb — silver, gold, spices, fine oil, the entire armory, and everything in the storehouses — Hezekiah shows everything. The comprehensiveness is repeated: lōʾ-hāyâ dābār ʾăšer lōʾ-herʾām — "there was nothing that he did not show them."
39.3: Isaiah's opening question: mā ʾāmĕrû hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh ûmēʾayin yāḇōʾû ʾēleykā — "what did these men say, and where did they come from?" Hezekiah answers proudly: mēʾereṣ rĕḥôqâ bāʾû ʾēlay mibābel — "from a distant land they came to me, from Babylon." The word rĕḥôqâ (distant) is the geographical marker that anticipates the later exile — what came from afar will take everything there.
39.4: mah rāʾû bĕbêtekā — "what did they see in your house?" — Isaiah presses. Hezekiah answers with complete candor: kōl ʾăšer bĕbêtî rāʾû — "everything in my house they saw." lōʾ-hāyâ dābār ʾăšer lōʾ-herʾîtîm bĕʾôṣĕrōtāy — "there was nothing in my treasuries that I did not show them." The repetition of "nothing not shown" from v.2 and v.4 frames the indictment — Hezekiah has confirmed his own guilt.
39.5: šĕmaʿ dĕbar Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "hear the word of YHWH of hosts" — the prophetic formula that introduces a hard word. The shift from conversation to oracle marks a change in register. Isaiah's words become the voice of YHWH, not his own assessment. The oracle will address the consequences of Hezekiah's hospitality to Babylon's representative.
39.6: hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm — "behold, days are coming" — the prophetic future formula. wĕnuśśāʾ kol-ʾăšer bĕbêtekā — "everything in your house will be carried off (nāśāʾ)" — the same verb as the Babylonian exile deportations. wĕʾăšer ʾāṣĕrû ʾăḇōteykā ʿad-hayyôm hazzeh bābel yūbāʾû — "what your fathers stored up until this day will be brought to Babylon." The treasure that Hezekiah showed becomes the treasure that will be removed — the showing was already the beginning of the losing.
39.7: ûmibānêkā ʾăšer yēṣĕʾû mimměkā — "and of your sons who will come from you" — Hezekiah's descendants will be taken. wĕhāyû sārîsîm bĕhêkal melek bābel — "they will be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon" (sārîs, H5631, eunuch/court official). This specific prophecy is regarded as fulfilled in the Babylonian exile — Israelite youths of the royal line serving in Babylonian courts (cf. Dan 1:1-7 where Daniel and his companions are exactly this). The use of sārîs may refer to court officials generally or specifically to castration.
39.8: wayyōʾmer Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾel-Yĕšaʿyāhû ṭôḇ dĕbar-Yhwh ʾăšer dibbartā — "Hezekiah said to Isaiah, 'The word of YHWH that you have spoken is good.'" The response is theologically proper — accepting the divine word — but the following clause reveals its limitation: kî yihyeh šālôm weʾĕmet bĕyāmay — "for there will be peace and truth in my days." Hezekiah is content because the disaster will not come in his lifetime. The commentary tradition has long noted the moral ambiguity: is this faith (accepting YHWH's sovereignty) or selfishness (relief that others will bear it)?

46.1: Bel (Bēl, H1078) is the Babylonian title of Marduk, chief deity of Babylon; Nebo (Nĕbô, H5015) is Nabu, god of scribal wisdom and son of Marduk — both among the most prominent gods of the empire. The verb kāraʿ (H3766, "bows down") paired with qāras ("stoops") ironically applies postures of worship to the gods themselves: they are the ones doing the bowing, not receiving it. Maśśāʾ (H4853) means both "burden/load" and, in prophetic literature, a weighty oracle — the pun stings: the idol-oracle is nothing but dead weight. The MKT renders "burdens on exhausted beasts" to capture the physical futility of transporting gods who cannot save themselves.
46.2: The verb qāras ("stoops") continues the mocking bowing posture. The crucial phrase is that the idols "cannot save (mālaṭ, H4422) the load" — the same verb YHWH uses for saving his people (v4: "I will carry you and save you"). Captivity (šĕbî, H7628) is the humiliating destination of both gods and their worshipers — the empire that exiled Israel will itself be exiled, carrying its helpless gods on carts. The MKT "they themselves go into captivity" captures the reversal: the gods cannot prevent their own exile, much less their worshipers'.
46.3: YHWH addresses "the house of Jacob and all the remnant of the house of Israel" — the two-fold address (bêt Yaʿaqōb and šĕʾērît bêt Yiśrāʾēl) encompasses both the northern and southern traditions, calling the whole covenant people to hear. The participle hăʿămusîm ("carried" or "borne as a burden," from ʿāmas, H6006) answers the burden-language of v1: unlike Bel and Nebo who are burdens on their carriers, Israel is carried by YHWH. The phrase "since before birth, borne since the womb" (min-beṭen, H990) traces the carrying back to Israel's origins — election and sustenance are co-extensive with existence itself.
46.4: The theological center of the chapter. ʾĂnî hûʾ ("I am he," H589 + H1931) is Deutero-Isaiah's signature divine self-identification formula, appearing at key moments throughout Isaiah 40–55 (41:4; 43:10, 13, 25; 48:12). This formula underlies the Greek egō eimi — the "I am" of John's Gospel echoes precisely this self-identifying divine speech. The verse strings four first-person verbs: ʿāśîtî ("I have made/created"), ʾeśśāʾ ("I will carry/bear"), ʾeśbol ("I will sustain/carry as a burden"), ʾămalēṭ ("I will deliver/save"). The verbs span creation, ongoing sustenance, and final salvation — YHWH carries his people from womb to gray hair, from origin to end. The MKT "I have made you, and I will sustain you; I will carry you and save you" preserves this four-verb sequence.
46.5: Mî-ʾĕdammeh-lî ("to whom will you compare me?") opens the incomparability challenge. The root dāmāh (H1819, "to liken/compare") appears three times in different conjugations — compare, equate, liken — a rhetorical tripling that exhausts the vocabulary of comparison. Notably, the same root underlies dĕmût ("likeness") in Genesis 1:26 and the making of an image after a likeness in v5-7. The irony: the idol-makers try to make something "like" God, but no comparison is possible — YHWH's uniqueness cannot be reduced to a dĕmût. The MKT adds "and make me equal" to render the step-parallelism of the three comparison terms.
46.6: The idol-making sequence: yāzûbû zāhāb ("they pour out gold") uses the verb for pouring molten metal; yišqĕlû ("they weigh") emphasizes the commercial transaction — the idol is a purchase, not a gift. Tsôrēp (H6884, "goldsmith/refiner") is a craftsman who transforms raw metal, but what is produced is ʾēl ("god") — the same word used for YHWH himself in v9 (ʾānî ʾēl, "I am God"). The irony compounds: the goldsmiths produce an ʾēl that must be carried (v7), while the true ʾēl carries them (v4). The MKT "makes it into a god" leaves ʾēl lowercase to mark the irony without being heavy-handed.
46.7: The helpless idol's lifecycle: it is hoisted (yiśśāʾuhû, from nāśāʾ, the same verb YHWH uses for carrying Israel), carried, set in place (yannîḥuhû), and then it simply stands (yaʿămōd) — motionless. The key phrase is mimĕqômô lōʾ yāmîš ("it cannot move from its place") — the idol is geographically fixed, utterly incapable of the dynamic presence YHWH exercises everywhere. The final line: it cannot answer (yaʿaneh) nor save (yôšîaʿ) — the two defining divine activities of prayer-and-response and deliverance are both beyond the idol's reach. "Rescues no one from trouble" (MKT) renders lōʾ yôšîaʿ with practical specificity.
46.8: The imperative zizkĕrû-zōʾt ("remember this") shifts the address from idol-critique to covenant demand. Hitʾōšāšû (H376 + root, "stand firm" or "take it to heart") is unusual — some read it as "act like men," others as "strengthen yourselves." The word pōšĕʿîm (H6586, "rebels/transgressors") identifies the audience not as innocent exiles but as those with a history of covenant defection — the call to remember is also a call to repent. The MKT "bring it to mind, you rebels" captures the confrontational tone without softening the identity the prophet assigns.
46.9: The double declaration: ʾānî ʾēl wĕʾên ʿôd ("I am God [ʾEl], and there is no other") followed by ʾānî ʾĕlōhîm wĕʾên kāmônî ("I am God [Elohim], and there is none like me"). The shift from ʾēl (the generic singular) to ʾĕlōhîm (the majestic plural) is deliberate — both divine names are claimed absolutely. ʾEpes (H657, "none other/nothing else") is stronger than the more common lōʾ — it means "end, cessation, there is nothing remaining beyond this." This verse is the theological summit of the chapter: all comparison has failed (v5), all idols are helpless (vv6-7), and now comes the unqualified divine claim of sole deity.
46.10: The signature of YHWH's uniqueness: maggid mērēʾšît ʾaḥărît ("declaring from the beginning the end") — note that rēʾšît (H7225, "beginning") and ʾaḥărît (H319, "end/latter things") frame all of history. This is not mere prediction but sovereign declaration: the same God who was at the beginning determines what the end will be. ʿĒṣāh (H6098, "counsel/plan/purpose") is the deliberate, reasoned intent of YHWH — not reactive to events but shaping them. The verb tāqûm (H6965, "will stand/rise") paired with ʾeʿĕśeh ("I will do/carry out") asserts that the divine plan is both stable (standing) and active (being executed). MKT "my plan shall stand" chooses the stability sense of qûm.
46.11: ʿĂyiṭ (H5861, "bird of prey/eagle") from the east is the Cyrus figure — the same raptor image used in 41:2 where YHWH "stirs up one from the east." The word ʿayiṭ connotes a swooping predator, fitting for the military campaigns Cyrus conducted across the ancient Near East. ʾÎš ʿĕṣātî ("man of my counsel/purpose") identifies Cyrus not by name here (as in 44:28; 45:1) but by divine designation — he is a person (ʾîš) instrumentalized by YHWH's plan (ʿēṣāh). The final couplet — dibbartî ʾap-ʾăbîʾennāh ("I have spoken, and I will bring it to pass") and yātsartî ʾap-ʾeʿĕśennāh ("I have planned, and I will do it") — closes with the word-act unity that defines YHWH's sovereignty: speaking and doing are never separated in his governance of history.
46.12: ʾAbbirê-lēb (H47 + H3820, literally "mighty-hearted" or "strong-of-heart") is translated variously as "stubborn of heart" (MKT), "stout-hearted," or "hard-hearted." The same root (ʾabbîr) can describe the noble bull or the mighty warrior — here it is ironized: those who think their strength of heart places them beyond need of God. Hārĕḥôqîm mitsĕdāqāh ("far from righteousness") uses tsĕdāqāh in its Deutero-Isaianic sense of saving-righteousness or vindication, not merely moral conduct — they are far from the salvation that YHWH is about to bring near (v13).
46.13: The chapter's resolution: tsĕdāqāh (H6666) here means not "moral righteousness" but divine vindication/salvation — the act by which YHWH demonstrates his righteous character by rescuing his people. In Deutero-Isaiah, tsĕdāqāh and tĕšûʿāh (H8668, "salvation") function as near-synonyms (cf. 51:6, 8; 56:1; 62:1). The verb nātattî (H5414, "I will give/place") takes Zion as the location of salvation's installation — it is not merely promised but will be placed there like a foundation stone. The final phrase lĕYiśrāʾēl tiпʾartî ("for Israel my glory") concludes the chapter with covenant identity: the salvation of Israel is itself the manifestation of YHWH's glory. The MKT "for Israel my glory" keeps the possessive, marking the glory as his own character displayed in Israel's redemption.

47.1: Come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon. The fall of Babylon is described through posture: from throne to ground-sitting. The virgin daughter imagery implies pride of untouched power -- Babylon has never been conquered. Sit on the ground without a throne strips the political symbol of authority: the throne gone, the ground is all that is left. The MKT preserves the address-form (O virgin daughter of Babylon) that treats the city as a person, allowing the shame-language to carry its full weight.
47.2: The imagery of captive women grinding at the mill and wading through streams -- manual labor and exposed vulnerability. The veil removed and the robe lifted expose the formerly veiled and robed queen. The MKT's specific clothing-items (veil, robe) follow the Hebrew's enumeration of the stripped dignities. Each item removed is a dignity taken: the veil (identity-privacy), the robe (status-display), the shod feet (social protection).
47.3: Your nakedness shall be exposed, your disgrace visible -- YHWH will take vengeance and will not relent (lo efga adam: I will not meet a man / not be dissuaded by anyone). The divine refusal to be negotiated out of judgment against Babylon is the counterpart to the divine compassion that could be interceded with for Israel. For Babylon, the cup of judgment is full and no mediator will succeed in emptying it.
47.4: YHWH is flagged -- the parenthetical identification of Israel's Redeemer: Our Redeemer -- the LORD of hosts is his name -- the Holy One of Israel. The bracket-verse inserts Israel's covenant title for YHWH into the oracle against Babylon. The Redeemer who is judging Babylon is specifically Israel's covenant God. The divine name YHWH of hosts (the army commander) identifies the one bringing Babylon low as the same one who called himself Israel's Kinsman-Redeemer.
47.5: Sit in silence and go into darkness, O daughter of the Chaldeans. The reversal of Babylon's previous position: formerly the mistress of kingdoms, now reduced to darkness and silence. The word mistress of kingdoms (geveret mamlakhot) is the highest political title -- over all kingdoms, not just one. The silence and darkness contrast with the Babylonian court's noise and splendor. The MKT preserves the title of greatness precisely because its loss is the point.
47.6: YHWH's self-disclosure of why he used Babylon: I was angry with my people, desecrated my heritage, handed them over to you. But Babylon showed no mercy -- she went beyond the instrument-role. On the elderly she laid an extremely heavy yoke. The instrument of divine discipline overstepped its mandate: using the people for plunder rather than correction. This excess of cruelty beyond the divine assignment is what turns the instrument into the target.
47.7: Olam (ancient time, disp 3) is flagged -- Babylon said I will be mistress forever. The claim to eternal power is the pride that precedes the fall. Le-olam here means permanently, forever -- Babylon claimed a permanence that belongs only to the LORD. The failure to consider how it would end (lo samti al-libbakh ahrit: not set on your heart its end/outcome) is the specific failure: pride so complete that the future is not contemplated.
47.8: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- who says in your heart, I am, and there is no one else beside me. The formula ani ve-afsi od (I am, and there is nothing more/no one else) is the usurpation of the divine self-description. In 45:6 and 45:18 this formula belongs to YHWH alone; here Babylon appropriates it. The widow and loss-of-children will not come -- Babylon's arrogant self-assurance is that her security is impervious. The yada is rendered who says -- the internal declaration of false omnipotence.
47.9: Both losses will come in a moment, in a single day: loss of children and widowhood. The completeness of the disaster is matched by its suddenness -- a single day. Despite the great power of Babylon's enchantments and sorceries, these losses will be total. The sorcery that Babylon has practiced from youth -- the whole apparatus of Babylonian astrology and divination -- will not prevent the sudden catastrophe.
47.10: You trusted in your wickedness -- the trust-vocabulary (batach) applied to evil as if it were a reliable foundation. No one sees me -- the denial of divine oversight. Your wisdom and knowledge led you astray (heshi'atekha: seduced/misled you) -- the Babylonian epistemic tradition (wisdom, knowledge, learning) became the agent of self-delusion. The ani ve-afsi od formula appears again: the knowledge that should have revealed YHWH instead reinforced self-sufficiency.
47.11: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged twice -- evil will come you will not know how to conjure away, disaster you will not be able to avert. The two yada-negatives: you will not know the charm (the correct ritual for averting); you will not be able to atone for it (kapper: cover/atone -- the sacrificial vocabulary of dealing with wrong). The whole Babylonian system of ritual knowledge for averting evil is useless against the divine decree.
47.12: The ironic invitation: stand by your enchantments and sorceries. The invitation is sarcastic -- perhaps you will succeed, perhaps you will inspire terror. The MKT's perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you will inspire terror preserves the ironic optative: maybe -- but both speaker and listener know it is futile. The catalogue of Babylonian occult expertise (enchantments, sorceries from youth) is precisely what will be shown to be nothing.
47.13: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- the advisers who divide the sky, gaze at the stars, give forecasts month by month are the astrologers. Let them stand up and save you -- the challenge to the astrologers to do what they claim. The MKT preserves the technical astronomical vocabulary: sky-dividers, star-gazers, monthly forecasters. Babylonian astrology was technically sophisticated; Isaiah is not dismissing the observation but the claim that such observation gives power.
47.14: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) is flagged -- there is no warm fire (gahalah) to sit beside it (nefesh: no coal for warming). The astrologers burned up like straw -- they cannot even save themselves from the flame. No warm ember for warming oneself. The MKT notes the nefesh here as not a flame warm enough to sit before. The fire that consumes the astrologers gives no warmth: a fire of pure destruction, not a fire of comfort.
47.15: Such are those you have dealt with -- your trading partners since your youth who now scatter, each in his own direction, with no one to save you. The commercial network (sochrekhah: your merchants) dissolves at the moment of crisis: each one goes off in his own direction. The social and commercial structure that depended on Babylon's power disintegrates exactly when it is most needed. The MKT's trading partners preserves the commercial vocabulary.

48.1: Elohim, emet (truth, disp 2), YHWH, and tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) are all flagged -- those who swear by the name of the LORD and invoke the God of Israel -- but not in truth or righteousness. Israel swears by the divine name (YHWH) and invokes Elohim of Israel, but the swearing is not be-emet u-vetsedaqah (in truth and in righteousness). The MKT renders emet as truth and tsedaqah as righteousness -- the paired terms for the quality of covenant speech that Israel lacks while using the correct formulae.
48.2: Elohim, YHWH, and qodesh (holiness, disp 2) are flagged -- they call themselves after the holy city (ir haqodesh: the city of holiness) and claim to rest on the God of Israel. The divine name YHWH of hosts is his name. The irony: the holy city's inhabitants use the city's holiness as a credential while not sharing its holiness. The MKT's holy city preserves ir haqodesh -- the holiness attributed to the city, not to the people claiming it.
48.3: The former things declared long ago went out from YHWH's mouth and were proclaimed; then suddenly the action came, and it happened. The pattern of prediction-then-fulfillment is YHWH's authentication method: the historical track record of announced-and-fulfilled events is the evidence that the same LORD who acted in the past is making new announcements that will also be fulfilled.
48.4: Because I knew you are stubborn -- iron neck muscle, bronze forehead. The anatomical metaphors for stubbornness: the neck that will not bow (iron-sinewed) and the forehead that will not show shame (bronze-faced). The advance declaration of judgment was given because of the known stubbornness: if YHWH had waited to declare until the event, the stubborn people would have said their idols did it.
48.5: I announced it from long ago, before it happened, so that you could not say, My idol did it; my carved image or cast idol commanded these things. The divine preemptive announcement of history is specifically designed to prevent idol-credit: the prophecy-before-fulfillment method is a form of copyright marking -- the announcement stakes the claim to the event before it happens, so no alternative claimant can appropriate the credit afterward.
48.6: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- you have heard; now look at all this; and will you not declare it? The witness-obligation follows the hearing: after YHWH's prophetic declaration is confirmed by events, the obligation is to declare it to others. From now on I announce new things, hidden things that you have not known. The hidden things (netsuroth: kept/guarded things) are in reserve -- YHWH has not yet declared everything; a store of new announcements remains.
48.7: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- you cannot say, Yes, I knew them. The new things are being created now, not from long ago; before today you had never heard of them. The radical newness prevents Israel's claim to prior knowledge. The refrain you cannot say I already knew this is the anti-self-congratulation clause: the new things belong to YHWH's initiative alone, not to Israel's perceptiveness.
48.8: Yada is flagged twice -- you have not heard, you have not known; from of old your ear has not been opened. The double yada-negatives (not heard, not known) describe habitual non-perception. For I knew (yada) that you would surely betray -- the divine knowledge of human faithlessness precedes and pre-accommodates Israel's repeated failure. The MKT's from of old your ear has not been opened renders the chronic failure of covenant hearing.
48.9: For the sake of my name I hold back my anger; for my honor (tehillati: my praise/glory) I restrain it, so that I do not cut you off. The divine self-restraint is motivated by the divine name: cutting off Israel would compromise the LORD's testimony before the nations. The restraint of judgment is not because Israel deserves mercy but because YHWH's name and testimony are at stake -- the same motivation given for the wilderness-rescue in Ezek 20:9,14,22.
48.10: I have refined you, but not as silver is refined -- I have tested you in the furnace of affliction. The refinement metaphor with a qualification: not as silver (completely removed from dross) but in the furnace of affliction (kur oni: the smelting pot of poverty/affliction). The partial character of the refining is honest: Israel has been refined but is not yet fully refined. The furnace of affliction is the Babylonian exile.
48.11: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged -- For my own sake, for my own sake, I act -- for how could my name be profaned? My glory I will not share with another. The double lema'ani lema'ani (for my own sake, for my own sake) is the most emphatic possible self-reference: the motivation for acting is entirely internal to YHWH. The refusal to share kavod (glory) echoes 42:8: I am the LORD, I will not give my glory to another. The MKT preserves the double repetition that intensifies the divine self-motivation.
48.12: Listen to me, O Jacob, and Israel, whom I called: I am he; I am the first; I am also the last. The I-am formula in triplicate: ani hu (I am he), ani rishon (I am first), ani acharon (I am also the last). Rev 1:17 and 22:13 apply this triple formula to the risen Christ: I am the first and the last and I am the Alpha and Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end. The MKT's exact rendering preserves the form that Revelation quotes.
48.13: My hand laid the foundation of the earth, my right hand spread out the heavens; when I summon them, they take their stand together. The creation claim as the basis for the identity claim of v12: the one who is first and last is also the creator of earth and heavens. The summoning of earth and heavens to stand together is the divine command over creation -- the creator's authority over what he made. The MKT's take their stand together renders ya'amdu yachdav: they stand as one at the summons.
48.14: YHWH is flagged -- The LORD loves him; he shall carry out his will against Babylon, and his arm shall be against the Chaldeans. The him is Cyrus (44:28; 45:1) -- YHWH's chosen instrument. The divine love for the instrument is notable: not merely using Cyrus but loving him and ensuring he succeeds. The MKT's carry out his will renders ya'aseh cheftso: do/accomplish his desire/pleasure.
48.15: I, even I, have spoken; I have called him; I have brought him; and he will succeed in his mission. The emphatic I-statements (four first-person verbs) place the entirety of Cyrus's mission in the divine initiative: spoken, called, brought, and guaranteeing success. The MKT preserves the anaphora of the four verbs. Cyrus's success is already secured by the divine promise before it happens historically.
48.16: Ruach (Spirit, disp 4) is flagged -- And now the Lord GOD has sent me, and his Spirit. The speaker shifts to the first person -- either the prophet or the Servant. The claim that the Lord GOD has sent both the speaker and his Spirit creates a triad: the Lord GOD (sender), the speaker (sent), and the Spirit (also sent). The MKT's his Spirit capitalizes, reflecting the divine Spirit sent alongside the speaker. This verse has been interpreted as an early trinitarian formula: the LORD sending both the Son and the Spirit.
48.17: Elohim and YHWH are flagged twice -- I am the LORD your God, who teaches you what is good for you, who leads you in the way you should go. The double divine title (LORD your God) introduces the teaching-and-leading metaphor: YHWH as teacher and path-guide. The specificity -- teaches you what is good for you -- implies that YHWH's instruction is not arbitrary law but what genuinely benefits the people. The path-leading image is the Psalm 23:3 pattern.
48.18: Tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) and shalom (peace, disp 2) are flagged -- If only you had listened to my commandments! Then your peace would have been like a river, and your righteousness like the waves of the sea. The counterfactual lament: the peace that was available but not received. Shalom as a river -- the steady, broad, inexhaustible flow of covenantal wholeness. Tsedaqah as the waves of the sea -- the continuous, rhythmic, overwhelming rightness that should have characterized Israel's life. The MKT preserves the two similes without merging them.
48.19: Your descendants would have been like sand, your children like its grains; their name would never have been cut off or destroyed before me. The conditional blessing unrealized: the Abrahamic promise of sand-numbered descendants (Gen 22:17) was available but forfeited through disobedience. The name that would never have been cut off contrasts with the real history of exile and dispersion. The lament of lost blessing is one of the most poignant moments in Second Isaiah.
48.20: YHWH is flagged -- the command to go out from Babylon and flee from Chaldea, declared with joy to the ends of the earth: the LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob. The redemption declaration is the gospel announcement (mevasser: herald of good news) that runs through 40:9; 52:7; 61:1. The MKT's declare this with a cry of joy renders the commanded response: not quiet departure but joyful proclamation. The ends of the earth are the audience, giving the redemption of Jacob universal significance.
48.21: They did not thirst when he led them through the deserts -- he made water flow from the rock. The new Exodus recall: the miracle of the rock in the wilderness (Exod 17:6; Num 20:11) is cited as the pattern for the new return from Babylon. Paul identifies the rock as Christ (1 Cor 10:4): the spiritual rock that followed them was Christ. The MKT's rendering follows the Hebrew's past-tense narrative of what YHWH did -- a historical claim, not just a metaphor.
48.22: YHWH is flagged; shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- There is no peace, says the LORD, for the wicked. The closing refrain of chapters 40-48 is a warning note: the redemption promises of the preceding chapters are not available to the wicked without repentance. The shalom that was a river (v18) is specifically denied to the resha'im (wicked). This refrain appears again at 57:21 as the closing statement of the second subsection (49-57), making it a structural marker of the book's sections.

49.1: šim'û 'iyyîm 'ēlay — Listen, O coastlands, to me. The Servant opens the second Servant Song (vv. 1–6) by addressing distant peoples directly. mibbeṭen 'immî hizkkîr šĕmî — from my mother's womb he named my name — the verb hizkkîr (Hiphil of זָכַר, to cause to be remembered/named) echoes the prophetic calling of Jeremiah (Jer 1:5: bĕṭerem 'eṣṣārkā babbèṭen yĕda'tîkā). Pre-natal election signals a calling of cosmic scope.
49.2: wayyāśem pî kĕḥereb ḥaddāh — He made my mouth like a sharp sword. Two weapon metaphors in sequence: the sword (ḥereb ḥaddāh, חֶרֶב חַדָּה — sharp sword) and the polished arrow (ḥēṣ bārûr, חֵץ בָּרוּר — a clean, selected arrow). Both are concealed — the sword in his hand's shadow (bĕṣēl yādô), the arrow in his quiver. The divine word is the weapon; its power is latent, held in reserve until the appointed moment. Rev 1:16 applies sword imagery to Christ's word.
49.3: 'abdî 'attāh yiśrā'ēl — You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified. The identification of the Servant as Israel (yiśrā'ēl) in v. 3, followed by the Servant's mission to Israel in v. 5, creates an interpretive tension: the Servant is both identified with Israel and distinguished from it. This is not textual confusion but deliberate complexity — the ideal Israel within Israel who accomplishes what the nation failed to do. The Hithpa'el 'etpā'ār — I will be glorified — is reflexive: YHWH is glorified in the Servant's work.
49.4: wĕ'ānî 'āmartî lĕrîq yāga'tî — But I said, I have labored in vain. The Servant's complaint uses the Pi'el yāga'tî (from יָגַע, to toil/labor) — denoting exhausting exertion. tōhû wĕhébel — for emptiness and vanity — the same pair (תֹּהוּ וָהֶבֶל) appears in Gen 1:2 and Eccl 1:2. The Servant's response is the pivot: mišpāṭî 'et-YHWH wĕpĕullātî 'et-'ĕlōhāy — my justice is with YHWH and my reward with my God — entrusting the outcome to the divine court rather than human success metrics.
49.5: wĕ'attāh 'āmar YHWH yōṣĕrî mibbeṭen — And now YHWH who formed me from the womb to be his servant. The verb yāṣar (יָצַר — to form, the potter's verb) marks the Servant as YHWH's crafted instrument, not self-selected. The purpose: lĕšôbēb̠ ya'ăqōb 'ēlāyw — to bring Jacob back to him — gathering dispersed Israel. wĕ'ănikal wĕ'ĕlōhāy hāyāh 'uzzî — though Israel is not gathered, yet I will be honored... and my God will be my strength — the concessive clause acknowledges apparent failure; divine sustaining is the ground of endurance.
49.6: wĕnĕtattîkā lĕ'ôr gôyim — I will give you as a light for the nations. The Servant's mission expands: first to restore Israel (v. 5), now to encompass all nations. lĕ'ôr gôyim (לְאוֹר גּוֹיִם) directly echoes 42:6 — the two Servant Songs share this climactic phrase. The scope: lihyôt yĕšû'ātî 'ad-qĕṣēh hā'āreṣ — to be my salvation to the end of the earth. NT: Luke 2:32 (Simeon's canticle), Acts 13:47 (Paul applies this to the Gentile mission).
49.7: lĭmĕga'ăl-nepeš — to one deeply despised. The participial phrase mĕga'ăl-nepeš (Pi'el participle of גָּעַל + נֶפֶשׁ, lit. one whom the soul abhors) is extreme language of rejection. The Servant is also mĕtā'ăb̠ gôy — abhorred by the nation — and le'ebed mōšĕlîm — slave of rulers. The reversal is sovereign: kings and officials will rise and bow down (qûmû sārîm yišŏtaḥăwû) — because YHWH who is faithful (ne'ĕmān) has chosen the Servant.
49.8: bĕ'ēt rāṣôn 'ănîtîkā — In a time of favor I have answered you. The divine speech opens with a dual temporal phrase: 'ēt rāṣôn (time of favor/acceptance) and yôm yĕšû'āh (day of salvation). Paul quotes this in 2 Cor 6:2, applying it to the eschatological now of Christ's gospel. wĕ'eṣṣārkā wĕ'ettĕnĕkā lĭbrît 'ām — I will keep you and give you as a covenant for the people — brît 'ām (בְּרִית עָם, covenant of people) is unique to Isaiah (cf. 42:6); the Servant is the covenant-person, not merely covenant-bearer.
49.9: lĕ'emōr laʾăsûrîm ṣĕ'û — saying to the prisoners, 'Come out!'. The Qal imperatives ṣĕ'û (go out!) and higālû (appear!) are the Servant's proclamation to the bound. Those in darkness are commanded to show themselves — darkness (ḥōšek) is the prison condition, not merely metaphor. yir'û — they shall graze — shifts from prisoner to flock imagery; the restored exiles are pastured by the roadways (bĕkol-šĕpāyim mir'êhem).
49.10: lō'-yir'ābû wĕlō'-yiṣmā'û — They shall not hunger, and they shall not thirst. The double negative (lō'... wĕlō') is emphatic: absolute provision is assured. šārāb̠ (שָׁרָב — scorching heat, mirage) and šèmeš (sun) will not strike them — wilderness hazards negated. The verb yĕnahălēm (Pi'el of נָהַל, to lead/guide to water) appears: YHWH himself leads them to springs of water. Rev 7:16-17 draws on this verse directly for the multitude before the throne.
49.11: wĕśamtî kol-hāray laddārek — I will make all my mountains a road. The cosmic highway motif (cf. 40:3-4): mountains become roads, raised highways (mĕsillātay yĕrūmmûn). This is divine geographical transformation to facilitate return. The passive yĕrūmmûn (Pual of רוּם — to be lifted up) frames the landscape as cooperative with the divine purpose.
49.12: hinnē 'ēlleh mērāḥôq yāb̠ō'û — Behold, these will come from far away. The returnees are gathered from four directions: north (ṣāpôn), west (yām, sea), and the land of Sinim (סִינִים — variously identified as Aswan/Syene in Egypt [LXX reads Persians]). The fourfold ingathering signals universality.
49.13: rannû šāmayim wĕgîlî 'āreṣ — Sing, O heavens, and rejoice, O earth!. The double imperative summons all creation to celebrate. pĕṣĕḥû hārîm rinnāh — break forth, mountains, into singing!. The ground: YHWH has comforted (niḥam, Pi'el perfect) his people and shown compassion (yĕraḥēm) on his afflicted.
49.14: wattō'mer ṣiyyôn 'ăzāb̠anî YHWH — But Zion said, YHWH has forsaken me. The complaint opens with the perfect 'ăzāb̠anî (has forsaken) and šĕkēḥanî (has forgotten) — both perfects of completed action from Zion's perspective. This is the existential cry of exile: divine abandonment experienced as fact, not merely felt. The divine response in vv. 15-16 answers not with denial but with escalating analogy.
49.15: hătiškāḥ 'iššāh 'ûlāh — Can a woman forget her nursing child?. The rhetorical question assumes the answer: inconceivable. 'ûl (עוּל, nursing infant) from the same root as 'ălāh — to suck. But the argument escalates: gam-'ēlleh tiš-kāḥnāh — even if these were to forget — wĕ'ānōkî lō' 'eškāḥēk — I will not forget you. Crucially, raḥēm (רַחֵם, to have compassion) shares its root with reḥem (רֶחֶם, womb) — the womb-bond is the linguistic ground of divine compassion (raḥamîm).
49.16: hēn 'al-kappayim ḥaqqōtîk — Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands. The verb ḥāqaq (חָקַק, Qal perfect) means to engrave or inscribe — not written in ink but cut into stone. Zion's walls (ḥômōtayik) are continually before YHWH. The idiom is of permanent indelible memory — the opposite of forgetting. Palms are the most exposed, most constantly visible part of the body; what is inscribed there is inescapable.
49.17: mĭharsāyik ûmĕḥarbĕ'ayik mimmēk yēṣē'û — Your builders will outrun your destroyers. The contrast: those who tear down (mĕḥarbĕ'ayik, from חָרַב — to lay waste) will leave, and builders (bōnāyik) will make haste. The Qal imperfect yēṣē'û — they go out — implies departure of enemies. Restoration begins in their absence.
49.18: ś'î-sābîb̠ 'ênayik ûrĕ'î — Lift your eyes, look around and see. All of them gather and come to Zion (kullām niqbĕṣû bā'û lāk). The clothing metaphor: kullām kakkallāh tilbāšîm — you shall put them all on as an ornament, like a bride. Returned children adorning their mother — Zion's restoration is expressed as bridal clothing.
49.19: kî ḥorbōtayik wĕšomĕmōtayik — For your waste places and your desolate places. The ruins of devastation (ḥorbōt) will now be too cramped for population. The Qal imperfect yĭhyû — will be — frames the reversal as certain future. wĕrāḥăqû bĕlā'ayik — those who swallowed you up will be far away.
49.20: 'ôd yō'mĕrû bĕ'oznayik bĕnê šikkulāyik — The children of your bereavement will yet say in your ears. Children born of exile (bĕnê šikkulāyim, from שִׁכֻּלִים — bereavement/childlessness) now cry out for space: ṣar lî hammāqôm gĕšāh-lî wĕ'ēšēbāh — the place is too cramped for me; make room for me to dwell. The paradox: the desolate exile produces a population abundance the land cannot hold.
49.21: wĕ'āmartî bĭlbāb̠ĕk mî yālad lî 'et-'ēlleh — Then you will say in your heart, who bore these for me?. Zion's bewildered question: she was bereaved, exiled, rejected — who are these returned children? The verbs stack up: šĕkûlāh wĕgalmûdāh gōlāh wĕsûrāh — bereaved and barren, captive and set apart. The astonishment of restoration is the point: YHWH's action defies Zion's expectations.
49.22: hinnē 'eśśā' el-gôyim yādî — Behold, I will lift my hand to the nations. The signal (nēs, נֵס — banner/standard) to nations and the ensign to peoples summons: wĕhēbî'û b̠ānayik bĕḥōṣen — they shall bring your sons in their bosom. The nations become servants of Zion's restoration — reversing the exile dynamic without coercion: they carry and bring.
49.23: wĕhāyû mĕlāk̠îm 'ōmĕnāyik — Kings shall be your foster fathers. The social hierarchy inverts: kings nurture ('ōmĕn, guardian/foster parent), queens nurse (mĕyaniqqôt). 'appayim 'ereṣ yišŏtaḥăwû-lāk — they will bow to the ground with their faces toward you — prostration with the face to the earth is the deepest obeisance. The recognition formula closes: wĕyāda'tî kî-'ănî YHWH — and you will know that I am YHWH.
49.24: hăyuqqaḥ mig-gibbôr malqôaḥ — Can the prey be taken from the mighty?. The rhetorical question anticipates a negative answer: of course not — ordinary captives cannot escape the tyrant's grip (wĕšib̠yê ṣaddîq yimmālēṭ, can the captives of the righteous escape?). YHWH's response in v. 25 overturns the assumption entirely.
49.25: wĕgam-šĕbî gibbôr yuqqāḥ — Even the captives of the mighty shall be taken away. The Niphal imperfect yuqqāḥ (from לָקַח) reverses the rhetorical expectation of v. 24. wĕ'et-yĕrîb̠ĕk 'ānōkî 'ārîb̠ — I myself will contend with those who contend with you — emphatic personal pronoun ('ānōkî) stresses divine first-person engagement; the legal verb rîb̠ (contend, bring suit) frames it as YHWH acting as Zion's advocate in the divine court.
49.26: wĕhakaltî 'et-mĕ'annayik 'et-bĕśārām — I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh. The hyperbolically grotesque image — oppressors consuming themselves (ka'āsîs, as new wine / like mead) — conveys the self-destructive collapse of the nations who crushed Zion. The chapter's closing recognition formula is the broadest yet: wĕyāda'û kol-bāśār kî 'ănî YHWH môšî'ĕk — all flesh will know that I am YHWH, your Savior. The Hiphil participle môšî'ĕk (from ישׁע, your Savior) closes the chapter on the universal acknowledgment that the God of Israel is the saving God of all humanity.

50.1: ʾê zeh sēfer kĕrîtût ʾimkĕkem — "where is the certificate of divorce of your mother?" — the rhetorical question assumes no such document exists. sēfer kĕrîtût (H3748) is the technical legal term for a writ of divorce (cf. Deut 24:1,3; Jer 3:8). YHWH challenges Israel to produce evidence of formal divorce — and cannot be produced. Parallel question: ûmî minnōšay ʾăšer mākartî etkem lô — "to which of my creditors did I sell you?" — the second legal scenario (debt-slavery) is also dismissed. hēn baʿăwōnōtêkem nimkartem — "for your iniquities you were sold" — the exile was self-caused through sin, not divine abandonment.
50.2: maddûaʿ bāʾtî wĕʾên ʾîš — "why was there no one when I came?" — the lament that YHWH's arrival for rescue met no response. qārāʾtî wĕʾên ʿōneh — "I called and there was no one who answered" — the failure of responsiveness mirrors the earlier covenant accusation (cf. 65:12; 66:4). Then the counter-challenge: hăqāṣōr qāṣĕrâ yādî mippĕdût — "is my hand too short to ransom?" (pĕdût, H6304, redemption/ransom). The rhetorical question expects "no" — power to save exists; the problem is Israel's unresponsiveness.
50.3: ʾalbîš šāmayim qadrût wĕśaq ʾāśîm kĕsûtām — "I clothe the heavens in blackness and make sackcloth their covering" — YHWH can reverse creation's light. The cosmic darkness imagery inverts the creation of light (Gen 1:3) and anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45). The śaq (sackcloth) of the heavens mirrors the human mourning garment — heaven mourns with the same clothing worn at funerals. YHWH's demonstration of power establishes that the failure of salvation is not YHWH's incapacity but human refusal.
50.4: ʾădōnāy Yhwh nātan lî lĕšôn limmudîm — "the Lord YHWH has given me the tongue of those who are taught/disciples" (limmudîm, H3929, the taught ones — plural of limmûd, from lāmaḏ, to teach/learn). The Servant is specifically characterized as a learner who speaks from instruction received, not from innate authority. lāḏaʿat lāʿût ʾet-yaʿēp dābār — "to know how to sustain with a word him who is weary" — the purpose of the instructed tongue is pastoral: to support the exhausted with speech. yaʿîr lî ʾōzen babbōqer babbōqer — "he wakens my ear morning by morning" — the daily spiritual formation; the repetition of babbōqer (morning) emphasizes the continuity of divine instruction.
50.5: ʾădōnāy Yhwh pātaḥ lî ʾōzen wĕʾānōkî lōʾ mārîtî — "the Lord YHWH opened my ear, and I was not rebellious" (mārar, H4784, to be rebellious). The opening of the ear by YHWH is the prerequisite for obedience — divine action precedes human response. The Servant's obedience is explicitly contrasted with Israel's rebellion in chs 1-39. ʾāḥôr lōʾ nasûgtî — "I did not turn backward" — the refusal to retreat under pressure. The steadfast obedience is a character description, not merely a single act.
50.6: gēwî nātattî lammakkîm ûlĕḥāyay lĕmōrĕṭîm — "I gave my back to those who strike (nākâ), and my cheeks to those who pluck out the beard." The beard-plucking (māraṭ, H4803, to pluck/pull smooth) was a profound degradation in ANE culture — it was done to captive enemies and was considered deeply shameful. pānay lōʾ hissatirtî mikelimmôt wārōq — "I did not hide my face from disgrace and spitting" (rōq, H7536, spittle). The three anatomical elements — back, cheeks, face — span the full range of body-oriented humiliation. Matt 26:67; Mark 14:65; John 19:1 each fulfill one or more of these Servant-song elements at the Passion.
50.7: wĕʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿăzor lî ʿal-kēn lōʾ niklāmtî — "but the Lord YHWH helps me; therefore I was not disgraced" — the divine assistance transforms the suffering from defeat into endurance. ʿal-kēn śamtî pānay kĕḥallāmîš — "therefore I set my face like flint" (ḥallāmîš, H2496, flint — the hardest stone, used for cutting tools). The flint-face is a metaphor for absolute determination in the face of opposition. Luke 9:51 — "he set his face to go to Jerusalem" (estēriksen to prosōpon autou) — echoes this Servant posture in Jesus's commitment to the Passion journey. wĕʾēdaʿ kî-lōʾ ʾēbôš — "and I know I will not be put to shame."
50.8: qārôb maṣdîqî — "near is the one who vindicates me" (ṣādaq in the hiphil, to declare righteous/acquit). The Servant shifts into forensic (courtroom) language — YHWH is his vindicator against accusers. mî yārîḇ ʾittî naʿămdâ yāḥad — "who will contend with me? let us stand up together." The legal challenge is issued to any would-be accuser. mî-baʿal mišpāṭî yiggaš ʾēlay — "who is the master of my lawsuit? let him approach me." Paul uses this forensic logic in Rom 8:33-34: "Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn?" — the Servant's vindication-by-YHWH becomes the basis for the believer's freedom from condemnation in Christ.
50.9: hēn ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿăzor lî mî hûʾ yarširʿēnî — "behold, the Lord YHWH helps me; who will declare me guilty?" — the divine vindication makes human condemnation futile. hēn kullām kabbegaḏ yiblû — "behold, all of them will wear out like a garment" — the accusers/opponents will decay while the Servant endures. ʿāš yōʾklēm — "the moth will eat them" — the same moth-eaten garment imagery as 51:8 (where YHWH's righteousness endures while the nations rot like moth-eaten wool). The language anticipates Heb 1:11 (the heavens wearing out like a garment) contrasted with Christ's permanence.
50.10: mî bākem yĕrēʾ Yhwh šōmēaʿ bĕqôl ʿabdô — "who among you fears YHWH and obeys the voice of his servant?" — the Servant's instruction is now mediated to the community. The one who walks in darkness (ʾăšer hālak ḥăšēkîm wĕʾên nōgah lô, "who walks in darkness and has no light") is called to trust (yiḇṭaḥ) in YHWH's name and rely (yiššāʿēn) on his God. The model of trust-in-darkness echoes Hab 2:4 ("the righteous will live by his faithfulness") and anticipates the NT call to walk by faith, not by sight (2 Cor 5:7).
50.11: hēn kullĕkem qōdĕḥê ʾēš mĕʾazzĕrê zîqôt — "behold, all of you who kindle fire and equip yourselves with burning torches" — those who manufacture their own light as a substitute for trusting in YHWH. lĕkû bĕʾûr ʾeškĕkem — "walk in the light of your fire" — the ironic permission to continue in self-made illumination. miyyādî hāyĕtâ-zōʾt lākem lĕmaʿăṣēbâ tišhābûn — "this you have from my hand: you will lie down in torment" (maʿăṣēbâ, H4620, pain/torment). The self-made fire — human wisdom, political maneuvering, idolatry — becomes the source of final torment. The chapter closes with the judgment oracle against self-reliance as the anti-type to the Servant's trust in YHWH (vv.4-9).

44.1: The address ʿabdî Yaʿaqōb ("my servant Jacob," H5650 + H3290) opens the section with a double covenant identity — Jacob the person and Israel the nation are one addressee. The parallel Yĕšurûn (H3484, "Jeshurun") appears only four times in the OT (Deut 32:15; 33:5, 26; here) — it is a poetic, honorific name for Israel meaning "the Upright One," derived from yāšār (straight/upright). The tension is intentional: YHWH calls Israel by its ideal name even as they have been anything but upright. The MKT "Jeshurun" preserves the archaic honorific precisely because its contrast with Israel's actual history is theologically significant.
44.2: Yōtsĕrĕkā (H3335, "your maker/potter") from the root yātsar (to form, as a potter forms clay) is paired with ʿōśĕkā ("your maker," from ʿāśāh) — two different verbs for making, emphasizing both the creative forming and the purposive making. The phrase mibbeṭen (H990, "from the womb") grounds YHWH's involvement at the very origin of Israel's existence, before self-consciousness and choice. ʾal-tîrāʾ ("do not fear") is the covenant reassurance formula — the same formula used at theophanies (Gen 15:1; 26:24) now applied to the returning exiles. The MKT "who formed you from the womb: do not be afraid" keeps the participial construction to connect making with the command not to fear.
44.3: The Spirit-outpouring promise: ʾetsōq-rûaḥ (H3332 + H7307, "I will pour out [the] Spirit") uses yātsaq, the verb for pouring water or oil — a liquid image for divine Spirit. Zarʿekā (H2233, "your offspring/seed") and tsĕʾetsāʾêkā (H6631, "your descendants") carry both biological and covenantal freight: the Spirit is promised to the physical line that carries the covenant. This verse is the river behind the Pentecost: Acts 2:17-18 cites Joel 2:28-29 for the same Spirit-outpouring promise, and Isaiah 44:3 stands behind both — the "offspring" who receive the Spirit are ultimately fulfilled in the new covenant community of Pentecost. The MKT "I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring" preserves the liquid-pour image.
44.4: The simile: ḵĕʿāsāb (H6212, "like grass") and bên yĕʾōrîm ("beside streams of water") — the covenant community flourishing like water-fed vegetation after the desert of exile. Aravim (H6155, "willows/poplars") beside mĕyim (water) recalls the water-beside-willows imagery of Psalm 137:2 inverted: there, the harps were hung on the willows in silence; here, the people spring up beside the water-channels in abundance. The image answers exile with fertility — the same trees of mourning become the landscape of restoration. The MKT "they will spring up among the grass, like willows by flowing streams" captures the abundant, water-fed spontaneity.
44.5: The covenant belonging verbs: yiqrāʾ bĕšem YHWH (H7121, "will call on the name of the LORD"), yitkaneh bĕšem Yiśrāʾēl (H3655, "will take the name of Israel" or "surname himself Israel") — the post-exile return of Gentile adherents who will identify with Israel's God and Israel's name. The four phrases trace an escalating commitment: "I belong to the LORD" / "call by the name of Jacob" / "write on his hand 'To YHWH'" / "name himself Israel." 1 Peter 2:9 ("you are a chosen people... God's own possession") applies precisely this covenant-belonging language to the new covenant community. The "hand" inscription evokes the mezuzah-and-phylactery tradition but now written on the person, not the doorpost.
44.6: The theological summit of this unit: ʾānî rīʾšôn wĕʾānî ʾaḥărôn (H7223 + H314, "I am the First and I am the Last") — YHWH's claim to encompass all of time, from beginning to end. The construction is emphatic: both clauses begin with ʾānî (I), making the personal divine assertion unmistakable. This exact formula is taken up verbatim by the risen Christ in Revelation 1:17 ("I am the First and the Last"), 2:8, and 22:13 — a direct, deliberate transfer of YHWH's exclusive temporal identity to Jesus. Ûmibbaltî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm (H1107 + H410, "and besides me there is no God") closes the verse with absolute monotheism: the incomparability challenge of chapter 40 reaches its sharpest formulation. The MKT "I am the First and I am the Last; besides me there is no God" preserves the dual-pronoun emphasis of the Hebrew.
44.7: The courtroom challenge: mî-kāmônî yiqrāʾ ("who is like me, let him call out") — YHWH issues a challenge to any rival deity to demonstrate the same temporal comprehensiveness. Yagîdehā ("let him declare it") and yaʿarkehā ("let him set it in order for me") are legal-courtroom terms — the trial imagery begun in chapters 40-41 continues. ûmê-śūmî ʿam-ʿôlām ("and since I appointed the ancient people") — since YHWH established Israel as the covenant community, no rival deity has matched the historical claim. The question "who has announced from of old the things to come?" is the challenge no idol can answer: prediction + fulfillment is YHWH's unique credential.
44.8: ʾal-tirĕḥādû wĕʾal-tîrāʾû ("do not tremble and do not fear") — the double negative command repeats the covenant reassurance with the stronger verb rāḥad (to tremble/be terrified) before the familiar yārēʾ (fear). Hǎlōʾ mēʾāz hišmaʿtîkā ("Have I not told you from of old and declared it?") — YHWH points to his own prophetic track record as the evidence for faith. The climax: wĕʾattěm ʿēdāy ("you are my witnesses") — Israel is the witness stand that YHWH's self-revelation has historical validity. ʾên tsûr biltî ("there is no rock besides me") — the rock metaphor (H6697) recalls Deut 32:4, 31 (YHWH as Rock), the foundation metaphor that Paul applies to Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4.
44.9: The idol polemic begins: yōtserê-pesel (H6459, "those who form a carved image") — pesel is the carved/hewn idol, from pāsal (to hew/carve). The verdict: kullām tōhû ("they are all worthless/chaos," H8414) — the same word used in Genesis 1:2 (tōhû wābōhû, "formless and void") and repeatedly in Deutero-Isaiah for worthlessness. The idol-makers' preferred things are worthless because ʿēdêhem ("their witnesses") neither see nor know (lōʾ yirʾû wĕlōʾ yēdāʿû) — the idols' inability to perceive is transferred back to their makers. The MKT "all who make idols are worthless" preserves the comprehensive kullām.
44.10: Mî-yātsar ʾēl ("who forms a god?") — the verb yātsar is now applied to idol-making: the same word used for YHWH "forming" Israel (v2) is used sarcastically for human "forming" of gods. Wĕfesēl nāsak (H5262, "pours out a metal image") — nāsak is the verb for casting metal, so the idol is both carved (pesel) and cast (nāsak). The question lĕbiltî hôʿîl ("for nothing useful") — the idol-maker's labor produces something that yields no benefit (yāʿal, H3276). The MKT "who forms a god, who pours out a metal image — for no useful purpose?" captures the rhetorical incredulity.
44.11: The craftsmen's solidarity: ḥăbērāyw (H2270, "his companions/associates") — the idol-makers form a guild, and they will all be shamed together (yēbōšû). Wĕhārāšîm hēmmāh meʾādām ("the craftsmen — they are only men") — the demythologizing move: behind the idol is not a divine craftsman but an ordinary human worker. Yitqabbětsû kullām yaʿămdû ("let them all assemble and take their stand") — court language again: even if every idol-maker stands together, they will be terrified and shamed. The MKT "let them all assemble and take their stand" preserves the legal-assembly sense of the challenge.
44.12: The blacksmith sequence: ḥārāš-barzel (H6213 + H1270, "iron craftsman") works with tongs (maʿătsād, H4621) and hammers (pattîš, H6360) in coals. The human vulnerability appears: wĕrāʿēb wĕʾên-kōaḥ ("he grows hungry and has no strength") — the god-maker gets tired and thirsty. The irony is stark: the deity he manufactures is immortal in the devotees' minds, but the craftsman who made it needs food and water to continue working. The MKT "he grows hungry and his strength fails, he drinks no water and is faint" captures the physical exhaustion that undercuts the pretension of the entire enterprise.
44.13: The carpenter sequence: ḥārāš-ʿētsîm (H6086, "carpenter/woodworker") stretches a measuring line (qāw, H6957), marks with a stylus (śered, H8279), shapes with planes (maqtsûʿôt, H4741), and outlines with a compass (mĕḥûgāh, H4230). The craftsman tools of precision — normally associated with skilled work — are applied to the making of what will become an idol. The result is kĕtōʾar ʾîš ("in the form of a human being") and kĕtipʾeret ʾādām ("like the beauty of a human") — the god is modeled on the human, not the reverse. This is the inverse of Genesis 1:26 (man made in God's image): the idol-maker makes god in man's image. Colossians 1:15 (Christ as "the image of the invisible God") is the answer to this perverse inversion.
44.14: The tree-selection process: the craftsman cuts down ʾerez (H730, cedar), takes tirzāh (perhaps cypress or holm oak, H8645), and ʾallôn (H437, oak) — trees that YHWH caused to grow over the years in the forest. The contrast is stark: YHWH is the one who planted the forests (v24, "I alone stretched out the heavens"); the craftsman harvests YHWH's creation to make a rival to YHWH. The wood that becomes the idol started as God's forest — it was raining on it, YHWH was sustaining it, and the craftsman then turns it into his "god." The MKT "he plants a cedar, and the rain nourishes it" preserves the divine-nurturance irony before the cutting.
44.15: The pivotal verse of the idol-polemic section: the wood is used for two purposes simultaneously. Yihyeh lĕʾādām lĕbāʿēr ("it becomes fuel for burning") — half burned for warmth (ḥammôtî, H2552, "I am warm"). And from the same tree: wĕʾap-ʿāśāh-ʾēl ("and he also makes a god") — the idol. The climax: yiśtaḥăweh lô ("he bows down to it"). The MKT "half of it he burns in the fire; over the half he eats meat" captures the mundane before the sacred act — the god is made from the same wood that cooked dinner. The theological point is devastating: the idol is a byproduct of fuel economy.
44.16: The detail: ḥeṣyô śārap bāʾēš ("half of it he burned in the fire") — on the literal half, he ʾāḵal bāśār ("ate meat") and was śābaʿ (satisfied/full). He warmed himself (ḥammōtî) and said: ʾāḥ ḥammōtî rāʾîtî ʾûr ("Ah, I am warm, I have seen the fire!"). The exclamation of ordinary warmth — "Ah, I see the fire!" — immediately precedes idol-worship in the next verse. The ordinary and the sacred are intertwined without any sense of incongruity in the craftsman's mind: that is the satire's point. The MKT "Ah, I am warm, I have seen the fire" preserves the artless exclamation that makes the subsequent worship so jarring.
44.17: The pivot: wĕšĕʾērîtô lĕʾēl ʿāśāh ("and the rest of it he makes into a god") — the residue of the wood becomes the deity. He yiśtaḥăweh lô ("bows down to it") and yitpallēl ʾēlāyw ("prays to it"). The prayer content: hatstsilēnî kî ʾēlî ʾāttāh ("save me, for you are my god!" H5337 + H410 + H859) — the most intimate covenant address (ʾēlî ʾāttāh, "my God, you are") directed at a piece of firewood. The irony is absolute: the saving-cry that belongs exclusively to YHWH is addressed to lumber. The MKT "save me, for you are my god!" preserves the direct address and urgency of the petition.
44.18: Lōʾ yādĕʿû wĕlōʾ yābînû ("they do not know and they do not understand") — the double epistemic failure that enables idol-worship. The reason: kî ṭāḥ mĕrĕʾôt ʿênêhem (H2902, "for their eyes are smeared/plastered over") — the verb ṭāḥ (to plaster/overlay) is used for coating walls; the eyes of the idol-worshiper are sealed with spiritual plaster, unable to see. Millĕhaskîl libbôtām ("their hearts from understanding") — the heart, the seat of discernment (śākal, H7919), is similarly sealed. 2 Corinthians 4:4: "the god of this age has blinded the minds of unbelievers, so that they cannot see the light of the gospel that displays the glory of Christ." The blindness of the idol-worshiper anticipates the blindness Paul describes.
44.19: The failed self-reflection: wĕlōʾ-yāšîb ʾel-libbô (H7725, "and no one calls it back to his heart/mind") — the idol-worshiper does not perform the reflective act of examining what he is doing. Dāʿat wĕlōʾ-tĕḇûnāh (H1847 + H8394, "neither knowledge nor discernment") — both cognitive capacities are absent. The quoted self-reflection that does not happen: ʾĕśāppenî tôʿabāh hûʾ (H8441, "surely it is an abomination!") — the word tôʿabāh is the strongest OT term for something utterly offensive to YHWH (used for the worst covenant violations). The idol-worshiper cannot bring himself to say what is obviously true: what he is doing is an abomination. The MKT "this thing in my hand is a detestable thing!" uses "detestable" for tôʿabāh to preserve the theological weight.
44.20: The verdict on the idol-worshiper: rōʿeh ʾēper (H7462 + H665, "feeds on ashes") — the shepherd-verb (rāʿāh) applied to ash-feeding; the one who should tend real flocks (like YHWH the shepherd) tends nothing but the residue of burned wood. Lēb hûttal (H5377, "a deceived heart") has led him astray — the heart that was not allowed to reflect (v19) has been deceived. Wĕlōʾ-yatssîl ʾet-napšô (H5337, "he cannot save his soul/himself") — the saving-cry of v17 ("save me!") goes unanswered because the idol cannot save. ʾAlōʾ šeqer bîmînî ("Is there not a lie in my right hand?") — the right hand that holds the idol holds a lie. The MKT "he feeds on ashes, a deluded heart has led him astray" captures both the feeding image and the heart-deception.
44.21: The turn to redemption: zĕḵor-ʾēlleh Yaʿaqōb wĕYiśrāʾēl ("Remember these things, O Jacob, O Israel") — after the idol polemic, YHWH commands Israel to remember (as opposed to the idol-worshiper who cannot remember, v19). ʿAbdî-ʾāttāh ("you are my servant") — the servant-identity counters the idol-worshiper's false servant relationship: Israel is servant to the living God. Yĕtsartîkā ʿebed-lî ʾāttāh ("I formed you, you are my servant") — the forming-language of v2 (yātsartîkā) recurs, grounding the servant relationship in the creative act. Yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-tiššāḵēḥ bî ("Israel, you will not be forgotten by me") — the covenant fidelity: YHWH does not forget, even if Israel has.
44.22: The forgiveness declaration: māḥîtî ḵeʿāb pĕšāʿekā (H4229 + H5645 + H6588, "I have swept away your transgressions like a cloud/thick mist") — māḥāh means to wipe away, blot out; ʿāb is a thick cloud or cloud mass. Ûḵeʿānān ḥattōʾtêkā ("and like a cloud your sins") — both pĕšāʿ (willful transgression) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin as failure/missing the mark) are swept away. The simile is perfect: morning mist dissolves completely and without trace — so the transgressions are gone. Šûḇāh ʾēlay kî gĕʾaltîkā ("Return to me, for I have redeemed you") — the call to return (šûḇ, H7725, repentance) is grounded in the already-accomplished redemption (gāʾal, H1350, to redeem as kinsman-redeemer). 2 Corinthians 5:19: "God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ, not counting people's sins against them." The cloud-swept forgiveness of Isaiah 44:22 is the same gospel Paul announces.
44.23: The creation doxology: rannû šāmayim ("sing for joy, O heavens") — sky, depths of the earth, mountains, forest, and every tree called to jubilate. This is the cosmic praise that follows the forgiveness of v22: the new exodus is a new creation event. The reason: kî-gāʾal YHWH Yaʿaqōb ("for the LORD has redeemed Jacob") and bĕYiśrāʾēl yitpāʾār ("and in Israel he will be glorified/beautified"). The verb pāʾar (H6286, to beautify/glorify) is YHWH being glorified in the act of Israel's redemption. Romans 8:22 places creation's groaning in the context of the redemption that will release it — the creation-rejoicing of Isaiah 44:23 is the answered groan of Romans 8.
44.24: YHWH's creation credentials: nōṭeh šāmayim lĕbaddî (H5186 + H905, "who stretches out the heavens alone/by myself") — lĕbaddî is emphatic: by myself, alone, without assistance. Rōqaʿ hāʾārets mî ʾittî ("who spread out the earth — who was with me?") — the rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. The Creator who acts alone is also the Redeemer who acts alone (v22). John 1:3: "Through him all things were made; without him nothing was made that has been made" — the alone-Creator of Isaiah 44:24 is the Logos through whom all creation came into being. The MKT "who alone stretched out the heavens, who spread out the earth by myself" maintains the double assertion of solitary action.
44.25: Mēpēr ʾōtôt baddîm (H6331 + H226 + H907, "who frustrates the omens/signs of liars/babblers") — baddîm refers to false prophets or soothsayers who speak empty things.
