Wĕqōsĕmîm yĕhōlēl (H7081 + H1984, "who makes diviners mad/foolish") — the Babylonian diviners, with their elaborate divination systems, are made into fools. Mēšîb ḥăḵāmîm ʾāḥôr ("who turns wise men back") — the wisdom of the empire is reversed by YHWH's action. 1 Corinthians 1:20: "Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?" Paul's critique of worldly wisdom stands in the same tradition: what looks like divine frustration of Babylon's wise men is a pattern that repeats in the cross.
44.26: Meqîm dĕbar ʿabdô (H6965, "who confirms the word of his servant") — the same verb (qûm) used in 46:10 for the divine plan that "stands": here it is applied to the prophetic word. The servant whose word YHWH confirms is the prophetic office (Isaiah himself), and the particular word being confirmed is the prediction of return and temple rebuilding. ʾāmar lîrûšālayim tûšāb ("says of Jerusalem, 'It shall be inhabited'") — the city that now lies in ruins is addressed by YHWH as the city that will be rebuilt. The prophetic word about restoration participates in the same "confirm/stand" language that applies to all of YHWH's counsel — his words do not return empty (55:11).
44.27: ʾĀmēr laṣṣûlāh ḥarābî ("who says to the deep, 'Dry up!'" H6723) — YHWH commands the deep (tsûlāh, H6683, the ocean abyss or deep waters) to dry up so that the rivers can be crossed. This is the new-exodus language: as YHWH dried the Red Sea (Exod 14:21-22) and later the Jordan (Josh 3:16-17), he will again dry the waters for the return from Babylon. Wĕnahărōtêkā ʾôḇîš ("and I will dry up your rivers") — the plural rivers may refer to the rivers of Babylon itself, or to the literal rivers crossed on the return journey. The MKT "who says to the deep, 'Dry up, I will make your rivers dry'" keeps the direct address to the waters.
44.28: ʾĀmēr lĕKōreš rōʿî (H3566 + H7462, "who says to Cyrus, 'He is my shepherd'") — the naming of Cyrus over 150 years before his reign is the chapter's most stunning historical prediction. But the theological weight falls on the word rōʿî ("my shepherd") — the same word as Psalm 23:1 ("The LORD is my shepherd," YHWH rōʿî). YHWH calls Cyrus his shepherd in precisely the sense that YHWH himself is shepherd: Cyrus will "tend" (accomplish the pastoral care of) YHWH's people by sending them home. Yiššālēm ḥeptsî (H7999, "will accomplish all my pleasure/purpose") — the divine purpose-language connects to the ʿēṣāh of 46:10. The final phrase: yûssad hêḵāl ("let the temple be founded/laid") — the temple-founding is the specific commission of the shepherd-king. John 10:11 ("I am the good shepherd") takes up the shepherd-king role in its fullness: Cyrus is a type of the one who is both king and shepherd in the fullest sense, who gives his life for the sheep.

56.1: šimrû mišpāṭ waʿăśû ṣĕdāqâ — "keep justice and do righteousness" — the ethical imperative that opens the chapter and frames the entire inclusion oracle: the welcome of foreigners and eunuchs is grounded in covenant obedience, not merely theological sentiment. kî qĕrôbâ yĕšûʿātî lābôʾ wĕṣidqātî lĕhiggālôt — "for my salvation (yĕšûʿâ) is near to come, and my righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ) to be revealed" — the eschatological imminence is the motivation for present ethical action. The pairing of yĕšûʿâ (salvation) and ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) — both expressing YHWH's character and act — is characteristic of Isaiah's theology of the coming restoration.
56.2: ʾašrê ʾĕnôš yaʿăśeh zōʾt — "blessed is the man who does this" — the beatitude structure (cf. Ps 1:1; 41:1; Matt 5:3-11) grounds the blessing in action, not status. ûben-ʾādām yĕḥăzeq bāh — "and the son of man who holds fast to it" (ḥāzaq, to be strong, to grasp firmly). šōmēr šabbāt mēḥallĕlô wĕšōmēr yādô mēʿăśôt kol-rāʿ — "keeping the Sabbath without profaning it, and keeping his hand from doing any evil" — the Sabbath-keeping (šāmar šabbāt) is the specific marker of covenant participation for the expanded community of vv.3-8. The Sabbath is the weekly enacted covenant sign.
56.3: wĕʾal-yōʾmar ben-hannēkār hannilweh ʾel-Yhwh lēʾmōr habdēl yabdîlēnî Yhwh mēʿal ʿammô — "let not the foreigner (ben-hannēkār, son of the foreign land) who has joined himself to YHWH say, 'YHWH will surely separate me from his people.'" The fear being addressed is the legal exclusion of Deut 23:3-8 (Ammonites, Moabites, and others excluded from the assembly). wĕʾal-yōʾmar hassārîs hēn ʾănî ʿēṣ yābēš — "and let not the eunuch say, 'Behold, I am a dry tree.'" The eunuch (sārîs, H5631) is excluded by Deut 23:1; the "dry tree" metaphor expresses his condition of having no descendants and therefore no memorial.
56.4: kî-kōh ʾāmar Yhwh lassārîsîm ʾăšer yišmĕrû ʾet-šabbĕtôtay — "for thus says YHWH to the eunuchs who keep my Sabbaths" — the response to the eunuch's fear is direct divine address. ûbāḥărû baʾăšer ḥāpāṣtî ûmaḥăzîqîm bibĕrîtî — "who choose what pleases me and hold fast my covenant" — the three criteria (Sabbath-keeping, choosing what pleases YHWH, holding fast the covenant) define the community membership in terms of character and allegiance rather than birth status. The inclusion is based on obedience, not on reversing the physical condition.
56.5: wĕnātattî lāhem bĕbêtî ûbĕḥômōtay yāḏ wāšēm — "I will give them in my house and within my walls a monument and a name (yāḏ wāšēm)" — yāḏ (hand/memorial stele) and šēm (name) together form the idea of a permanent public monument — more enduring than biological descendants. ṭôḇ mibbānîm ûmibbānôt — "better than sons and daughters" — the reversal is complete: what the eunuch lacked (children as memorial) is exceeded by what YHWH gives. šēm ʿôlām ʾettēn-lô ʾăšer lōʾ yikkārēt — "an everlasting name I will give him that shall not be cut off" — the permanent name (šēm ʿôlām) is the ultimate reversal of the eunuch's fear of namelessness.
56.6: ûbĕnê hannēkār hannilwîm ʿal-Yhwh lĕšārĕtô — "and the foreigners who join themselves to YHWH, to serve him" — lāwâ (to join oneself to, to attach to) is the same root as "Levi" — the joining of foreigners to YHWH mirrors the tribal name that meant "attached." ûlĕʾahăbâ ʾet-šēm Yhwh lihyôt lô laʿăḇādîm — "to love the name of YHWH, and to be his servants." kol-šōmēr šabbāt mēḥallĕlô ûmaḥăzîqîm bibĕrîtî — "everyone who keeps the Sabbath without profaning it and holds fast my covenant" — the Sabbath is again the covenant marker for this extended community.
56.7: wahăḇîʾôtîm ʾel-har qoḏšî wĕśimmahttîm bĕbêt tĕpillātî — "I will bring them to my holy mountain and make them joyful in my house of prayer" — the phrase bêt tĕpillātî (my house of prayer) is the crux. ʿôlōtêhem wĕzibḥêhem lĕrāṣôn ʿal-mizbĕḥî — "their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar." kî bêtî bêt-tĕpillâ yiqqārēʾ lĕkol-hāʿammîm — "for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples (kol-hāʿammîm)" — the universalist climax. Jesus quotes this phrase in Mark 11:17; Matt 21:13; Luke 19:46 when cleansing the temple, indicting the traders who have turned the house of all-peoples prayer into a commercial operation, and specifically a den of robbers (mĕʿarat pārīṣîm) drawn from Jer 7:11.
56.8: nĕʾum ʾădōnāy Yhwh mĕqabbēṣ nidḥê yiśrāʾēl — "oracle of the Lord YHWH, who gathers the outcasts (nidḥê, scattered/banished ones) of Israel" — the ingathering is already underway for Israel. ʿôd ʾăqabbēṣ ʿālāyw lĕniqbāṣāyw — "I will gather still more to him besides those already gathered" — the gathering extends beyond Israel to the foreigners and eunuchs of vv.3-7. The word ʿôd (still/again) signals that the community expansion is not finished; YHWH will continue adding to his people.
56.9: kol-ḥayyat haśśādeh ʾĕtāyû lĕʾākōl kol-ḥayyat bayyāʿar — "all you beasts of the field, come to eat; all you beasts in the forest." The sudden shift from the inclusion oracle (vv.1-8) to a summons to wild animals is jarring. The animals are summoned to consume something — what? The context (vv.10-12) identifies the corrupt leaders as the target: the watchmen who fail, the shepherds who only think about their own appetite, are abandoned to become the prey of wild beasts.
56.10: ṣōpāyw ʿiwwĕrîm kullām — "his watchmen (ṣōpeh, H6822, lookout/sentinel) are all blind" — the failure of Israel's spiritual leadership. lōʾ yāḏĕʿû kullām — "they have no knowledge at all." kullām kĕlāḇîm ʾillĕmîm lōʾ yûklû linbōaḥ — "they are all dumb dogs that cannot bark" — the watchdog who cannot bark is the most specific failure: the guard animal's one function is warning, and it fails to give warning. The blind watchman and the silent watchdog are images of pastoral and prophetic failure. hōzîm šōkĕḇîm ʾōhăḇê lānûm — "dreaming, lying down, loving to slumber."
56.11: wĕhakkĕlāḇîm ʿazzê-nepeš lōʾ yāḏĕʿû śobʿâ — "the dogs have a mighty appetite (ʿazzê-nepeš, strong of soul/appetite) and are never satisfied" — the insatiable desire is the defining character of the failed leadership. wĕhēmmâ rōʿîm lōʾ yāḏĕʿû hāḇîn — "and they are shepherds who have no understanding." The word rōʿeh (shepherd, leader/pastor) connects to the Good Shepherd imagery of 40:11: the shepherd who gathers lambs contrasted with these shepherds who think only of their own appetite. kullām lĕdarkām pānû ʾîš lĕbiṣʿô miqṣēhû — "they have all turned to their own way, each one to his own gain (beṣāʿ, unjust gain, plunder)."
56.12: lĕkû ʾeqqĕḥâ-yāyin wĕnisĕbĕʾâ šēkār — "Come, let me get wine and let us drink strong drink" — the self-invitation of the corrupt leaders to continued indulgence. wĕhāyâ kāzeh māḥār yôm gādôl yōtēr mĕʾōd — "and tomorrow will be like this day, even much more so" — the presumptuous confidence in continuation (cf. Luke 12:19-20's rich fool: "Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years"). The chapter ends with this damning characterization of Israel's leadership: blind watchmen, insatiable dogs, presumptuous drinkers — the antithesis of the obedient, covenant-keeping foreigners and eunuchs of vv.1-8 who receive YHWH's welcome.

57.1: hazzaddîq 'ābad — the righteous one perishes. The verb 'ābad (אָבַד) typically denotes destruction or premature loss — the same word used for the wicked perishing in judgment. Its application to the righteous is deliberately jarring. wĕ'ênennû mêbîn — and no one takes it to heart — the root bîn (to understand/consider) in Hiphil negated: the community lacks the spiritual perception to recognize what the righteous man's death means.
57.2: yābô' šālôm — he enters into peace. The Qal imperfect yābô' frames entry into peace as the righteous one's destination. yānûaḥû 'al-miškĕbôtām — they rest on their beds — the plural miškĕbôt (beds) generalizes to the class of the upright. hōlēk nĕkōḥô — who walk in their uprightness — the participle describes continuous integrity of life. The word šālôm here (v. 2) and in vv. 19 and 21 creates a structural inclusio: peace is the righteous one's entrance; peace is what YHWH creates and proclaims; peace is what the wicked cannot have.
57.3: wĕ'attem qĭrĕbû hēnnāh — but you, draw near here. The sharp turn from consolation to accusation. The addressees are bĕnê 'ōnenāh — sons of a sorceress — the root 'ānan (to practice divination). The pair zera' mĕnā'ēp̄ wattizĕnèh — offspring of the adulterer and the loose woman — uses both cultic and sexual unfaithfulness language. In Hebrew prophetic idiom, covenant violation is adultery; the charge layers literal and figurative dimensions.
57.4: 'al-mî tit'annāgû — Against whom do you mock?. The Hithpa'el tit'annāgû (from עָנַג — to delight/sport with) is ironic: those who mock and stick out the tongue (tarhîbû peh ta'ărîkû lāšôn) against the righteous are themselves children of transgression (yaldê-pèša') and offspring of deceit (zera' šāqer).
57.5: hannĕḥāmîm bā'ēlîm taḥat kol-'ēṣ ra'ănān — burning with lust among the oaks, under every green tree. The participle hannĕḥāmîm (from חָמַם — to be hot, heated with passion) indicates ongoing ritual acts at high places. The phrase taḥat kol-'ēṣ ra'ănān — under every leafy/green tree — is formulaic for Canaanite fertility cult (cf. Deut 12:2; 2 Kgs 17:10). šōḥaṭê hayyĕlādîm — slaughtering children — the Pi'el participle of שָׁחַט (to slaughter/butcher, the term for ritual killing) applied to child sacrifice in the ravines (neḥālîm).
57.6: bĕḥĕlĕqê-naḥal ḥelqēk — among the smooth stones of the stream is your portion. The smooth stream stones (ḥĕlāqê naḥal) were apparently used as cult objects — their smoothness (from ḥālaq, smooth) creates a wordplay with ḥēleq (portion/lot). YHWH's accusation is ironic: they have chosen smooth stones as their portion rather than YHWH himself. gam-lāhem šāpaktĕ nèsek — to these you have poured out a drink offering (nesek). The irony intensifies: YHWH asks whether he should relent at this (hă'al-'ēlleh 'ennāḥēm)?
57.7: 'al-har-gābōah ûniśśā' śamtĕ miškāb̠ēk — on a high and lofty mountain you have made your bed. The high mountain is the cultic high place (bāmāh). The word miškāb̠ (bed, couch) used in a cultic context carries the double meaning of both the altar-bed and the sexual charge of the fertility rite. šām 'ālît liz-bôaḥ zāb̠aḥ — there you went up to offer sacrifice.
57.8: 'aḥar haddèlet wĕhamĕzûzāh śamtĕ zikrōnēk — behind the door and the doorpost you have set up your memorial. The mĕzûzāh (doorpost, from which the modern mezuzah takes its name) is where the Shema was to be inscribed (Deut 6:9). Here pagan cultic symbols are placed where YHWH's word belongs — the most intimate domestic space perverted. kî mē'ittî gālît — for you have uncovered yourself away from me — the verb gālāh (to uncover/expose) is the standard term for sexual exposure and for exile; both meanings are active.
57.9: wattāšûrî lamèlek bāšèmen — you went to the king with oil. The identity of hammelek (the king) is debated — possibly Molech (מֹלֶךְ, the deity requiring child sacrifice) or a foreign monarch. wattarbî mĕrĕqqāḥayik — and multiplied your perfumes — lavish preparation for an illicit liaison. wattišlĕḥî ṣir-rayik 'ad-mērāḥôq — sent your envoys far off, down to Sheol.
57.10: bĕrōb̠ darkēk yāga'tĕ — in the length of your journey you grew weary. The same verb yāga' (to toil/grow weary) used by the Servant in 49:4 — but there in noble perseverance; here in futile idolatrous pilgrimage. lō' 'āmartĕ nôaš — you did not say, 'It is hopeless' — the noun nôaš (despair, hopelessness) is from אָנַשׁ; they found the energy to persist in apostasy. ḥayyat yādēk māṣā't — the life of your hand you found (i.e., enough to keep going), so you were not weakened.
57.11: wĕ'et-mî dā'agtĕ wattîrĕ'î — Whom did you dread and fear?. The rhetorical question exposes the root of apostasy: fear of the wrong party. wĕ'ōtî lō' zāk̠artĕ — me you did not remember — the verb zākar (to remember, with covenantal force) negated. The silence of YHWH is named: hălō' 'ănî maḥašèh ûmē'ōlām — Have I not been silent, even for a long time? — divine patience misread as absence.
57.12: 'ănî 'aggîd ṣidqātēk — I will declare your righteousness. The irony is severe: YHWH will declare their righteousness — but the declaration will expose it as empty. wĕ'et-ma'ăśayik wĕlō' yô'îlûk — but your works will not profit you — the Hiphil of יָעַל (to be profitable) negated; the cultic efforts yield nothing.
57.13: bĕzā'aqēk yaṣṣîlûk qibbuṣayik — when you cry out, let your collection of idols deliver you. The sarcasm is pointed: let the gathered idols (qibbuṣayik, your collected things) rescue them. wĕyiśśā' kūllām rûaḥ — all of them will be carried off by the wind — the wind (rûaḥ) takes the idols away; the idols cannot save even themselves. The contrast: wĕhahôsèh bî yinḥal-'āreṣ — but the one who takes refuge in me will inherit the land — the covenant inheritance language.
57.14: sōllû sōllû paqqû-dārek — Build up, build up, prepare the way!. The doubled imperative sōllû sōllû (Pi'el of סָלַל, to cast up a highway) signals urgency; cf. 40:3. hārîmû mikšôl middèrek 'ammî — remove every obstacle from the way of my people. The divine highway motif shifts register from judgment (vv. 3–13) to restoration. The second great movement of the chapter begins here.
57.15: kî kōh 'āmar rām wĕniśśā' — for thus says the high and lofty one. The phrase rām wĕniśśā' — high and lifted up — is identical to Isa 6:1 ('ădōnāy yōšēb̠ 'al-kisse' rām wĕniśśā'), the vision of YHWH's throne. šōkēn 'ad — who inhabits eternity — the participle šōkēn (dwelling, tabernacling) with 'ad (eternity/forever). The divine paradox: wĕ'et-dakkā' ûšĕpal-rûaḥ — yet also with the crushed and lowly of spirit — YHWH dwells in the utterly high place AND in the contrite human heart simultaneously. The infinitives of purpose: lĕhaḥăyôt rûaḥ šĕpālîm wĕlĕhaḥăyôt lēb̠ nidkā'îm — to revive the spirit of the lowly and revive the heart of the crushed.
57.16: kî lō' lĕ'ôlām 'ārîb̠ — for I will not contend forever. The verb rîb̠ (to contend, bring a legal dispute) is the same word used when YHWH wages a covenant lawsuit against Israel (cf. 49:25). Here its limit is asserted. wĕlō' lāneṣaḥ 'eqṣôp̄ — I will not always be angry. The reason: kî-rûaḥ mĕlĕpānāy ya'ătôp̄ — for a spirit would faint before me — if YHWH sustained unrelenting anger, the creature could not endure; mercy is woven into the structure of creation.
57.17: bĕ'awōn biṣ'ô qāṣaptî — because of the iniquity of his unjust gain I was angry. The noun beṣa' (בֶּצַע) — unjust gain, dishonest profit — is the cause of divine anger (qāṣap̄, Qal perfect). wĕ'akkēhû hāstēr wĕ'eqṣōp̄ — I struck him; I hid myself and was angry — the hiding of YHWH's face (hāstēr, Hiphil infinitive absolute of סָתַר) is used as divine discipline (cf. 8:17; 54:8).
57.18: dĕrāk̠āyw rā'îtî wĕ'erp̄ā'ēhû — I have seen his ways, yet I will heal him. The sequence is theologically striking: YHWH sees the wayward path and heals regardless. The verb rāp̄ā' (to heal) in Qal imperfect ('erp̄ā'ēhû) — future decisive healing. wĕ'anḥēhû wĕ'ăšallēm nĕḥumîm lô — I will lead him and restore comfort to him — the Pi'el 'anḥēhû (guide/lead) and Piel 'ăšallēm (restore/repay) frame restoration as both direction and restitution.
57.19: bôrē' nîb̠ śĕpātāyim — creating the fruit of lips. The verb bārā' (to create, the Genesis 1 word) applied to the divine speech-act: YHWH creates what the lips speak. šālôm šālôm — the doubled noun is an intensifying superlative: complete peace. Recipients: lārāḥôq wĕlaqārôb̠ — to the far and the near — universalizing the scope of peace. 'āmar YHWH ûrĕpā'tîw — says YHWH, and I will heal him — the healing of v. 18 now embedded in the proclamation.
57.20: wĕhārĕšā'îm kayyām nigrāš — but the wicked are like the tossing sea. The Niphal participle nigrāš (from גָּרַשׁ — to drive/toss out) describes chronic unrest. kî hašqēṭ lō' yûkal — for it cannot be quiet — the sea of wickedness has no capacity for šālôm. wayyigrĕšû mêmāyw rèfeš wāṭît — its waters toss up mire (rèfeš) and mud (ṭît). The image is the exact antithesis of the living water and rest offered in vv. 18-19.
57.21: 'ên šālôm 'āmar 'ĕlōhāy lārĕšā'îm — There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. This is the chapter's closing line and its structurally decisive verdict — mirroring 48:22 almost exactly. The inclusio with v. 2 (šālôm... yābô' — the righteous enter peace) is now closed: peace is the exclusive inheritance of the righteous. The wicked cannot receive what YHWH creates (bôrē' šālôm šālôm, v. 19) because they have not turned from the ways that preclude it. The divine title here is 'ĕlōhāy — my God — the prophet's own intimate relationship underscoring the authority of the verdict.

58.1: qĕrā' bĕgārôn — cry aloud, literally cry in the throat. The noun gārôn (גָּרוֹן, throat/neck) rather than simply qôl (voice) — this is visceral, full-bodied proclamation. kaššôpār hārēm qôlĕkā — like a trumpet lift your voice — the shofar is the instrument of covenantal summons, blown on holy days and at times of national reckoning. The content of the proclamation: haggēd lĕ'ammî piš'ām — declare to my people their transgression.
58.2: wĕ'ôtî yôm yôm yidrōšûn — yet they seek me day after day. The distributive yôm yôm (day by day) signals habit and regularity. wĕda'at dĕrāk̠ay yaḥpāṣûn — and delight to know my ways — the verb ḥāpēṣ (to delight in, desire) is the same root that appears in v. 3 for the people's self-serving pleasure; YHWH uses their own word back at them. k̠ĕgôy 'ăšer-ṣĕdāqāh 'āśāh — as if they were a nation doing righteousness — the comparison exposes the performance-gap between appearance and reality.
58.3: lāmmāh ṣamnû wĕlō'-rā'îtā — Why have we fasted, and you do not see?. The complaint (Qal perfect ṣamnû — we fasted) meets the divine counter: hēn bĕyôm ṣomĕk̠em timṣĕ'û-ḥēpeṣ — behold, on your fast day you find your own pleasure. The noun ḥēpeṣ (חֵפֶץ — delight, desire, business) is the same word as v. 2 for knowing YHWH's ways; in fasting they pursue their own ḥēpeṣ, not his. wĕk̠ol-'aṣṣubbêkem tinhagû — and all your workers you drive hard — the Qal imperfect tinhagû (from נָהַג, to drive/drive cattle) applies cattle-driving language to human laborers on fast days.
58.4: hēn lĕrîb̠ ûlĕmaṣṣāh taṣûmû — behold, you fast for quarreling and strife. The nouns rîb̠ (quarrel, lawsuit) and maṣṣāh (contention, strife) expose the social fabric of their fasting: it accompanies conflict rather than penitence. wĕlĕhakkôt bĕ'egrōp̄ rèša' — to strike with a wicked fist — physical violence on fast days. The conditional: lō'-taṣûmû kayyôm — you will not fast as you do today — implying that such fasting is not real fasting at all.
58.5: hăk̠āzeh yihyeh ṣôm 'ebĕḥārehu — Is this the fast I choose?. The rhetorical question (expecting a negative answer) defines what the false fast looks like: lak̠pôp̄ k̠'agmōn rō'šô — to bow one's head like a reed — the bulrush ('agmōn, אַגְמוֹן) bends in the wind and springs back; the posture is mechanical, not sincere. wĕśaq wā'ēper yāṣîa' — to spread sackcloth and ashes beneath oneself — the outward signs of mourning without inner transformation. YHWH's indictment: hălāzeh tiqrā' ṣôm — will you call this a fast?
58.6: hălō' zeh ṣôm 'ebĕḥārehu — Is this not the fast I choose?. The positive counterpart to v. 5's rhetorical negation. Four infinitives define the true fast: (1) pattēaḥ ḥarṣubbôt-rèša' — to loosen the bonds of wickedness — the Pi'el pattēaḥ (open/loosen) applied to the fetters (ḥarṣubbôt) of oppression; (2) hatter 'ăguddôt môṭāh — to undo the straps of the yoke; (3) wĕšallēaḥ rĕṣuṣîm ḥopšîm — to let the oppressed go free — the Pual participle rĕṣuṣîm (crushed ones, from רָצַץ); (4) wĕk̠ol-môṭāh tĕnattēqû — to break every yoke.
58.7: hălō' pārōs lārā'ēb̠ laḥmĕkā — Is it not to break your bread with the hungry?. The verb pāras (פָּרַס — to break, divide) describes not giving leftovers but the active breaking of one's own bread. waʿăniyyîm mĕrûdîm tābî' bāyit — bring the homeless poor into your house — the adjective mĕrûdîm (wanderers, from רוּד — to roam) describes the rootless destitute. kî-tir'eh 'ārôm ûk̠issîtô — when you see the naked and you cover him — the conditional kî makes the encounter a test of the observer's character. The final clause: ûmibĕśārĕkā lō' tit'allām — and do not hide yourself from your own flesh.
58.8: 'āz yibbāqa' kaššaḥar 'ôrĕkā — then your light shall break forth like the dawn. The verb bāqa' (בָּקַע — to split, cleave, break open) is the word used for the Red Sea splitting (Exod 14:21); applied to light, it is visceral rather than gradual — light forces its way through. ûrukātĕkā mĕhērāh tiṣmaḥ — your healing shall spring up speedily — the verb ṣāmaḥ (to sprout/grow) applied to healing; restoration is organic and rapid. wĕhālak̠ lĕpānêkā ṣidqĕkā — righteousness leads as a vanguard; k̠ĕbôd YHWH ya'asĕpĕkā — the glory of YHWH is the rear guard.
58.9: 'āz tiqrā' wĕYHWH ya'ăneh — then you will call and YHWH will answer. The prayer-response connection is restored by the ethical fast of vv. 6-7. Three practices that must cease: (1) môṣē' 'ĕṣba' — pointing the finger (accusation, contempt); (2) dibbĕr-'āwen — speaking wickedness/falsehood; (3) the broader cluster of oppressive behavior. The double Hiphil construction tāsēr mimmĕkā môṭāh — remove the yoke from your midst — the yoke of vv. 6 recurs as the condition for answered prayer.
58.10: wĕtāpēq lārā'ēb̠ napšĕkā — if you pour yourself out for the hungry. The verb pûq (Hiphil, tāpēq) means to bring forth, to pour out — the object is napšĕkā (your soul/self), the whole person offered as sustenance. wĕnèpeš na'ănāh taśbîa' — and satisfy the desire of the afflicted — the Pi'el taśbîa' (from שָׂבַע, to be satisfied) frames the action as truly meeting need, not token giving. Then: wĕzāraḥ baḥōšek̠ 'ôrĕkā — your light will rise in the darkness.
58.11: wĕnāḥăkā YHWH tāmîd — YHWH will guide you continually. The verb nāḥāh (Qal imperfect, to lead/guide) describes YHWH as the ongoing guide — not a one-time direction but continuous leading. wĕhiśbîa' bĕṣaḥṣāḥôt napšĕkā — and satisfy your desire in scorched places — ṣaḥṣāḥôt (parched, burning wastes) is the most inhospitable terrain; provision there is pure miracle. ûk̠môṣā' mayim 'ăšer lō'-yĕk̠azzĕb̠û mêmāyw — like a spring of water whose waters do not lie — the verb kāzab̠ (to lie/deceive) applied to springs that run dry; a spring that lies is one that promises water but fails. YHWH is the unfailing spring.
58.12: ûb̠ānû mimmĕkā ḥorbôt 'ôlām — your ancient ruins shall be rebuilt. The perfect b̠ānû used as future certain: those who come from you (your descendants) will rebuild what has been desolate for generations. The honorific titles: qārā' lĕkā gōdēr-pèreṣ — the repairer of the breach — the noun pèreṣ (breach, gap in a wall) is used in covenant contexts for the breaking down of divine protection; repairing it is covenant restoration. mĕšōbēb̠ nĕtîb̠ôt lāšèb̠et — the restorer of streets to dwell in.
58.13: 'im-tāšîb̠ mišabbāt raglĕkā — if you turn back your foot from the Sabbath. The idiom šûb̠ raglĕkā (restrain your foot) draws on walking-in-the-way imagery: the Sabbath is a day one does not travel on for one's own business. miṣṣ ōt ḥēp̄ĕṣĕkā bĕyôm qodšî — from doing your pleasure on my holy day — the noun ḥēpeṣ (pleasure/business) recurs from v. 3 and v. 2; the whole chapter is structured around who gets to pursue their ḥēpeṣ. wĕqārā'tā laššabbāt 'ōneg — and call the Sabbath a delight — 'ōneg (עֹנֶג, delight/luxury) shares the root with hit'annag (v. 14); the Sabbath redesignated from burden to luxury.
58.14: 'āz tit'annaḡ 'al-YHWH — then you will delight yourself in YHWH. The Hithpa'el of עָנַג (to be delicate/luxurious — the same root as 'ōneg in v. 13) is reflexive: taking delight for oneself in YHWH. The chapter closes with the promise: wĕhirk̠ab̠tîkā 'al-bāmôtê 'āreṣ — I will make you ride on the heights of the earth — the Hiphil of רָכַב̠ (to ride/mount); YHWH as the one who makes Israel mount to the heights. ûhăʾăk̠altîkā naḥălat ya'ăqōb̠ 'āb̠îkā — and feed you with the heritage of Jacob your father — covenant inheritance language closes the chapter: the true fast restores covenant standing. kî pî YHWH dibbēr — for the mouth of YHWH has spoken — the prophetic authentication formula.

59.1: hēn lōʾ-qāṣĕrâ yad-Yhwh mîhôšîaʿ — "the hand of YHWH is not too short to save" — the opening refutation of a false diagnosis. The same rhetorical question about YHWH's "shortened hand" appears in 50:2 as a challenge to Israel; here it is answered before it is asked. wĕlōʾ-kābĕḏâ ʾoznô miššĕmôaʿ — "nor is his ear heavy from hearing" — deafness and weakness are both denied. The problem is not YHWH's incapacity but the people's moral condition (v.2).
59.2: kî ʾim-ʿăwōnōtêkem hāyû mabdîlîm bênêkem ûbên ʾĕlōhêkem — "but your iniquities have separated (bāḏal, H914, to divide, to make a distinction) you from your God" — the theology of sin as relational separation rather than merely legal violation. The iniquities have become a wall between parties who should be together. wĕḥaṭṭōʾôtêkem histîrû pānîm mikkkem — "and your sins have hidden his face from you" — the hiding of YHWH's face (histîr pānîm) is the withdrawal of his covenantal presence, the most serious covenant judgment (cf. Ps 13:1; 27:9; Isa 8:17).
59.3: kî kappêkem niggĕʾălû baddām — "for your hands are defiled with blood" (gāʾal, H1351, to defile/desecrate — a cultic term for making unclean). wĕʾeṣbĕʿōtêkem beʿāwōn śipĕtôtêkem dibbĕrû-šeqer — "and your fingers with iniquity; your lips have spoken lies." The anatomy of sin — hands, fingers, lips, tongue — covers the full range of sinful actions from violent deed to deceptive speech. The inventory of body parts implicated in sin is similar to Paul's catena in Rom 3:13-17.
59.4: ʾên-qōrēʾ ḇĕṣedeq wĕʾên nišpāṭ beʾĕmûnâ — "no one calls out in righteousness, and no one pleads in faithfulness" — the legal domain (calling out for justice, pleading before courts) is corrupted. bāṭôaḥ ʿal-tōhû wĕdabbēr-šāwĕʾ hārô ʿāmāl wĕhôlîḏ ʾāwen — "trusting in chaos and speaking lies; they conceive mischief and give birth to iniquity" — the reproduction metaphor (hārâ, to conceive; yālad, to give birth) frames sin as generative, producing offspring that continue beyond the original act.
59.5: bêṣê ṣipʿônî biqēʿû — "they hatch the eggs of a viper" (ṣipʿônî, H6848, a venomous serpent — adder or cobra). wĕqûrê ʿakkābîš yĕʾōrōgû — "and weave the spider's web." The viper-egg and spider-web pair the production of lethal poison with the crafting of deceptive traps. The hatchlings of the viper become serpents (v.5b), and the spider's web is useless as covering (v.6). Both images describe sin that appears productive but produces only danger and futility.
59.6: qûrêhem lōʾ-yihyû lĕbegeḏ wĕlōʾ yitkassû bĕmaʿăśêhem — "their webs will not serve as clothing; they cannot cover themselves with what they have made" — the futility of sinful manufacture. The spider's web looks like fabric but provides neither covering nor warmth. maʿăśêhem maʿăśê-ʾāwen ûpōʿal ḥāmās bĕkappêhem — "their works are works of iniquity, and deeds of violence are in their hands" — the climax of the body-inventory that began in v.3.
59.7: raglêhem lārāʿ yārûṣû — "their feet run to evil" — the swiftness of sin. Paul quotes vv.7-8 in Rom 3:15-17 as part of his catena of OT sin-texts proving universal human guilt: "Their feet are swift to shed blood; in their paths are ruin and misery; and the way of peace they have not known." ûmahărû lišpōk dam-nāqî — "and they hurry to shed innocent blood" (dam nāqî, innocent blood — a category of sin that defiles the land, Num 35:33; Deut 19:10).
59.8: derekh šālôm lōʾ yāḏĕʿû — "the way of peace (šālôm) they do not know" — the absence of peace-knowledge is a comprehensive moral description: they neither know how to make peace nor recognize it. wĕʾên mišpāṭ bĕmaʿgĕlôtām — "there is no justice in their tracks." nĕtîḇōtêhem ʿiqqĕšû lāhem — "their paths they have made crooked for themselves" (ʿāqaš, H6140, to make crooked/perverse). Paul uses this verse in Rom 3:17 to close the catena, making peace-ignorance the capstone of the universal human condition before Christ.
59.9: lāken rāḥaq mimmennû mišpāṭ wĕlōʾ taśśîgēnû ṣĕdāqâ — "therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not reach us" — the community shifts to first-person confession. The distance of justice is not cosmic but self-created: they have driven it away through the behaviors of vv.3-8. nĕqawweh lāʾôr wĕhinnēh-ḥōšek — "we wait for light but behold, darkness" — the inversion of expectation: salvation anticipated but darkness experienced.
59.10: nĕgašĕšâ kĕʿiwrîm qîr ûkĕʾên ʿênayim nĕgašēšâ — "we grope for the wall like the blind; we grope like those who have no eyes" — the blindness metaphor extends the darkness of v.9 to incapacity for navigation. kāšalnû ḇāṣŏhorāyim kānešep — "we stumble at noon as in the twilight" — the paradox of midday darkness (cf. Deut 28:29: the covenant curse of groping at noon). bammĕšammānîm kayyĕmētîm — "among those in their prime, we are like dead men."
59.11: nĕhĕmeh kaddōbîm kullānû — "we all growl like bears" (hāmâ, H1993, to growl/roar/groan). wĕkayyônîm hāgōh nĕhgeh — "and moan plaintively like doves" — the contrast of bear-growl and dove-coo captures the double register of the community's anguish: fierce desperation and plaintive mourning together. nĕqawweh lammišpāṭ wĕʾayin lîšûʿâ rāḥăqâ mimmennû — "we wait for justice but there is none; for salvation, but it is far from us" — the waiting (qāwâ, cf. 25:9; 40:31) produces only distance, not arrival.
59.12: kî rabbû pĕšāʿênû negd-eykā wĕḥaṭṭōʾôtênû ʿānnâ bānû — "for our transgressions are multiplied before you, and our sins testify against us" (ʿānâ, H6030, to testify, to answer back — the sins become witnesses in the divine court). pĕšāʿênû ʾittānû waʿăwōnōtênû yĕdaʿănûm — "our transgressions are with us, and we know our iniquities" — the confession deepens: the sins are present (not past), known (not hidden), and multiple.
59.13: pāšôaʿ ûkahēš bYhwh ûnāsôg mēʾaḥar ʾĕlōhênû — "transgressing and denying YHWH, and turning away from following our God" — the three-fold pattern: transgression (active violation), denial (of YHWH's claims), and retreat (withdrawal from his following). dabbēr-ʿōšeq wĕsārâ hārô wĕhāgô millib dĕḇārê-šāqer — "speaking oppression and revolt, conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words." The origin of the lying words is the heart (lēḇ) — the seat of moral orientation in Hebrew anthropology.
59.14: wĕhussag ʾāḥôr mišpāṭ — "justice is turned back" (nāsag, H5253, to be driven back, to retreat) — the reversal of the proper order: what should advance (justice) retreats; what should come near (righteousness) stands far away. ûṣĕdāqâ mērāḥôq taʿămod — "and righteousness stands far off." kî-kāšĕlâ bāreḥôḇ ʾĕmet — "for truth has stumbled in the public square (rĕḥôb)" — the public legal space (the city gate) has become a place where truth falls. wĕnĕkōḥâ lōʾ-tûkal lābôʾ — "and integrity cannot enter."
59.15: wattĕhî hāʾĕmet neʿĕderet — "truth has disappeared/is absent" (ʿādar, H5737, to be absent, to be lacking — the same root as the shepherd who calls his sheep and one is missing). wĕsār mērāʿ mištōlēl — "and the one who turns from evil makes himself a prey (šālal)" — the moral inversion: righteousness is predatory behavior. wayyar Yhwh wayyeraʿ bĕʿênāyw kî ʾên mišpāṭ — "YHWH saw it, and it displeased him that there was no justice" — the divine observation (rāʾâ) and moral response (rāʿaʿ bĕʿênāyw, evil in his eyes) introduce the divine intervention of vv.16-17.
59.16: wayyar kî-ʾên ʾîš wayyištômem kî ʾên maphgîaʿ — "he saw that there was no man, and was appalled (šāmam, H8074, to be desolate, horrified) that there was no one to intercede (pāgaʿ, H6293, to intercede, to meet/touch — the intercessor makes contact between two parties)" — the divine horror at the total vacuum of human mediation. No prophet, no priest, no judge, no king can mediate between YHWH and the people. wayyôšaʿ lô zĕrōʿô ûṣiḏqātô hîʾ sĕmākātthu — "his own arm brought him salvation, and his righteousness supported him" — YHWH becomes his own mediator.
59.17: wayyilbaš ṣĕdāqâ kašširyôn — "he put on righteousness as a breastplate" (širyôn, H8302, coat of mail/breastplate). wĕkôbaʿ yĕšûʿâ bĕrōʾšô — "and a helmet of salvation on his head" (kôbaʿ, H3553, helmet). Paul in Eph 6:14,17 distributes this armor to the believer: "having put on the breastplate of righteousness... take the helmet of salvation." What YHWH wore as the divine warrior is now given to those who are in Christ to wear. wayyilbaš bigdê nāqām tilibūšet — "he put on garments of vengeance for clothing" — and wrapped himself in zeal (qinʾâ) as a mantle.
59.18: kĕʿal gĕmûlôt kĕʿal yĕšallēm — "according to their deeds, so he will repay" — the retributive formula. The recompense is distributed to three groups: ṣārāyw (his adversaries), ʾōyĕḇāyw (his enemies), and the coastlands (ʾiyyîm) — i.e., the full width of opposition, from close enemies to the distant nations. The divine warrior's campaign addresses the complete spectrum of those who oppose YHWH's covenant purposes.
59.19: wĕyîrĕʾû mimmāʿăraḇ ʾet-šēm Yhwh ûmimmiẑraḥ-šemeš ʾet-kĕḇōḏô — "they will fear the name of YHWH from the west, and his glory from the rising of the sun" — the universal fear (reverence/worship) that results from the divine warrior's actions. East and west together represent the totality of geographic space. kî-yābōʾ kanāhār ṣār rûaḥ Yhwh nōssĕsâ bô — "for the enemy will come like a rushing stream; the Spirit of YHWH will drive him away" — a difficult verse: either the enemy comes like a flood and YHWH's Spirit drives him away, or YHWH himself comes as a flood-tide driven by his Spirit.
59.20: ûḇāʾ lĕṣiyyôn gōʾēl — "a Redeemer (gōʾēl, H1350, the kinsman-redeemer) will come to Zion" — the arrival formula. Paul quotes this in Rom 11:26 ("and in this way all Israel will be saved, as it is written, 'The Deliverer will come from Zion'") to argue that Israel's partial hardening (11:25) is temporary — the eschatological Deliverer (Christ at his return) will complete what the current mission to the gentiles is preparing. ûlĕšābê pešaʿ bĕyaʿăqōb — "and to those who turn from transgression in Jacob" — the gōʾēl's arrival is conditioned by repentance, or more precisely, his arrival produces repentance.
59.21: wĕʾănî zōʾt bĕrîtî ʾôtām ʾāmar Yhwh — "as for me, this is my covenant with them, says YHWH" — the covenant formula introducing the new covenant elements. rûḥî ʾăšer ʿāleykā ûdĕḇāray ʾăšer-śamtî bĕpîkā — "my Spirit that is upon you and my words that I have placed in your mouth" — the two covenant instruments: Spirit and Word. lōʾ-yāmûšû mipîkā ûmippî zarʿăkā ûmippî zeraʿ zarʿăkā — "shall not depart from your mouth, nor from the mouth of your offspring, nor from the mouth of your offspring's offspring" — the triple-generation promise makes the covenant's persistence explicit: Spirit-and-Word covenant is permanent across generations. This new covenant of Spirit and Word is what Jer 31:33 and Ezek 36:27 also describe.

51.1: Šimʿû ʾēlay ("hear me") opens a series of three calls (vv1, 4, 7) each addressed to a different audience. The first is addressed to those who rōdĕpê tsedeq (H7291 + H6664, "pursue righteousness") and seek YHWH — covenant-seekers within the community. Habbiṭû ʾel-tsûr ḥutstabtem (H5027 + H6697, "look to the rock from which you were hewn") — the rock (tsûr) here is the quarry of origin, not primarily a stability metaphor. The same word applies to YHWH as rock in Deuteronomy 32:4 and underlies 1 Corinthians 10:4 ("that rock was Christ"). The MKT "look to the rock from which you were hewn" preserves the quarrying image — the covenant people's origins are carved out of something solid and pre-existing.
51.2: Habbiṭû ʾel-ʾAbrāhām ʾăbîkem ("look to Abraham your father") — the quarry of v1 is now identified: Abraham is the geological source of the covenant nation. Wĕʾel-Śārāh tĕḥôlēlkem (H2342, "to Sarah who bore you in travail") — the verb ḥûl/ḥîl (to writhe/give birth) emphasizes the birth-pain of origin. The crucial theological claim: kî-ʾeḥād qĕrāʾtîw ("for he was one when I called him") — Abraham was only one person when YHWH called him, yet YHWH wāʾărabbēhû (H7235, "multiplied him"). The logic: if YHWH could multiply one barren couple into a nation, he can multiply the remnant of exile into restoration. Romans 4:19-21 anchors faith in exactly this logic — Abraham's one-ness against impossible odds is the pattern of faith.
51.3: The consolation promise: kî-niḥam YHWH Tsiyyôn (H5162, "for the LORD has comforted Zion") — the same nāḥam root that opens the entire section at 40:1 ("comfort, comfort my people," naḥămû naḥămû). The desolate places of Zion will become like ʿēden (H5731, Eden/delight) and her wilderness like gan-YHWH (H1588, "the garden of the LORD"). The garden-of-God imagery is the new creation: Revelation 22:1-2 takes up precisely this language (the river of life, the tree of life, the garden city). The restoration from exile is the first movement of a restoration-to-Eden trajectory that ends in the new Jerusalem. Śāśôn wĕśimḥāh yimmātsēʾ bāh ("joy and gladness will be found in her") — the city of mourning (Lam 1) is the city of joy restored.
51.4: The second audience-call: haqšîbû ʾēlay ʿammî ("pay attention to me, my people") — now addressed to the covenant community at large rather than the righteousness-seekers of v1. Kî tôrāh mēʾittî tētsēʾ (H8451 + H3318, "for Torah will go out from me") — the Law-as-instruction emanating from YHWH is the content of the good news. Wĕmišpāṭî lĕʾôr ʿammîm ʾargîaʿ (H4941 + H216, "and my justice will be a light for the peoples I will establish") — the mišpāṭ (justice/ordinance) extends to the peoples (ʿammîm, plural — Gentiles), and the light-imagery picks up 42:6 and 49:6 ("light to the nations"). John 1:9 ("the true light that gives light to everyone") and John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world") are the fulfillment of this cosmic-light promise.
51.5: Qārôb tsidqî (H7138 + H6664, "my righteousness/vindication is near") — tsedeq in Deutero-Isaiah carries the force of saving-vindication, not merely moral rectitude. Yātsāʾ yišʿî (H3318 + H3468, "my salvation has gone out") — salvation as something that moves from YHWH outward into the world. The ʾiyyîm (H339, coastlands/islands) will hope in YHWH and his zĕrôaʿ (H2220, arm/strength) — the same divine-arm language that will return in v9. Matthew 12:21 cites Isaiah 42:4 for the same coastlands/nations hope fulfilled in Jesus. The "arm" that the coastlands hope in is the arm that raised Christ from the dead (Rom 8:11).
51.6: The cosmic transience: śĕʾû laššāmayim ʿênêkem ("lift your eyes to the heavens") and wĕhabbîṭû ʾel-hāʾārets mittāḥat ("look at the earth beneath") — both poles of the cosmos are invoked, then declared mortal. Šāmayim kĕʿāšān nāmĕlāḥû (H4414, "the heavens will vanish like smoke") — mālaḥ means to dissolve or wear away; the heavens are as permanent as morning mist. Wĕhāʾārets kabbegĕd tibleh (H1086, "the earth will wear out like a garment") — the clothing-metaphor for creation's temporality. But yĕšûʿātî lĕʿôlām tihyeh ("my salvation will be forever") — YHWH's deliverance (yĕšûʿāh) outlasts the cosmos. 2 Peter 3:10-12 describes the dissolution of the heavens by fire; Hebrews 1:11-12 quotes this passage applied to Christ: the heavens wear out like a garment, but the Son remains. The MKT "the heavens will vanish like smoke" keeps the liquid-dissolution image.
51.7: The third audience-call: šimʿû ʾēlay yōdĕʿê tsedeq ("hear me, you who know righteousness") — those who have internalized YHWH's character. ʿAm tôrātî bĕlibbām (H8451 + H3820, "the people in whose heart is my Torah") — the internal Torah is the covenant ideal of Jeremiah 31:33 ("I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts"). The command: ʾal-tîrĕʾû ḥerpat ʾĕnôš ("do not fear the reproach of men") — the covenant community has internalized the law yet still faces human contempt. The parallel command in 50:7-9 ("I have set my face like flint") anticipates Christ's face-setting toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51). The Torah-in-the-heart community is the community that will not be shaken by human opinion.
51.8: The garment-moth simile recurs: kî ḵabeged yōḵĕlēm ʿāš (H6211, "for the moth will eat them like a garment") and ḵatsemer yōḵĕlēm sās (H5597, "the worm will eat them like wool") — the persecutors who reproach the covenant people are as perishable as cloth. But wĕtsidqātî lĕʿôlām tihyeh ("my righteousness will be forever") — YHWH's vindication endures while human reproach dissolves. Hebrews 1:11-12 quotes the garment-wearing-out imagery (from v6) and applies it to the Son's eternity: the created order is the garment that wears out, but Christ remains unchanged. The garment metaphor runs through vv6-8 as a structural thread, applying the same perishability to both the physical cosmos and the human oppressors.
51.9: The arm-awakening call: ʿûrî ʿûrî liḇšî-ʿōz zĕrôaʿ YHWH (H5782, "awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD!" H2220) — the repeated imperative intensifies the call; the divine arm is personified and summoned. ʿûrî kîmê qedem ("awake as in the days of old") — the appeal is to precedent: the historical exodus. Hammakket Rahab (H7294, "who struck Rahab") — Rahab is a mythic sea-monster, here = Egypt/chaos (cf. 30:7; Job 26:12; Ps 87:4; 89:10). Mĕḥôlelēt tannîm (H8577, "who pierced the dragon") — the tannîm is the sea-dragon, the chaos-creature defeated at creation and at the Reed Sea. Revelation 12:7-9 takes up this dragon-defeat language for Christ's defeat of Satan. The arm that struck Rahab is the arm that raises Christ (Acts 2:24: "freed from the agony of death").
51.10: The Reed Sea memory: hălōʾ-ʾatt hîʾ hammĕḥarĕbet yām ("was it not you who dried up the sea") — the direct address to the divine arm continues. Mê tĕhôm rabbāh (H8415, "the waters of the great deep") — tĕhôm rabbāh is the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2, here applied to the Red Sea crossing. The crossing becomes a type of cosmic creation: dividing the chaos-waters is what YHWH does both at creation and at exodus. Šāmāh maamaqqê-yām derek lāʿĕbōr gĕʾûlîm ("who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to cross") — the redeemed (gĕʾûlîm, from gāʾal, kinsman-redeemer) pass through the dry seabed. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 applies the Red Sea crossing as a type of baptism — the exodus-passage becomes the pattern of Christ-passage.
51.11: The return-of-the-redeemed: ûpĕdûyê YHWH yĕšûbûn (H6299 + H7725, "and the redeemed of the LORD will return") — the verb pādāh (to ransom/redeem by payment) distinguishes this from the kinsman-redeemer (gāʾal) of v10: both redemption modes are invoked across vv10-11. Ûbāʾû Tsiyyôn bĕrinnāh ("they will come to Zion with singing") — this verse is nearly identical to 35:10, the great return-from-exile passage. Wĕśimḥat ʿôlām ʿal-rōʾšām ("everlasting joy upon their heads") — the crown of joy as a permanent garment. Śāśôn wĕśimḥāh yassîgû ("joy and gladness will overtake them") — not pursued but overtaking: the joy catches up to the returnees. Nāsû yāgôn wāʾanāḥāh ("sorrow and sighing will flee") — the exile's sighing flees as the return-joy advances. Revelation 21:4: "He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain."
51.12: The divine self-disclosure: ʾānôḵî ʾānôḵî hûʾ mĕnaḥămkĕm ("I, I am he who comforts you") — the doubled personal pronoun (ʾānôḵî ʾānôḵî) and the divine hûʾ ("he") combine to form the emphatic self-identification. This is the ʾānî hûʾ formula of 41:4; 43:10; 44:6; 46:4 — the same formula transferred into the context of comfort: the God who is first-and-last, who carries Israel from birth to gray hair, is also the one who comforts. Mî-ʾattāh wattîrĕʾî mēʾĕnôš yāmût ("who are you that you are afraid of a mortal who dies?") — the rhetorical challenge: how can the community fear a human being who is as perishable as grass (v12b), when the comforter is the eternal God? The comfort of John 14:16-17 ("I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Comforter") stands in this same tradition.
51.13: Wattiškaḥ YHWH ʿōśekā (H7911, "you have forgotten the LORD who made you") — the verb šāḵaḥ (to forget) stands in sharp contrast to the divine lōʾ ʾeškaḥek ("I will not forget you") of 49:15. Israel's forgetting is not matched by YHWH's forgetting — asymmetrical faithfulness is the covenant pattern. The description of YHWH the maker: nōṭeh šāmayim (H5186, "who stretched out the heavens") and yōsēd ʾārets (H3245, "who laid the foundations of the earth") — both creation acts. The fear of the oppressor's fury is called out as irrational: ûʾayyēh ḥămat hammētsîq ("and where is the wrath of the oppressor?") — the oppressor's rage is already past tense in YHWH's account.
51.14: Mihĕr tsōʿeh lĕhippātēaḥ (H4116 + H6808, "the crouching prisoner quickly will be released") — the exile in chains will not die in the pit. Wĕlōʾ-yiḥsar laḥmô ("and his bread will not be lacking") — the basic provision of bread: not just freedom but sustenance. The MKT "the captive exile shall speedily be released" captures the urgency of māhar (hastened). This verse is embedded in the context of YHWH as creator-comforter (vv12-13): the same God who stretched out the heavens and laid the earth's foundations will hasten the release of each individual prisoner. The grandiose and the particular are united in YHWH's care.
51.15: YHWH identifies himself through creation: wĕʾānôḵî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā rōgaʿ hayyām (H7280, "I am the LORD your God who stirs up the sea") — rāgaʿ means to agitate or still; the same root can mean both to disturb the sea and to calm it (cf. Job 26:12). Wayehĕmû gallāyw ("so that its waves roar") — the sea is under YHWH's command: he both stirs it and stills it. YHWH Tsĕbāʾôt šĕmô (H6635, "the LORD of Hosts is his name") — the cosmic military title: the one who commands the heavenly armies also commands the seas. Mark 4:39 ("He rebuked the wind and said to the waves, 'Peace! Be still!'" and the sea was calm) is the Christological fulfillment of the sea-controlling divine identity: what YHWH claims here, Jesus does in Galilee.
51.16: Wāʾāśim dĕbāray bĕpîkā (H7760 + H1697, "I have put my words in your mouth") — the prophetic commission formula, but here given to Zion/Israel as a community, not just the individual prophet. Ûbĕtsēl yādî kissîtîkā (H6738 + H3027 + H3680, "I have covered you with the shadow of my hand") — divine protection figured as overshadowing: the same image applied to the individual Servant in 49:2 ("in the shadow of his hand he hid me"). Linṭôaʿ šāmayim wĕliyĕsōd ʾārets ("to plant the heavens and to lay the foundations of the earth") — the new-creation language: YHWH "plants" the new heavens (v16) as he stretched out the old ones. Wĕlēʾmōr lĕTsiyyôn ʿammî-ʾāttāh ("and to say to Zion, 'You are my people'") — the covenant-restoration formula that answers Hosea's reversal (lōʾ-ʿammî, "not my people," Hos 1:9 reversed in Hos 2:23 / Rom 9:25-26).
51.17: The awakening of Zion: hitʿôrĕrî hitʿôrĕrî qûmî Yĕrûšālayim (H5782, "wake up, wake up, rise up, Jerusalem") — the doubled imperative mirrors the doubled divine-arm awakening of v9 (ʿûrî ʿûrî). The mutual awakening is structural: first the divine arm is summoned (v9), then the city responds (v17). ʾăšer šātît miyyad YHWH ʾet-kôs ḥămātô ("who drank from the hand of the LORD the cup of his wrath") — kôs ḥēmāh (H3563 + H2534, "cup of wrath") is the judgment-cup Jerusalem drank at the Babylonian destruction. The cup image is critical for understanding Mark 14:36 ("take this cup from me") — Christ in Gethsemane takes the cup of divine wrath that Jerusalem had drunk, so that it may be taken from her (v22). Kôs tarʿēlāh šātît (H8653, "the cup of staggering you drank") — the double cup: both wrath and its physical effect of stupefied staggering.
51.18: The abandonment within the city: ʾên-mĕnahēl lāh (H5148, "there is no one to guide her") — the city is without a guide-shepherd. Mikkol-bānîm yāldāh ("of all the sons she bore") — none of her sons can lead her out of disaster. Wĕʾên maḥăzîq bĕyādāh (H2388 + H3027, "and no one to take her by the hand") — the empty hand: the covenant people abandoned without someone to grasp them and lead them. This desolation stands in contrast to YHWH who in v12 identifies himself as the comforter — the sons have failed, but the divine father has not. Isaiah 63:9 picks up the same guiding-hand image: "in his love and mercy he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old."
51.19: The double disaster: štayim hēnnāh qōrĕʾôt ʾôtāk ("these two things are calling to you") — two pairs of twin-disasters form four total: šôd ûšēber (H7701 + H7667, "devastation and destruction") and rāʿāb wāḥereb (H7458 + H2719, "famine and sword"). The four words for disaster cover both external military calamity (šôd) and internal structural collapse (šēber), both deprivation (rāʿāb) and violence (ḥereb). Mî yānûd lāk ("who will mourn for you?") — the rhetorical desolation: no consoler, no mourner. This verse is the depth of the lament before the turn: the city that v22 announces will receive the cup removed. The MKT "devastation and destruction, famine and sword" keeps the four Hebrew terms as two pairs to mirror the Hebrew rhetorical structure.
51.20: The exhausted sons: bānayik ʿullĕpû (H5968, "your sons have fainted") — the same root as swooning/fainting from exhaustion or blow. Šāḵĕbû bĕrōʾš kol-ḥûtsôt ("they lie at the head of every street") — prostrate in the public thoroughfares, the most visible spaces of the city. Kĕtôʾ bammikĕmār (H8377 + H4364, "like an antelope caught in a net") — the wild animal that cannot escape, thrashing uselessly against the trap. Mĕlēʾîm ḥămat YHWH ("full of the wrath of the LORD") — the sons are saturated with the divine wrath (the same ḥēmāh of the cup in v17); the wrath has been internalized as the visible collapse of the community. The MKT "like an antelope in a net" preserves the hunting metaphor that makes the helplessness visceral.
51.21: The turn: lāḵēn šimʿî-nāʾ zōʾt ʿāniyyāh ("therefore hear this, you afflicted one") — lāḵēn (therefore) marks the pivot from lament to announcement. Wĕšikōrat wĕlōʾ miyyāyin (H7910, "drunk, but not from wine") — the stupor of judgment is not the ordinary drunkenness of excess but the divine-judgment drunkenness of the cup of wrath. The phrase echoes Acts 2:15 ("these people are not drunk as you suppose") in reverse: there the disciples are accused of wine-drunkenness but are Spirit-drunk; here Jerusalem is not wine-drunk but wrath-drunk. The connection is ironic: the Spirit-filling of Pentecost replaces the wrath-cup of judgment — the same outward appearance, opposite cause and effect.
51.22: The cup transferred: kōh-ʾāmar ʾădōnayik YHWH ("thus says your Lord, YHWH") — the divine guarantee. Hinnēh lāqaḥtî miyyādek ʾet-kôs hatarʿēlāh (H3947 + H3563 + H8653, "behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering") — the cup is removed from Jerusalem by divine action. ʾet-qubbaʿat kôs ḥămātî lōʾ-tôsîpî lištôt-āh ʿôd ("the bowl of my wrath's cup you will no longer drink") — the double cup (kôs + qubbaʿat) is finished for Jerusalem. Mark 14:36: "Take this cup from me." Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the moment when he asks to receive what v22 promises Jerusalem — the cup Jerusalem will no longer drink is the cup the Son will drink in her place. The transfer of the cup is the mechanism of substitutionary atonement embedded in Deutero-Isaiah's comfort-poetry. The MKT "the cup of staggering" keeps the Hebrew double-noun construction.
51.23: The cup handed to the oppressors: wĕśamtîhā bĕyad-mĕyassayik (H3254 + H3013, "I will put it in the hand of those who afflict you") — what Jerusalem drank, her oppressors will now drink. ʾĂšer-ʾāmĕrû lĕnapšek šĕḥî wĕnaʿăbōr ("who said to you, 'Bow down so we can walk over you'") — the humiliation of conquest: the defeated are made into human pavement. Wattāśîmî kāʾārets gēwek wĕkahûts laʿōbĕrîm ("you made your back like the ground, like the street for those crossing") — the prostration of the conquered city, its citizens trampled. Revelation 6:10 (the martyrs' cry for justice) and 14:10 (the oppressors drink the wine of God's wrath) are the eschatological fulfillment of this verse: the cup-transfer of v22 ends with the oppressors drinking the wrath that the redeemed no longer bear. The judgment is real, final, and fully divine — the one who removes the cup from Jerusalem hands it to her persecutors.

53.1: Who has believed what we proclaimed? — the opening question of the fourth Servant Song. Aman (believe/trust, disp 2): to believe the report is to entrust oneself to the message's truthfulness and to the one it describes. The question implies that the report is not self-evidently persuasive — the Servant's manner does not commend itself to natural observation (v 2). The arm of the LORD links back to 51:5, 9; 52:10: the same redemptive power the exiles prayed for has been revealed — but in a form that was not expected. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 10:16 to explain Israel's unbelief at the gospel proclamation.
53.2: The Servant's origins: like a tender shoot from dry soil, no attractive form or majesty to draw attention. The dry soil and absence of beauty are deliberate anti-messianic markers — this figure does not fit the kingly warrior expectation. The commentary of John 1:46 (Can anything good come out of Nazareth?) and 1 Cor 1:23 (a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles) inhabit exactly this gap between expectation and reality.
53.3: The Servant is despised and rejected by people — a man who yada suffering (knew/was familiar with, disp 2). Yada here is experiential knowledge — intimate acquaintance with suffering, not mere observation. Like one from whom people hide their faces: the Servant's suffering is so severe that observers avert their eyes. We held him of no account — the communal speakers confess that they dismissed the Servant. This verse is the turning point where the speakers recognize their error.
53.4: Yet he was the one who took up our sicknesses and carried our pain. The contrast (aken — surely, yet) corrects the misreading: we thought he was being struck by God; in fact he was bearing ours. The taking-up vocabulary (nasa) is the same used for the scapegoat (Lev 16:22) and for priestly sin-bearing. Matthew quotes this verse (Matt 8:17) applying it to Jesus' healing ministry — the bearing of infirmities was the pattern of the Servant's entire ministry, not only his death.
53.5: He was pierced because of our transgressions, crushed because of our iniquities. The preposition is min + cause — his wounds are directly caused by our sin. The punishment that brought us shalom was upon him (shalom, disp 2): the chastisement (musar) that produces comprehensive wellbeing for us fell on him. By his wounds we are healed — the healing is grounded in the wounding. Shalom here is not merely peace as reconciliation but the full restoration of every dimension of life that was broken by sin. This is the most direct substitutionary statement in the Hebrew Bible; the NT applies it to the atonement at 1 Pet 2:24.
53.6: The communal confession: we all went astray like sheep; each turned to his own way. The sheep-going-astray image is universal and specific — the aimless wandering of the sheep who has no shepherd. The LORD laid on him the iniquity of us all — the active subject is YHWH, and the verb (hifgiya) means to cause to meet, to lay upon. The cosmic transaction is not the Servant's unilateral act but YHWH's purposeful placing of the community's collective guilt upon the Servant. This is the heart of substitutionary atonement.
53.7: The Servant's silence under oppression: he never opened his mouth. The doubled simile — lamb to slaughter, sheep before shearers — emphasizes absolute non-resistance. This is not weakness but purposeful submission to the role YHWH has assigned. Philip uses this verse in Acts 8:32-35 to explain the gospel to the Ethiopian eunuch; the early church consistently read the silence as Jesus' silence before Pilate (Mark 14:61, 15:5).
53.8: By a perversion of justice he was taken away — a legal inversion; the just one is condemned by unjust process. Who gave any thought to his fate? — his generation did not consider the meaning of what was happening. He was cut off from the land of the living — the language of violent premature death. Because of my people's transgression, the stroke fell on him — again the substitutionary logic: the stroke aimed at the transgressors meets the Servant instead.
53.9: He was assigned a grave among the wicked, yet his burial was with the rich. The double burial notice corresponds to the paradox of the entire song: the Servant ends where he does not deserve to end because his death was not for his own sins. He had done no violence, and no deceit was in his mouth — the Servant's moral innocence is explicit. The NT applies v 9 to Jesus' burial in Joseph of Arimathea's tomb (Matt 27:57-60).
53.10: Yet it was the LORD's will to crush him and make him suffer. The crushing is YHWH's purposive act, not merely permitted tragedy. When his nefesh (soul/life, disp 3) is given as a guilt offering (asham) — the asham is the reparation offering of Lev 5-6, given when one has incurred liability by trespassing on what belongs to another. The Servant's nefesh — his whole self, his life — is the reparation that settles the debt. The result: he will see offspring, prolong his days, and YHWH's purpose will prosper. Life follows the asham-death; vindication follows crushing.
53.11: Out of the anguish of his nefesh (soul, disp 3) he will see and be satisfied. The sight after suffering — the Servant sees the result of his asham and is content. By his knowledge my righteous servant will make many righteous — yada knowledge (disp 2): the Servant's experiential knowing, the same knowing-suffering of v 3, is now the instrument of justification. He bears their iniquities (yisbol — same root as v 4). The many who are made righteous are the counterpart of the we all who went astray (v 6).
53.12: Therefore I will give him a portion among the great, and he will share the plunder with the strong — because he poured out his nefesh (soul, disp 3) to death and was counted among the transgressors. Three grounds for the vindication-reward: he poured out his nefesh, he was numbered with rebels, he bore the sin of many and made intercession for transgressors. The intercession continues after the atoning death — the Servant's advocacy does not end at the cross. Luke 22:37 quotes this verse as Jesus' own self-interpretation at the Last Supper.

54.1: The barren woman who never gave birth is called to sing — the reversal of the deepest human shame in the ancient world. The children of the desolate woman will be more than the children of the one who is married. The barren-Zion metaphor follows directly from the Servant's death and vindication (53:10-12): the offspring the Servant sees after his asham-death are the children of barren Zion's abundance. Paul quotes this verse in Gal 4:27 as the freedom of the heavenly Jerusalem — the mother of those born of promise, not of law.
54.2: Enlarge the tent — the nomadic image of a family whose expansion requires a bigger dwelling. Zion must prepare for growth she cannot yet see. Do not hold back, lengthen your cords, strengthen your pegs — the structural details of tent-expansion make the command concrete. The faith called for is to act on the promise before seeing the evidence.
54.3: You will spread out to the right and to the left; your descendants will reclaim nations and resettle desolate cities. The expansion language uses the same vocabulary as the Abrahamic land promises (Gen 28:14). Zion's multiplication fulfills the patriarchal covenant — another link in the chain from Abraham (51:2) through the Servant to the restored community.
54.4: Do not be afraid; you will not be put to shame. For you will forget the shame of your youth and remember no more the disgrace of your widowhood. Two past shames: the youth (Egypt, the formative humiliation) and the widowhood (the exile, the loss of the divine husband). Both are swallowed up in the new marriage YHWH announces in v 5.
54.5: Your Maker is your husband — the two most comprehensive divine titles collapse into the marriage metaphor. YHWH of hosts (creator of the cosmos, commander of its armies), the Holy One of Israel (the utterly transcendent one), the God of the whole earth — all these describe the one who is entering marital covenant with Zion. The shock of the verse is that the incomprehensibly great God is simultaneously the intimate covenant partner of a ruined city.
54.6: The LORD has called you back like a wife who was abandoned and heartbroken — the exile is reread as marital estrangement, not divorce. The wife of one's youth is the first and most beloved; the call back is a restoration to the original relationship's fullness, not merely a legal reconciliation.
54.7: For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with great compassion (rahamim) I will bring you back. The ratio is important: exile is a brief moment; return is great compassion. The quantitative imbalance mirrors the Servant's logic (53:4-6): a bounded act of suffering produces unbounded redemptive result. Rahamim (compassion) shares its root with rehem (womb) — this is maternal-gut compassion, the most visceral human tenderness.
54.8: In a surge of anger I hid my face from you for a moment, but with hesed olam (everlasting covenant love, hesed disp 4, olam disp 3) I will have compassion on you. Hesed is the covenant-loyalty love grounded in the covenant YHWH initiated, not in the worthiness of its object. It is everlasting (olam) — not subject to the fluctuations of anger. The anger was real but momentary; the hesed is constitutive of YHWH's character and therefore permanent. The contrast olam-hesed vs. moment-anger is the theological heart of this passage.
54.9: The Noah covenant becomes the interpretive framework: just as YHWH swore that the waters of Noah would never again cover the earth — and has kept that oath — so he swears never again to be angry with or rebuke Zion. The Noah oath is the precedent for the Zion oath. Both are unconditional, unilateral divine commitments grounded in YHWH's own character, not in human performance.
54.10: Though the mountains should move and the hills be shaken, my hesed (covenant love, disp 4) will not move from you, and my covenant of shalom (berit shalom, berit disp 3, shalom disp 2) will not be shaken. Mountains and hills — the most geologically stable features of the ancient world — become the measure of divine commitment: even if they move, YHWH's covenant will not. The covenant of peace bundles two of the highest-disp terms: the binding formal agreement (berit) and the comprehensive wellbeing (shalom) that is both the content and the goal of the covenant.
54.11: Zion is addressed directly as afflicted city, tossed by storms and without comfort — the same desolate figure as the barren woman of v 1, now about to be rebuilt with precious stones. The gemstone-rebuilding of vv 11-12 is a reversal of the rubble of 51:17-20: the same city lying in ruins will be refounded in splendor. The materials (sapphires, rubies, sparkling jewels, precious stones) recall both the gemstones of the high priest's breastplate and the foundation stones of the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:19-20).
54.12: The architectural specifics — towers, gates, walls — all of precious stones. The gate of sparkling jewels is the point of entry into the city; the precious-stone wall is what protects it. Beauty and security are unified in the rebuilt Zion. Revelation 21 draws directly on this imagery for the new Jerusalem.
54.13: All your children will be taught by the LORD, and great will be their shalom (peace, disp 2). The promised outcome of the covenant of shalom (v 10) is that all Zion's children receive divine instruction directly. This is the new covenant of Jer 31:33-34 — no longer needing to teach neighbor or brother, for all will know YHWH. Jesus quotes this verse in John 6:45 (they will all be taught by God) as the condition of coming to him.
54.14: You will be established in tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3); you will be far from oppression and will not fear. The community's foundation shifts from precarious human power to YHWH's righteousness — the same tsedaqah that the exiles were called to pursue (51:1) and that YHWH was about to enact (51:5). When YHWH's righteousness is the foundation, terror cannot take root.
54.15: If anyone attacks you, it is not my doing — YHWH disavows the coming attacks before they happen. The threat from outsiders will ultimately fail: whoever assembles against you will fall before you. The passive falling is YHWH's active judgment; the city needs only stand in its righteousness.
54.16: YHWH claims credit for creating the blacksmith who forges weapons and the destroyer who wields them. This is the most totalizing sovereignty claim in the passage: the one who makes the weapons and the one who deploys them are both YHWH's creatures. He is not threatened by the weapons made against Zion because he created their makers.
54.17: No weapon formed against you will succeed, and every tongue that accuses you in court you will refute. The legal and military threats are both covered. This is the inheritance (nahalah) of the servants of YHWH, and their tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is from me. The closing verse reframes the entire chapter: safety, vindication, and righteousness are not Zion's own achievement but her covenant inheritance — received from YHWH, declared by him. Nahalah (inheritance) is the Promised Land vocabulary applied to the eschatological community.

55.1: The invitation to all who are thirsty opens without qualification — not all of Israel but all who recognize their need. Come to the waters; come, buy and eat; buy wine and milk without money and without price. The economic paradox — buy without money — strips away the normal mechanism of exchange and declares that what YHWH offers is beyond market logic. Thirst and hunger are the condition of exile; the invitation offers exactly what exile cannot provide. The whole nefesh (soul, disp 3) — the whole self — is what thirsts, and the whole self is what YHWH feeds. Jesus draws on this imagery in John 7:37 and Rev 22:17.
55.2: Why do you spend money on what is not bread, and your wages on what does not satisfy? The question diagnoses the exile's futile labor: working for Babylon, acquiring what cannot feed the soul. Listen carefully to me and eat what is good; let your nefesh (soul, disp 3) delight in richness. The alternative to dissatisfying toil is to incline the ear to YHWH — hearing is the mode of access to the banquet. This is the economics of grace: the satisfying food is received by attention, not by labor.
55.3: Incline your ear and come to me; hear, and you will live; and I will make an everlasting covenant (berit olam, berit disp 3, olam disp 3) with you — my reliable hesed (covenant love, disp 4) promised to David. The Davidic covenant — the unconditional promise of 2 Sam 7 — is the model and the content of what YHWH now extends to the whole community. The individual king's covenant is democratized: all who hear and come participate in the Davidic promises. Berit olam and hesed David are the two deepest covenant terms, stacked together to name what YHWH is offering.
55.4: David was made a witness, leader, and commander for the peoples — his role was never merely national. The Davidic covenant always had a universal scope (Ps 2, 72). By democratizing the Davidic covenant (v 3), YHWH is extending the universal witness-function to the whole restored community.
55.5: You will summon a nation you did not know, and a nation that did not know you will run to you, because of the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel. The expansion echoes 54:3 and the Abrahamic promise: Zion becomes the center to which nations stream. The motivation is entirely YHWH's initiative — the nations are drawn not by Israel's attractiveness but by YHWH's glory revealed through Israel.
55.6: Seek the LORD while he may be found; call on him while he is near. The urgency is temporal — there is a window. This is not simply a timeless invitation but a kairos-moment call. The exilic community stands at the turning point when YHWH is near in the sense that the announcement of return (52:7-12) has been made; delay means missing the moment of return.
55.7: Let the wicked forsake his way and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return to the LORD, who will have mercy (rahem — womb-compassion), and to our God, who will abundantly pardon. The return YHWH requires is not first geographic (return from Babylon) but moral (forsake wickedness). The pardon is abundant — yarbeh lisloak, literally will multiply to pardon. YHWH's forgiveness is not reluctant or minimal but extravagant. The double mercy (rahem and pardon) grounds the call to return.
55.8: For my thoughts are not your thoughts, and your ways are not my ways. This is the foundation of the abundant-pardon verse: YHWH pardons more than humans think possible because he thinks and acts on a different plane. The for connects the limitless pardon (v 7) to the limitless otherness of YHWH's mind (vv 8-9). This is also the ground for the sovereign initiative of vv 1-6: YHWH invites, acts, and accomplishes in ways that transcend human calculation.
55.9: As the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts higher than your thoughts. The spatial image gives scale to the qualitative difference. This is not skepticism about human reason but an assertion that YHWH's thoughts and ways belong to a different order of magnitude. The invitation to seek the LORD (v 6) is therefore both urgent and humble: we seek one whose ways we cannot anticipate or control.
55.10: The word of YHWH is compared to rain and snow that come down from heaven — they do not return until they have accomplished their purpose: watered the earth, made it sprout, given seed and bread. The hydrological cycle becomes a metaphor for the efficacy of divine speech: just as precipitation achieves its agricultural purpose without fail, so YHWH's word achieves its purpose without fail.
55.11: So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish what I purpose and succeed in the thing for which I sent it. The word sent here is the entire promise-complex of chapters 40-55: comfort, return, Servant, covenant. This verse is the retroactive authorization of everything proclaimed in these chapters. The word does not merely describe what will happen; it is itself the agent that brings it about. John 1:1-3 and Heb 1:2-3 inhabit the logic of this verse in their identification of the eternal Word as the agent of creation and redemption.
55.12: For you will go out with joy and be led forth in shalom (peace, disp 2). The exodus-language: go out (yetse'u) and be led forth (tuvalun) are exodus verbs. But the second exodus is marked by joy and shalom rather than haste and fear (52:12). The mountains and hills shall burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands — creation joins the processional, extending the singing-ruins of 52:9 to the entire natural world. The new exodus is a cosmic event, not merely a political one.
55.13: Instead of the thorn the cypress shall come up, and instead of the brier the myrtle shall come up. The transformation of the wilderness through which the exiles return is a sign (ot) and an everlasting memorial (olam, disp 3) that will not be cut off. The sign function of the transformed landscape: creation's changed state is a standing testimony to what YHWH has done. The covenant of shalom (54:10) and the everlasting covenant (55:3) are written in the renewed creation as well as in the community's life.

60.1: qûmî 'ôrî — Arise, shine!. Both verbs are Qal imperatives feminine singular addressing Zion as a person. The imperative of 'ûr (אוֹר — to be/become light) is rare in this Qal form; more commonly the sun shines — here Zion herself is commanded to radiate. kî bā' 'ôrēk̠ — for your light has come — the Qal perfect bā' frames the light's arrival as already accomplished from the divine perspective. ûk̠ĕbôd YHWH 'ālayik̠ zāraḥ — and the glory of YHWH has risen upon you — the verb zāraḥ (זָרַח) is the sunrise word (cf. Ps 84:12); YHWH's glory rises on Zion as the sun rises over the horizon.
60.2: kî-hinnēh haḥōšek̠ yĕk̠assè-'āreṣ — for behold, darkness covers the earth. The verb k̠āsāh (Pi'el, yĕk̠assè — to cover completely) describes total envelopment. wa'ărāp̄el lĕ'ummîm — thick darkness the peoples. The contrast: wĕ'āl-ayik̠ yizraḥ YHWH — but upon you YHWH will rise — the same sunrise verb (zāraḥ) as v. 1, now with YHWH as subject. ûk̠ĕb̠ôdô 'ālayik̠ yērā'eh — and his glory will appear upon you.
60.3: wĕhāl ĕkû gôyim lĕ'ôrēk̠ — and nations shall walk by your light. The verb hālak̠ (to walk) with prepositional lĕ (toward/by) — nations navigate by Zion's light as mariners navigate by a star. ûmĕlāk̠îm lĕnōgah zarḥēk̠ — and kings to the brightness of your rising. The noun nōgah (radiance, brilliance — from נָגַהּ) and the infinitive/construct zarḥēk̠ (your sunrise). The reversal of ch. 2's darkness is complete: what was thick darkness over nations becomes a luminous beacon drawing them.
60.4: ś'î-sābîb̠ 'ênayik̠ ûrĕ'î — lift up your eyes around and see. The imperative ś'î (Qal imperative feminine of נָשָׂא — to lift) combined with ûrĕ'î (and see). The returnees: sons from far (mirrāḥôq) and daughters mĕ'ummānôt 'al-ṣad — carried at the side. The verb 'āman (Pi'el participle, mĕ'ummānôt) means nursed/carried — the daughters are not infants but are escorted/supported at the side (cf. Num 11:12).
60.5: 'āz tirĕ'î wĕnāhārtĕ — then you will see and be radiant. The verb nāhar (נָהַר) is deliberately polysemous: it means both to flow/stream (as a river) and to be radiant/shine (as a face lit with joy). Both meanings are active — Zion floods with joy and glows with it. wĕpāḥad wĕrāḥab̠ lĕb̠āb̠ēk̠ — your heart shall tremble and expand — pāḥad (to tremble with awe) and rāḥab̠ (to widen/enlarge) as simultaneous responses; awe-struck expansion.
60.6: šipʿat gĕmallîm tĕk̠assēk̠ — a multitude of camels shall cover you. The noun šip̄'āh (abundance, multitude) with gĕmallîm (camels) — caravans from Midian and Ephah and Sheba bringing gold (zāhāb̠) and frankincense (lĕb̠ônāh). The combination of gold and frankincense is specific; Matthew 2:11 (the Magi's gifts) draws on this passage. ûtĕhillot YHWH yĕb̠aśśērû — and they shall bring good news of the praises of YHWH — the Pi'el yĕb̠aśśērû (from בָּשַׂר — to bring good news; same root as bĕśôrāh and NT euangelion).
60.7: kol-ṣō'n qēdār yiqqāb̠ĕṣû lāk̠ — all the flocks of Kedar shall be gathered for you. The Niphal yiqqāb̠ĕṣû (to be gathered) as divine passive — it is YHWH who gathers them. Rams of Nebaioth ('êlê nĕb̠āyôt) shall minister on the altar. wĕb̠êt tip̄'artî 'ăp̄ā'ēr — and I will glorify my glorious house — the Piel 'ăp̄ā'ēr (from פָּאַר — to beautify, adorn, glorify) applied to the temple by YHWH himself.
60.8: mî-'ēlleh k̠ā'āb̠ tĕ'ûp̄ēnāh — who are these that fly like a cloud?. The image is of ships and doves converging on Zion from the sea. wĕk̠ayyônîm 'el-'ărubbōtêhem — and like doves to their windows/dovecotes — 'ărubbāh (latticed window, opening) is the word used for the windows of heaven in Gen 7:11; doves return instinctively to their home-openings.
60.9: kî-lî 'iyyîm yĕqawwû — for to me the coastlands wait. The verb qiwwāh (Pi'el — to wait, hope intensely) shares its root with tiqwāh (hope); the coastlands' waiting is active, expectant hope. wĕ'oniyyôt taršîš bārî'šōnāh — ships of Tarshish in the lead. lĕhābî' bānayik̠ mērāḥôq — to bring your sons from far off; zāhāb̠ām ûk̠isp̄ām — their gold and silver with them, lĕšēm YHWH 'ĕlōhayik̠ — for the name of YHWH your God.
60.10: ûb̠ānû b̠ĕnê-nēk̠ār ḥômōtayik̠ — foreigners shall build your walls. The noun nēk̠ār (נֵכָר — foreign, alien) marks these builders as Gentiles — the social reversal is complete. ûmalkêhem yĕšārĕtûnĕk̠ — and their kings shall serve you — the Pi'el yĕšārĕtûnĕk̠ (from שָׁרַת — to minister, serve in the sense of priestly/royal service) applied to foreign kings. The theological ground: kî b̠ĕqiṣpî hikkîtîk̠ ûb̠irĕṣônî riḥamtîk̠ — for in my wrath I struck you but in my favor I have had mercy on you.
60.11: wĕpit ĕḥû šĕ'ārayik̠ tāmîd — your gates shall be open continually.
